title: The Beast Unchained
content:
The bass thumped through Liam Chen's chest like a second heartbeat.

Sweat and perfume hung thick in the air of the rooftop party, mixing with the sharp tang of cheap beer. Bodies pressed against him from all sides, laughing, grinding, living. Someone shoved a red cup into his hand. He took a sip. Bitter. He took another anyway.

"Bro, you good?" Marcus shouted over the music, his gold chain glinting under the string lights. "You look like you've seen a ghost."

Liam forced a smile. "Just tired."

Tired was a lie. Terrified was closer.

The moon hung overhead, fat and full and wrong. It always felt wrong. Like something pressing against the inside of his skull, waiting to break through. The watch on his wrist read 11:47 PM. Thirteen minutes until midnight. Thirteen minutes until his twenty-first birthday ended and whatever came next began.

He'd blacked out during every full moon since he was sixteen. Always woke up somewhere strange. Once in a tree. Once in the river. Once in the neighbor's backyard with his clothes torn to ribbons and blood caked under his fingernails.

His parents said it was sleepwalking. The doctors said seizures. Liam knew better.

He just didn't know what.

"You sure?" Marcus squinted at him. "You're pale as hell, man."

"I'm fine." Liam drained the cup. The alcohol did nothing. "Just need some air."

He pushed through the crowd, past girls in short dresses and guys flexing for Instagram stories. The roof door led to a stairwell. Empty. Quiet. The moon shone through a small window, casting silver light across the concrete floor.

11:52.

His skin itched.

Not on the surface. Underneath. Like something was trying to claw its way out from beneath his bones. He scratched his forearm. The itch spread. He scratched harder, drawing red lines across his skin.

11:55.

The pain hit.

Not gradual. Not a warning. A fist of fire slammed into his gut and exploded outward. Every nerve in his body lit up at once. His knees buckled. He hit the floor hard, cracking his chin against the concrete.

*No. No no no. Not here. Not now.*

The moon screamed through the window. He could hear it. A high, keening note that vibrated through his teeth and rattled his spine.

His bones began to break.

The sound was worse than the pain. Wet snaps like twigs under boots. His arms twisted. His legs stretched. His spine curved and cracked and reformed into something no longer human. Muscle tore and regrew. Skin split and sealed.

He tried to scream. His throat reshaped. The sound came out wrong.

*Animal. Beast. Wolf.*

The last human thought that passed through his mind was *I'm sorry* — and then there was nothing.

Nothing but the moon.

Nothing but the hunt.

---

Consciousness returned in fragments.

First: smell. Pine needles and damp earth. Copper — thick and metallic. Blood. So much blood.

Second: sound. Wind through leaves. Water dripping. A heartbeat. Fast. Human. Terrified.

Third: sight.

Liam opened his eyes to a canopy of trees. Moonlight filtered through the branches in silver shafts, illuminating a nightmare.

He was naked. Crouched in a clearing he'd never seen before. His body ached with a deep, satisfied exhaustion — like after the hardest workout of his life, multiplied by a hundred.

His hands — no, not hands. They were hands again. Human hands. But they were caked with drying blood up to the elbows.

The blood was everywhere. Splattered across his chest. Streaked over his face. Pooling in the hollows of his collarbone. He opened his mouth to breathe and tasted iron.

*Whose blood?*

He looked around the clearing. Empty. No bodies. No signs of struggle except for broken branches and torn earth. But the blood was fresh. Still warm in places.

The human heartbeat came from behind him.

Liam spun — and moved faster than he'd ever moved in his life. One moment he was on the ground. The next he was standing, balanced on the balls of his feet, muscles coiled and ready.

A woman stood at the edge of the clearing.

She was young. Maybe his age. Dark hair fell past her shoulders in a tangled curtain. Her clothes were torn — a ripped jacket, a scratched cheek. She held a hunting knife in front of her with both hands, trembling.

"I don't want to hurt you," she said.

Her voice shook. Her eyes didn't.

They were silver. Not grey. Not blue. True silver, like liquid mercury caught in a jar. They glowed faintly in the moonlight.

"What—" Liam's voice cracked. "What happened? Did I—"

"You shifted." She lowered the knife a fraction. "First time?"

*Shifted.*

The word hit him like a train. He'd heard it before. In movies. In books. In the dark corners of forums he'd visited at 3 AM when he couldn't sleep and the moon pressed too hard against his mind.

Werewolves.

"I'm not—" he started.

"You are." She stepped closer. The knife stayed ready. "The full moon called your bloodline. It always does. You're lucky you didn't kill anyone."

"Did I kill—"

"No." She shook her head. "Deer. Two of them. You hunted and fed and collapsed. I've been watching you for the past three hours."

*Watching me.* The words should have been creepy. Instead, they made him feel something else. Something animal. *She saw me change. She knows what I am.*

"Who are you?"

"Someone who's been looking for you." She sheathed the knife. "My name is Mei. I'm from the Crescent Moon Pack. And you, Liam Chen, are the last living heir of the Wolf King bloodline."

The name hit him like ice water.

Wolf King. He'd heard that too. In the same dark corners of the internet. In legends whispered by people who smelled wrong and moved too quietly in the dark. The Wolf King was a myth. A story. A title passed down through an ancient bloodline that hadn't surfaced in a century.

"That's impossible," he said.

"You just ripped apart two deer with your bare teeth." Mei raised an eyebrow. "Nothing's impossible."

Liam looked down at his hands. The blood was drying now, flaking off in dark red scales. He flexed his fingers. They moved perfectly. No pain. No damage. But underneath the skin, he could feel something new. A current. A warmth. A power that hummed in time with his heartbeat.

And underneath that — the moon. Still pulling. Still calling.

"We don't have much time," Mei said. "The Rogue Moon Pack knows about you. They've been tracking the bloodline for years. When you shifted tonight, you lit up like a beacon. They'll be here by sunrise."

"Rogue Moon—"

"Bad people." Her voice hardened. "They want your blood. Literally. The Wolf King's essence can be extracted during the first seven days after awakening. They'll use it to strengthen their Alpha."

"Seven days?"

"The Blood Moon ceremony. Seven days from now, the moon reaches its peak. If you haven't mastered your transformation by then, the wolf takes permanent control. You become a beast. Mindless. Hungry."

Liam's stomach turned. He remembered the feeling of giving up control. The terror of watching his own body move without his permission. The hunger — God, the hunger — that had consumed him completely.

"The wolf," he whispered. "It's still there. I can feel it."

"Good." Mei smiled. It wasn't a warm smile. "That means you're still alive. If you couldn't feel it, you'd already be lost."

She walked toward him. Close enough to touch. Her silver eyes searched his face, looking for something. Whatever she found made her nod once.

"You're stronger than you know," she said. "The Wolf King bloodline doesn't awaken in weak hosts. You survived your first shift. You didn't kill a human. You didn't lose yourself completely. That's more than most can say."

"What do I do now?"

"Come with me. Learn to control it. Master your bloodline before the Blood Moon." She extended her hand. "Or stay here and wait for the Rogues to find you. Your choice."

The wind shifted. Liam caught her scent — jasmine and rain and something sharp underneath. Like moonlight distilled into a physical form. It sent a shiver down his spine that wasn't entirely fear.

He took her hand.

Her fingers were cold. Strong. She pulled him to his feet with surprising force.

"Good," she said. "First lesson: clothes. You can't fight a war naked."

Liam laughed. It came out raw and broken, but it was a laugh. "Is that in the werewolf handbook?"

"The unwritten rules." Mei turned and walked into the trees. "Stay close. Keep quiet. And whatever you hear — don't look back."

He followed.

The forest swallowed them. Dark and alive and humming with things he couldn't name. His new senses painted the world in layers — the whisper of mice in their burrows, the rustle of owls in the branches, the distant howl of something that made his blood answer.

Behind them, in the clearing, Liam's abandoned watch ticked past midnight.

Day one of seven had begun.

title: The Scent of Moon-Spilled Blood
content:
The warehouse smelled like rust and old rain.

Liam pressed his back against the concrete wall, chest heaving. The chain-link fence gate still rattled from where he'd kicked it open. His hands wouldn't stop shaking.

*She knew his name.*

He wiped blood from his split lip — when had that happened? His knuckles were raw, skin peeled back to pink meat. The full moon hung fat and silver through the broken skylight above, and something in his chest *pulled* toward it like a fish on a line.

"Get up."

The voice came from the shadows. Low. Female. Familiar.

Liam's head snapped up. The woman from the alley stood ten feet away, arms crossed, leaning against a support beam like she owned the place. Moonlight caught the edge of her jaw, the curve of her hip. She wore black tactical pants and a fitted tank top that showed off the coiled tension in her shoulders.

"You're bleeding on the floor," she said.

"Who the hell are you?"

"Someone who just saved your life." She pushed off the beam and walked toward him, boots clicking on wet concrete. "Those rogues would have ripped your throat out if I hadn't interrupted."

"They were *werewolves*."

"No shit."

Liam's brain felt like it was trying to process a traffic accident in slow motion. The alley. The glowing eyes. The way his body had *moved* without his permission. He looked down at his hands again.

The knuckles were already healing.

"What's happening to me?"

The woman stopped three feet away. Close enough that he caught her scent — jasmine and steel, with something wild underneath. Her eyes were the color of honey in sunlight, and they studied him like he was a puzzle she'd been trying to solve for years.

"My name is Mei Lin," she said. "And you're a Wolf King."

"I'm a *what*?"

"An alpha bloodline that hasn't manifested in three hundred years." She pulled a knife from her boot — silver blade, bone handle, wrapped in leather cord. Liam flinched, but she just tossed it on the ground between them. "Test yourself. Cut your palm."

"What? No."

"You want proof?" Mei Lin's voice hardened. "Cut your palm. If you heal before the blood hits the floor, you're what I say you are."

Liam stared at the knife. The blade gleamed under the moon.

He picked it up.

The cut was quick — a shallow line across his left palm. Blood welled up, dark red, and then...

It stopped.

The skin knitted together like someone had hit rewind on a film reel. Three seconds, maybe four. No scar. No pain. Just smooth, unbroken flesh.

Liam's vision went gray at the edges.

"There's more," Mei Lin said. "The blackouts during full moons. The dreams of running through forests. The way you've always felt *wrong* in your own skin." She crouched down, bringing her face level with his. "You've been fighting this your whole life, Liam. You don't have to fight anymore."

"How do you know my name?"

"I've been watching you since you turned eighteen. Three years of full moons. Three years of making sure the rogues didn't find you before you awakened."

The words hit him like a punch to the chest. "You've been *stalking* me?"

"Protecting you. There's a difference."

"Not from where I'm standing."

Mei Lin's jaw tightened. She stood and walked to the warehouse's broken window, staring out at the moon. "The rogue pack that attacked you tonight — they're called the Shadow Fangs. They've been hunting for a Wolf King bloodline for decades. Your awakening sent out a pulse that every werewolf within fifty miles felt."

Liam's stomach dropped. "So more are coming."

"Already on their way." She turned back to face him. "You have seven days."

"Until what?"

"The Blood Moon ceremony. It happens when the moon reaches its deepest red phase. If you haven't mastered your transformation by then, your soul belongs to the wolf forever."

"Mastered my—" Liam stood up so fast his head spun. "I don't even know what I *am*. You're talking about ceremonies and bloodlines and rogue packs like this is some kind of—"

"War," Mei Lin cut in. "It's a war. And you're the weapon both sides want."

Silence stretched between them. Somewhere outside, a dog howled. The sound made Liam's skin prickle with goosebumps.

"Why me?"

"Because the Wolf King bloodline chooses its vessel. It picked you for a reason."

"What reason?"

Mei Lin's expression flickered — something that might have been pain, or regret, or both. "The last Wolf King died in the Great Purge of 1892. The Shadow Fangs cut out his heart while he was still breathing and used it to power their alpha. Without a Wolf King to balance the clans, the rogue packs have been growing stronger for a century."

"And you want me to... what? Fight them?"

"I want you to survive long enough to try."

Liam laughed. It came out hollow and broken. "I work at a *coffee shop*. I live in a studio apartment with a leaky faucet and a roommate who steals my protein bars. I'm not a warrior. I'm not a king. I'm just..."

He trailed off, because the truth was staring him in the face.

He'd never fit in. Never felt like he belonged anywhere. The restlessness that kept him up at night, the way his temper would flare hot and dangerous, the hunger that food never quite satisfied.

*You've been fighting this your whole life.*

Mei Lin walked back to him. This time, she stopped inches away. Up close, he could see the scars — thin white lines across her collarbone, a jagged one through her left eyebrow. She smelled like gunpowder and survival.

"I know this is a lot," she said. "But you don't have time to process it. The Shadow Fangs will regroup and attack again before sunrise. I can teach you the basics — how to call your wolf, how to control the shift — but you have to trust me."

"Trust you? I don't even know you."

"You don't have to know me. You just have to decide if you want to live."

The warehouse creaked around them. Wind rattled the skylight. Liam looked down at his healed palm, at the smooth skin that had been bleeding thirty seconds ago.

Somewhere deep in his chest, something stirred. A beast waking from a long, long sleep.

"One question," he said.

"Ask."

"That night in the alley — the first time I blacked out. I was eighteen. There was a man with a knife."

Mei Lin went very still.

"He was trying to rob me," Liam continued. "I remember the blade. I remember the fear. And then I woke up three blocks away with blood under my fingernails and no idea how I got there."

The silence stretched like a wire about to snap.

"I never found his body," Liam said. "But I found his wallet. In my pocket."

Mei Lin's honey-colored eyes held his gaze. She didn't blink.

"He was a Shadow Fang scout," she said. "Sent to evaluate your bloodline strength. When you killed him, it bought you three more years of relative safety. They assumed the Wolf King had awakened somewhere else."

"I *killed* him."

"You protected yourself. Your wolf knew what to do, even if your human mind didn't."

Liam's hands were shaking again. But this time, it wasn't fear.

It was rage.

All those years of feeling broken. All those nights waking up in strange places with no memory. The therapists, the medications, the way his parents had looked at him like he was a bomb waiting to go off.

And now he knew why.

"I want to learn," he said. The words came out rough, scraped raw. "I want to control it. I want to make sure I never black out again."

Mei Lin nodded slowly. "That's the first step."

"What's the second?"

She reached into her jacket and pulled out a folded piece of paper. When she opened it, Liam saw a map — hand-drawn, detailed, marked with symbols he didn't recognize.

"This is the location of the first Blood Moon temple. It's hidden in the mountains outside the city. Every Wolf King before you trained there."

"Trained for what?"

Mei Lin's expression hardened into something ancient and sad.

"To die properly."

She handed him the map. His fingers brushed hers, and for a split second, he saw it — a flash of memory that wasn't his. A man with amber eyes standing in moonlight. A woman with her throat cut, blood pooling at his feet. The scent of jasmine and iron.

Then it was gone.

Mei Lin pulled her hand back like she'd been burned.

"Don't touch me again," she said. Her voice was ice.

"What was that?"

"Nothing. A side effect of my bloodline." She turned away, but not before Liam saw the fear flicker across her face. "We need to move. Dawn is three hours away, and I can smell them getting closer."

"Smell who?"

She was already walking toward the warehouse's back exit. "The Shadow Fangs sent a tracking team. They've got our scent."

Liam's heart hammered. He looked at the map in his hands, at the symbols he couldn't read, at the woman who knew more about him than he knew about himself.

Seven days.

Seven days to become a king, or die trying.

He folded the map and shoved it in his pocket.

"Mei Lin."

She paused at the door.

"Why did you save me tonight? Why not just let them take me?"

The moonlight caught her face, and for a moment, she looked almost human. Almost vulnerable.

"Because my grandmother was the last Wolf King's mate," she said. "And she watched him die. I made a promise that I wouldn't let it happen again."

Her voice cracked on the last word.

Then she pushed open the door and stepped into the night.

Liam followed.

The city stretched out before them, towers of glass and steel gleaming under the moon. Somewhere in those shadows, monsters were hunting. And somewhere in his blood, a wolf was waking up.

"The temple," he said. "How do we get there?"

Mei Lin looked at him. Her eyes glowed faintly gold in the dark.

"We run."

She shifted.

It happened in the space between one breath and the next — her bones breaking and reforming, her skin rippling into dark silver fur, her spine arching as her body grew and twisted. The sound was wet and terrible and beautiful.

Where Mei Lin had stood, a wolf now sat.

She was massive — easily the size of a horse, with eyes like molten gold and a coat that seemed to drink the moonlight. She looked at Liam and let out a soft chuff.

*Come,* the sound seemed to say. *Follow.*

Liam's blood sang.

The beast in his chest surged forward, and for the first time in his life, he didn't try to hold it back.

He ran.

The warehouse walls blurred past. The chain-link fence tore like paper as he crashed through it. His legs moved faster than they ever had, his lungs burned, but the wolf inside him howled with joy.

*Yes. This. This is what we were meant for.*

Behind him, the Shadow Fangs' howls split the night.

They were coming.

And Liam — the boy who'd never belonged anywhere — was finally going home.

The rooftops blurred beneath his feet as he

title: THE MOON'S FIRST TEST
content:
The alley smelled of wet concrete and copper.

Liam pressed his back against the brick wall, fingers digging into the mortar. His chest heaved. The streetlights flickered overhead, casting long shadows that twisted like living things.

"You're shaking."

He looked down at his hands. They trembled, but not from cold. The nails had thickened. Darkened. When he blinked, the world sharpened into impossible detail — every crack in the pavement, every grain of dust suspended in the air.

"What did you do to me?" His voice came out rough, like gravel scraping against stone.

The woman stood three meters away, arms crossed. She'd pulled her hood down, revealing sharp features and eyes that caught the moonlight like pools of mercury. Her hair fell in black waves past her shoulders, and when she moved, the air seemed to part around her.

"I didn't do anything." She tilted her head. "That was all you."

"Bullshit." Liam pushed off the wall. His legs felt wrong — too strong, too fast. Every step ate ground like he'd been walking on springs his whole life. "I've never— I blacked out. I always black out during full moons. But this time I remember everything."

"You remember because you didn't shift."

The words hit him like a punch to the chest.

"I didn't—" He stopped. Looked down at his body. Normal. Human. Fingers still ten, toes still ten, no fur, no claws, no snout. "But the moon was full. It's always full."

"Full moons trigger the shift in common wolves." She stepped closer. The movement was fluid, predatory. "You're not common, Liam Chen. Your bloodline runs deeper than most."

"How do you know my name?"

"I know a lot of things." She stopped an arm's length away. Close enough that he caught her scent — jasmine and something else, something sharp and electric. "I know your mother was human. I know your father never told you what he was. I know the Fang's Breath pack has been hunting you since you turned eighteen."

The world tilted.

"My father was a truck driver. He died when I was twelve. Heart attack."

"Convenient story." Her lips curved, but there was no warmth in it. "Zhao Wei was the Wolf King's right hand before the purge. He didn't die of a heart attack. He was executed."

Liam's vision went red.

"Shut up." The words tore from his throat. "You don't know anything about my father."

"I know he died protecting you. I know he burned his own soul to seal your bloodline so the other packs couldn't sense you." She held his gaze, unflinching. "And I know that seal broke three hours ago when you turned twenty-one."

The streetlight above them exploded.

Glass rained down. Liam threw his arms up, shielding his face. When he looked again, the woman hadn't moved. She stood in the sudden darkness, and her eyes glowed silver.

"You need to come with me."

"Like hell I do." He backed away. His heart hammered against his ribs. "I don't know you. I don't know what you are. I don't know any of this."

"You know it's true." She followed each step he took backward. "You feel it. The strength. The senses. The hunger."

His stomach clenched.

Because she was right. He did feel it. A pressure building behind his eyes, a heat spreading through his veins. The hunger she mentioned — it wasn't for food. It was for something else. Something primal.

"I can help you control it." Her voice dropped. Soft now. Almost gentle. "Or you can let it consume you. Those are your only choices."

"And if I choose neither?"

"Then the other packs will find you." She gestured at the sky. The clouds had parted, revealing the full moon in all its terrible glory. "That seal your father died to create? It's gone now. Every alpha within a hundred kilometers felt your bloodline ignite. They'll be coming."

"Let them come." The words felt foreign in his mouth. Braver than he felt. "I didn't ask for this. I don't want it."

"It doesn't care what you want." She grabbed his wrist. Her grip was iron. "The moon chose you the moment you were born. All you can do now is survive long enough to understand why."

He tried to pull away. Her fingers didn't budge.

"I'm not going anywhere with you."

"Yes, you are." She smiled, and this time it reached her eyes. "Because in seven days, it's the Blood Moon. And if you haven't learned to control the shift by then, your soul belongs to the wolf forever."

A phone buzzed.

His phone.

Liam fumbled for it with his free hand. The screen showed an unknown number. He answered without thinking.

"Liam Chen." The voice on the other end was male, smooth as silk, cold as winter steel. "I'm told you've finally awakened. Congratulations."

"Who is this?"

"My name is irrelevant. What matters is that I represent interests that would very much like to meet you." A pause. "I have a proposition."

"I'm not interested."

"You will be." The voice laughed, soft and unpleasant. "Because I know why your father really died. I know what the Wolf King hid before the purge. And I know that the woman standing next to you right now is the reason your bloodline was sealed in the first place."

Liam's eyes snapped to the woman.

She'd gone still. Her grip on his wrist tightened.

"Don't listen to him," she said.

"Too late for that, I'm afraid." The voice on the phone continued. "Ask her about the Nightfall Pact. Ask her about the promise she made to your father. Ask her why she was there the night he died."

The line went dead.

Silence stretched between them, thick and heavy. The woman's hand remained locked around his wrist, but her face had paled.

"What's the Nightfall Pact?" Liam asked.

She didn't answer.

"What promise did you make to my father?"

Her jaw tightened. The silver in her eyes flickered, dimmed, then flared brighter than before.

"He asked me to protect you," she said finally. "I agreed."

"From what?"

"From yourself." She released his wrist. Stepped back. The distance between them felt like a canyon. "Your father knew what you would become when the seal broke. He knew the wolf inside you was different. Stronger. Hungrier." She met his eyes. "He asked me to kill you if you couldn't control it."

The words hung in the air, cold and final.

Liam's blood ran ice.

"He asked you to—"

"I said yes." Her voice was flat. "I gave him my word. The Nightfall Pact is binding. Unbreakable." She raised her hand, and moonlight pooled in her palm, coalescing into a blade of silver light. "If you can't master the shift before the Blood Moon, I will end you myself."

The blade hummed with power.

Liam stared at it. At her. At the woman who was supposed to save him, who was instead his executioner.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you deserve to know the truth." She closed her fist. The blade dissolved into motes of light. "And because I'd rather train you than kill you."

"Some choice."

"It's the only one you have." She turned and started walking toward the mouth of the alley. "Come. We have seven days. We start now."

Liam didn't move.

The moon hung overhead, pregnant with light. The wolf inside him stirred, hungry and impatient. Everything he'd known about his life, his father, himself — lies.

And somewhere in the city, the man on the phone was laughing.

He took a step forward.

Then stopped.

His phone buzzed again.

Unknown number. Same as before.

He answered.

"You made the right choice," the voice said. "But I need you to understand something, Liam. The woman you're following? She's not your ally. She's your jailer." A pause. "I can tell you how to break the pact. I can tell you how to become strong enough to kill her before she kills you."

"And what do you want in return?"

"Your cooperation." The voice was silk wrapped around steel. "The Blood Moon rises in seven days. When it does, the Wolf King's bloodline will awaken fully. And I want to be there when it happens."

The line went dead again.

Liam looked up.

The woman had stopped at the alley's entrance. She was watching him, moonlight silvering her features, hand half-raised like she expected him to run.

He didn't run.

He walked toward her, phone clutched in his fist, questions burning in his chest.

She'd promised his father she'd kill him.

The man on the phone promised he could stop her.

And somewhere in the darkness, the rogue pack was closing in.

Seven days.

He had seven days to figure out who to trust.

The moon watched, patient and merciless, as he stepped out of the shadows and into the war he never asked to join.

title: The Taste of Moon-Steel
content:
The shower ran cold for ten minutes before Liam dared step out.

His reflection stared back from the fogged mirror. Same brown eyes. Same sharp jaw. Same scar above his left eyebrow from the time he'd fallen out of a tree at twelve. But something had shifted behind those eyes. Something ancient and patient that watched the world with ancient hunger.

He pressed a hand to his chest. The skin was smooth now. No wound. No scar. Just the faint outline of a tattoo he'd never gotten — a crescent moon wrapped in chains, dark as spilled ink.

*That's new.*

The bathroom door rattled. "Liam? You okay in there? You've been in for like an hour."

Marcus. His roommate since freshman year. The guy who ate instant noodles at 3 AM and left his socks everywhere.

"Yeah. Coming out."

Liam wrapped a towel around his waist and opened the door. Marcus stood there holding a bag of chips, mouth half-open.

"Dude. You look... different."

"Didn't sleep well."

"No, I mean —" Marcus waved the chips at him. "You look like you hit the gym for three years in one night. Your shoulders. Your jaw. Even your eyes are doing that thing."

"What thing?"

"The thing where you look like you could kill someone and smile about it."

Liam walked past him to his bedroom. "I'm fine."

"You were screaming last night. Like, *screaming* screaming. I almost called 911."

Liam stopped. His hands gripped the doorframe. "I don't remember."

"You never do. That's the problem." Marcus's voice went quiet. "It's been happening since you turned twenty-one. Every full moon. You lock yourself in your room and I hear —" He swallowed. "I hear things I can't explain. Cracking bones. Growling. Last night I saw shadows moving under your door that didn't make sense."

*He knows more than he's saying.*

Liam turned around. "Marcus. Have you seen this before? Before college?"

Marcus's face went pale. He looked at the floor. "I was told not to say anything."

"Told by who?"

"Your grandmother."

The words hit like a punch. His grandmother had died when he was sixteen. Cancer. He'd held her hand while she went. But in her final weeks, she'd gripped his wrist with surprising strength and whispered: *When the moon calls, don't answer. Not until you're ready.*

He'd thought it was morphine talking.

"When did she tell you this?"

"Freshman orientation. She showed up at my dorm. Said you'd need a friend who understood, even if you didn't." Marcus laughed bitterly. "I thought she was crazy. A sweet old lady with dementia. But then the first full moon hit and I heard you howling."

Liam's blood ran cold. "Howling."

"Like a wolf. A big one." Marcus's hands were shaking. "I didn't sleep for a week. But she'd made me promise. She said if I told you too early, it would *destroy you.* That you had to come into it naturally or the bloodline would reject you and you'd die."

*Die.*

The word echoed in Liam's skull. He remembered the darkness from last night. The hunger. The power that had torn through his veins like wildfire. And somewhere in that chaos, he'd felt something fighting him. Something that wanted to take over permanently.

"Is there more?"

Marcus nodded slowly. "She gave me a box. Said to give it to you when you started asking questions."

"Where is it?"

"Under my bed. I've been hiding it for three years."

---

The box was made of dark wood, bound in silver straps engraved with symbols that seemed to move when Liam looked at them directly. No lock. Just a clasp shaped like a wolf's head with ruby eyes.

His fingers touched the silver.

Pain exploded through his hand. He jerked back, hissing. The skin on his fingertips was red, blistered.

"Silver burns?" Marcus whispered.

"Apparently."

Liam looked at his burned fingers. They were already healing, the blisters shrinking, new skin forming. His grandmother had known. She'd planned for this.

"Get me a towel. I'll open it without touching the silver."

Marcus handed him a t-shirt. Liam wrapped it around his hand, flipped the wolf-head clasp, and lifted the lid.

Inside lay three things.

A leather journal, worn and stained. A silver dagger with a blade that seemed to absorb light. And a folded letter addressed in his grandmother's handwriting:

*For Liam. When the Wolf King awakens.*

He opened the letter with trembling hands.

*My dearest grandson,*

*If you're reading this, you've survived the first Awakening. I wish I could have been there to guide you, but the old blood has rules that even I cannot break. You had to face the wolf alone, prove you were strong enough to hold the leash.*

*You did. I knew you would.*

*By now you've noticed changes. The strength. The healing. The hunger that whispers at the back of your mind. That is the Wolf King bloodline — the oldest and most powerful of the werewolf clans. We have guarded this secret for three hundred years, waiting for the right vessel.*

*That vessel is you.*

*But power attracts predators. There are other packs who would kill to claim your blood. The Silver Fang clan has hunted our family for generations. They know the Awakening is coming. They will find you.*

*You have seven days until the Blood Moon. On that night, you must complete the Ritual of Ascension at the Moonstone Altar in our ancestral village. Fail, and your soul belongs to the wolf forever. You will become a mindless beast, driven by instinct and hunger.*

*I have left you tools. The journal contains everything I know. The dagger is made of moon-steel — the only weapon that can kill a werewolf. Carry it always.*

*The last thing: trust no one. The Silver Fang has spies everywhere. Even your closest friends may be enemies.*

*But there is one who will help you. A woman marked by the moon. She will find you when the time is right.*

*I love you, Liam. I always have. And I am sorry I could not give you a normal life.*

*Forgive me.*

*— Grandmother*

Liam read the letter three times. His hands didn't stop shaking until the third reading.

"A woman marked by the moon?" Marcus leaned over his shoulder. "Sounds romantic."

"This isn't a joke."

"I know. I'm scared too." Marcus pointed at the dagger. "But you're going to need help. And I'm your roommate. That makes me your pack now."

Something warm kindled in Liam's chest. He looked at Marcus — the guy who left his socks everywhere, who ate noodles at 3 AM, who had kept a secret for three years because an old woman asked him to.

"Thank you."

"Don't thank me yet. We have a lot of research to do." Marcus picked up the journal. "And I'm not letting you face this alone."

The door exploded inward.

Liam moved without thinking. He grabbed Marcus, shoved him behind the bed, and drew the moon-steel dagger in one fluid motion. The blade sang as it left the sheath.

Three men stood in the doorway. They wore black suits that did nothing to hide the bulk of their bodies. Their eyes glowed yellow in the dim light. Their teeth — when they smiled — were too sharp.

"Liam Chen," the lead man said. His voice was gravel and broken glass. "The Wolf King awakens, and he's a college student with a roommate." He laughed. "How disappointing."

"Who are you?"

"Silver Fang. We've been watching you since birth." He stepped forward. "Your grandmother killed my father. It's only fair I return the favor with her grandson."

Liam felt the wolf stir inside him. The hunger. The rage. The whisper of ancient power begging to be unleashed.

*Give me control. I'll kill them all.*

*No.*

*They'll kill you. Let me out.*

*I said no.*

The lead man pulled a blade from his coat — silver, like his grandmother's. "This will hurt. I promise."

Marcus threw a lamp at his face.

The man dodged, but it bought them two seconds. Liam grabbed Marcus's arm and ran for the window. Three stories down. Concrete below.

"Are you crazy?!"

"We're werewolves now!" Liam kicked the window frame, shattering glass and wood. "Jump with me!"

Marcus stared at the drop. "I'm NOT a werewolf!"

The men were closing in. Liam grabbed Marcus, pulled him close, and leaped.

They hit the ground hard. Liam felt his ankle snap, then heal, snap again, heal again. Marcus groaned beneath him, alive but bleeding from a cut on his forehead.

"Run."

They ran.

Through alleys, over fences, across streets. Liam's legs moved faster than they ever had. He smelled everything — the garbage in the dumpsters, the exhaust from cars, the sweat on Marcus's skin. He heard the Silver Fang chasing them, their footsteps rhythmic and predatory.

They burst into a park. Empty. Dark. The moon hung overhead, almost full.

*Three more days until the Blood Moon.*

A figure stood at the center of the park. A woman. Long black hair. Silver eyes that glowed in the moonlight. She wore a leather jacket and carried a blade that matched his.

The woman marked by the moon.

"You're late, Wolf King," she said. "And you brought friends."

Liam's wolf howled in recognition. *Mate.*

The Silver Fang emerged from the treeline behind them. Six now. More than he could fight.

The woman smiled. "I'll handle this. Go to the address in the journal. I'll find you when it's safe."

"How do I know I can trust you?"

She met his eyes. Something ancient passed between them. A bond that had existed before either of them was born.

"Because I've been waiting for you my whole life, Liam. And I'm not about to let them take you now."

The Silver Fang charged.

She moved like water, like wind, like moonlight made flesh. Her blade caught the first man's throat before he could scream.

"GO!"

Liam grabbed Marcus and ran.

Behind them, the sounds of battle echoed through the night. Snarls. Cries. The wet sound of steel meeting flesh.

And as they disappeared into the darkness, Liam heard the woman's voice in his head, clear as a bell:

*Find the Moonstone Altar. I'll keep them busy. But Liam?*

*You have less time than you think.*

*The Blood Moon rises in three days.*

*And the wolf is already hungry.*

title: The Taste of Silver
content:
The chains bit into Liam's wrists like frozen snakes.

He hung suspended from the ceiling of what used to be a slaughterhouse. Rusted hooks dangled beside him. The concrete floor beneath his bare feet held dark stains that weren't rust.

Three hours since they'd taken him. Maybe four. Hard to track time when your skull felt split open and your veins burned like someone had replaced his blood with acid.

The door groaned open.

Boots on concrete. Two sets. One heavy, one light.

"Still awake." Marcus circled him, hands in the pockets of his leather jacket. The beta's face looked different in the dim light. Harder. Older. "Most pups would've passed out by now."

"Not a pup." Liam's voice came out cracked. "Not yours."

"You think that matters?" Marcus stopped in front of him. The chain clinked as Liam shifted weight. "The blood moon doesn't care about your feelings. It cares about blood. Old blood. Your blood."

"I don't—"

"You don't know anything." Marcus grabbed his chin, forced his head up. "Your mother never told you. Your father ran before you were born. And now you're twenty-one with a dead alpha's soul sleeping in your bones."

Liam's breath caught.

"What?"

"Alexander Bloodmoon." Marcus released him, stepped back. "Last true Wolf King. Died thirty years ago. No heir. No pack. Just a legend that old wolves whisper about when they're drunk."

The name hit Liam like a physical blow. Something deep in his chest answered it. Recognized it.

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" Marcus pulled out his phone, tapped the screen, turned it toward Liam.

A photograph. Old. Grainy. A man with silver-white hair and eyes that glowed amber even in the black-and-white image. He stood on a mountainside, surrounded by wolves that looked more like shadows than animals.

Same jawline as Liam's reflection. Same scar above the left eyebrow.

"Your father."

"No." Liam shook his head. The chains rattled. "I never knew my father. He left before—"

"He left to die." Marcus pocketed the phone. "The Eclipse Council executed him. Tore out his heart with silver blades while he was still in wolf form. Made sure every pack in the territory watched."

The room spun.

"Why?"

"Because he refused to bow." Marcus's voice dropped. "Because he wanted to end the clans. The bloodlines. The old ways that keep us fighting each other instead of growing stronger."

"And you killed him."

"I wasn't there." Something flickered in Marcus's eyes. Shame? Grief? "I was seventeen. Your age. I didn't know what the Council was doing until it was done."

"Doesn't matter." Liam pulled against the chains. Metal groaned. "You're working with them now. You took me for them."

"No." Marcus stepped closer. "I took you because the Council knows about you. They've known since your first moon. They've been watching. Waiting for you to turn twenty-one."

"Why?"

"Because the bloodline awakens at twenty-one. The Wolf King's power passes to the heir. And the Council wants to make sure that heir never claims his throne."

The door burst open.

Light flooded in. Liam squinted, saw shapes moving. Three men. No, four. All carrying something that glinted silver.

"Marcus." A voice like gravel in a blender. "Step away from the bloodline."

Marcus didn't move.

"Council business, Marcus. You know the laws."

"I know." Marcus's hands came out of his pockets. Empty. Open. "I also know what happens when the blood moon rises and there's no Wolf King to lead the hunt."

"You're a beta. Your opinion doesn't matter."

"It matters when I'm the only one in this room who's seen an Execution Day."

The silver glinted brighter. Liam smelled it now. Cold. Sharp. Like a dentist's drill against raw nerve.

"Last warning, Marcus."

Marcus turned. Looked at Liam. His eyes held something that might have been apology.

"Run when you get free," he whispered. "Don't stop running until you find the Moonlit Temple."

Then he spun and slammed into the nearest Council agent.

The chain holding Liam snapped.

He hit the ground hard. Pain flared through his shoulder. He looked up and saw Marcus trading blows with all four men, silver blades slicing through the air, blood spraying across the concrete.

"GO!"

Liam ran.

Bare feet on cold concrete. Through the slaughterhouse, past hanging meat hooks, out a broken window into the night.

Behind him, a howl cut through the darkness.

Not human. Not wolf.

Something in between.

He didn't look back. He just ran, branches whipping his face, rocks cutting his feet, the taste of silver still burning on his tongue.

The forest swallowed him.

---

He collapsed near a stream two hours later.

His lungs burned. His legs shook. Blood dripped from a dozen cuts he didn't remember getting.

The moon hung overhead. Full. Silver. Watching.

*The bloodline will awaken.*

Marcus's words echoed in his skull.

*The Wolf King's power passes to the heir.*

He looked at his hands. Normal hands. Pale skin. A cut on his thumb from breaking a window.

Nothing special.

Nothing kingly.

Then the pain hit.

Not in his muscles. Not in his bones.

In his blood.

Like every vein in his body turned to liquid fire. He screamed, clawed at his arms, watched his skin ripple and shift beneath his fingers.

His bones cracked. Rearranged. His spine arched backward until he heard something pop.

The wolf wanted out.

He fought it.

Pressed his face into the cold stream, let the water fill his lungs, coughed it back up. Anything to stay human. Anything to stay himself.

*You can't fight forever.*

The voice came from inside. Deep. Ancient. Not his own.

*I've been waiting thirty years. You think a little water will stop me?*

Liam's vision blurred. The moon split into two. Three. A dozen silver eyes staring down at him.

"Who are you?" he gasped.

*You know who I am.*

"Alexander."

*The blood remembers. Even if you don't.*

Pain exploded through his chest. He looked down and saw something impossible—a glowing symbol over his heart. A crescent moon wrapped around a wolf's head.

The mark of the Wolf King.

*Seven days,* the voice said. *Seven days until the blood moon ceremony. If you don't master me by then, I will master you.*

"Master you?" Liam laughed, blood on his lips. "I don't even know how to shift."

*Then learn fast, boy.*

The pain receded. Slowly. Like a tide pulling back from shore.

Liam lay in the stream, water flowing over his broken body, staring up at the moon.

He'd found out who his father was.

He'd found out the Council wanted him dead.

He had seven days to learn how to become a king.

And somewhere out in the darkness, the woman with moonlight in her scent was waiting for him.

If he survived the night.

A twig snapped behind him.

He didn't move. Didn't breathe.

Footsteps. Soft. Deliberate.

A shadow fell over him.

"Liam."

Her voice. The woman from the alley. The one with the moonlit eyes.

He turned his head.

She stood over him, silver hair falling past her shoulders, eyes glowing faintly in the darkness. She held out her hand.

"The Moonlit Temple," she said. "I can take you there. But we have to go now."

Liam stared at her hand.

Trust her?

Trust anyone?

The mark on his chest still burned. Alexander's voice still echoed in his skull. Marcus had given his life to free him.

"Who are you?" he asked.

She smiled. Sad. Ancient. Familiar.

"My name is Luna," she said. "And I was your father's mate."

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Moonlit Temple

I stared at her hand. His mate. My father's mate.

"You're lying."

She didn't flinch. "I'm not."

"Marcus never mentioned you."

"Marcus didn't know everything." Her eyes flickered with something—pain, maybe. "Alexander kept me hidden. For good reason."

I pushed myself up. My arms shook. Every muscle screamed. The symbol on my chest still glowed faintly, pulsing with my heartbeat.

"If you were his mate, where were you when he died?"

Luna's jaw tightened. "In hiding. Waiting for you."

"Waiting for me?" I laughed. It came out broken. "I've been twenty-one years without any of this. Without knowing I was some kind of... king. Without knowing my father was murdered. And you were just waiting?"

"You think I wanted to wait?" Her voice cracked. First time I'd heard anything but control. "I watched them kill him. I felt him die through the bond. Every second of it. I wanted to burn the whole Council to ash."

"Then why didn't you?"

"Because he made me promise." She stepped closer. Her scent hit me—moonlight and something else. Something warm underneath. "He knew they'd come for you someday. So I hid. I waited. I built a temple in the middle of nowhere and prayed to the moon that you'd survive long enough for me to find you."

The mark on my chest pulsed again. Alexander's voice stirred at the back of my skull. *She's telling the truth.*

*How do I know that?*

*Because I can feel her. Through you. The blood remembers.*

I closed my eyes. Took a breath. Opened them.

"Fine. Take me to your temple."

She nodded. Turned. Started walking.

I followed.

---

The forest changed as we moved deeper.

The trees grew thicker. Older. Their branches twisted together overhead, blocking out the moon until we walked in almost total darkness. But Luna moved like she'd been born in these woods. Never stumbled. Never hesitated.

I caught up to her after ten minutes. Barely.

"The Council," I said, gasping. "How many of them are there?"

"Twelve. One for each bloodline."

"And they all want me dead?"

"Not all." She ducked under a low branch. "Some want you alive. To control you. Use you as a figurehead."

"Which is worse?"

"Debatable."

We walked another mile. The cuts on my arms started closing. I felt them knitting together—too fast, too clean. The wolf's healing. I didn't ask how I knew that. I just knew.

"Your father," Luna said suddenly, "was the strongest alpha in three centuries. He united four clans under one banner. Made peace with the Moon Children. Almost changed everything."

"Moon Children?"

"Witches. Moon worshippers. We share blood with them, actually. Wolf and witch. Same root, different branches."

I filed that away. "So what went wrong?"

"He was too strong. Too loved. The Council saw him as a threat. They always do." She glanced at me. "You have the same mark he did. The same bloodline. The same seven days."

"Seven days until the Blood Moon ceremony. I know."

"Do you know what happens if you fail?"

"Alexander becomes me. I become the wolf."

"Worse." She stopped. Turned. Her eyes burned silver in the darkness. "You become a beast. No thoughts. No control. Just hunger. You'll kill everyone you've ever loved and not even remember their faces."

I swallowed. "That's... comforting."

"It's the truth. The Wolf King bloodline is the strongest because it's the most dangerous. The wolf inside you isn't just an animal—it's a personality. A will. Alexander's will. If you can't dominate it, it dominates you."

"How do I dominate it?"

She started walking again. "First, you learn to shift."

"I don't even know where to start."

"You do." She didn't look back. "It's in your blood. Your bones. You've been fighting it your whole life without knowing. Every full moon you blacked out? That was the wolf trying to break free."

"I thought I was just... broken."

"You were never broken, Liam. You were caged."

We emerged into a clearing.

The temple rose from the earth like it had grown there. Stone walls covered in moss. Carvings of wolves and moons and figures I didn't recognize. A massive archway that glowed faintly with blue light.

Luna walked toward it. I followed.

Inside, the air smelled different. Clean. Ancient. Torches lined the walls, burning with silver flames that cast no smoke. At the center of the main chamber stood a stone altar, carved with the same crescent-and-wolf symbol that burned on my chest.

"Welcome to the Moonlit Temple," Luna said. "Your father built it. For moments like this."

"For hiding?"

"For training." She turned to face me. "You have seven days to learn what took your father thirty years."

"Great. No pressure."

"Sit."

I sat. Cross-legged on the cold stone floor.

"Close your eyes."

I closed them.

"Find the wolf."

"That's it? That's your instruction?"

"Find him, Liam. Not the voice. Not Alexander. The wolf. The part of you that wants to run wild under the moon. The part you've been suppressing since you were a child."

I breathed. Focused. Tried to feel something other than the ache in my bones.

Nothing.

"I can't."

"Yes, you can. It's not about thinking. It's about feeling. Stop trying to understand and start trying to sense."

I tried again. Let my mind go blank. Let my heartbeat slow.

And then I felt it.

A warmth in my chest. Low. Deep. Like an ember buried in ash. I reached for it—

Something snapped.

My eyes flew open. My vision went red. The world sharpened—every sound, every smell, every flicker of torchlight. I felt my teeth lengthen. My fingers curl into claws.

"Good," Luna said. "Now stop."

I couldn't. The red kept spreading. My heartbeat pounded in my ears. The wolf surged forward and I didn't know how to hold it back.

*Let go,* Alexander whispered. *It's easier.*

*No.*

*You'll hurt yourself.*

*I don't care.*

I gritted my teeth. Forced the wolf back. Felt it claw and scratch and scream inside my skull.

And then—silence.

I collapsed forward, gasping. My hands were human again. My teeth normal. Sweat dripped from my forehead onto the stone.

"First shift," Luna said. "Partial. Uncontrolled. But you brought it back."

"That was the first shift?"

"That was the first attempt." She knelt in front of me. "You did well. Most wolves lose themselves the first time."

"I almost did."

"But you didn't." She studied me. "You have your father's stubbornness."

"Is that a compliment?"

"It's an observation." She stood. "We'll train again at dawn. Rest now."

"Rest? I just—"

"You've been awake for thirty-six hours. You've been poisoned. Hunted. You watched a man die for you. Your body needs to recover."

I wanted to argue. I didn't have the energy.

She led me to a small room off the main chamber. A bed. A window. Moonlight pouring through.

"Sleep," she said. "Tomorrow, we start for real."

She left.

I lay down. Stared at the ceiling. The mark on my chest still glowed. Alexander's presence hummed at the edge of my awareness.

*She's strong,* he said.

*Yeah.*

*Stronger than she looks.*

*I noticed.*

*She loved me. Does she love you? Or just what you represent?*

I didn't have an answer.

I closed my eyes. Let the exhaustion pull me under.

---

I woke to screaming.

Not mine. Not human.

Wolves. Dozens of them. Howling in the distance.

I bolted upright. Luna stood in the doorway, silver blade in hand, eyes burning.

"They found us," she said. "The Council's hunters. They're surrounding the temple."

"How many?"

"Too many."

She tossed me a knife. I caught it. Felt the weight.

"Can you fight?"

"I don't know."

"Find out."

She turned and ran.

I followed.

The temple's front doors burst open as we reached the main chamber. Wolves poured through—not shifted, but close. Eyes glowing. Teeth sharp. Claws extended.

At their head stood a man in black armor. Tall. Scarred. Smiling.

"Luna," he said. "It's been a long time."

"Varik." Her voice dripped venom. "I was hoping you'd died."

"Not yet." His eyes found me. "So this is the cub. Doesn't look like much."

"He's more than you can handle."

"We'll see."

He moved.

Fast. Faster than I could track.

Luna met him halfway. Their blades clashed. Sparks flew.

The other wolves rushed past them, straight at me.

I raised my knife.

Swing.

The first one went down. Clawed my arm. I barely felt it.

Second one leaped. I dodged. Stabbed. He fell.

Third. Fourth.

They kept coming.

My arm burned. My lungs screamed. The wolf inside me stirred, hungry, wanting out.

*Let me,* Alexander said. *Let me help.*

*No.*

*You'll die.*

*Maybe.*

I ducked under a claw. Drove my knife up. Felt it bite flesh.

But there were too many.

A wolf tackled me from the side. I hit the stone floor. My knife skittered away. Teeth closed on my shoulder—

And then the wolf yelped. Released me.

I looked up.

Luna stood over me. Blood on her face. Silver blade dripping.

"Get up," she said.

I got up.

Varik lay crumpled against the wall. Dead? Unconscious? I couldn't tell.

The remaining wolves hesitated.

"Run," Luna said. "Tell your masters the Wolf King's heir is protected. Tell them the Moonlit Temple remembers its oath."

They ran.

I watched them disappear into the forest.

My shoulder burned. I touched it. Came away red.

"You're hurt," Luna said.

"I'm fine."

"You're not." She grabbed my arm. Pulled me toward the altar. "Sit."

I sat.

She pressed her hand to my wound. Mumbled something I couldn't understand. A warmth spread through my shoulder. The pain receded.

"Moon magic," she said. "Another thing you'll need to learn."

"I thought I just needed to master the wolf."

"The wolf is part of it. But the Blood Moon ceremony requires more than strength. It requires balance."

"Balance?"

"Between man and wolf. Between power and control. Between light and—"

She stopped.

Her eyes went wide.

"What?"

She didn't answer. She was staring at the altar.

I turned.

The crescent moon symbol was glowing. Not the dim pulse from before. Bright. Blazing. Like it was alive.

"Impossible," she whispered.

"What?"

"The temple responds to blood. Alexander's blood. But I thought—" She looked at me. "You're not just an alpha, Liam. You're not just the Wolf King's heir."

"Then what am I?"

She stared at the glowing symbol. At me. At the blood dripping from my shoulder onto the stone.

"You're the Moon's chosen," she said. "The prophecy. The one they

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: Secrets of the Temple

Liam's hand hovered inches from hers.

*Your father's mate.*

The words didn't make sense. They couldn't make sense. Alexander had been dead for thirty years. This woman looked twenty-five, maybe thirty at most.

"You're lying," he said.

Luna's smile didn't waver. "Ask me something only Alexander would know."

"Like what?"

"The scar on his left shoulder. From a rogue alpha in the Siberian pack. He told me it hurt worse than childbirth. I told him he'd never survive childbirth, so his opinion was invalid."

Liam blinked. That sounded like something his father would say. Based on what Marcus had told him, anyway.

"He also told me that if he ever had a son, he'd name him after his grandfather. Liam. Means 'strong-willed warrior' in Gaelic."

The water had gone cold. Liam sat up slowly, every muscle screaming in protest. "How are you still alive? Marcus said the whole pack died."

Luna's eyes flickered with something ancient and painful. "Because I wasn't there. I was pregnant. Alexander sent me away three days before the massacre. Told me to hide until the blood moon passed."

"What happened to the baby?"

Silence stretched between them. A night bird called somewhere in the darkness.

"She didn't survive the birth. The stress. The grief. My wolf went into mourning and couldn't hold the pregnancy." Luna's voice stayed flat, controlled. Too controlled. Like she'd practiced saying those words a thousand times.

"I'm sorry," Liam said. Because what else could you say to someone who'd lost everything?

"Don't be. It was thirty years ago." She held out her hand again. "The Moonlit Temple. We need to move before the Rogue King's trackers find us."

Liam took her hand. Her grip was firm, warm, nothing like the cold touch he expected from someone who'd spent three decades alone.

They moved through the forest in silence. Luna moved like water—silent, flowing, never disturbing a single leaf. Liam crashed through branches like a wounded moose.

"Your footwork needs work," she said without turning.

"I almost died tonight."

"Excuses won't keep you alive."

They reached a cliff face an hour before dawn. Solid granite, moss-covered, stretching fifty feet into the darkness. Dead end.

"We're trapped," Liam said.

Luna walked to the wall and pressed her palm against a crack that looked like every other crack. Stone groaned. A section of the cliff slid sideways, revealing a narrow passage just wide enough for one person.

"The temple," she said. "Built by the first Wolf King three centuries ago. Hidden from human eyes by blood magic and moonlight."

Liam followed her into the darkness. The passage curved downward, steps carved into the living rock. Torches flickered to life as they passed, flame burning silver instead of orange.

"How does that work?"

"Moonfire. Burns as long as the moon exists. The first king bound the temple to the lunar cycle."

The passage opened into a chamber that stole Liam's breath.

Cathedral ceilings rose a hundred feet above. Pillars carved to look like wolves in mid-transformation supported the roof. Moonlight streamed through cracks in the stone above, pooling in silver puddles on the floor. And in the center of the room—

A statue. A man mid-shift, half-human, half-wolf, arms raised to the sky. His face was frozen in ecstasy, not pain.

"Alexander," Luna whispered. "Carved by the temple's first priestess. She said he was the most beautiful wolf she'd ever seen."

Liam walked around the statue. His father's face. Younger than the photos Marcus had shown him, but unmistakable. The same jawline. The same stubborn set to his brow.

"He looks happy."

"He was. Before the war. Before the Council turned on him." Luna touched the statue's hand. "He loved being the Wolf King. Loved the power, the responsibility, the weight of it all. He used to say that being alpha wasn't about ruling—it was about serving. Protecting those who couldn't protect themselves."

"What happened?"

"The Rogue King happened. And the Council's fear." She turned to face him. "Alexander wanted to unite the clans. End the centuries of bloodshed. But some wolves don't want peace. They want power."

"The Rogue King."

"Kael. Former alpha of the Shadowfang pack. He murdered his own father to claim the title, then challenged Alexander for the throne. When he lost, he gathered the outcasts, the broken, the wolves who preferred hunting humans to protecting them."

"And the Council sided with him?"

"The Council sided with whoever would keep them in power. When Alexander refused to bow to their demands, they branded him a traitor and ordered his execution." Luna's jaw tightened. "Three hundred wolves attacked our territory that night. Alexander held them off long enough for me to escape. Killed forty-seven before they brought him down."

Liam looked at his father's statue. At the peaceful face of a man who'd died fighting for what he believed in.

"I'm not him," Liam said. "I can't be him."

"No one's asking you to be." Luna moved closer. "But you carry his blood. His power. And in seven days, the blood moon will force that power to the surface. If you haven't learned to control it by then—"

"The wolf takes over. I know."

"Do you? Because I don't think you understand what's at stake." She grabbed his chin, forced him to meet her eyes. "If you lose yourself to the wolf, you won't just be a mindless beast. You'll be a weapon. The Wolf King bloodline is the most powerful in existence. Kael will capture you, break you, and use you to destroy every pack that stands against him."

"Then teach me."

"I intend to." She released his chin. "But first, we need to see what we're working with."

She led him to a circular chamber at the temple's heart. The floor was inlaid with silver runes that glowed at their approach. A pool of water sat in the center, perfectly still, reflecting the moonlight above.

"Kneel," Luna said.

Liam knelt. The stone was cold against his knees.

"Close your eyes. Reach inside yourself. Find the wolf."

He closed his eyes. Felt nothing but exhaustion and pain and the lingering taste of copper on his tongue.

"I don't feel anything."

"You're not trying. The wolf isn't separate from you. It is you. Your anger, your fear, your hunger—that's the wolf. Stop fighting it and let it surface."

Liam breathed. Let his mind drift. Thought about the men who'd tried to kill him. The Council who'd ordered his death. The father he'd never met who'd died protecting him.

Anger rose. Hot. Sweet. Familiar.

Something stirred in his chest. A presence. Ancient and vast and hungry.

*There you are.*

Alexander's voice. Or the wolf's. It didn't matter anymore.

"Good," Luna said. "Now open your eyes."

He opened them. The pool's surface showed not his reflection, but a battlefield. Wolves clashing under a red moon. Blood soaking the earth. A figure standing on a hill of corpses, arms spread wide, laughing.

"Kael," Luna said. "This is what awaits if you fail."

The image shifted. A woman with silver hair, her throat torn out, lying in a pool of moonlight. Luna.

"If Kael captures you, he will use the bond between us to find me. And when he does, he'll make me watch as he tears the Wolf King bloodline out of you and claims it for himself."

The image shifted again. Liam saw himself standing on that same hill of corpses. His eyes were silver. Empty. His hands were covered in blood.

"That's what happens if the wolf wins."

Liam looked away from the pool. "How do I stop it?"

"Acceptance." Luna knelt beside him. "The wolf isn't your enemy. It's your partner. Your other half. You can't dominate it or suppress it. You have to become one with it."

"How?"

"Seven trials. One for each day until the blood moon. Each trial will force you to confront a different aspect of your nature. Fear. Anger. Pride. Grief. Love. Sacrifice. Power."

"That's a lot for a week."

"The blood moon doesn't wait." She stood. "Your first trial starts now."

Liam stood, his legs shaky. "What do I have to do?"

"Fight me."

Before he could respond, Luna moved. Faster than anything he'd ever seen. Her fist connected with his solar plexus, driving the air from his lungs. He doubled over, gasping.

"Get up," she said. "Your father never stayed down. Neither will you."

Liam straightened. His ribs screamed. His vision swam.

"Again," she said.

She came at him again. This time, he saw it coming. Dodged. Barely. Her fist grazed his ear, and he felt the wind of its passing.

"Better. But not good enough."

She swept his legs. He hit the stone floor hard. Pain exploded through his spine.

"The wolf would have sensed that attack before it happened. Why didn't you?"

"I don't know how!"

"Then learn. Feel the air. Feel my intent. Your wolf knows what I'm going to do before I do. Trust it."

Liam pushed himself up. Blood dripped from his nose. His vision doubled.

*Trust it,* Alexander's voice echoed.

*Trust me.*

Liam closed his eyes. Let the anger rise. Let the presence in his chest unfurl.

When Luna attacked again, he felt it. A shift in the air. A tremor in the stone. He opened his eyes and caught her wrist.

She smiled. "Good. Now hold on."

She twisted, broke his grip, and slammed her elbow into his jaw.

The world went white.

When his vision cleared, he was on his back, staring at the cathedral ceiling. Luna stood over him, not even breathing hard.

"Day one," she said. "We start again at sunrise."

Liam groaned. "I can't do this."

"You can. You will. Because if you don't—" She gestured at the pool, still showing the image of a world bathed in blood. "—that's what happens."

She turned and walked toward the passage.

"Where are you going?"

"To hunt. You need meat to rebuild your strength. And I need to make sure Kael's trackers haven't found us yet."

"Wait—"

She paused at the entrance. Looked back. Her eyes glowed silver in the darkness.

"One more thing, Liam. Something I didn't tell you."

"What?"

"Your father didn't die in the massacre. He died protecting you."

Liam's blood went cold. "What?"

"The night I escaped, I wasn't alone. Alexander gave me something. Something more important than my life." She touched her stomach. "I lied about the baby. She survived. And she grew up in hiding, protected by temple priestesses, never knowing who she really was."

"Who?"

Luna's eyes met his. And in them, he saw something that made his heart stop.

"Her name is Maya. And she's been watching you your whole life."

The torches flickered. Luna disappeared into the darkness.

Liam lay on the cold stone, his father's statue watching over him, the mark of the Wolf King burning on his chest.

*Her name is Maya. And she's been watching you your whole life.*

The woman from the alley. The one with moonlight in her scent.

His sister.

Above the temple, the moon began to wane.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Weight of Thrones

The word hit Liam like a silver bullet to the chest.

"Mate." He repeated it, let it sit on his tongue, tasted the implications. "You're telling me—"

"I'm your mother." Luna's hand remained extended, steady, patient. "By bond, not by blood. Your birth mother died hours after you were born. Alexander asked me to raise you. To protect you."

Liam pushed himself up from the stream. Water cascaded off his torn shirt, mixing with blood that was already clotting faster than it should. His body felt wrong. Stretched. Like his skin was a size too small.

"Then where were you?" His voice came out harder than he intended. "For twenty-one years. Where were you?"

"Watching." She lowered her hand. "From the shadows. The Council killed Alexander for trying to unite the clans. They would have killed you too if they knew you survived. I made sure they didn't."

"By leaving me with a human family?"

"By giving you a chance to live." Her eyes flickered with something raw. Pain. Regret. "I erased your scent. Masked your bloodline. Made you invisible to every wolf in the city. Do you know what that cost me?"

"Tell me."

"I haven't shifted in twenty-one years." She pulled up her sleeve. Scars crisscrossed her forearm—not silver burns, but self-inflicted wounds. Ritual marks. "Every full moon, I had to cut the wolf back. Suppress my nature. Wear it so you could hide yours."

Liam looked at the scars. Counted them. Twenty-one. One for each year.

"Why now?" he asked. "Why show yourself now?"

"Because the bloodline awakened. Because the Council knows you exist. Because Marcus is dead and the Moonlit Temple has no protector." She stepped closer. "And because you have six days left. Not seven. Six."

The stream gurgled between them. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled. Not a werewolf—just a regular animal. But Liam's spine stiffened anyway. His ears twitched, picking up sounds he shouldn't be able to hear. The heartbeat of a rabbit thirty yards away. The rustle of leaves in a breeze he couldn't feel.

"The blood moon ceremony," he said. "What happens if I fail?"

"You become a beast. Eternal. Mindless. The wolf consumes your human soul and you become nothing but hunger and instinct." She paused. "Your father's brother—Theodore—he failed his ceremony. We had to put him down three weeks later. He killed seventeen humans before we caught him."

Liam's stomach turned. "Seventeen?"

"They were hunters. Poachers. Men who killed wolves for sport." Luna's jaw tightened. "But Theodore didn't know that. He didn't know anything. He was just a monster wearing my friend's face."

A breeze swept through the clearing. Liam caught her scent properly for the first time—moonlight and lavender, just like the alley. But underneath it, something else. Wolf. Old. Powerful.

"You said you could take me to the Moonlit Temple."

"I can."

"Then why haven't you already?"

"Because the Council isn't the only thing hunting you." She reached into her jacket and pulled out a photograph. Worn at the edges. Faded. Two men stood shoulder to shoulder—one with Liam's eyes, one with silver hair and a cruel smile.

"The man on the left is your father. The man on the right is Elias Voss. He was Alexander's beta. His closest friend." Luna's voice dropped. "He's also the one who sold your father to the Council."

Liam studied the photograph. His father looked happy. Trusting. His arm draped over Elias's shoulder like they were brothers.

"How do you know?"

"Because I was there." She tucked the photograph away. "Elias led the ambush. He was the one who drove the silver blade through Alexander's heart. And now he knows you're alive."

"How?"

"The mark." She pointed at his chest. "When the bloodline awakened, it sent a pulse through every alpha wolf in the region. Elias felt it. The Council felt it. Even the rogues felt it. You lit up like a beacon, Liam. Every wolf with a drop of royal blood knows you exist now."

A cold realization settled in his gut. "Marcus said the rogues were hunting me for my bloodline."

"Marcus was right. But the rogues are amateurs. Mercenaries." Luna's eyes hardened. "Elias is something worse. He's organized. Connected. And he's been waiting thirty years for a chance to claim the throne Alexander denied him."

"Throne?"

"The Wolf King's throne. It's not a metaphor." She turned and started walking. "The blood moon ceremony isn't just about mastering your wolf. It's about claiming your birthright. The power of every Wolf King who came before you. Alexander's power."

Liam followed, his bare feet silent on the forest floor. He noticed things he hadn't before—the way his vision sharpened in the darkness, the way his muscles moved with fluid grace even through the pain.

"And if Elias gets to the Temple first?"

"He'll perform the ceremony himself. Steal your bloodline. Become the Wolf King in your place." She glanced back at him. "And then he'll hunt you down and drain every drop of your blood to make the claim permanent."

They walked in silence for an hour. The forest thinned, giving way to rocky terrain. Mountains rose in the distance, their peaks painted silver by the moon. Liam's body healed as they moved—the cuts sealing, the bruises fading, the fire in his veins settling into a low, constant hum.

The voice—Alexander—didn't speak again. But Liam felt him. Waiting. Watching. Like a predator in the shadows of his own mind.

"The Temple is in the mountains," Luna said, pointing toward a peak that seemed to pierce the stars. "Three hours if we move fast. The Council has watchers posted along every route, so we'll have to take the old paths."

"Old paths?"

"Ancient trade routes. Used before the war. Before the clans fractured." She veered left, toward a narrow crevice between two boulders. "The passage is tight. You'll have to crawl."

Liam followed her into the darkness. The crevice was barely shoulder-width, the rock cold and damp against his skin. He moved on hands and knees, following the sound of Luna's breathing, trusting her because he had no other choice.

Ten minutes in, the passage opened into a cavern.

Liam stopped. Stared.

The cavern was vast—the size of a cathedral—its walls covered in murals that glowed faintly blue. Wolves. Battles. Kings kneeling before a moon that took up the entire ceiling. And in the center of the room, a stone altar carved with the same crescent-and-wolf symbol that burned on his chest.

"What is this place?" he whispered.

"A shrine. One of seven." Luna ran her fingers along the nearest mural. "The first Wolf King built them. Blood altars, connected by ley lines. Every clan leader who completes the ceremony leaves a piece of their power here."

She touched a section of the mural showing a wolf with eyes like molten gold. "That one is yours."

Liam approached. The wolf in the mural stared back at him—not painted, but *watching*. Its eyes tracked his movement. Its fur seemed to shift in the torchlight.

"Touch it," Luna said.

"Why?"

"Because it's your birthright. Because you need to understand what you're fighting for." She stepped back. "And because the altar recognizes its kind."

Liam reached out. His fingers brushed the stone.

Fire exploded through his arm.

Not pain—*knowledge*. Images flooded his mind. A throne room filled with wolves bowing. His father's face, young and fierce, speaking words in a language Liam didn't understand. A woman with Luna's eyes, holding a newborn baby, crying and smiling at the same time.

*Hold him,* the woman said. *He has your stubbornness.*

*He has your strength,* Alexander replied. *But I'll teach him mercy.*

The vision shifted. Blood. Screams. Elias Voss standing over Alexander's body, holding a heart in his hand, eating it raw while the Council watched.

*The Wolf King is dead,* Elias said, blood dripping down his chin. *Long live the King.*

Liam pulled his hand back. His chest heaved. Tears streamed down his face—he hadn't noticed when they started.

"He killed him," Liam whispered. "He ate his heart."

"That's how the transfer works." Luna's voice was hollow. "The bloodline carries the power. But the heart carries the soul. Every Wolf King who has died in battle has been consumed by his killer."

"And if I die?"

"Elias will eat yours too. Or the Council will. Or the rogues." She met his eyes. "That's the world you were born into, Liam. A world where kings are devoured."

He looked at his hands. They were shaking. Not from fear—from rage.

"The Temple," he said. "How do I claim the throne before Elias finds me?"

"You complete the ceremony. But it requires three things." Luna held up her fingers. "One: the blood of a Wolf King. You have that. It's in your veins."

She held up a second finger. "Two: the heart of a true wolf. Not a werewolf—a real wolf. You have to kill it yourself and offer its heart to the moon."

"And three?"

"The sacrifice of something you love." She paused. "That part, no one can help you with. The ceremony demands proof that you're willing to lose everything for the power to protect everything."

Liam's mind went blank. "What does that mean?"

"It means the Temple will test you. Show you visions. Give you choices." Luna's voice cracked. "Your father had to choose between saving me or claiming the throne. He chose the throne. And I spent thirty years wondering if I could have saved him from the monster he became."

"I'm not Alexander."

"No. You're not." She smiled, sad and broken. "He was a king. You're still a boy who doesn't know how to shift. But you have six days to learn. Six days to become what this world needs."

Liam turned back to the mural. The golden-eyed wolf stared at him. Waiting.

"Take me to the Temple," he said.

Luna nodded. She led him through the cavern, past more murals, past altars covered in offerings that had turned to dust centuries ago. The passage narrowed again, then opened into the night.

The mountain loomed above them. And at its peak, a structure of white stone caught the moonlight, glowing like a second moon.

"The Temple of the Wolf King," Luna said. "You have until dawn to enter. After that, the wards seal until the next blood moon."

"Six days," Liam said.

"Six days."

He started climbing.

The path was steep, treacherous, lined with rocks that cut his feet and branches that scratched his arms. But he didn't stop. Didn't slow. The mark on his chest burned brighter with every step, pulling him forward, calling him home.

Behind him, Luna followed in silence.

Above him, the Temple waited.

And somewhere in the darkness below, Elias Voss raised his head. Smelled the air. Smiled.

"Found you," he whispered.

He began to climb.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Weight of Blood

Luna's hand remained extended. Water dripped from Liam's clothes, mixing with the blood that still seeped from a dozen shallow wounds. The word echoed in his skull. *Mate.* His father's mate. The woman who smelled of moonlight and danger.

"You're lying."

She didn't flinch. "I haven't told you a single lie since we met. Omitting some things, yes. But not lying."

"That's the same thing."

"No." Her eyes held his. "Lying is choosing falsehood. Omitting is choosing timing. There's a difference."

Liam pushed himself to his feet. His legs screamed. His ribs felt like cracked glass. He stood a head taller than her, but somehow she seemed larger. Older. Like she'd been standing in that spot for centuries, waiting for him to catch up.

"You knew Marcus," he said. It wasn't a question.

"Yes."

"You knew he was going to die tonight."

"I knew he was willing to."

Liam's fist connected with her jaw before he knew he'd thrown it. The impact sent shockwaves up his arm. She stumbled back two steps, touched her face, looked at the blood on her fingers. Then she looked at him. No anger. No surprise. Just understanding.

"Feel better?"

"No." His hand hurt. His chest hurt. Everything hurt. "He raised me. He was the only family I had, and you just—you just watched him die."

"I watched him choose to die." Luna lowered her hand. The cut on her lip was already healing, the skin knitting together like time was reversing. "Marcus knew what would happen the moment he took you from that house. The Council tracks their own. They found him within hours. He had two choices—run with you and get caught eventually, or give you a head start and buy you time."

"He bought me seven days."

"He bought you seven days *alone.* There's a difference." She stepped closer. "The Council doesn't know about me. They don't know about the Temple. Marcus kept those secrets even from the people he trusted most."

"Why?"

"Because he knew I'd find you. He knew the blood moon would call you to me." She touched her chest, right where Liam's mark burned. "I feel it too, Liam. The pull. Your father and I were bound by the old magic. When he died, that bond should have died with him. But it didn't. It passed to you."

Liam's stomach turned. "I'm not my father."

"No. You're not." Her voice softened. "You're something else entirely. Something the Council fears so badly they're willing to break their own laws to stop you."

A howl cut through the night. Close. Too close.

Luna's head snapped toward the sound. "They tracked you through the stream. We have to move."

"I can barely stand."
