"Then learn to run." She grabbed his arm. Her grip was iron. "The Temple is two miles east. If we make it before they circle around, we live. If we don't—"

Another howl. Closer. Then a third, from the opposite direction.

"They're herding us."

"Toward what?"

"Toward the ridge." Luna's eyes scanned the treeline. "There's a clearing up ahead. Open ground. They want us in the open."

"Then we don't go to the clearing."

"We don't have a choice." She pointed east. "The Temple is through that clearing. The only entrance is on the far side. We go through, or we die in the trees."

Liam looked at the darkness ahead. He could feel them now—the wolves. Not just hear them. *Feel* them. Like pressure changes in the air. Six of them. No, seven. Circling. Waiting.

"How do you know they won't follow us into the Temple?"

"Because the Temple is warded. Old magic. Blood magic." She met his eyes. "Your father's blood runs through the stones. The Council's wolves can't enter."

"They can't, or they won't?"

"They *can't.*" She squeezed his arm. "But the wards only recognize alpha blood. Which means you have to cross the threshold first."

"Why me?"

"Because if I go first, the wards will seal behind me. You'll be trapped outside with them."

Liam stared at her. The moonlight caught her silver hair, her pale skin, her eyes that seemed to hold entire galaxies. She looked like she belonged in a painting. Like she belonged to another time entirely.

"You said you were his mate. That means you were an alpha too."

"Was." Something flickered in her eyes. Pain. Old and deep. "I gave that up when Alexander died. The wards don't recognize me anymore. They recognize his blood. *Your* blood."

The howls grew louder. Closer. Liam could hear them moving through the underbrush now. Cracking twigs. Heavy breathing. The scrape of claws on stone.

"Fine." He took a step toward the clearing. "But if this is a trap, I'll—"

"You'll what? Kill me?" She almost smiled. "I've been dead for thirty years, Liam. There's nothing you can do to me that hasn't already been done."

She moved past him, silent as smoke, and disappeared into the trees. Liam followed.

---

The clearing was larger than he expected. A hundred yards of open grass, bathed in silver moonlight, surrounded by a ring of ancient oaks. At the far end stood a structure that didn't belong—a stone archway, covered in vines, with symbols carved into its surface that seemed to writhe in the darkness.

"The Temple entrance," Luna whispered. "Through the arch, down the stairs, and we're safe."

"What's the catch?"

"The catch is that they know where we're going." She pointed to the treeline. "Look."

Liam followed her gaze. Shadows moved between the trees. Not just one or two. Dozens. The Council had called in reinforcements.

"How many?"

"Twenty. Maybe more."

"And we have to cross a hundred yards of open ground."

"Yes."

"While they watch."

"Yes."

"This is a terrible plan."

"Do you have a better one?"

Liam thought about it. He thought about Marcus, bleeding out on the floor of a safehouse. He thought about the voice in his head—Alexander's voice—telling him he had seven days to master the Wolf King's power. He thought about the mark on his chest, still burning, still waiting.

"Yeah." He stepped forward. "I do."

He walked into the clearing.

The wolves emerged from the trees. Not in human form—in their true shapes. Massive. Dark-furred. Eyes that glowed like embers. They formed a semicircle around him, blocking the path to the archway, cutting off any chance of retreat.

The largest of them stepped forward. His fur was black as oil, his eyes red as blood. When he spoke, his voice came out wrong—human words forced through a wolf's throat.

"Liam Cross. By the authority of the Alpha Council, you are charged with unlawful awakening, destruction of Council property, and harboring forbidden bloodlines. Surrender now, and your death will be merciful."

"My name is Liam *Kingston.*" The words came out stronger than he felt. "And I'm not surrendering to anyone."

The wolf's lips curled back. "Then you choose the hard way."

"I choose my own way." Liam looked past the wolf, toward the archway. He could feel the wards now—a hum in his blood, a pull in his chest. The Temple wanted him. Called to him. Like it recognized him.

*It does,* Alexander's voice whispered. *It knows its master.*

"I'm not a master of anything."

*Not yet. But you will be.*

The wolves moved. Not all at once—they were too disciplined for that. Two from the left, one from the right, testing his reactions. Liam backed toward the center of the clearing. His heart pounded. His hands shook. He had no weapon, no training, no idea how to fight.

But he had the blood.

He reached inside himself, the way Alexander had shown him, and pulled.

The mark on his chest exploded with heat. His vision went white. His bones screamed. For a moment, he thought he was shifting—felt his skin start to tear, his spine start to crack—but then he stopped. Held it. The power pushed against his control, demanding release.

*Not yet,* he told it. *Not until I'm ready.*

The wolves hesitated. They felt it too—the shift in the air, the pressure change. The lead wolf's eyes widened.

"He's drawing on the bloodline. Stop him!"

They charged.

Liam ran.

Not toward the archway. Toward the lead wolf.

The wolf lunged. Liam dropped, rolled, felt claws rake across his back. Pain exploded, but he didn't stop. He came up inside the wolf's guard, grabbed its fur, and *pushed.*

The power erupted from his chest.

Light. Heat. Something that felt like thunder. The wolf flew backward, crashing into two others. The line broke. For one second, one beautiful second, there was a gap.

Liam sprinted.

The archway was thirty yards away. Twenty. Ten. He could feel the wards humming, singing, reaching for him like hands in the dark. The trees on either side exploded with wolves. They poured from the shadows, teeth bared, eyes burning.

He wasn't going to make it.

Then Luna stepped out of the darkness.

She moved like water. Like wind. Like something that had never been entirely human. Her silver hair streamed behind her, and her eyes—her eyes were no longer just glowing. They were *blazing.*

"Get down!" she shouted.

Liam dropped.

She raised her hands. The air around her rippled. And then the moonlight itself seemed to bend, to twist, to *gather* in her palms. She threw it forward like a spear.

The light hit the first wave of wolves and sent them scattering. Not killing—just pushing. Buying time. The archway was five yards away.

"Go!" Luna grabbed his arm, dragged him forward. "The wards—you need to cross first!"

Liam stumbled through the archway.

The world changed.

The sound of the forest vanished. The howls, the growls, the pounding of paws—gone. Silence so complete it hurt. He stood in a stone corridor, ancient and dark, with symbols on the walls that pulsed with faint silver light.

Behind him, Luna stepped through.

The wards flared. A wall of white light erupted across the archway, sealing it shut. On the other side, Liam could see the wolves—frozen, staring, their alpha throwing himself against the barrier again and again.

"He can't get through," Luna said. "We're safe."

Liam turned to her. His back was bleeding. His chest was burning. His entire body felt like it was falling apart.

"Safe," he repeated. "You call this safe?"

"I call it alive." She met his eyes. "Which is more than Marcus got."

The name hit him like a punch. He looked away. Down the corridor. Into the darkness.

"How long do we have?"

"Seven days. Until the blood moon."

"And then what?"

She was quiet for a long moment. Then: "And then you either become the Wolf King, or you die trying."

Liam stared into the darkness of the Temple. Somewhere in there were answers. Somewhere in there was his father's legacy. Somewhere in there was the power that the Council feared so badly they'd killed for it.

He took a step forward.

Then another.

Behind him, Luna followed.

And in the distance, deep in the

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Traitor's Scent

The Temple of the Moonlit Grove rose from the forest floor like a living thing. Roots coiled around stone pillars. Vines crawled across engraved walls. Moonlight filtered through a ceiling of woven branches, casting silver patterns across the marble floor.

Liam pressed his palm against his chest. The mark still burned. Three days since the full moon. Three days since Alexander's voice first spoke in his skull. Three days of running, hiding, learning.

And still not enough.

"You're pushing too hard."

Luna's voice came from behind him. Soft. Patient. The way she'd spoken every day since pulling him from that stream.

"I don't have time to push easy."

He turned from the altar. The temple's main chamber stretched fifty feet high, its walls covered in murals depicting wolves of every size, every color, every phase of the moon. At the center stood a crescent-shaped stone basin filled with water that glowed faintly blue.

"The ceremony isn't the only deadline." Luna walked past him, her bare feet silent on the cold stone. "The Shadowfang pack has been tracking us since the alley. They'll find this place eventually."

"Then teach me faster."

She stopped at the basin. Dipped her fingers into the glowing water. Ripples spread outward, distorting the moonlight reflection.

"I can't teach you what you refuse to accept."

"What's that supposed to mean?"

Luna turned. Her silver eyes held his. In the dim light, she looked younger than her centuries. Looked like someone who'd watched empires rise and fall, who'd loved a king and buried him too.

"Alexander didn't become the Wolf King by fighting the wolf," she said. "He became it by embracing it. The power isn't in restraint. It's in surrender."

"I'm not surrendering to anything."

"Then you'll die." She said it flat. No emotion. Just fact. "The Shadowfang alpha has killed three heirs in the last decade. Each one thought they could fight the change. Each one lost themselves in the blood moon ceremony."

Liam's jaw tightened. "You could have led with that."

"You weren't ready to hear it." She dipped her hand into the basin again, cupped the glowing water, brought it to her lips. Drank. "You are now."

Steam rose from the basin. The water began to swirl, faster and faster, until it formed a small vortex. Images flickered across its surface—a forest at night, a pack of wolves running, a figure standing alone on a cliff.

"The ceremony binds the wolf and the man," Luna said. "If either side resists, the soul shatters. The body becomes a vessel for instinct alone. A mindless killer."

"Like the rogues."

She nodded. "Like the rogues."

Liam stepped closer to the basin. The image shifted. Showed a man with dark hair and golden eyes. Alexander. His father. Standing in this very temple, facing the same basin, the same choice.

"He didn't resist," Liam said.

"Alexander embraced the wolf. Accepted it. Loved it." Luna's voice cracked. Just slightly. "That's why he was the strongest alpha in a thousand years."

"What happened to him?"

She was silent for a long moment. The water stilled. The images faded.

"The Council happened."

The words hung in the air. Heavy. Sharp. Liam felt something cold settle in his chest.

"The Council killed him."

"He was too powerful. Too independent. The old alphas feared what he could become." Luna's hands gripped the basin's edge. Her knuckles went white. "So they invited him to a summit. Promised peace between the clans. And when he arrived, they attacked. Fifteen alphas against one. It took them hours to bring him down."

"And my mother?"

"Human. Killed by a Council assassin three months before you were born. They wanted to eliminate the bloodline entirely. But Marcus—" She stopped. Breathed. "Marcus hid you. Raised you as his own. Told no one, not even the Moonlit Temple, where you were."

The mark on Liam's chest pulsed. Warm. Almost comforting.

"Why tell me now?"

"Because you need to understand what you're fighting." Luna met his eyes. "The Council isn't just hunting you because you're Alexander's son. They're hunting you because they know what you could become."

"And what's that?"

"The end of their world."

The temple's entrance burst open. A wolf the size of a horse stood silhouetted against the night, its fur black as oil, its eyes burning red. Foam dripped from its jaws. Behind it, shadows moved. More wolves. More eyes.

Luna moved faster than Liam could track. She was between him and the door in a heartbeat, silver light gathering in her palms.

"How did they find us?"

A figure stepped through the pack. Tall. Wearing a leather coat. Face hidden beneath a hood. The wolves parted for him like water around stone.

"Luna." The voice was male. Familiar. "You always were too sentimental."

The figure lowered his hood.

Liam's blood turned to ice.

Marcus stood in the temple doorway.

Not dead. Not wounded. Standing straight, dressed in clean clothes, eyes sharp and cold. The same Marcus who'd raised him. The same Marcus who'd taught him to fight. The same Marcus who'd bled out in the alley three days ago.

"Surprised?" Marcus smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "You should be. I spent twenty years planning this."

Liam's hands shook. His vision tunneled. Everything he'd believed, everything he'd trusted, collapsed into a single, burning question.

"You're Shadowfang."

"I'm their alpha." Marcus stepped forward. The wolves followed, surrounding the temple in a crescent of teeth and hunger. "I've been their alpha for thirty years. Alexander's death? I orchestrated it. The Council's fear? I cultivated it. Your existence?"

He stopped ten feet away. Spread his arms.

"I've been waiting for this moment since the day you were born."

Luna's silver light flared. "You lied to him. To me. To everyone."

"I lied to protect a weapon." Marcus's eyes locked onto Liam. "You think the Wolf King bloodline is about power? It's about control. The ability to command every wolf, every pack, every alpha on the continent. Alexander had it. And I saw what he did with it."

"He wanted peace."

"He wanted a new world order." Marcus's voice hardened. "And I wanted that power for myself. But the bloodline doesn't pass through murder. It passes through blood. Through the heir."

"You raised me."

"I raised a vessel." Marcus took another step. "The Shadowfang pack has a ritual. Ancient. Forbidden. It allows an alpha to consume the Wolf King's power. To take it for himself."

Liam felt the floor beneath his feet. Felt the mark burning against his chest. Felt Alexander's voice stirring in the depths of his mind.

*He's not your father.*

"I know."

*He's never been your father.*

"Shut up."

Marcus raised an eyebrow. "Talking to yourself already? The wolf must be getting restless."

"Luna." Liam kept his eyes on Marcus. "How many can you take?"

"Not enough."

"Then we run."

"No." Luna stepped forward. Her silver light intensified, spreading outward in waves, pushing the wolves back. "You run. I'll hold them."

"Luna—"

"I've been waiting for this too." She looked over her shoulder. Smiled. That same sad, ancient smile. "Go. Find the Council. Find the truth. And when the time comes—"

Silver light exploded. Wolves howled. The temple shook.

"—remember who you are."

Liam ran.

He burst through a side passage, roots scraping his arms, stones cutting his feet. Behind him, the sounds of battle—snarls, screams, the crash of stone against stone. He didn't look back.

The passage twisted, descended, opened into a cavern filled with moonlight. A pool of glowing water at its center. The same pool from the dream. The same pool where Alexander had stood.

Liam fell to his knees at the edge.

"I don't know what to do."

The water rippled. A reflection looked back at him. But not his reflection.

A wolf with golden eyes.

*Stop fighting.*

"I can't."

*You can. You have to.*

"I'm not strong enough."

The reflection shifted. Became a man. Alexander. His father. Standing beside him in the water, hand on his shoulder.

*You are my son.*

"I don't know who that is."

*Then find out. But do it fast.*

The cavern shook. Dust fell from the ceiling. Somewhere above, Luna was buying him time.

Liam looked at his reflection. At the wolf waiting beneath his skin.

Seven days. He'd had seven days.

Three were gone.

And the man who raised him wanted him dead.

He plunged his hands into the glowing water. Felt power surge up his arms. Felt the wolf stir, stretch, wake.

*Yes,* Alexander whispered. *Let me in.*

And Liam made a choice.

He did.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Traitor's Teeth

Luna helped me to my feet.

Her touch sent a shock through my system—not electricity, not pain, but something deeper. Recognition. My blood sang at her contact, my skin tingled where her fingers pressed against my arm.

I jerked away.

"You were his mate," I said, the words tasting like copper. "And you didn't tell me?"

"Would you have believed me if I had?"

"No."

"Then we're in agreement." She wiped blood from my cheek with a efficiency that spoke of practice. "The Moonlit Temple is two hours north. We need to move before—"

A howl split the night.

Close. Too close.

Luna's eyes flashed. "They found your scent trail. The rogues have a tracker—someone with your father's bloodline but twisted. Corrupted."

"Great. Another relative I didn't know about."

"Not a relative." She grabbed my wrist, pulled me toward the treeline. "An experiment. The Council has been trying to replicate the Wolf King bloodline for decades. They failed. Miserably. But they created monsters in the process."

Monsters. Right. Because my life wasn't complicated enough.

We ran.

The forest blurred past. My body should have been broken—Marcus had nearly killed me, I'd shifted for the first time, I'd collapsed in a stream and almost drowned. But something had changed. The pain was still there, distant now, like a memory of fire. My muscles moved faster than they should. My eyes pierced the darkness.

The mark on my chest thrummed with each heartbeat.

*You're finally using me,* Alexander said. *Took you long enough.*

*Shut up.*

*I can help you run faster. I can help you—*

*I said shut up.*

Luna led us through a maze of ancient oaks, her silver hair streaming behind her like a banner. She moved like she'd been born in these woods. Which, I realized, she probably had. How old was she? How long had she been waiting?

"How did you find me?" I asked, dodging a low-hanging branch.

"I've been watching you since you left the city."

"Creepy."

"Practical." She jumped a fallen log, landed without breaking stride. "Marcus wasn't the only one who swore to protect Alexander's bloodline. I made a vow the night your father died. I've kept it."

"For twenty-one years?"

"Twenty-one years, three months, and seven days."

We broke through the treeline into a clearing. An old hunting cabin sat in the center, windows dark, roof sagging. Safe house, maybe. Or a trap.

Luna headed straight for it.

"We can rest here for an hour," she said, pulling keys from her pocket. "There's a basement. Concrete walls. Silver-lined. It'll mask your scent."

"You have a silver-lined basement."

"Your father was paranoid. He had good reason."

She unlocked the door. Stepped inside.

The blade came out of nowhere.

A flash of steel. Luna's body went rigid. The tip of a silver dagger pressed against her throat, held by a man I'd never seen before. Tall. Gaunt. Wearing a Council uniform.

"Hello, Luna," he said. "The Council sends its regards."

Three more figures emerged from the shadows behind him. All armed. All wearing that same gray uniform with the crescent moon insignia.

Luna didn't flinch. "Aldric. I thought you were dead."

"Disappointed?"

"Surprised." She smiled, slow and dangerous. "Your wife always said you were too stubborn to die."

Aldric's face twisted. He pressed the dagger deeper. A bead of blood rolled down Luna's throat.

My blood boiled.

No. Not boiled. *Roared.*

The mark on my chest blazed. Heat spread through my veins like wildfire. My vision sharpened, colors bleeding into ultraviolet, and I saw them—not as people, but as targets. Heartbeats. Pressure points. The exact angle to break Aldric's wrist, disarm him, drive his own dagger through his throat.

I moved before I could think.

Three steps. A blur of motion. My hand caught Aldric's wrist and *twisted*. Bone cracked. He screamed. The dagger clattered to the floor. I grabbed his collar, lifted him off the ground, and slammed him against the cabin wall so hard the wood splintered.

"Call them off," I said.

My voice wasn't my own. It was deeper. Older. The voice of something that had been waiting thirty years to speak again.

Aldric's eyes went wide. "The mark—he has the mark—"

"I know what I have." I squeezed his throat. "Call. Them. Off."

The three other agents had their weapons drawn. Silver daggers. Silver bullets. Standard Council issue. They wouldn't kill me—the Council wanted me alive for the Blood Moon ceremony. But they'd hurt me. Hurt Luna.

Luna.

I glanced at her. She was watching me with an expression I couldn't read. Shock? Pride? Fear?

"Liam," she said quietly. "Don't kill him."

"Why not?"

"Because he knows where the Temple is. And we need a guide."

I looked back at Aldric. His face was purple. His hands clawed at my grip. He was seconds away from passing out.

"Talk," I said.

He gasped. "The Temple—it's not where you think. It moves. Every full moon. I can take you there."

"Or you can lead us into a trap."

"Can't—trap—need you alive—Council wants you alive—"

"Liar."

"I'm not!" Spit flew from his lips. "The Temple is the only place the Council can't touch you. It's warded by ancient blood. Your father's blood. No one can enter without the Wolf King's permission."

"And you expect me to believe you'll just walk us right in?"

"I have no choice. If I go back empty-handed, they'll kill me. If I take you to the Temple, you might let me live." He swallowed. "I'm a coward. I've always been a coward. That's why I'm still alive."

I stared at him. His heartbeat was fast. Erratic. But I couldn't smell deception. Couldn't taste the sour tang of a lie.

*He's telling the truth,* Alexander said. *Doesn't mean he's not dangerous.*

*I know.*

I released him.

Aldric collapsed, gasping. The other agents didn't move. They were waiting for orders that would never come.

Luna stepped past me, picked up the fallen dagger, and pressed it against Aldric's back. "Get up. You're taking us to the Temple."

He rose on shaky legs. "And them?" He gestured at his team.

"Leave them." Luna's voice was ice. "Tell the Council you were ambushed. We killed the others. You barely escaped."

"And if they check the bodies?"

"They won't find any." She looked at me. "Liam. Do it."

"Do what?"

"Show them why the Council is right to fear you."

The mark burned. Alexander's voice whispered in the back of my mind. *Show them.*

I didn't want to. I didn't want to become the monster Marcus had warned me about. The monster that had lurked in my blood for twenty-one years, waiting for the moon to call it out.

But Luna was watching. Aldric was watching. The three agents were watching, their faces pale, their weapons trembling.

I closed my eyes.

And I let the wolf rise.

Not a full shift. Something in between. My bones didn't break. My skin didn't tear. But when I opened my eyes, I knew they weren't human anymore. I could feel the amber glow, the vertical pupils, the sharpening of my teeth.

The agents screamed.

I didn't move. Didn't attack. I just stood there, half man, half wolf, and let them see what was coming for them.

They ran.

All three of them. Dropped their weapons and ran into the forest like the hounds of hell were at their heels.

Maybe they were.

Aldric stared at me, his face bloodless. "You're—you're really him. Alexander's son."

"Take us to the Temple," I said. "Or I'll show you what else I can do."

He nodded. Swallowed. Led us out the back door into the night.

The walk was silent.

Two hours through the forest, following paths that didn't exist on any map. Aldric moved with the confidence of someone who'd walked these woods a hundred times. Luna stayed close to me, her hand never far from the dagger at her belt.

I could feel the moon through the canopy. Pulling at me. Calling to the wolf in my blood.

*Seven days,* Alexander said. *You've already wasted one.*

*I know.*

*You need to learn control. You need to learn the bloodline. You need to—*

*I know!*

Silence. Then, softer: *I'm trying to help you, boy. I don't want to take over. I never wanted that. But if you don't master me by the Blood Moon, I won't have a choice.*

*Why?*

*Because the ritual doesn't care about what we want. It only cares about who's stronger. And right now, Liam... you're not.*

The trees parted.

A building stood in the clearing ahead. Not a temple. Not a cabin. It looked like a mausoleum, stone walls covered in ancient carvings, a single iron door set into the face.

The Moonlit Temple.

Aldric stopped twenty feet away. "I can't go further. The wards will kill me."

Luna stepped forward. Pressed her hand against the door. It glowed silver at her touch.

"It recognizes you," I said.

"I told you. I was your father's mate." She looked back at me, and for the first time, I saw the grief in her eyes. Real. Raw. Twenty-one years old. "I've been here before. Many times. It remembers me."

The door swung open.

Darkness inside. Ancient. Waiting.

Luna held out her hand. "Come. There's something you need to see. Something your father left for you."

I took her hand.

We stepped inside.

The door slammed shut behind us.

And somewhere in the darkness, a voice laughed. Not Alexander's. Older. Colder.

*Welcome home, Wolf King.*

The mark on my chest burned like a brand.

*We've been waiting.*

title: The Betrayal of Blood
content:
The Temple of the Moonlit Goddess rose from the mountain like a frozen waterfall—white stone carved into cascading waves that caught the dying sunlight and threw it back in fragments of gold and rose. Liam stood at the base of the steps, counting. Three hundred and seven. Each one slick with moss older than any city he'd ever walked.

Luna climbed beside him, her silver hair pulled back in a practical braid. She hadn't spoken in two hours. Not since they'd crossed the river and entered the territory marked by ancient wolf sigils carved into the bark of dead oaks.

"Your father rebuilt this place," she said finally, not looking at him. "After the Purge of '89, when the Council burned the original temple to the ground. Alexander carried the stones himself. One by one. Up this same mountain."

Liam's chest still ached where the mark had burned through his skin. The pain had faded to a dull throb, but he felt it with every heartbeat—a second pulse beneath his ribs, synced to something vast and patient.

"Why didn't he come with you?"

Luna stopped. Her hand touched the carved moon crest above the temple doors. "Because I killed him."

The words hung in the mountain air. Cold. Final.

Liam's wolf stirred. Not hostile. Watchful. The beast inside him recognized something in her voice that his human ears couldn't decode.

"Explain."

Luna pushed the doors open. They swung inward without a sound, revealing a chamber lit by candles floating in pools of dark water. The ceiling opened to the sky above, and moonlight spilled through like liquid silver.

"Alexander's bloodline was cursed. Not by enemies. By the Moon herself." She walked to the center of the room, where a circular pool reflected nothing but darkness. "Every Wolf King before him went mad within five years of claiming the throne. The power consumes them. Burns them from the inside until only the wolf remains."

"And my father?"

"He asked me to end him." Luna turned. Her eyes glowed. "I held him while he shifted for the last time. I whispered his name until the wolf retreated. And when he was himself again, I drove a silver blade through his heart."

Liam's vision went red. Not anger. Something else. Memory. Blood in a stream. A woman's scream. A child crying in the dark.

"I remember," he whispered.

"No. You remember what the blood shows you." Luna stepped closer. "Alexander planted those memories in your bloodline before he died. A failsafe. So you would know the truth if anyone tried to lie."

"Then tell me something I don't know."

"The Council didn't want you dead because of your bloodline. They wanted you dead because of what your bloodline carries." She reached into the pool and pulled out a blade. Silver. Wet. Dripping with darkness that wasn't water. "The Wolf King's curse isn't madness. It's the voice of the First Wolf. The one who devoured the Moon's daughter ten thousand years ago."

Liam's hand went to his chest. The mark burned cold.

"The First Wolf speaks to every heir. Promises power. Promises control. But every king who listened became a monster." Luna held the blade out to him. "Your father gave me this knife. Told me to give it to you when you were ready."

"Ready for what?"

"To make a choice." Her voice cracked. Just once. "Drink from the pool and accept the full power of the Wolf King. You'll be unstoppable for seven days. But on the Blood Moon, the First Wolf will claim your soul."

"And the other choice?"

Luna's hand shook. "Let me kill you now. Painless. Quick. Before the curse takes root."

The candles flickered. Liam's reflection stared up from the dark pool—a young man with shadows under his eyes and a wolf crouched behind his pupils.

"Those aren't the only two options."

"Liam—"

"Alexander didn't build a temple and plant memories in my blood just so I could die in a pool of moonwater." He stepped past her, toward the altar at the far end of the chamber. "He left something else. I can feel it."

The altar was bare stone. Smooth. Cold. But when Liam touched it, symbols blazed to life—words in a language older than the clans, older than the Council, older than the Moon herself.

"What does it say?" Luna asked.

Liam read the words aloud. His voice didn't sound like his own.

*"When the heir of blood and moon*
*Stands where shadow meets the noon*
*Let the silence break its fast*
*Wake the daughter from the past."*

The temple shook.

From the pool behind them, something rose. Water streamed from a figure made of moonlight—a woman's shape, translucent and terrible. Her eyes were twin stars. Her hair flowed like liquid night.

Luna dropped to her knees. "Goddess."

The figure looked past her. Straight at Liam. When she spoke, her voice was the sound of wind through dead leaves and the crash of waves against stone.

*"Alexander's son. You carry my murderer's blood."*

"The First Wolf," Liam said. "He killed your daughter."

*"And your father trapped me here. In this temple. In this pool. A cage of moonlight and memory."* She drifted closer. Her hand passed through his chest without touching. *"He thought he could contain my grief. But grief is not a river. It is the ocean. It does not stop. It only waits."*

"You want revenge."

*"I want justice."* Her form flickered. For a moment, Liam saw something else—a girl with silver hair and Luna's face, laughing in a field of white flowers. *"My daughter loved him. Loved the wolf who wore his skin. And when the First Wolf tore her apart, Alexander did nothing. He let it happen. He stood with his brothers and watched her die."*

"Alexander wasn't the First Wolf."

*"No. He was worse. He was the one who could have stopped it. And he chose not to."*

Liam's mark burned so cold he thought his chest would freeze. But he didn't back away.

"What do you want from me?"

The goddess smiled. It was not a kind smile.

*"I want you to kill the First Wolf. Permanently. Not seal him. Not cage him. End him so completely that even the Moon forgets his name."*

"And if I do?"

*"Then I will teach you to master the transformation. I will give you power beyond any Wolf King who came before. And I will let you live."*

Luna grabbed his arm. "Liam, don't. She's manipulating you. This is how the curse works—it always offers you exactly what you want."

"Can you teach me to control the shift in seven days?"

Luna hesitated.

"Can you?"

"No," she whispered. "No one can. That's why your father—"

"Then I don't have a choice." Liam turned back to the goddess. "I'll do it."

The goddess's form solidified. Became almost human. Almost warm.

*"Swear it on the blood that binds you."*

"I swear."

*"On the Moon that watches."*

"I swear."

*"On the daughter you will never know."*

Liam's throat tightened. "I swear."

The pool exploded upward. Moonwater engulfed him, filled his lungs, burned through his veins like liquid starlight. He saw everything—the First Wolf's birth in the age before ages, the slaughter of the Moon's daughter, Alexander's betrayal, the centuries of kings who wore the curse like a crown.

And he saw her. The daughter. A girl with silver hair and eyes like twin eclipses, standing in a field of white flowers, waiting for someone who would never come.

*"Seven days,"* the goddess whispered in his skull. *"Find the First Wolf's heart. Destroy it. Or become his vessel forever."*

The water receded.

Liam collapsed on the stone floor, gasping. His skin steamed in the cold air. His mark glowed so bright it cast shadows on the walls.

Luna knelt beside him. "What did she show you?"

"The First Wolf's heart." He coughed, tasted blood. "It's not a thing. It's a person. A werewolf. Someone carrying the original bloodline."

"Who?"

Liam looked up at her. The answer was already forming in his mind, planted there by the goddess like a seed in fertile ground.

"The Alpha of the Council of Elders."

Luna went pale. "That's impossible. The Council purged the original bloodline centuries ago. There's no way—"

"She showed me." Liam pushed himself up. His body felt different. Lighter. Stronger. More dangerous. "The current Alpha is a descendant of the First Wolf. He's been hiding in plain sight, manipulating the clans, waiting for the Blood Moon."

"Who?"

Liam met her eyes. "Marcus's father."

Luna's hand flew to her mouth. "Elder Theron? He's been on the Council for forty years. He's the one who ordered the attack on your father's pack."

"Because Alexander knew the truth. He found out what Theron was planning, and Theron had him killed." Liam's claws extended without his permission. Silver. Sharp. Beautiful. "Theron doesn't want me dead because of my bloodline. He wants me dead because I'm the only one who can expose him."

The temple doors exploded inward.

Figures in black poured through the entrance—dozens of wolves in half-shift, eyes burning gold and red. At their center stood a man in white robes, his face weathered by decades of authority, his eyes carrying the same ancient weight Liam had seen in the goddess's gaze.

Elder Theron.

"Liam Blackwood," he said, his voice smooth as oil. "You've been a very difficult boy to find."

Liam stepped forward. Luna moved to block him, but he held up a hand.

"You killed my father."

"I ended a threat to the Council's stability." Theron smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "And I'll end you too, if you make me."

"The goddess told me everything."

Theron's smile faltered. For just a moment, Liam saw fear flicker behind those ancient eyes.

"Then you know I can't let you live."

"No." Liam's shift began—not the painful, uncontrolled transformation from before, but something smooth. Intentional. His bones sang as they rearranged. His skin rippled with silver fur. "You can't let me live. But you can't stop me from dying."

He charged.

Theron's guards moved to intercept, but Luna was faster—she met them with claws extended, silver hair whipping like a battle flag. The temple erupted into chaos.

Liam crashed into Theron, driving him back through the shattered doors and onto the mountain steps. They tumbled down the wet stone, trading blows that cracked the ancient rock beneath them.

"You're strong," Theron grunted, pinning him. "But you don't know how to use it."

"Teach me."

Liam headbutted him. Cartilage crunched. Theron howled, and for a split second, his control slipped.

Liam saw it. The heart of the First Wolf, pulsing beneath Theron's ribs. A darkness so complete it swallowed light.

He reached for it.

Theron's claws raked across his chest, opening four wounds that steamed in the cold air. Liam screamed but didn't stop reaching.

His fingers touched the darkness.

The world went white.

When his vision cleared, he was lying on the steps, staring up at a

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Sleeping City

I took her hand.

It was colder than I expected. Not human-cold. Not corpse-cold either. Something in between. Like touching moonlight made solid.

Luna pulled me to my feet. My body screamed. Every muscle, every bone, every inch of skin that had been torn and half-healed protested. I swayed. She caught me.

"You're worse than I thought," she said.

"You're not the first woman to tell me that."

She didn't laugh. Her eyes stayed fixed on the treeline behind me. "They're coming. The Council's hunters. They tracked your blood."

"I don't have a car."

"I know."

"How far is this temple?"

"Three hours on foot. Fast."

I looked down at my legs. They were still shaking. Blood dripped from a gash on my thigh I didn't remember getting. "I can't run three hours."

"No. You can't."

She grabbed my arm and pulled me forward. Not toward the trees. Toward the stream. The water was shallow here, barely covering our ankles, but she splashed through it like she knew exactly where she was going.

"What are you doing?"

"Covering our scent. Water breaks the trail. Oldest trick in the book."

I stumbled after her, leaving bloody footprints in the mud. We followed the stream for maybe ten minutes before she cut left, into denser forest. The branches clawed at my face. Roots grabbed my feet. I fell twice. Each time she pulled me up without a word.

"Why are you helping me?" I asked.

"I told you. I was your father's mate."

"That's not an answer."

She stopped so suddenly I nearly ran into her. Turned. Those moonlit eyes pinned me in place. "You want the truth, Liam? The whole ugly truth?"

"Yes."

"Your father didn't die in a rogue attack. He was assassinated. By the Council. Because he refused to participate in the Blood Moon ceremony."

The words hit me like a punch to the chest. "What?"

"The ceremony doesn't just awaken the Wolf King's power. It binds it. Makes it controllable. Makes the King a tool of the Council. Alexander refused to be their puppet. So they killed him. Made it look like rogues. And they've been hunting his bloodline ever since."

I stared at her. The stream gurgled behind us. Somewhere, an owl called.

"Why didn't they kill me? When I was a kid?"

"Because they didn't know you existed. Your mother hid you. Changed your name. Erased every trace of your connection to Alexander. She died protecting that secret."

My hands were shaking. Not from the cold. "How do you know all this?"

"Because I was there. When they killed him. I held him while he died."

The words hung in the air between us. Heavy. Final.

"You loved him," I said.

"More than anything." Her voice cracked. Just slightly. "And I failed to protect him. I won't fail to protect his son."

She turned and kept walking.

I followed.

---

We moved through the forest for another hour. The pain didn't get better. It got worse. Every step sent fire through my veins. The mark on my chest pulsed with heat, like a second heartbeat.

*She's telling the truth,* Alexander's voice said.

"Shut up," I muttered.

*You can't shut me up. I'm in your blood. In your bones. You carry me everywhere now.*

"I'll find a way."

*You'll need me. Before this is over, you'll beg me to take control.*

"Never."

Luna looked back at me. "Who are you talking to?"

"Your dead boyfriend."

She didn't react. Just nodded. "He speaks to you?"

"Won't shut up."

"He's trying to protect you. In his own way."

"By taking over my body?"

"He knows what's coming. He knows you're not ready. And he knows the Council won't wait seven days."

I stopped. "What does that mean?"

Luna turned to face me fully. The moonlight caught her silver hair, made it glow like she was part of the night itself. "The hunters aren't just tracking you. They're herding you. Toward the Blood Moon site. They want you there early. Before the ceremony. So they can contain you."

"Contain me?"

"Chain you. Drug you. Whatever it takes to ensure you can't fight during the binding ritual. They need you compliant. Weak."

"Why not just kill me?"

"Because the power has to be transferred willingly. If you die, the Wolf King bloodline dies with you. They need you alive. Broken. But alive."

I laughed. It came out bitter and raw. "So I'm either dead or a puppet. Great options."

"There's a third option."

"What?"

"Awaken fully. Before the ceremony. Claim the Wolf King's power on your own terms. Then destroy the Council before they can bind you."

I stared at her. "And how exactly do I do that?"

"You need to master three things. The shift. The mark. And the ancestor."

"The ancestor?"

"Alexander. The part of him that lives in you. You need to stop fighting him and start working with him."

*She's smarter than she looks,* Alexander said.

"I don't trust him," I said.

"You don't have to trust him. You have to use him. There's a difference."

I opened my mouth to argue. Then I heard it.

A howl.

Close. Too close.

Luna's eyes went wide. "Run."

We ran.

The forest became a blur of dark shapes and shadows. Branches whipped past. My lungs burned. My legs screamed. But I kept moving. Because behind us, the howls multiplied. One became three. Three became five.

"Can't outrun them," I gasped.

"I know."

"Then what—"

She grabbed my arm and yanked me sideways. We tumbled down a steep embankment, rocks and dirt raining around us. I hit the bottom hard. Luna landed beside me, already on her feet.

"Where are we?"

She pointed.

I looked up.

The mountain loomed above us. Massive. Ancient. And carved into its face was a city.

Not a temple. Not a ruin.

A city.

Stone buildings rose in terraces, connected by bridges and staircases carved from the mountain itself. Lights flickered in windows. Torches. Candles. Signs of life.

"What is this place?"

"The Moonlit Temple," Luna said. "What you're seeing is the outer settlement. The temple is deeper inside the mountain. But this is where we'll be safe."

"How many people live here?"

"Hundreds. Maybe thousands. Descendants of the original Wolf King's supporters. Exiles. Outcasts. Anyone who refused to bow to the Council."

The howls grew louder. Closer.

"They can't follow us inside," Luna said. "The city is warded. Old magic. Older than the Council. But we have to reach the gates before they catch us."

I looked at the city. At the walls. At the gates that stood maybe a hundred meters away.

Then I looked at my legs. They were done. Finished. I couldn't take another step.

"I can't," I said.

"You can."

"I can't, Luna. I'm out."

She grabbed my face. Forced me to look at her. "Your father ran this same gauntlet. Wounded. Bleeding. Hunted. And he made it. Because he refused to give up. You're his son. You have his blood. His stubbornness. His will. Now move."

She let go and ran.

And somehow, impossibly, I ran after her.

The gates loomed ahead. Open. Waiting. I could see figures inside. People. Watching.

Twenty meters.

The first wolf broke from the trees behind us.

Ten meters.

The second. Third. A pack of them, eyes burning with hunger.

Five meters.

I dove through the gates.

And slammed into something solid.

Not a wall. A person.

I looked up.

An old man stood over me. Ancient. Wrinkled. But his eyes—his eyes were the same silver as Luna's. The same silver as the moon.

"So," he said. "The Wolf King returns."

He held out his hand.

And I saw the mark on his palm.

The same crescent moon. The same wolf's head.

The same symbol that burned on my chest.

"Welcome home, nephew."

title: Chapter 14
content:
# CHAPTER 14: The Taste of Memories

The Temple of the Moonlit Grove wasn't a temple.

It was a cave.

Luna led him through three miles of dense forest, never once slowing, never once checking if he followed. Her silver hair caught the moonlight like strands of liquid pearl. Her bare feet made no sound on the leaves.

Liam's bare feet crunched everything.

"You couldn't have warned me about the shoe situation?" he muttered.

"Shifters don't wear shoes."

"I'm not a shifter yet."

She glanced back. "You will be. Sooner than you think."

The cave entrance appeared between two massive oaks—roots twisted together like grasping fingers, forming an archway draped in moss and hanging vines. Inside, darkness so absolute it felt solid.

Luna stepped through without hesitation.

Liam followed.

And immediately understood why the Council had never found this place.

The cave smelled wrong. Not in a bad way—in a *blocked* way. Like someone had taken his sense of smell and muted it. The air felt heavy against his skin. The sounds of the forest died the moment he crossed the threshold.

"Wards," Luna said, her voice echoing off stone walls he couldn't see. "Your father's work. They mask the temple from the Council's hunters, dampen scents, confuse tracking abilities. Even the strongest alpha would walk right past this cave and see nothing but another oak tree."

"How did you find it?"

"I helped build them."

The cave opened into a chamber.

Liam stopped breathing.

The cavern stretched a hundred feet high, its ceiling covered in crystals that pulsed with soft blue light—like veins of frozen moonlight. Water trickled down the walls, feeding a pool in the center of the room. The pool glowed. Actually glowed. Silver mist rose from its surface, curling upward like living smoke.

And around the pool, carved into the stone floor, were symbols. Dozens of them. Circles within circles, lines connecting moons of different phases, wolves chasing tails that never ended.

"What is this place?"

Luna walked to the pool's edge. She knelt, dipped her fingers in the water, and the glow intensified. "This is where your father first shifted. Where he learned to master the Wolf King's power. Where he—" Her voice cracked. "Where he said goodbye to me before he went to face the Council."

"Face them for what?"

She didn't answer.

Liam limped to the pool's edge. His reflection stared back—pale, bruised, eyes sunken. The mark on his chest had settled into a dull ache, the crescent moon and wolf head glowing faintly through his torn shirt.

"Seven days," he said. "That's what Alexander told me. Seven days until the Blood Moon ceremony."

"He's not wrong."

"But he's not telling me everything either."

Luna's eyes met his. For a moment, the ancient sadness in them flickered, replaced by something sharper. Wariness. "What do you mean?"

"Marcus. Before he died. He said the Council wanted me dead because of what I am. But he also said you were there when it happened. When I was born." Liam's hands balled into fists. "You knew my mother. You knew my father. You were their *friend*. And I found out who I was from a dying stranger in a warehouse, not from you."

The accusation hung in the air.

Luna looked away first.

"You're right," she said quietly. "I should have told you. Should have found you years ago. But the Council has eyes everywhere, and the moment they knew you existed—" She shook her head. "I couldn't risk it. Couldn't risk leading them to you before you were ready."

"Ready for what?"

"To become what you were born to be."

"The Wolf King."

"No." She stood. Turned to face him fully. "The *catalyst*."

The word hit like a punch.

"What catalyst?"

Luna walked around the pool, her fingers trailing through the glowing water. "The Wolf King's power doesn't just make you stronger, faster, more dominant. It binds all werewolves together. Every shifter, every pack, every rogue—they're all connected through your bloodline. The Wolf King is the anchor that holds the werewolf world together."

"I didn't ask for this."

"It doesn't matter what you asked for." Her voice hardened. "Your father didn't ask for it either. But when he awakened, he had a choice. Use the power to unite the clans, or let the Council use it to destroy anyone who opposed them."

"He chose to unite them."

"He tried." Luna's jaw tightened. "He spent five years traveling between packs, settling disputes, forging alliances. He was going to call a Great Council—the first in a century—to establish a new system of laws. One that protected shifters from the Council's tyranny."

"What happened?"

"The Council happened." She stopped at the far side of the pool. "They invited him to a peace summit. Promised to hear his proposals. He went alone—didn't want to seem aggressive." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "They used silver chains laced with wolfsbane. Took him three days to die."

Liam's blood burned.

*Alexander.*

The voice in his head stirred. Not angry. Not sad. Something else. Something that felt almost like... acceptance.

*She's telling the truth.*

"Alexander," Liam said aloud. "He's... he's in my head. Talking to me."

Luna's eyes widened. "He's already manifested?"

"He said he's been waiting thirty years."

She paled. "That's not possible. The Wolf King's spirit only awakens in the heir during the Blood Moon ceremony. Unless—" She stopped. Stared at him. "Unless the bloodline is more unstable than we thought."

"What does that mean?"

"It means—"

The cave shook.

Dust rained from the ceiling. Crystals flickered. The pool's glow dimmed, then flared back to life.

Luna's head snapped toward the entrance. "They found us."

"Who?"

She grabbed his arm. Her grip was iron. "The Council's hunters. They must have tracked you from the city."

"But the wards—"

"The wards hide scent. They don't hide the *sound* of an earthquake." She dragged him toward the far wall. "There's a passage. It leads to the underground river. We can—"

The entrance exploded.

Stone shattered. Light flooded the cave—harsh, white, artificial. Liam threw up his arm, blinked through the brightness, and saw them.

Three figures stood in the broken entrance.

The one in front was a woman. Tall. Dark skin. Hair cropped close to her scalp. Her eyes glowed amber—alpha eyes—and her smile showed canines that were already lengthening.

"Well, well." Her voice was honey over broken glass. "Luna of the Moonlit Temple. Hiding a fugitive in your little sanctuary." She stepped over the rubble. "You always were sentimental."

Luna moved in front of Liam. "Let him go, Elena. He's done nothing to you."

"He's the Wolf King's heir." Elena's smile widened. "That's enough."

"You know what your masters plan to do with him."

"I know what they *pay* me to do with him." Elena cracked her neck. "And I *really* need the money."

She moved.

Liam didn't see her cross the room—just felt the wind as Luna met her mid-charge. They collided with a sound like thunder. Claws raked. Teeth snapped. Fur sprouted and receded as both women shifted in combat, landing blows too fast to track.

The other two hunters circled.

Liam backed toward the passage Luna had pointed to. His hands shook. His heart hammered. The mark on his chest burned hotter than ever.

*You can't run,* Alexander said. *You're the Wolf King.*

"Don't," Liam whispered. "Don't you dare."

*Shift.*

"I don't know how."

*Then learn.*

Elena slammed Luna into the cave wall. Rock cracked. Luna crumpled, blood streaming from a gash on her forehead.

Elena turned to Liam.

"Last chance," she said. "Come quietly. Or I bring you in pieces."

Liam's blood screamed.

The mark on his chest blazed.

And for the first time in his life, he stopped fighting it.

He let the wolf rise.

The pain was beyond anything he'd experienced. Bones splintered and reformed. Muscles tore and knitted. His spine curved, his jaw extended, his fingers became claws—

And then it stopped.

Liam looked down at his hands.

Still human.

*Not good enough,* Alexander growled. *You're fighting me again.*

"I'm not—"

*You are. Let go.*

"I don't—"

*LET GO.*

Elena lunged.

Liam's body moved before his mind caught up.

He ducked under her swing, grabbed her arm, and threw her over his shoulder. She crashed into the pool. The glowing water erupted, splashing across the cave walls, sizzling where it touched the hunters' weapons.

One of them screamed. His silver blade dissolved mid-swing.

Liam stared at his hands.

*You didn't shift,* Alexander said. *But you borrowed my strength.*

Elena climbed out of the pool. Her amber eyes had gone red. "You're going to pay for that."

She shifted.

Full shift.

Her body twisted, expanded, furred over. In seconds, a seven-foot wolf stood where the woman had been. Muscles rippled under gray-black fur. Saliva dripped from fangs as long as Liam's fingers.

She charged.

Liam didn't think.

He ran.

The passage was dark, tight, winding. He squeezed through, scraping his shoulders on rough stone, hearing Elena's claws scrabbling behind him.

The sound of water grew louder.

The passage ended in a drop.

Liam looked down.

Twenty feet below, a river rushed through darkness. Fast. Cold. Impossible to survive.

Behind him, Elena growled.

"Jump," Luna's voice said.

He turned. She was there, limping, bleeding, but alive. She'd followed him through the passage.

"The river leads to the underground lake. There's a tunnel to the surface. I'll hold her off."

"Luna—"

"GO."

He jumped.

The water hit him like a wall of ice. The current grabbed him, spun him, dragged him under. He fought, kicked, broke the surface, gasped for air—

And saw Elena on the edge above.

But she wasn't looking at him.

She was looking at something behind him.

Liam turned in the water.

A hundred glowing eyes stared back.

The underground lake wasn't empty.

It was full of wolves.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Blood That Binds

The Temple of Echoed Bones stank of old death.

Liam crouched behind a collapsed pillar, his fingers digging into ancient stone that crumbled like wet bread. Three days since Luna had pulled him from the stream. Three days of running, hiding, learning. His body ached in places he didn't know existed. The mark on his chest burned constantly now, a second heartbeat pulsing beneath his skin.

*They're close,* Alexander growled. *The rogue pack has your scent.*

"I know."

Liam pressed his forehead against the cold stone. The temple ruins stretched before him, a maze of broken arches and shattered altars dedicated to gods no one remembered. Moonlight poured through a hole in the ceiling, illuminating a single intact statue at the center—a wolf with three eyes, its mouth open in an eternal howl.

The Rogues had cornered him here. Twelve of them. Maybe more.

He'd counted six distinct heartbeats in the shadows. The others were the quiet ones—the hunters who could mask their pulse, their breath, their very presence until they were close enough to taste your fear.

*You can't fight twelve,* Alexander said.

"Then what do you suggest?"

*Run. Live. Fight another day.*

"I'm tired of running."

Liam closed his eyes. Three days of training with Luna. Three days of learning what it meant to carry the Wolf King's blood. She'd taught him to sense the shift coming, to push it back or let it flow. She'd taught him that the Moonlit Temple was more than a place—it was a bloodline, a legacy, a family that had been waiting for him.

She'd also taught him that the Council had killed his father not because he was dangerous, but because he refused to bow.

*The Rogues answer to someone,* Luna had said, her silver eyes reflecting the moon. *Someone who wants the Wolf King bloodline extinct. Someone who's been hunting it for thirty years.*

"Someone who knew my father," Liam had whispered.

*Someone who was his brother.*

The memory made his chest tighten. His uncle. A man whose name Luna refused to speak. A man who had built the Rogue army, who had twisted the old laws, who had been waiting for the heir to surface so he could finish what he started.

And now that heir was hiding in a pile of bones, listening to death approach.

A footstep. Close. Too close.

Liam's eyes snapped open.

The Rogue stood three feet away, his wolf half-shifted, claws extended, fangs gleaming in the moonlight. He sniffed the air, head tilted, nostrils flaring. His eyes scanned the pillar Liam hid behind—then looked past it.

He hadn't seen him.

*He will,* Alexander said. *Your scent is everywhere. You're bleeding.*

Liam looked down. A cut on his forearm from the chase. Fresh blood. He pressed his hand against it, but the damage was done. The Rogue's head snapped back toward the pillar. His nostrils flared again. His lips pulled back from his teeth.

"He's here," the Rogue growled.

The shadows came alive.

Six wolves exploded from the darkness, converging on the pillar. Liam didn't think. He moved. His body reacted before his mind caught up, pushing off the stone, rolling, coming up with a chunk of broken altar in his hand. He threw it at the first wolf's face, heard bone crack, saw the creature stumble.

But there were five more.

Claws raked his back. He screamed, spun, caught a glimpse of yellow eyes and bared fangs. His fist connected with something soft. A jaw. A throat. He didn't know. He didn't care.

*Let me out,* Alexander roared. *Let me take them.*

"No."

*You'll die.*

"Then I'll die human."

The wolves circled. Liam stood in the center, his back against the three-eyed wolf statue, blood dripping from a dozen wounds. His vision swam. His legs shook. The mark on his chest burned so hot he expected to see smoke rising from his shirt.

The largest wolf stepped forward. Gray fur. One eye missing. Scars crisscrossing his muzzle like a roadmap of violence.

"The heir," he said, his voice a rumble that came from somewhere deep. "The Council will pay well for your head."

"Tell them I said hello."

The wolf laughed. A horrible sound, like rocks grinding together. "I'll tell them you begged."

He lunged.

Time slowed.

Liam saw the claws coming. Saw the path they would take—across his throat, through his jugular, ending everything. He saw his death in the wolf's single eye.

And then he saw something else.

The symbol on his chest exploded with light.

Not burning. Not painful. *Alive.*

The wolf's claws stopped an inch from his throat. The creature hung in the air, frozen, his single eye wide with confusion. Around them, the other wolves were also frozen, their bodies locked in place like statues in a museum of violence.

*What—*

The light expanded. Liam felt it push outward from his chest, a wave of pure silver energy that washed over the temple. The wolves screamed. Not in anger. In *fear.*

Then they ran.

All of them. In an instant. The largest wolf scrambled backward, his claws scraping against the stone floor, his tail tucked between his legs. He didn't look back. None of them did.

They fled into the darkness, and the temple fell silent.

Liam collapsed to his knees.

His chest heaved. His hands trembled. The mark on his chest still glowed, but fainter now, like an ember dying.

*What was that?* he asked.

Alexander was quiet for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was different. Softer. Almost reverent.

*That was the Wolf King's command. The power to bend lesser wolves to your will.*

"I didn't do anything."

*You didn't have to. The blood knows its own. Even Rogues remember, deep down, who the true alpha is.*

Liam stared at his hands. Normal hands. Still bleeding. Still human.

But something had changed. He could feel it. A thread connecting him to something vast and ancient, a web of power that stretched across the city, across the country, across the world.

*You're not just the heir anymore,* Alexander said. *You're the King. And kings don't run.*

"I'm not ready."

*You have four days. Make them count.*

Liam pushed himself to his feet. His body screamed in protest, but he ignored it. He had to move. The Rogues would regroup. They'd come back with more wolves, stronger wolves, wolves who might not be affected by the command.

He needed to find Luna. He needed answers.

He needed to know who his uncle really was.

A sound. From the shadows. Not footsteps. Not breathing.

A whisper.

*Liam.*

He spun. The temple was empty. The wolves were gone. The moonlight still poured through the hole in the ceiling, illuminating the three-eyed wolf.

*Liam.*

The voice came from the statue. The wolf's stone eyes—all three of them—were glowing. A faint amber light that pulsed like a heartbeat.

"Who are you?"

*You know who I am. You've always known.*

The statue's mouth moved. Stone ground against stone. The howl became words.

*I am the first. The origin. The wolf who became a god.*

"The First Howl."

*You've heard the stories. Now you must live them.*

Liam stepped closer. The statue's eyes followed him. "What do you want?"

*To warn you. The blood moon ceremony is not what you think. It is not a trial. It is a trap.*

"A trap for who?"

*For you. For your father's brother. For everyone who carries the Wolf King's blood. The ceremony was designed to destroy the bloodline, not preserve it.*

Liam's blood ran cold. "Luna didn't tell me this."

*Luna doesn't know. She was never told. Only the statues remember. Only the stones hold the truth.*

"Why are you telling me now?"

*Because you awakened the command. Because you proved you are worthy. Because the blood moon rises in four days, and if you attend the ceremony, you will die.*

The light faded. The statue's eyes went dark. The temple fell silent again.

Liam stood alone in the ruins, his heart pounding, his mind racing.

Four days.

A ceremony that would kill him.

An uncle who wanted him dead.

And a woman who might not be who she claimed to be.

He looked up at the moon through the hole in the ceiling. Full. Silver. Watching.

"I'm coming for you," he whispered. "Whoever you are. Whatever you've done. I'm coming."

The moon didn't answer.

But somewhere in the darkness, a wolf howled.

And Liam knew the hunt had only just begun.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Price of Trust

Luna's words hung in the air like smoke. *Your father's mate.* Liam's blood went cold despite the fever still burning through his veins.

"You're lying."

She didn't flinch. "I never lied to you. I just didn't tell you everything."

He pushed himself up from the stream, every muscle screaming. Water dripped from his clothes. His hands trembled. "You watched me almost die tonight. You let Marcus—" His voice cracked. "You let him sacrifice himself."

"Marcus made his choice." Luna's silver eyes held something ancient. Something that had seen too many deaths. "He knew what you are. What you have to become."

"And what's that? Your pawn?"

"Your father's legacy." She stepped closer. The moonlight caught her face, and for a moment Liam saw lines that shouldn't be there. Faint. Like scars beneath her skin. "Alexander wasn't just the Wolf King. He was the last hope for peace between clans. When he died, the balance shattered. The Northern Pack, the Shadow Fangs, the Crimson Claws—they've been tearing each other apart for thirty years. And now the rogues are rising because they smell weakness."

Liam laughed. Bitter. Broken. "I'm a janitor. I clean toilets for a living. I can't even shift."

"You will."

"How do you know?"

"Because I've been training heirs for three centuries." She turned and walked toward the tree line. "And you're not the first Wolf King I've guided."

Three centuries. The number hit him like a punch to the chest. He followed because he didn't have anywhere else to go. Because Alexander's voice had gone silent. Because the rogues were hunting him, and the Council wanted him dead, and the only person who seemed to know anything was this woman who smelled of moonlight and secrets.

They walked in silence for an hour. The forest grew darker. Older. The trees twisted into shapes that shouldn't exist naturally. Roots curled like fingers through the soil. The air thickened until it felt like breathing through wet cloth.

"Where are we going?"

"The Moonlit Temple isn't on any map," Luna said without turning. "It exists between worlds. Between the human realm and the wolf realm. Your father built it as neutral ground."

"Neutral ground for what?"

She stopped. Turned. Her eyes glowed brighter now. Unnatural. "For the Blood Moon Council. The four original clan leaders. They haven't gathered in thirty years. Not since Alexander died."

"Why would they gather now?"

"Because the Wolf King has returned." She held his gaze. "And because I've been sending messages for the last three hours. They're waiting for you."

Liam's stomach dropped. "You told them about me?"

"They would have found out anyway. Better to control the narrative."

"Control the—" He grabbed her arm. She let him. "I don't know anything about these clans. I don't know the politics. I don't know who to trust. And you just threw me into a meeting with four ancient werewolf leaders?"

Luna looked at his hand on her arm. Then at his face. "You have seven days, Liam. Six now. You think you have time to learn etiquette? You think the rogues will wait while you read history books?" She pulled free. "The clans need to see you. Smell you. Know you're real. If they think you're weak, they'll tear you apart before the blood moon even rises."

"And if I am weak?"

"Then we all die." She turned and kept walking.

The temple appeared between one blink and the next. One moment, there was nothing but twisted trees and darkness. The next, a massive structure rose from the earth like a mountain carved into a cathedral. Moonstone pillars. Silver veins running through black marble. A door that looked like it was made of solid moonlight.

Liam stopped breathing.

"It's beautiful," he whispered.

"It's a fortress," Luna corrected. "The only place where clan blood isn't allowed to spill. Break that rule, and the temple itself will kill you."

"Good to know."

She pushed open the door. The light inside was soft. Silver. Like standing inside a cloud. Four figures waited in the center of a circular chamber. Four thrones carved from different materials—one of ice, one of obsidian, one of bone, one of living wood.

The ice throne held a woman with skin so pale she looked carved from frost. Her hair was white. Her eyes were blue. She wore a gown that shimmered like frozen water.

The obsidian throne held a man built like a war god. Black armor. Black fur draped over his shoulders. Scars across every inch of visible skin. His eyes were yellow. Hungry.

The bone throne held someone who didn't look human at all. A creature wrapped in shadows, face hidden behind a mask of teeth. It didn't move. Didn't blink. Just watched.

The living wood throne was empty.

Luna bowed. "The Wolf King has arrived."

The woman of ice spoke first. Her voice crackled like frost forming on glass. "He's a child."

The war god leaned forward. "He doesn't even smell like a shifter."

The creature in bones said nothing. Just tilted its head. Waiting.

Liam stepped forward. His legs shook. His heart hammered. But Marcus had died for him. Alexander's blood ran through his veins. He wouldn't cower.

"I'm Liam," he said. "And I didn't ask for any of this. But I'm here. So either help me or get out of my way."

Silence.

The ice woman's lips curled. "He has Alexander's fire. At least."

The war god stood. He was seven feet tall. Easy. "Fire doesn't win wars. Teeth do. Claws do. Blood does." He walked toward Liam, slow and deliberate. "You're not ready. You'll get us all killed."

"Then train me."

The war god stopped. Blinked. "What?"

"Train me. You think I'm weak? Prove it. Break me. Teach me. I have six days to become a king. So train me like you would train a soldier."

The man's yellow eyes flickered. Something shifted in his expression. Respect? Surprise? He turned to Luna. "You didn't tell me he had balls."

"He didn't know he had them either," Luna said. "But they're there."

The war god laughed. It sounded like rocks grinding together. "Fine. I'll train you. But you won't survive the first session."

"I'll survive."

"You'll wish you hadn't."

The creature in bones finally spoke. Its voice was wrong. Like multiple people talking at once. "The Shadow Fangs have gathered at the border. Three hundred strong. They know the Wolf King has awakened. They mean to strike before the ceremony."

The ice woman stood. "The Northern Pack will hold the line. For now." She looked at Liam. "But we can't fight a war and protect you at the same time. Learn fast, boy. Or we all burn."

The creature in bones stood. "The Crimson Claws have not decided which side to take. They wait. They watch. They will choose based on what they see in the next six days."

Everyone looked at Liam.

He felt the weight of their expectations like a physical thing. Four clan leaders. Three hundred rogues. An ancient war. A dead father's ghost whispering in his blood.

"Six days," he said. "I won't let you down."

The war god grabbed his shoulder. Squeezed. Hard enough to bruise. "You won't let yourself down. That's what matters."

The meeting ended. Luna led him through winding corridors, past chambers filled with relics and weapons and tapestries depicting battles he couldn't imagine. Finally, she stopped at a small room with a bed and a window that looked out at the moon.

"Rest," she said. "Training starts at dawn."

"Luna."

She paused.

"Why didn't you tell me? About my father? About you?"

She turned. For a moment, she looked human. Vulnerable. "Because I've been alive for six hundred years. I've watched everyone I've ever loved die. Alexander was the last. And when I saw you in that alley, covered in blood, fighting for your life..." She shook her head. "I couldn't lose you too. Not yet."

She left before he could respond.

Liam lay on the bed. Stared at the ceiling. The mark on his chest still glowed faintly. Alexander's voice whispered at the edge of his consciousness. *She loved me. She'll love you too. But love makes people reckless.*

"Then what do I do?"

*Survive. That's all that matters. Survive until the blood moon.*

"And then?"

Silence.

Liam closed his eyes. Sleep took him like a wave.

He dreamed of silver eyes. Of moonlight. Of a woman with scars beneath her skin and centuries of grief in her smile.

And in the dream, someone whispered his name.

Not Luna. Not Alexander.

A voice he'd never heard before.

*Liam. Wake up. They're coming.*

He snapped awake.

The room was dark. The moon had moved. Hours had passed.

And the door was opening.

A figure stepped through. Tall. Male. Reeking of blood and rot.

"Hello, little king," the figure said. "I'm here to collect on a debt."

Liam rolled off the bed as claws raked through where his head had been. He hit the floor, scrambled, grabbed the first thing his hands found—a metal candlestick.

The figure laughed. "That won't save you."

"Maybe not." Liam's blood burned. His bones ached. The wolf clawed at the inside of his skin. "But it'll slow you down."

He swung.

The candlestick connected with the figure's skull. Cracked. Bent.

The figure didn't flinch.

"You're going to have to do better than that."

Liam's vision blurred. The mark on his chest blazed. Alexander's voice roared in his skull.

*Let me out.*

"No."

*LET ME OUT OR YOU DIE.*

The figure lunged.

And Liam stopped fighting.

He opened the door inside himself.

The wolf surged forward. His bones cracked. His skin tore. Pain exploded through every nerve.

And then—silence.

He looked down at his hands.

They weren't hands anymore.

They were paws. Massive. Covered in black fur. Claws like daggers.

He opened his mouth and roared.

The sound shook the walls. Shook the temple. Shook the very air.

The figure stumbled backward. "Impossible. He's not supposed to be able to shift until the ceremony."

Liam—no, the wolf—lunged.

Teeth found flesh. Blood flooded his mouth. Hot. Copper. Alive.

The figure screamed.

Somewhere in the distance, Luna's voice called his name.

But the wolf didn't care.

The wolf was hungry.

The wolf wanted more.

And Liam, trapped inside his own body, couldn't stop it.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Weight of Crowns

The runes pulsed beneath Liam's palms like a second heartbeat.

He'd been kneeling in the Moonlit Temple's central chamber for what felt like hours, surrounded by symbols that burned silver every time he touched them. Luna stood at the edge of the circle, her silver hair catching the ethereal light, her face unreadable.

"Again," she said.

"I've done it twelve times."

"Then do it thirteen."

Liam bit back a curse and pressed his hands flat against the stone floor. The runes flared. Heat crawled up his arms, through his chest, settling in the mark over his heart. The wolf stirred. Hungry. Impatient.

*She's testing you,* Alexander said. *Stop thinking. Feel.*

"Easy for you to say," Liam muttered. "You're dead."

*Dead. Asleep. Same thing. Focus.*

The runes responded to his bloodline. That's what Luna had explained. The Moonlit Temple wasn't just a sanctuary—it was a lockbox. Built by the first Wolf Kings to store knowledge that couldn't be trusted to memory. Every time Liam touched the runes, fragments of the past bled into his consciousness.

He saw battles fought centuries ago. Heard the screams of wolves who'd lost themselves to the blood rage. Felt the weight of a crown that had crushed every king who'd worn it.

And always, always, he saw Alexander's face.

Not the wolf. The man. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Eyes that held the same gold flecks Liam saw in the mirror. Standing beside a woman with silver hair.

Luna.

"You're thinking about him again," she said.

Liam opened his eyes. "You were his mate. Why didn't you tell me?"

"Because you weren't ready."

"And I am now?"

She stepped into the circle. The runes didn't burn her feet. They curved around her like water around a stone. "Your father didn't want you to know about me. He thought it would put you in danger."

"He was dead, Luna. What danger could there be?"

"The same danger that killed him."

Liam stood. His knees cracked. The mark on his chest throbbed in rhythm with his heartbeat. "You're going to have to give me more than vague warnings. I have five days until the Blood Moon. Five days to learn how to fight rogues, navigate Council politics, and keep myself from becoming a mindless beast. I need answers."

Luna studied him. Her eyes held the same ancient weight he'd seen in Marcus's gaze. The same sadness.

"The Silverwood Pack didn't kill Alexander," she said.

Liam's blood went cold. "What?"

"He died fighting them. That part is true. But they weren't the ones who struck the killing blow." She paused. "Your father was assassinated. By someone close to him. Someone who knew his weaknesses. His patterns. His hunting grounds."

The words hit like a physical blow. "One of his own pack?"

"No. Someone who wanted his bloodline to end."

"Who?"

Luna's jaw tightened. "I don't know their name. But I know their symbol. A broken crescent. Three claw marks through it."

The mark of the Shadowmoon Sect.

Liam had heard Marcus mention them once. A splinter group of wolves who believed the Wolf King bloodline had become corrupted. That the ancient power should be destroyed, not inherited. He'd assumed they were a myth. Boogeyman stories to scare young wolves into obedience.

"They're real," he said.

"Very real. And they know you're alive."

"How?"

"The awakening. Every time a Wolf King's power surfaces, it sends a ripple through the supernatural world. The Sect felt it. The Council felt it. Every pack within a thousand miles felt it." She stepped closer. "You've been marked since the moment you shifted. The only question is who finds you first."

Liam's hands trembled. He pressed them flat against his thighs. "Then why are we wasting time with runes? Teach me to fight. Teach me to shift at will. Give me something I can use."

"These runes *are* something you can use. They're the only thing that can suppress the blood rage during the full moon. Without them, you'll lose yourself completely."

"Like Alexander did?"

She flinched. Just slightly. But he saw it.

"Your father never wanted the crown," she said softly. "He was forced into it. His brother died in the Blood Moon ceremony. The wolf consumed him. Alexander had to take the throne or watch the pack fall apart."

"There was another heir?"

"He didn't tell you. Of course he didn't." Luna turned away. "The Wolf King bloodline doesn't pass through a single line. It awakens in whoever is strongest. Alexander's brother, Damian, was the original heir. But he wasn't strong enough. The wolf broke him."

Liam's stomach twisted. "And if I'm not strong enough?"

"Then you'll die. Or worse. You'll become what Damian became."

"What did he become?"

Luna faced him. Her eyes glowed bright silver. "A monster. The Sect took him in after his transformation. Used him as a weapon. Forced him to hunt his own bloodline." She paused. "He's the one who killed your mother."

The air left Liam's lungs.

He'd always been told his mother died in childbirth. A complication. Nothing more. Marcus had said it with such finality that Liam never questioned it. Never had reason to.

"Your father found Damian three days later," Luna continued. "They fought for an entire night. By morning, Alexander was wounded. Damian was dead. But the damage was done. The Sect had what they needed."

"Which was?"

"Proof that the bloodline could be corrupted. That the Wolf King's power wasn't sacred. It was a weapon. And weapons could be stolen."

Liam's hands clenched into fists. "So the Sect's been waiting for me. For thirty years."

"Longer. They've been hunting the bloodline since the first Wolf King fell. Every time a new heir awakens, they try to claim them. Sometimes they succeed." Her voice dropped. "Sometimes they don't."

"But Alexander stopped them."

"He did. At the cost of his life. The Sect never forgave him for killing Damian. They've been rebuilding their strength ever since. Waiting for the next heir."

Waiting for him.

Liam looked down at his hands. The hands that had shattered a window. The hands that had clawed at his own skin during his first shift. The hands that had held Marcus as he died.

He wasn't ready for this. He was a college student. A guy who'd spent the last three years studying business and avoiding his roommate's questions about why he slept through every full moon. He wasn't a king. He wasn't a warrior.

But the mark on his chest said otherwise.

"Teach me," he said. "Everything. The runes. The shifting. The history. I need to know it all."

Luna's expression softened. Just for a moment. "You remind me of him."

"Alexander?"

"No. Damian. Before he fell." She shook her head. "The same fire. The same desperation. The same fear that you'll hurt the people you love."

"I don't have anyone to hurt."

"That's what Damian said too. Right before he killed his own mother."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

Liam wanted to argue. Wanted to tell her she was wrong. That he was different. That he'd never—

But he remembered the rage. The burning in his blood. The way his vision had turned red when the rogues attacked. He remembered wanting to tear them apart. Wanted to feel their flesh between his teeth.

He'd held back. Barely.

"What if I can't control it?" he asked.

Luna met his gaze. "Then I'll do what Alexander couldn't."

"You'd kill me."
