No golden spires. No marble columns. No grand gates guarded by warriors in ceremonial armor. Instead, the temple was hidden inside a mountain—a cave entrance covered by a waterfall, the stone walls carved with ancient symbols that pulsed with faint silver light.

Luna parted the waterfall with a gesture, and the water obeyed like it was alive.

"Welcome to the last sanctuary of the Wolf Kings," she said.

Liam stepped inside.

The cave opened into a vast chamber, lit by crystals embedded in the ceiling. Symbols covered every surface—crescent moons, howling wolves, intricate patterns that seemed to shift when he looked at them directly. In the center stood a stone altar, and above it, a blade made of pure moonlight.

"The Moonblade," Luna said. "Forged from a fragment of the blood moon itself. Only the true heir can wield it."

Liam approached the altar. The blade hummed as he drew near, vibrating with an energy that made his teeth ache.

"Touch it."

"What?"

"Touch it. If you're Alexander's son, it will recognize you."

Liam reached out. His fingers brushed the hilt.

Pain exploded through his arm.

Not the burning pain of transformation. Something deeper. Something that reached into his soul and pulled. Images flashed through his mind—a man with his face, standing on a battlefield, blood on his hands, a crown of silver on his head. Alexander. His father. Fighting. Dying. Screaming.

*The Council betrayed me.*

The vision shattered.

Liam stumbled back, gasping. The Moonblade glowed brighter than before, pulsing like a heartbeat.

Luna's face had gone pale. "It responded to you."

"What does that mean?"

"It means you're the heir." She swallowed. "But it also means the Council will feel it. Every elder, every alpha, every member of the Council's inner circle just sensed the Moonblade's awakening. They know you're here."

"Then we have—"

A crash echoed through the cave.

Luna spun, her body shifting, claws extending, eyes blazing silver. "We have less time than I thought."

Shadows moved at the entrance. Not wolves. Not Rogues.

Figures in black robes. Hooded. Masked. A dozen of them, standing in formation, their leader stepping forward with a calm that made Liam's blood freeze.

"Luna." The leader's voice was smooth. Familiar. "I was wondering when you'd bring him here."

Luna snarled. "Elder Marcus."

Marcus.

But not the Marcus who had died in his arms. This Marcus had silver eyes—cold, calculating, alive. The same face. The same voice. The same scars on his jaw.

"You're dead," Liam whispered.

"Am I?" The elder smiled. "Or did I simply let you believe what you needed to believe to lead you here?"

Luna moved in front of Liam, her body blocking him from view. "You son of a bitch. You faked your death."

"I faked nothing. I allowed you to *assume* I was dead. There's a difference." Marcus's smile widened. "The Council needed the heir to surface. The bloodline needed to awaken. And you needed to bring him to the one place where he could be... contained."

Liam's blood turned to ice. "You're working with the Council?"

"I *am* the Council." Marcus stepped forward, his robes parting to reveal a silver crescent moon embroidered over his heart. "I've been the Council's leader for the past fifteen years. Every move you've made, every escape, every 'ally' who found you—all orchestrated. All leading to this moment."

Luna's claws extended fully. "You won't touch him."

"I won't have to." Marcus raised his hand.

The other figures in black robes moved. Fast. Too fast.

Before Liam could react, chains wrapped around his arms, his legs, his chest. Silver chains. They burned where they touched, searing his skin, making the wolf inside him howl in agony.

"Luna!" he screamed.

She turned, but Marcus was already behind her. A blade pressed against her throat.

"You always were too predictable, Luna." Marcus's voice was soft, almost affectionate. "Alexander's weakness. And now, his son's."

Luna's eyes met Liam's. Fear. Rage. Something else.

*Run,* she mouthed.

But the chains tightened, and the silver poison seeped into his veins, and the last thing Liam saw before darkness claimed him was the Moonblade—still glowing, still pulsing, still waiting for a king who might never come.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Ward That Binds

Dawn broke gray and cold over the mountains.

Liam hadn't slept. Couldn't sleep. Every time he closed his eyes, he saw Marcus's face. Heard the wet sound of steel leaving flesh. Felt the spray of blood across his own skin.

He sat on the temple steps, watching the sun bleed through the clouds. Luna stood behind him, silent. She'd been silent for hours. Since she'd carried him through the forest. Since she'd laid him on a stone bed and pressed herbs into his wounds. Since she'd told him who she was.

His father's mate.

That meant she was his mother. Or close enough. And she'd let him believe he was alone for twenty-one years.

"Why now?" His voice cracked. He hadn't used it since yesterday.

Luna moved to sit beside him. Close enough that he caught her scent—moonlight and rain and something older. Something that smelled like the forest before humans.

"You weren't ready before."

"Ready?" He laughed. Bitter. Broken. "I was attacked by rogues. I watched a man die for me. I have a voice in my head that wants to take over my body. And I have seven days to become a king. When exactly was I supposed to be ready for any of this?"

She didn't flinch. Didn't look away. "The bloodline awakens at twenty-one. Not a day earlier. Not a day later. It's been written in our bones since the first Wolf King walked the earth."

"Convenient."

"It's not convenient. It's cruel." Her voice softened. "I've watched you from a distance your whole life. Every birthday. Every full moon. Every time you blacked out and woke up somewhere you didn't recognize."

Liam's stomach turned. "You knew about the blackouts?"

"I caused them."

He stood. Turned. Stared at her. The woman who'd saved him. The woman who'd fed him. The woman who'd let him suffer for years without explanation.

"What?"

"The wolf inside you—Alexander—he's been fighting to break free since you were a child. I suppressed him. Put a ward on your bloodline that kept him contained. If I hadn't, you would have shifted at thirteen and been hunted down before you learned to control it."

"Thirteen?" His voice rose. "I could have known about all of this at thirteen?"

"And done what?" She stood too. Her eyes flashed silver. "Fought the Shadow Fang alone? Run to the Council for protection? They would have killed you the moment they sensed Alexander's blood. The only reason you survived this long is because I kept you hidden."

"Hidden?" He paced. His hands shook. "You call that living? I had no family. No answers. Every full moon I woke up in some ditch with blood on my hands and no memory of how I got there. I thought I was going insane."

"You weren't ready."

"Stop saying that!"

His voice echoed across the mountain. Birds scattered from a nearby tree. The wind carried his shout into the valley below.

Luna didn't move. Didn't speak. She just watched him with those ancient eyes. Eyes that had seen centuries. Eyes that had watched his father die.

"I'm sorry." She said it like she meant it. Like she'd been holding it in for decades. "I'm sorry for every night you spent confused and afraid. I'm sorry for every lie I told. I'm sorry I couldn't protect your father. I'm sorry I couldn't protect you."

Liam stopped pacing. Stood still. Let the words sink in.

"But I'm not sorry I kept you alive. And if you want to hate me for that, hate me. I'll carry it. I've carried worse."

He looked at her. Really looked. Saw the lines around her eyes. The gray in her silver hair. The weight on her shoulders that she'd been carrying for thirty years.

"How long?" he asked. "How long have you been alone?"

"Since the night your father died. Since I felt his bloodline go silent. Since I knew the Shadow Fang had taken everything from me."

"Except me."

"Except you." She reached out. Her hand hovered near his cheek. "You're all I have left of him, Liam. You're all I have left of anything."

He didn't pull away. Didn't lean into it either. Just stood there. Caught between anger and grief and a desperate need to trust someone.

"The ward," he said. "The one that suppressed Alexander. Is it still there?"

Luna's hand dropped. Her expression shifted. Something dark passed through her eyes.

"No. It broke when you turned twenty-one. That's why the pain hit you so hard. The bloodline flooded in all at once. It was supposed to be gradual. A slow release over years of training."

"Supposed to be?"

"When I placed the ward, I bound it to a physical object. A charm. A piece of jewelry I left with you when you were a baby."

Liam's blood ran cold. "What charm?"

"A silver chain. With a pendant shaped like a crescent moon."

He remembered. He'd worn it as a child. A gift from the orphanage director. She'd said it was found with him. The only thing his parents left behind.

He'd lost it when he was twelve. Dropped it in the river during a school trip. Never found it again.

"I lost it," he whispered.

"No." Luna's voice dropped. Hard. Sharp. "It was taken."

"What?"

"The night you turned twelve, I felt the ward weaken. I came to check on you. Found the chain gone. Found a rogue's scent in your room. They'd been watching you for years, Liam. Waiting for the bloodline to ripen."

The bloodline ripen. Like a fruit waiting to be picked. Like prey waiting to be hunted.

"They have it?"

"The Shadow Fang has it. And if they know how to break it properly, they can use it to track you anywhere. Feel your emotions. Sense your location. Control the wolf inside you."

Liam's hand went to his chest. To the mark over his heart. It pulsed. Warm. Alive.

"That's why they found me at the warehouse. That's why they knew where I'd be."

Luna nodded. "The ward was a cage and a leash. I bound your bloodline to keep you safe, but I also bound it to myself. As long as I was near, I could feel you. Guide you. Protect you."

"And now?"

"Now it's gone. And the Shadow Fang has a piece of you they can use as a weapon."

The weight of it crushed down on him. Every choice. Every mistake. Every moment of his life had been shaped by forces he couldn't see. He was a puppet. And the strings were made of silver.

"Then we get it back."

Luna's eyes widened. "Liam—"

"We get it back. We find the Shadow Fang. We take what's mine. And then I learn to control this power before the blood moon."

"You don't understand. The Shadow Fang has been building their army for thirty years. They have warlocks. Spies. Warriors trained to kill our kind. You've been awake for one day. You can't even shift."

"Then teach me."

He turned to face her fully. His eyes—his human eyes—held hers.

"I have seven days. Teach me to shift. Teach me to fight. Teach me to be the king my father couldn't be."

Luna studied him. Long and hard. Like she was searching for something. Some sign that he was ready. Some proof that he would survive.

"There's a reason Alexander failed," she said. "He was strong. Fast. The most powerful alpha in a century. But he trusted the wrong people. He loved the wrong woman. And he paid for it with his blood."

"Then I won't make the same mistakes."

"You'll make your own. That's what heirs do." She stepped closer. Close enough that he could see the silver flecks in her irises. "But I'll tell you this, Liam. The wolf inside you—Alexander—he's not just a memory. He's a ghost. A spirit bound to the bloodline. He can guide you. He can teach you. But he can also consume you."

"Marcus said the same thing."

"Marcus was right." She reached out. Touched his chest. Felt the mark beneath his shirt. "If you let him take control for too long, you'll lose yourself. Become a vessel for the Wolf King's power without the Wolf King's soul. A puppet wearing your skin."

"Then I won't let him."

"It's not that simple."

"It never is."

A sound echoed across the mountain. Low. Deep. A howl that seemed to come from everywhere at once.

Luna's head snapped up. Her eyes blazed silver.

"They're close."

"Who?"

"The Shadow Fang. They tracked you here." She grabbed his arm. Pulled him toward the temple. "We have to move. Now."

"How did they find me?"

Luna stopped. Looked at him. And for the first time, he saw fear in her eyes.

"Because I'm still bound to the charm. And as long as I'm alive, they can feel me through it."

The temple doors slammed shut behind them. Darkness swallowed everything.

And somewhere in the forest below, a hundred wolves began to howl.

title: The Traitor's Scent
content:
The word hit Liam like a silver bullet to the chest.

*Mate.*

He lay in the stream, water numbing his wounds, staring up at the woman who claimed to be his father's mate. Luna. The name fit her—pale skin that seemed to drink in moonlight, silver hair that moved like water, eyes that held centuries of sorrow and steel.

"You're lying," he said.

She didn't flinch. "I knew you'd say that."

"Alexander died thirty years ago." Liam pushed himself upright, ignoring the scream of his muscles. "You look twenty-five."

"Werewolf mates age differently when bonded." She lowered her hand but didn't step back. "When Alexander died, part of me died too. I've been frozen ever since. Waiting."

"Waiting for what?"

"For you to wake up."

The stream gurgled between them. Somewhere in the trees, an owl called. Liam's enhanced hearing picked up a heartbeat—steady, calm, hers. Not a lie then. But not the whole truth either.

"If you're his mate," he said slowly, "why didn't you raise me? Why did Marcus take me?"

Something flickered in Luna's eyes. Pain. Guilt. Rage.

"Because I was the one who led them to your father."

Liam's blood went cold. The mark on his chest burned hotter.

"Explain."

"The Council captured me three days before the full moon. Tortured me for information about Alexander's bloodline." Her voice stayed flat, emotionless, like she'd rehearsed this confession a thousand times. "I held out for two days. Broke on the third. I told them about the safe house in Montreal. His schedule. His security details."

"You sold him out."

"Yes."

"And now you're here. Offering to help me."

"Yes."

Liam laughed. It came out bitter, broken, nothing like a king's laugh should sound.

"Why shouldn't I kill you right now?"

"Because I'm the only one who knows where the Moonlit Temple is." She met his gaze without flinching. "And because you need me."

"I need Marcus. And he's dead because of people like you."

"Marcus is dead because the Council has a traitor inside the resistance." Luna stepped closer, boots splashing in the stream. "Someone gave up his location. Someone told them you were coming to the apartment."

Liam's fists clenched. "Marcus said it was safe."

"Marcus was wrong. Or Marcus was betrayed." She tilted her head. "Think about it. How did they know exactly when to hit? How did they know the apartment's back entrance? The one that wasn't marked on any map?"

The words hit like cold water. Marcus had checked the perimeter. Twice. Said it was clean. Said they had ten minutes minimum.

They'd had thirty seconds.

"Who?" Liam asked.

"I don't know. But the Temple does. They have records going back four hundred years—every operative, every mole, every traitor who's ever worked for the Council." Luna's eyes glowed brighter. "If you want to know who killed Marcus, that's where you'll find the answer."

"And if I don't want to know?"

"Then you die. Maybe tonight. Maybe tomorrow. But definitely before the blood moon." She held out her hand again. "The Council knows you're awake. They know Alexander's bloodline activated. Every hunter in the city will be tracking you by sunrise."

As if on cue, howls echoed in the distance. Not wolves. Werewolves. Close. Getting closer.

Liam looked at her hand. Pale. Slender. The same hand that had betrayed his father.

The same hand that might be his only way out.

"One wrong move," he said, "and I'll tear your throat out."

"I'd expect nothing less from Alexander's son."

He took her hand. She pulled him up, stronger than she looked, and didn't let go.

"We need to move. Now."

They ran.

The forest blurred past—trees, rocks, streams, all melting into shadow and moonlight. Luna led, moving through the darkness like she'd been born in it, her silver hair streaming behind her like a banner.

Liam followed, pushing his body harder than he knew it could go. The wounds from the apartment were already closing, muscle knitting together, skin sealing over. The wolf inside him howled with approval.

*Fast,* Alexander said. *Good. But not fast enough.*

*Shut up.*

*She's lying, you know.*

*I know.*

*Then why are you following her?*

*Because she's the only lead I have.*

Alexander's laugh rumbled through his skull. *Or because she smells like him. Like your father. Like home.*

Liam stumbled, caught himself, kept running.

The old wolf was right. Luna's scent was familiar in a way that made his chest ache. Not just moonlight. Not just danger. Something deeper. Something that pulled at instincts he didn't know he had.

*Mate bond doesn't just affect the couple,* Alexander said. *It affects the pack. The bloodline. You're smelling her loyalty to your father. Her grief. Her guilt.*

*I don't care.*

*You should. It's making you blind.*

They broke through the tree line onto a gravel road. An old pickup truck sat waiting, rusted, license plates from three different states stacked in the bed.

Luna opened the driver's door. "Get in."

"Where are we going?"

"The Temple moves. It's currently located in the basement of an abandoned church thirty miles north."

"Currently?"

"The Council's been hunting it for decades. We change locations every three days." She started the engine, the truck coughing to life. "We're lucky. It's only two hours away."

The howls were closer now. Liam threw himself into the passenger seat as Luna floored the gas pedal, gravel spraying behind them.

The truck fishtailed onto the main road, engine screaming, headlights cutting through the darkness.

"Seatbelt," Luna said.

"What?"

"Seatbelt. I drive fast."

Liam clicked it on just as she took a sharp turn at sixty miles an hour, the truck's suspension groaning in protest.

"They'll track us by scent," he said.

"They'll track us by scent, sound, and the massive glowing target on your chest." Luna glanced at him. "The Temple has suppressants. Mufflers. Anti-tracking wards. We just have to get there first."

The howls grew louder. Closer. Liam looked in the side mirror and saw eyes—dozens of them, glowing red in the darkness, pouring out of the forest.

"Company," he said.

"I see them."

The wolves ran on two legs, then four, then two again, shifting mid-stride to gain speed, gain traction, gain purchase on the asphalt. They were faster than the truck. Faster than anything Liam had ever seen.

"They're alpha-class," Luna said. "Council's elite hunters. They won't stop until we're dead or captured."

"Great. Any good news?"

"The Temple knows we're coming. They'll have a welcome party."

"A welcoming party of what?"

"Hopefully friendlies."

The first wolf hit the truck's tailgate, claws screeching against metal. Liam turned in his seat, watched it scramble up, fangs bared, eyes fixed on him through the rear window.

"Hold on," Luna said.

She slammed the brakes.

The wolf flew over the cab, tumbled across the hood, and disappeared under the wheels. The truck bounced.

Then kept going.

"One down," she said.

"Twenty more to go."

Another wolf hit the passenger door. Claws punched through the metal, inches from Liam's leg. He kicked the door, felt the impact travel up his spine, heard the wolf snarl and fall away.

"They're herding us," Luna said. "Look."

The wolves weren't attacking directly anymore. They ran alongside the truck, flanking it, forcing it toward a side road.

"Don't take that turn," Liam said.

"I know."

She kept going straight. A wolf leaped onto the hood, smashed through the windshield, reached for her throat with claws like silver knives.

Liam moved without thinking.

He grabbed the wolf by the throat, felt bone crush under his fingers, threw it out the shattered windshield. It hit the road, rolled, didn't get up.

Luna stared at him. "You're stronger than I thought."

"I'm full of surprises."

The turn was coming up fast. The wolves pressed closer, forcing them toward it, the pack moving as one, a single organism with a single goal.

"We can't outrun them," Liam said.

"We don't have to outrun them." Luna reached under her seat, pulled out a canvas bag, tossed it in his lap. "We just have to outlast them."

He opened the bag. Silver bullets. Lots of them. And a revolver that looked older than he was.

"I don't know how to use this."

"Learn fast."

She took the turn.

Not the one the wolves wanted.

The opposite one.

The truck slammed into something—a barrier, a tree, Liam didn't see—and spun, tires screaming, metal folding, the world becoming a blur of glass and pain and moonlight.

Then everything stopped.

Liam hung upside down, held in place by his seatbelt. Blood dripped from a cut on his forehead. The engine ticked. Somewhere, a wolf howled.

"Luna?"

No answer.

He turned his head. She was still in her seat, unconscious, blood running down her face.

The howls grew closer.

"Luna, wake up."

Nothing.

Liam unbuckled his belt, dropped to the roof of the cab, crawled out through the broken windshield. The truck had flipped into a ditch, headlights still on, illuminating the treeline.

Eyes watched him from the darkness.

Dozens of them.

One stepped forward. A man—tall, broad, scarred, with eyes that burned like embers. He wore a Council uniform, black and silver, a wolf's head insignia on his chest.

"Liam Blackwood," he said. "By order of the Lunar Council, you are charged with unlawful awakening, harboring rogue bloodlines, and resisting arrest."

"Never heard of it."

"You're not under arrest." The man smiled. "You're under sentence."

The wolves moved in.

Liam reached into the canvas bag, pulled out the revolver, aimed it at the man's chest.

"I don't want to kill anyone."

"Good. That'll make it easier."

The Council hunter shifted mid-stride, clothes tearing, bones cracking, fur ripping through skin. He landed on four paws, twice the size of the other wolves, his eyes still burning with human intelligence.

An alpha.

He charged.

Liam pulled the trigger.

Click.

Empty.

The alpha leaped, jaws open, fangs aimed at his throat.

And a wall of silver fire erupted between them.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Temple's Secret

I stared at Luna's hand for what felt like hours. The stream gurgled beneath me, cold water numbing the fresh wounds across my body. The mark on my chest still pulsed with residual heat, Alexander's presence lingering at the edges of my consciousness.

"Your father's mate," I repeated. The words tasted strange. Bitter. "That would make you my mother."

"No." She knelt beside me, her silver hair brushing the water's surface. "I was Alexander's mate, yes. But I'm not your mother. The bloodline doesn't work that way."

"Then what are you to me?"

"Your teacher." Her hand remained extended. "If you want to survive the next six days."

I took her hand. Her grip was iron. She pulled me upright with a strength that didn't match her slender frame. I swayed, blood rushing to my head, and she caught me before I collapsed back into the stream.

"We need to move," she said. "The rogues will have tracked your scent by now. Marcus bought you time, but not much."

"Marcus." The name hit like a punch to the chest. "He's dead."

"Yes." No emotion in her voice. Flat. Professional. "He knew the cost when he chose to free you."

"You don't sound upset."

"I've been alive for two hundred years, Liam. I've buried more friends than you've met people." She turned and began walking into the trees. "Grief is a luxury I can't afford right now."

I followed, limping, every muscle screaming. The forest pressed in around us, dark and alive with sounds I couldn't identify. Crickets. Wind. Something larger moving in the shadows.

"How did you find me?"

"Your father's blood calls to mine." She didn't look back. "The mate bond never truly fades. When you awakened, I felt it. Like a bell ringing in my chest."

"A bell."

"Metaphor." She pushed aside a branch, revealing a narrow path I hadn't noticed. "You'll need to learn to sense the bonds between wolves. It's one of the first lessons."

"I haven't even learned to shift yet."

"You will. Tonight."

I stopped. "Tonight? I can't even—"

"You can't afford not to." She turned, and her eyes caught the moonlight. They weren't just glowing anymore. They were molten silver, swirling with something ancient and terrible. "The rogues won't wait for you to be ready. The Council won't wait. The Blood Moon won't wait. You have six days to master what took your father thirty years."

"Then I'm going to fail."

"Probably." She resumed walking. "But I'd rather you fail trying than fail hiding."

We walked for another hour. The path wound through the forest, up hills, across streams that seemed to appear out of nowhere. I lost track of direction. North, south, it all blurred together. My body moved on autopilot, pain fading to a dull background hum.

Then the trees opened.

The temple stood in a clearing surrounded by standing stones. Each stone was twice my height, carved with symbols that seemed to shift when I looked at them directly. The temple itself was older than anything I'd ever seen. Stone blocks fitted without mortar, covered in moss and vines. A doorway arched in the center, dark and inviting.

"This is it," Luna said. "The Moonlit Temple. Built by the first Wolf King three thousand years ago."

"Three thousand years?"

"Werewolves have been here longer than humans remember. Longer than history records." She walked toward the entrance. "The temple is a place of power. A place where the veil between worlds is thin."

"Veil between worlds?"

She stopped at the threshold. "There's something you need to understand about your bloodline, Liam. Something Marcus didn't tell you. Something I'm not sure he knew."

I waited.

"The Wolf King isn't just a title. It's a connection. A link to the original wolf spirit that first merged with humanity. The first werewolf." She turned to face me. "That spirit is still alive. Trapped in a realm between worlds. And every Wolf King carries a piece of it in their blood."

"Alexander's voice. Inside my head."

"Yes." She nodded. "But it's not Alexander. Not really. It's the spirit using Alexander's voice because that's the only way you can understand it."

Cold spread through my chest. "Then who was I talking to?"

"The wolf. The original. The one who wants to break free through your bloodline." She stepped closer. "The seven-day countdown isn't about you mastering the shift. It's about the Blood Moon weakening the barriers enough for the wolf spirit to claim your body permanently."

"That's not what Alexander said."

"Alexander was a vessel, just like you. He fought the spirit for thirty years. In the end, it killed him." Her voice dropped. "It killed him because he refused to give in. Because he chose death over becoming the wolf's puppet."

I stumbled back. "Then what's the point of training? Of any of this? If I'm just going to die or become a slave—"

"There's a third option." She reached into her jacket and pulled out a small leather pouch. "The Wolf King bloodline is the strongest because it contains the original wolf spirit. But original doesn't mean pure. It means corrupted."

"Corrupted how?"

"The spirit was bound for a reason. Three thousand years ago, the first Wolf King trapped it because it was too dangerous to be free. It had gone mad. Consumed by hunger and rage." She opened the pouch and tipped something into her palm. A silver ring. Ancient. Worn. "This ring was forged by the first King to control the spirit. To keep it contained."

"You have the ring?"

"I have one of them." She held it up. "There were seven. Each one bound a different aspect of the spirit. Strength. Speed. Senses. Healing. Endurance. Instinct. And the seventh—" She paused. "The seventh bound the spirit's will."

"Where are the others?"

"Lost. Scattered across the world. Hidden by Wolf Kings who feared what would happen if they were gathered together."

"Then how do I—"

"You don't." She closed her hand around the ring. "You don't gather them. You don't seek them. The ring I have will help you control the spirit long enough to survive the Blood Moon. But it's not a cure. It's a bandage."

I stared at the silver glinting between her fingers. "Why do you have it?"

"Because Alexander gave it to me." Her jaw tightened. "The night before he died. He knew what was coming. He knew he couldn't stop it. But he hoped I could use it to help the next Wolf King."

"That's why you found me."

"That's why I found you." She held out the ring. "Take it."

I reached for it. My fingers brushed the silver—

Fire exploded through my hand.

I screamed. Dropped to my knees. The ring clattered to the ground, and Luna snatched it up before I could touch it again.

"What the hell?" I gasped, clutching my hand. The skin where I'd touched the ring was red, blistering, like I'd grabbed a hot stove.

"Silver," Luna said. "It burns our kind. The same metal that can kill us."

"Then how am I supposed to wear it?"

"You're not." She tucked the ring back into the pouch. "Not yet. First, you need to build your tolerance. Learn to control your body's reaction to silver. Otherwise, the ring will kill you before it helps you."

"How do I build tolerance?"

She smiled. It was not a comforting smile.

"The same way you build anything." She turned and walked into the temple. "Pain."

I followed her into the darkness. The tunnel stretched deeper than I expected, sloping downward into the earth. Torches lined the walls, burning with blue flames that cast no shadows.

"She's lying to you."

Alexander's voice. Or the spirit using his voice. I couldn't tell anymore.

"Shut up."

"She wants the rings for herself. She was my mate. I know her better than anyone."

"I said shut up."

"She'll use you to find the other rings. Then she'll take them all and become the Wolf Queen."

The tunnel opened into a cavern. Massive. Cathedral-like. Stalactites hung from the ceiling like frozen waterfalls, and in the center of the room stood a stone altar covered in ancient carvings.

"This is where the first King bound the spirit," Luna said. "The ritual chamber."

I stepped forward, and the mark on my chest began to burn. Brighter than before. Hotter. The symbol over my heart glowed through my shirt, and the carvings on the altar began to pulse in response.

"What's happening?"

"The temple recognizes you." Luna's eyes were fixed on the altar. "It knows the bloodline. It's calling to the spirit inside you."

The voice in my head roared.

*LET ME OUT.*

I grabbed my chest, doubled over. The pain was worse than the silver. Worse than the transformation. It felt like something was clawing its way out of my ribcage.

"Fight it," Luna said. "You have to fight it."

"I can't—"

*YOU CAN'T STOP ME. I'VE BEEN WAITING THREE THOUSAND YEARS.*

My vision went white. The world dissolved into noise and light and pain.

When I opened my eyes again, I was standing at the altar. My hands were on the stone. The carvings were glowing bright enough to hurt.

And Luna was on her knees behind me, blood streaming from her nose, a knife in her hand.

"What did I do?" I whispered.

She looked up at me. Her silver eyes were wide. Afraid.

"You called the spirit," she said. "You called it, and it answered."

Behind her, the shadows in the cavern began to move. To gather. To take shape.

A figure emerged from the darkness.

Tall. Broad-shouldered. Black fur covering its body, eyes like molten gold, teeth bared in a grin that held no humanity.

The wolf.

Not a wolf. *The* wolf. The original. The one trapped for millennia.

It looked at me and spoke in a voice that cracked the stone walls.

"Hello, son."

Luna screamed.

The wolf lunged.

And everything went black.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Taste of Silver

The temple walls breathed.

Liam pressed his palm against the stone, felt it pulse beneath his fingers like a living heart. Faint blue light traced veins through the rock, illuminating symbols he couldn't read but somehow understood. *Blood.* *Bone.* *Moon.* *Sacrifice.*

"Don't touch that."

He pulled his hand back. Luna stood in the doorway, her silver hair pulled back, her face harder than he'd seen it. Three days since she'd found him in that stream. Three days of running, hiding, learning. Three days until the blood moon.

"What is this place?" he asked.

"A sanctuary." She stepped inside, her bare feet silent on the cold floor. "Built by the first Wolf King. Before the clans fractured. Before the Council existed."

Liam looked around the circular chamber. Pillars rose toward a ceiling he couldn't see, disappearing into darkness. The blue light pulsed in rhythm with his heartbeat. With the wolf inside him.

*She's hiding something.*

Alexander's voice had grown stronger. Sharper. Less like a whisper and more like a second set of thoughts running parallel to his own.

*She brought you here for a reason.*

"I know," Liam muttered.

"Know what?" Luna asked.

"Nothing." He moved away from the wall, flexed his fingers. They'd healed faster today. The cuts from last night's training session had closed within an hour. His senses sharpened hourly—he could hear Luna's heartbeat from across the room, smell the river on her skin, taste the copper of old blood in the air.

"You're progressing faster than expected," she said.

"Alexander helps."

Her expression flickered. Pain? Regret? It vanished before he could read it. "Does he speak to you often?"

"All the time." Liam walked to the center of the chamber, where a circular depression held stagnant water. His reflection stared back at him—but the eyes weren't his. They glowed gold. "He tells me about the old wars. About the Council's betrayal. About you."

Luna went still.

"He says you were his mate. That you fought together during the Great Schism. That you were the one who—" He stopped. The words caught in his throat.

"Who killed him?"

Liam nodded.

Luna walked to the pool's edge. Her reflection joined his in the water. Where his eyes glowed gold, hers were silver. Two moons in a dark sky.

"It's true," she said. "I ended Alexander's life. And I would do it again."

"Why?"

"Because he was losing himself. The Wolf King's power—it doesn't just change your body. It changes your soul. Alexander became something else. Something that would have destroyed everything we fought for." She met his gaze. "The same thing that's happening to you right now."

*She lies.*

Alexander's voice thundered through his skull. Liam winced, pressed his palms to his temples.

*She was jealous. Power-hungry. The Council promised her control over the northern territories if she eliminated me. She took their offer.*

"Stop," Liam gasped.

"Liam?" Luna reached for him.

"Don't touch me." He stumbled back. His vision blurred, split, reformed. Two Lunas stood before him. One with silver hair and sad eyes. One with fangs and blood on her hands.

*Seven days,* Alexander whispered. *That's how long I lasted after her betrayal. Seven days of agony while she watched. Seven days of—*

"I said STOP."

The word exploded from his chest. Blue light erupted from the symbols on the walls, shot across the chamber, struck the pillars. The ground shook. Cracks spiderwebbed across the stone floor.

When the light faded, Luna was on her knees.

Her hands covered her face. Blood dripped between her fingers.

"Luna—"

"Stay back." Her voice was broken. Raw. "You have more control than I thought."

"I didn't mean to—"

"Yes, you did." She lowered her hands. Blood ran from her nose, her eyes, her ears. "You wanted to hurt me. Alexander wanted to hurt me. You're becoming him faster than I anticipated."

Liam looked at his hands. They were normal again. Pale skin. Short nails. No claws. But he could feel the wolf just beneath the surface, pacing, waiting, *hungry.*

"How do I stop it?" he asked.

"You don't." Luna stood, wiping blood from her face. "You learn to share. The Wolf King's power isn't something you control. It's something you negotiate with."

"Negotiate?"

"Every time you shift, you give him ground. Every time you use his strength, he takes something from you. By the time the blood moon rises, you'll either be a partnership or a parasite."

*She speaks truth.*

Alexander's voice was quieter now. Almost tired.

*But she doesn't tell you the cost of partnership.*

"What cost?" Liam asked aloud.

Luna's eyes widened. "You can hear him right now?"

"Clear as I hear you."

"Impossible. The bond shouldn't be that strong until after the ceremony."

*She doesn't know about the mark.*

Liam looked down at his chest. The crescent moon and wolf's head glowed faintly beneath his shirt. He'd kept it hidden. Even from her.

"What aren't you telling me?" Luna demanded.

"What aren't *you* telling me?" he shot back.

Silence stretched between them. The blue light pulsed. Somewhere in the darkness above, stone scraped against stone.

"I loved him," Luna said finally. "More than I've loved anything. But Alexander became a monster. The power twisted him. Made him paranoid, violent, dangerous. The Council didn't have to convince me to kill him—they just had to show me what he'd become."

"And you think I'll become the same."

"I know you will. It's not a question of if, Liam. It's a question of when."

*She's wrong.*

"Then why save me?" he asked. "Why bring me here? Why train me at all if you think I'm doomed?"

Luna walked to the pool. The water had stilled. Her reflection stared up at her—younger, softer, a woman who hadn't yet killed her king.

"Because I saw something in your eyes that I didn't see in his. Doubt. You're afraid of the power. Alexander embraced it. That difference might save you."

"Or get me killed."

"Or get you killed." She turned to face him. "But we don't have time for philosophy. The Council knows you're alive. They've tracked you here somehow. We have three days until the blood moon, and you still can't shift on command."

"I shifted last night."

"Partially. You got claws and fangs, then passed out for six hours." She crossed her arms. "You need to shift fully. Hold it. Fight in it. Before the ceremony."

"Then teach me."

"I can't teach you. I can only push you." She pointed toward a passage on the far side of the chamber. "Through there. There's something you need to see."

Liam hesitated. Every instinct screamed at him to run. Alexander howled warnings in his skull. But Luna's eyes held something he hadn't seen before—fear. Not of him. For him.

He walked toward the passage.

The tunnel narrowed quickly. The walls pressed in. The blue light dimmed until he was walking in complete darkness. His enhanced senses reached out, touched nothing but stone and silence and the faint scent of—

*Silver.*

He stopped.

The passage opened into a chamber. Smaller than the first. Circular. Lit by a single beam of moonlight that somehow pierced the stone ceiling above.

In the center of the room stood a pedestal.

On the pedestal rested a sword.

It was ancient. The blade was dark, almost black, etched with symbols that matched the ones on his chest. The hilt was wrapped in leather so old it had fossilized. The guard was shaped like crescent moons, their points curving inward.

"Touch it," Luna said from behind him.

"That's silver."

"Pure silver. Forged in moonlight. Quenched in the blood of the first Wolf King."

Liam's skin crawled. Every cell in his body screamed at him to flee. Silver was poison to werewolves. A single touch could burn through flesh. A wound from a silver blade could kill even an elder.

"Why would I touch that?"

"Because it's the only thing that can kill a Wolf King." Luna stepped past him, approached the pedestal. "And because if you can survive holding it, you can survive the ceremony."

*She tests you.*

Alexander's voice was sharp. Alert.

*The sword is mine. I forged it. I bled into it. It will recognize my bloodline.*

"How do you know I can survive?" Liam asked.

"I don't." Luna picked up the sword. It didn't burn her hands. "I survived because Alexander never wanted me dead. But you—" She held it out to him. "You need to prove you're not him."

The blade gleamed in the moonlight.

Liam reached out.

His fingers touched the hilt.

Pain exploded through his arm. White-hot. Agonizing. He felt his skin blister, his flesh sizzle, his bones scream. But he didn't let go.

*Hold.*

Alexander's voice was calm now. Almost peaceful.

*The pain is the price. The power is the reward.*

Liam gritted his teeth. Blood dripped from his palm, ran down the blade, sizzled where it touched the silver. The symbols on the sword began to glow. Red. Then gold. Then blue.

The pain stopped.

The sword hummed in his hand. Alive. Aware. *His.*

"Impossible," Luna whispered.

Liam looked at his palm. The burns were healing. But something else was happening—the mark on his chest was spreading. Tendrils of light crawled across his skin, up his neck, down his arm, wrapping around the sword's hilt.

"What's happening to me?" he asked.

Luna's face had gone pale. "The sword is bonding with you. That shouldn't be possible. Not until the ceremony."

"Why not?"

"Because the sword contains Alexander's soul. His consciousness. His *will.*" She backed away. "If it's bonding with you now, that means—"

*She finally understands.*

Alexander's laugh echoed through Liam's skull. Loud. Triumphant. *Hungry.*

*That means I never left the sword.*

*I've been waiting in the blade.*

*Waiting for you.*

Liam tried to drop the sword. His fingers wouldn't obey. The light from the mark consumed his arm, his chest, his face.

*The blood moon rises in three days,* Alexander said. *But I don't need the ceremony. I just need an heir foolish enough to pick up my sword.*

Liam's vision went white.

The last thing he heard was Luna screaming his name.

Then the wolf inside him opened its eyes.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Taste of Truth

The temple was burning.

Liam stood on the hilltop, watching orange flames devour the ancient stone structure where he'd spent the last five days learning to control the Wolf King's power. Smoke rose in thick columns, blotting out the stars. The symbol over his heart pulsed hot, then cold, like a warning heartbeat.

"No," Luna whispered beside him. Her silver hair caught the firelight, turning it to molten gold. "The sacred texts. The transformation rituals. Everything we needed for the ceremony—"

"It's a message," Liam cut in. His voice came out harder than he intended. Older. "They know we're here."

The past five days had been a blur of pain and progress. Luna had taught him to find the wolf without losing himself—to let the beast surface in controlled bursts. He'd learned to sharpen his hearing until he could count heartbeats from a mile away. To strengthen his bones until they could withstand a direct hit from a rogue's claws.

But he still couldn't fully shift.

Every time he tried, Alexander's voice would rise like a tidal wave, threatening to drown his consciousness. *You're not ready,* the ancient Wolf King would growl. *You fight me instead of working with me.*

And now this.

"I count eight," Liam said, his enhanced vision picking out dark shapes moving through the burning temple's courtyard. "Maybe more inside."

"Rogues," Luna confirmed. Her hand found his, squeezed once. "But not just any rogues. Look at their movements—disciplined, coordinated. These are the Shadow Fang's elite."

The name sent ice through his veins. Marcus had mentioned them before he died. The Shadow Fang was the most brutal rogue faction in existence—werwolves who'd rejected the Council's authority and embraced their primal nature completely. They didn't just want power. They wanted chaos.

"Why now?" Liam asked. "The ceremony isn't for two more days."

"Because they know you're not ready." Luna's eyes glowed brighter. "Without the Blood Moon ritual, you can't fully access the Wolf King's power. You're vulnerable. And vulnerable Wolf Kings don't live long."

Something moved in the shadows between them and the temple. Fast. Too fast for a human. Liam dropped into a fighting stance, his claws extending before he consciously willed them.

"Show yourself," he called out.

A figure stepped into the firelight. Tall. Lean. Wearing a mask of black bone that covered everything except his eyes—eyes that burned like embers in a dying forge.

"Impressive," the figure said, his voice distorted by the mask. "Your father had the same instinct. Always knew when danger was coming."

"Who are you?"

"I'm the one who killed him."

Liam's vision went red. His claws lengthened. His bones cracked as the wolf surged forward, demanding release. But Luna's voice cut through the rage.

"He's baiting you. Don't—"

Too late.

Liam launched himself forward, closing the distance in three heartbeats. His claws raked through the mask-wearer's chest, shredding fabric and flesh. But the rogue didn't fall. Didn't even stagger. He just smiled—Liam could feel it behind the mask.

"Good," the rogue said. "But not good enough."

He moved. Faster than anything Liam had ever seen. A blur of motion, and suddenly Liam was on his back, gasping, a boot pressing down on his chest—directly over the Wolf King's mark.

"See?" the rogue said, leaning down. "The bloodline makes you strong. But strength without control is just another weapon for your enemies to use."

"Get off him." Luna's voice. Cold as winter steel.

The rogue looked up. Luna stood ten feet away, her body wreathed in moonlight, her eyes fully silver now. Blue energy crackled around her fingertips.

"Ah, the Moon Priestess," the rogue said, almost fondly. "I wondered when you'd show your true colors. Did you think we didn't know? That we couldn't smell the lunar magic on you?"

"Step away from him, Viktor."

So the mask had a name. Viktor laughed, a dry, rasping sound. "Or what? You'll bind me to the moon? We both know you can't perform high-level lunar magic without the temple's artifacts. And those"—he gestured at the burning building—"are currently ash."

Luna's hands trembled. The blue energy flickered. Died.

"That's what I thought." Viktor removed his boot from Liam's chest, but didn't step back. Instead, he crouched down, bringing his masked face inches from Liam's. "Listen carefully, boy. I could kill you right now. End the Wolf King bloodline forever. But that would be merciful. And I don't do mercy."

"What do you want?" Liam managed through gritted teeth.

"I want you to complete the ceremony. I want you to become the Wolf King. I want you to challenge the Council, tear down their ancient laws, and start the war that's been brewing for centuries."

"You're insane."

"Probably." Viktor stood. "But I'm also patient. The Shadow Fang will be watching, Wolf King. Complete the ritual. Survive the Blood Moon. And when you come for the Council—we'll be waiting."

He turned and walked toward the burning temple. The other rogues fell in behind him like shadows returning to their master.

Liam struggled to his feet, his chest aching where Viktor's boot had pressed. "Why would you help me? You killed my father!"

Viktor paused. Didn't turn around.

"Alexander was weak," he said. "He had the power to change everything. To free our kind from the Council's tyranny. But he fell in love. He chose a human woman over his duty. He chose peace over revolution."

"Love isn't weakness."

"Tell that to your dead father."

The words hit like a physical blow. Liam's claws extended again. The wolf howled inside him, demanding vengeance.

But Luna's hand found his shoulder. Gentle. Steady.

"He's gone," she said. "And we have more immediate problems."

She pointed at the temple. The flames had spread to the surrounding forest. A wall of fire was moving toward them, cutting off their escape routes.

"The underground tunnels," Liam said, remembering Luna's lessons about the temple's layout. "There's an exit that leads to the eastern ridge."

"The tunnels are filled with smoke. We'd suffocate before we reached the exit."

"Then we go through the fire."

Luna stared at him. "Through it?"

"The wolf can survive it. I can survive it." He looked at her. "Trust me."

She hesitated. Then nodded.

They ran.

The heat hit them first—a wall of pressure that stole the breath from Liam's lungs. Then the flames. Orange and red and hungry, reaching for them like living things.

Liam's blood responded. The Wolf King's mark flared to life, covering his body in a shimmering barrier of silver light. The fire parted around him, unable to touch his skin.

But Luna had no such protection.

She gasped as the flames licked at her arms, her legs. Her silver hair began to singe at the edges. She stumbled, and Liam caught her, pulling her close.

"Hold on to me," he said.

"I can't—the fire—"

"Just hold on."

He wrapped his arms around her and ran.

The barrier of light expanded, covering Luna as well. The fire screamed in protest, but it couldn't reach them. Couldn't touch them.

Thirty seconds. That was all it took. But it felt like hours.

They burst through the other side of the fire wall, tumbling down a slope and landing in a cold stream. The water shocked Liam's system, extinguishing the last embers clinging to his clothes.

Luna lay beside him, coughing, her skin red and blistered in places. But alive.

"Your barrier," she whispered, staring at him with wide eyes. "That's not supposed to be possible. Not until after the ceremony."

"I don't know how I did it. I just... needed to protect you."

Something flickered in her eyes. Recognition? Fear? Hope?

"You're accessing his power," she said. "Alexander's power. The Wolf King's power. Without the ritual, without the moon's blessing."

"Is that bad?"

"It's impossible." She sat up, wincing at her burned skin. "Unless..."

"Unless what?"

Luna looked at him, and for the first time since they'd met, she looked afraid.

"Unless you're not just the Wolf King's heir," she said slowly. "Unless you're something else. Something the ancient texts only whispered about."

"What are you talking about?"

"The prophecy. The one the Council buried centuries ago." She swallowed. "It said there would come a Wolf King who could access the bloodline's full power before the Blood Moon ceremony. A king who didn't need the moon's blessing because he carried the moon inside him."

"That's impossible," Liam said, echoing her earlier words.

"Is it? Your father was the Wolf King. Your mother was human. But what if she was something more? What if she was—"

A howl cut through the night. Close. Too close.

Liam's ears pricked. He counted six heartbeats. Moving fast. Surrounding them.

"More rogues," he said.

Luna struggled to her feet. "We need to keep moving. There's a safe house two miles east. We can rest there, regroup."

"And then what?"

She met his eyes. The silver in them had intensified, swirling like a storm.

"And then we find out what you really are."

The howls grew closer. Liam pulled Luna to her feet, and they disappeared into the darkness.

Behind them, the temple continued to burn. And somewhere in the smoke and shadows, Viktor watched them go.

"Run, little king," he whispered. "Run fast. Because when the Blood Moon rises, you'll need to be ready for what's coming."

He touched his mask where Liam's claws had torn through it.

"And what's coming is going to change everything."

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Taste of Betrayal

Luna's words hung in the frozen air between them. *Your father's mate.*

The stream gurgled against Liam's back. Water soaked through his torn clothes, numbing his wounds. He should feel something—shock, anger, betrayal. Instead, he felt nothing. Just a hollow space where emotions used to live.

"You're lying."

"I'm not." She crouched beside him, and the moonlight caught her face. Same silver hair. Same luminous eyes. But now he saw what he'd missed before—the ancient sadness carved into her features. The weight of decades behind every glance.

"My father died thirty years ago. You look twenty-five."

"Werewolf aging." She extended her hand again. "We stop aging after our first shift. I was twenty-three when Alexander marked me. I've been twenty-three for three decades."

Liam pushed himself upright. His ribs screamed. His left arm wouldn't stop shaking. He ignored all of it and stared at her hand like it was a snake about to strike.

"You knew. The whole time. In the alley, you knew who I was."

"Yes."

"And you didn't tell me."

"Would you have believed me?"

"No." He stood, swaying, grabbing a tree branch for support. "But you could have tried. Marcus died because I didn't know what I was. Because I was blind and weak and—"

"Marcus died because he chose to." Luna's voice sharpened. "He knew the risks. He knew what it meant to protect the Wolf King's heir. He made his choice, Liam. Don't dishonor it by pretending you could have saved him."

Liam's fist connected with the tree trunk. Bark shredded. Blood welled between his knuckles. The pain was good. Clean. It pushed back the chaos in his head.

"Why now? Why show up now?"

"Because the blood moon is in six days." Luna stood, brushing dirt from her knees. "Because you've awakened. Because the Council knows you exist, and they've already sent their best hunters to find you. And because"—she paused, something flickering in her eyes—"because I made a promise to Alexander. To keep you alive. To make sure you reached the ceremony."

"What ceremony?"

"The Ascension. The blood moon ceremony where the heir claims the Wolf King's power completely." Her voice dropped. "Or loses his soul to the beast forever."

Liam's hand went to his chest. The mark still pulsed, warm against his palm. *Seven days,* Alexander had said. *If you don't master me by then, I will master you.*

"Marcus said something similar. Before he died."

"Marcus knew more than most. He was your father's beta. His closest friend." Luna's eyes softened. "He watched you from a distance your whole life. Made sure the Council never found you. Gave up his pack, his status, everything to keep you hidden."

The information hit like a physical blow. Liam's legs gave out. He slumped against the tree, sliding down until he sat in the mud.

Marcus had known. For twenty-one years, Marcus had known. The camping trips. The full moon "accidents." The way he'd always been there, just outside the frame of Liam's life.

"He never told me."

"He couldn't. The bloodline protects itself. If he'd told you before your awakening, the knowledge would have driven you mad. The Wolf King's power needs a vessel ready to contain it."

"Ready?" Liam laughed. Bitter. Broken. "I can't even shift. I almost drowned in a stream trying to stop my own bones from rearranging."

"Then you have a lot to learn in very little time." Luna reached into her jacket and pulled out a silver chain. A pendant hung from it—a crescent moon wrapped around a wolf's head. Identical to the mark on his chest.

"This was Alexander's." She held it out. "He gave it to me the night before he died. It contains the location of the Moonlit Temple. A sanctuary hidden from the Council. There's someone there who can train you."

Liam stared at the pendant. It glowed faintly, pulsing in rhythm with his heartbeat.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't." Luna's eyes met his. "Trust is earned. And I haven't earned yours. But the Council has already found your scent. They'll be here within the hour. You can come with me, or you can die alone in these woods. Your choice."

The silence stretched. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled. Another answered. Closer.

"Fine." Liam grabbed the pendant. The moment his fingers touched the silver, a surge of energy shot through his arm. Images flashed—stone walls, candlelight, a woman with ancient eyes. A mountain hidden behind a waterfall.

The Moonlit Temple.

He shoved the pendant into his pocket. "Let's go."

---

They ran.

Luna moved like water—silent, fluid, her feet barely touching the ground. Liam stumbled behind her, crashing through branches, gasping for air. His body screamed. Every cut, every bruise, every cracked rib demanded he stop.

He didn't.

The forest blurred past. Trees. Rocks. A stream he splashed through without slowing. Behind them, howls grew louder. Closer.

"How many?" he gasped.

"Seven. Eight. Hard to tell." Luna didn't look back. "They're tracking your blood. The scent is fresh."

Liam looked at his hands. Still bleeding. He'd left a trail a blind hunter could follow.

"I need to stop the bleeding."

"No time. We reach the river, the water will mask your scent."

They broke through a wall of bushes and hit the riverbank. The water churned, dark and fast, at least fifty meters wide. Too far to swim.

"Now what?"

Luna grabbed his arm. Her grip was iron. "Hold your breath."

"Wait—"

She jumped, dragging him with her.

The cold hit like a punch. Liam's lungs seized. Water flooded his nose, his mouth, his ears. He thrashed, but Luna held him tight, pulling him deeper, past rocks and roots, into the darkness beneath the surface.

The current grabbed them. They tumbled. Liam lost all sense of direction—up, down, sideways, everything spinning. His lungs burned. Spots danced in his vision.

Just as he thought he'd drown, Luna pulled him upward.

He broke the surface gasping, coughing, choking. They were in a cave. Dim light filtered through cracks in the stone ceiling. Water dripped from stalactites. The river flowed past, disappearing into darkness ahead.

"We made it." Luna dragged herself onto a rocky ledge, pulling him after her. "This tunnel leads to the temple. Three hours on foot."

Liam lay on his back, staring at the stone ceiling, trying to remember how to breathe.

"Three hours. While eight hunters track us."

"They won't find the entrance. It's hidden."

"You keep saying that. Hidden. Safe. Secret." He turned his head to look at her. "Every time someone says that, someone dies."

Luna's expression didn't change. But something flickered in her eyes. Guilt? Regret?

"Get up. We keep moving."

---

They walked in silence.

The tunnel twisted and turned, sometimes narrow enough to squeeze through, sometimes opening into vast caverns lit by glowing moss. Liam's clothes dried stiff with blood. His body settled into a rhythm of pain—dull, constant, almost comforting.

"You loved him."

It wasn't a question. Luna's step faltered.

"Yes."

"What happened? The night he died."

She was quiet for so long Liam thought she wouldn't answer. Then: "The Council happened. Victor happened."

"Victor?"

"Victor Blackwood. The Council's leader. The one who wants you dead." Her voice hardened. "He was Alexander's brother."

Liam stopped. "Uncle. My uncle wants me dead."

"Victor doesn't see it that way. He sees you as a threat. A rival bloodline that could challenge his control of the Council." Luna turned to face him, her eyes glowing in the dim light. "Alexander was the true Wolf King. Victor is a pretender. He seized power after your father's death and has been hunting the bloodline ever since."

"Why didn't he kill me when I was a child?"

"Because he didn't know you existed. Alexander hid your mother's pregnancy. Only Marcus and I knew. And Marcus was very good at keeping secrets."

"Until now."

"Until now." Luna resumed walking. "Victor has informants everywhere. Someone betrayed Marcus. Someone told the Council where to find you."

The words sank in. Someone had sold them out. Someone Marcus trusted.

"Who?"

"I don't know. But I intend to find out."

They emerged into a massive cavern. Water fell from a crack in the ceiling a hundred meters above, creating a perpetual waterfall that crashed into a crystal-clear pool. Behind the waterfall, carved into the rock, stood a temple.

Stone columns. Intricate carvings of wolves and moons. Torches that burned with silver flames.

The Moonlit Temple.

Liam stared. It was beautiful. Ancient. Powerful.

"It was built a thousand years ago," Luna said. "By the first Wolf King. It's been hidden ever since."

"Who's inside?"

"Someone who's been waiting for you even longer than I have."

She led him toward a side entrance, through a narrow corridor, into a circular chamber. Candles lined the walls. In the center, an old woman sat cross-legged on a stone platform. Her hair was white. Her face weathered by centuries. Her eyes—when she opened them—were silver.

"Liam." Her voice was ancient. Wise. Heavy with time. "I've been waiting."

"Who are you?"

"I am the Temple Keeper. I have trained every Wolf King for the past three hundred years." She smiled, and her teeth were sharp. "And you have much to learn in very little time."

Liam stepped forward. Something felt wrong. The air was too still. The candles didn't flicker.

Behind him, Luna moved.

The blade pressed against his throat before he could react. Cold. Silver. Burning against his skin.

"I'm sorry, Liam." Luna's voice was quiet. Broken. "But Victor has my family. My real family. My daughter."

The woman on the platform stood. Her form shifted—wrinkles smoothed, hair darkened, eyes turned cold and cruel.

"Hello, nephew." Victor Blackwood smiled. "Did you really think I'd let Alexander's heir live?"

Liam's blood ran cold.

The Temple was a trap. Luna was a traitor. And the man holding him captive was the Wolf King's murderer.

The silver blade pressed deeper.

"I'm sorry," Luna whispered. Tears streamed down her face. "I had no choice."

Victor laughed. "There's always a choice, Luna. You just chose wrong."

He raised his hand. Silver energy gathered in his palm.

Liam closed his eyes.

And the wolf inside him roared.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Traitor in the Dark

The tunnel stretched forever.

Luna moved ahead of me, her silver hair catching fragments of light from some unseen source. I followed, my body still screaming from the transformation that had nearly torn me apart. Every step sent fire through my muscles. Every breath tasted like copper.

"The Moonlit Temple," I said, my voice echoing off damp stone walls. "You said my father built it."

"He did." Luna didn't turn around. "Two hundred years ago. Before the Council turned on him."

"Two hundred years?" I stopped walking. "My father died thirty years ago."

She stopped too. Her shoulders tensed. Then she turned, and I saw something in her eyes I hadn't seen before. Pain. Old. Deep. The kind that never healed.

"Alexander was murdered thirty years ago," she said. "But he built the temple two centuries before that. Time moves differently for Wolf Kings. You'll understand soon enough."

"Explain it to me now."

"Later." She turned back and kept walking. "We're almost there."

I wanted to push. Wanted to grab her shoulder and demand answers. But the voice in my head—Alexander's voice—whispered caution.

*She's not lying,* he said. *But she's not telling you everything either.*

*What do you know?*

*I know she was my mate. I know she loved me. And I know she buried me in a place the Council would never find.*

The tunnel ended at a door of black iron. No handle. No hinges. Just a flat surface covered in symbols that glowed faintly when Luna touched them.

"The temple recognizes blood," she said. "Yours. Mine. Alexander's." She pressed her palm flat against the iron. The symbols flared white-hot. The door groaned. Then it swung open, revealing a chamber that took my breath away.

The Moonlit Temple wasn't a building. It was a cavern. Massive. Cathedral-like. Stalactites hung from the ceiling like crystal chandeliers, each one pulsing with soft silver light. The walls were covered in murals—wolves fighting, wolves running, wolves bowing to a figure with a crown of moonlight. And in the center of the cavern, resting on a pedestal of black stone, sat a sword.

Not a normal sword. The blade was made of something that looked like frozen moonlight. The hilt was wrapped in dark leather, and the guard was shaped like two crescent moons facing each other.

"That's not a sword," I whispered.

"It's a fang." Luna walked toward it, her footsteps echoing. "The Fang of Lycaon. Forged from the tooth of the first werewolf. It can kill anything. Including a Wolf King."

"Why would I need to kill a Wolf King?"

She turned to face me. Her eyes glowed brighter now. "Because when the Council finds you—and they will find you—they won't come alone. They'll bring the Blood King."

"The Blood King?"

"Your father's brother. Your uncle." She paused. "The one who betrayed him."

The words hit me like a physical blow. I had an uncle. An uncle who killed my father. An uncle who was coming for me.

"How long?"

"Three days. Maybe four." Luna walked back toward me, stopping just inches away. "The Blood Moon ceremony is in seven days. Your uncle will want to be there. He'll want to watch you lose yourself to the wolf."

"Why?"

"Because if you become the wolf, the Wolf King bloodline dies with you. The power goes dormant. And your uncle becomes the most powerful werewolf alive."

I looked at the sword. The Fang. It glowed softly, like it was waiting for me.

"Can I kill him with that?"

"Yes." Luna's voice was barely a whisper. "But it comes with a price."

"Of course it does."

"The Fang feeds on its wielder. Every time you use it, it takes a piece of your humanity. Use it too much, and you become the wolf permanently. No ceremony needed."

I stared at the blade. At the pedestal. At the murals on the walls showing Wolf Kings of the past, wielding that same weapon, fighting wars I couldn't imagine.

"Show me how to use it," I said.

Luna nodded. Then she turned and pointed to the far wall. "First, you need to learn what you're fighting against."

The wall rippled. The murals shifted. And suddenly I was looking at a scene I recognized—the alley where I'd fought the rogues. But this time, I saw something I'd missed. Someone standing on the roof, watching. A figure in a dark coat. Face hidden. But the posture was familiar.

"That's not your uncle," Luna said. "That's someone else."

"Who?"

"Someone who's been following you since the night you awakened." She paused. "Someone who's been reporting your movements to the Council."

My blood went cold. "There's a traitor."

"Yes." Luna's eyes met mine. "And they're closer than you think."

The words hung in the air like smoke. I thought about everyone I'd met since the awakening. Marcus—dead. The rogues—all dead. The woman who saved me in the alley—

I looked at Luna.

"Don't," she said. "I loved your father. I would never—"

"Then who?"

She didn't answer. Instead, she walked to the pedestal and picked up the Fang. The blade flared white. The cavern shook. Dust fell from the ceiling.

"The traitor will reveal themselves when the time is right," she said. "But I can tell you one thing."

"What?"

"They're not alone."

The doors behind us slammed shut. Iron bars slid across the entrance. Locking us in.

Luna's face went pale. "He's here."

"Who?"

She didn't answer. She didn't need to. Because the air changed. Turned thick. Heavy. Like something ancient and terrible was breathing down my neck.

A voice echoed through the cavern. Deep. Cold. Familiar in ways that made my skin crawl.

"Nephew."

I turned.

A man stood at the far end of the cavern. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Silver hair like Luna's, but shorter. Eyes like molten gold. He wore a coat of black leather, and his hands were covered in scars.

He smiled. His teeth were too sharp.

"I've been looking for you."

Luna stepped in front of me, the Fang raised. "Darius."

"Luna." The man—my uncle—bowed mockingly. "Still guarding the throne? Still pretending you're loyal to a dead king?"

"I'm loyal to his son."

"His son." Darius laughed. It was a horrible sound. Like bones grinding together. "Look at him. He can barely stand. He doesn't know how to shift. He doesn't know how to fight. And you think he can become the Wolf King?"

"Alexander was weak once too."

"Alexander is dead."

The words hit like a punch. Luna flinched. I saw her grip tighten on the Fang.

"I'm going to give you one chance," Darius said. "Step aside. Let me take the boy. And I'll let you live."

Luna didn't move.

"Two chances."

Still nothing.

"Three."

She raised the Fang. "You'll have to kill me first."

Darius sighed. "I was hoping you'd say that."

He moved.

I didn't see him cross the distance. One moment he was fifty feet away. The next, he was in front of Luna, his hand wrapped around her throat, lifting her off the ground.

The Fang clattered to the stone floor.

"Luna!" I lunged forward, but something slammed into my chest—invisible, crushing—and threw me against the wall.

"Stay." Darius's voice was calm. Bored. "I'll deal with you in a moment."

Luna gasped, clawing at his hand. Her feet kicked. Her eyes started to dim.

And then I felt it.

The fire in my blood. The voice in my head. Alexander. Screaming.

*FIGHT.*

Something broke inside me.

The world went silver.

And the Wolf King rose.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Traitor in the Temple

The tunnels beneath the Moonlit Temple stretched like veins through the earth. Liam counted steps. Two thousand. Three thousand. The air grew colder, damper, thick with the smell of wet stone and something metallic he didn't want to identify.

Luna walked ahead, her silver hair catching light from the glowing crystals embedded in the walls. She hadn't spoken since they'd entered the hidden passage beneath the altar room.

"You said there were others," Liam said. His voice echoed. "Other packs, other factions. People who want the Wolf King to return."

She stopped. Turned.

"There are."

"Then why are we hiding?"

Luna's eyes flickered. Not fear. Something else. Calculation.

"Because not all of them want the Wolf King," she said. "Some want the bloodline. There's a difference."

She turned and kept walking.

Liam followed, his claws scraping against stone. He'd learned to shift his hands after three days of Luna's training. Partial transformations. Controlled. The mark on his chest pulsed with each heartbeat, a constant reminder of the ceremony six days away.

Six days. The thought sat in his stomach like a stone.

The tunnel opened into a chamber. Circular. High ceiling. A single pool of water in the center, glowing faintly blue. Around the edges, shadows moved.

Liam's instincts screamed. He dropped into a fighting stance, claws extended, teeth bared.

"Easy," Luna said. "They're friends."

The shadows stepped forward. Four figures. Three men, one woman. All wearing the same silver crescent pendants around their necks.

The woman stepped first. Dark skin. Shaved head. Eyes that burned gold in the dim light.

"Luna," she said. "You actually found him."

"Found him, saved him, trained him," Luna said. "Tala, this is Liam. Liam, Tala. She leads the Crescent Guard."

Tala studied him. Her gaze moved from his claws to his eyes to the mark visible through his torn shirt.

"He's young," she said.

"He's Alexander's son."

"That doesn't make him ready."

Liam's jaw tightened. "I'm standing right here."

Tala's lips curved. "I know. I'm testing your temper. You passed."

One of the men laughed. "She does that. Gets annoying."

He stepped forward. Tall. Lean. A scar running from his temple to his jaw. He extended a hand.

"I'm Kai. Welcome to the resistance."

Liam took his hand. The grip was firm, but not challenging. Respectful.

"We have a problem," Tala said. She moved to the pool and gestured. The water rippled, then stilled. An image formed. A compound. Stone walls. Watchtowers. A gate with a wolf's head sigil.

"The Ironclaw compound," Luna said.

"Was," Tala corrected. "Last night, they were attacked. Forty-seven dead. Including the alpha."

Liam felt the blood drain from his face. "The Council?"

"Worse." Tala's voice dropped. "One of their own. The alpha's second-in-command turned. Opened the gates. Let the Crimson Pack inside."

"Crimson Pack?" Liam asked.

Luna's expression hardened. "The Council's attack dogs. Werewolves who've given up their humanity entirely. They serve the Blood Moon Elders directly."

Kai spit on the ground. "Traitors to the species."

"How many?" Liam asked.

"In the attack? Thirty. But there are more. The Crimson Pack numbers in the hundreds. And they're getting stronger."

"Because of the blood moon," Liam said.

Tala nodded. "Every full moon, they grow more powerful. More feral. And after the ceremony—" She stopped.

"After the ceremony, what?"

No one spoke.

Luna stepped to his side. Her hand touched his arm, light, almost hesitant.

"If the Council completes the blood moon ceremony with you dead and no heir, the Wolf King's power scatters. It returns to the moon, waits for another bloodline to awaken. But if they capture you alive—"

"They can take the power. Transfer it."

"Yes."

Liam's stomach turned. "To who?"

"The Elders don't say. But there are rumors." Kai's voice dropped. "A new bloodline. One bred in secret. Engineered. They've been working on it for decades."

"Bred for what?"

"To be the perfect vessel. No humanity. No weakness. Just the wolf. All the power of the Wolf King, none of the conscience."

The room went silent.

Liam looked at his hands. His claws. The mark on his chest that still burned with his father's voice.

"Alexander fought for balance," he said slowly. "For the human and the wolf to coexist. If they create something that's only wolf—"

"It would be a monster," Luna finished. "A king with no heart. No mercy. The clans would become his army. The humans would become livestock."

"And the blood moon ceremony is seven days away."

"Six," Tala corrected. "You lose a day every time you sleep."

Liam's mind raced. "Then we hit them first."

"Excuse me?"

"We hit the Crimson Pack. Before they expect it. Take out their leadership, scatter their forces. Buy ourselves time."

Kai laughed. "You've been a wolf for a week. You want to attack the most dangerous pack in existence?"

"No. I want to cripple them. There's a difference."

Tala's eyes narrowed. "You have a plan?"

Liam didn't. Not yet. But he remembered Marcus. Marcus who had died so he could escape. Marcus who had believed in him when no one else did.

"I'll have one by morning."

---

Sleep didn't come.

Liam lay on a stone bench in one of the temple's side chambers, staring at the ceiling. The crystals pulsed. The mark on his chest pulsed. Everything pulsed.

Alexander's voice hadn't spoken since the training sessions. But Liam felt him. A presence in the back of his mind. Waiting.

*You're hiding something.*

The thought surfaced unbidden. He tried to push it down.

*From Luna. From the others.*

He sat up. Rubbed his face.

There was something wrong with the temple. He'd felt it since they arrived. A wrongness in the air. In the way the shadows moved.

He stood. Walked to the door. Pressed his ear against the wood.

Voices. Low. Urgent.

"—should have told him."

"Told him what? That his father's death wasn't an accident?"

Liam's blood went cold.

"That it was planned. From the beginning."

A pause. Then Luna's voice. Quiet. Broken.

"I know."

"You were there, Luna. You saw what happened."

"I saw Alexander fall. I saw the blade. I saw—"

"You saw the traitor's face."

Another pause. Longer.

"I saw," Luna said. "But I never told the Council. I never told anyone."

"Because?"

"Because the traitor was someone I loved."

Liam's hand moved before he thought. The door swung open.

They were in the main chamber. Tala. Kai. Luna. Three faces turned to him, caught in the crystal light.

"You lied to me," Liam said.

Luna's eyes widened. "Liam, I can explain—"

"The traitor. The one who killed my father. You know who it is."

"I know."

"And you've been protecting them."

"For thirty years."

"Why?"

Luna's face crumpled. For the first time since he'd met her, she looked fragile. Old. Broken.

"Because the traitor," she whispered, "is my brother."

The room held its breath.

Kai stepped forward. "Liam, there's more you need to know."

"More?" Liam's laugh was hollow. "More than the woman I trusted hiding my father's killer for three decades?"

"Your father knew," Tala said quietly.

"What?"

"Alexander knew Luna's brother betrayed him. He knew before the attack. And he forgave him."

"That doesn't make sense."

"Your father was not a normal alpha," Luna said. Tears streaked her face. "He saw things others couldn't. He saw that the war would only end if someone broke the cycle of vengeance. He let my brother live because he believed—"

"He believed wrong."

Liam's claws extended. The mark on his chest blazed.

"Where is he?"

"Liam—"

"WHERE IS HE?"

Luna's eyes met his. And in them, he saw the answer before she spoke.

"He's here. In the temple."

The words hit like a punch.

"He's been here the whole time. Watching. Waiting."

"No," Luna said. "Not waiting. Guarding. He's the one who's been protecting this place. Protecting you."

"Protecting me?" Liam's laugh echoed off the stone walls. "Your brother killed my father."

"He killed him because your father asked him to."

The room went silent.

"What?"

Luna's voice broke completely. "Your father was dying. A disease in his blood. The Wolf King's power was consuming him. He had seven days left. Seven days of agony. He asked my brother to end it. To make it quick. To make it painless."

"You're lying."

"I wish I was."

"Then why didn't you tell me?"

"Because your father made me promise. He said when you were ready, you'd understand. He said the truth would find you when you needed it most."

"And you think now is when I need it?"

"Look at your chest, Liam. Look at your hands. You have six days until the blood moon ceremony. You have six days to decide what kind of king you're going to be. One who rules through vengeance. Or one who breaks the cycle."

Liam stood frozen. The mark on his chest burned. Alexander's voice whispered in the back of his mind.

*She's telling the truth.*

*You knew?*

*I knew.*

*And you didn't tell me?*

*Some truths, son, can only be learned through pain.*

Liam closed his eyes. Breathed. Let the anger wash through him. Over him. Past him.

When he opened them, his claws had retracted.

"Take me to him."

Luna nodded. She led him through the temple, past the training grounds, past the armory, past a door he hadn't noticed before. It opened into a small room. A single candle. A bed. A chair.

In the chair sat a man. Grey hair. Grey eyes. A scar across his throat that made speech impossible.

But he could write.

He held up a board. Words scrawled in charcoal.

*I'm sorry.*

Liam stared at the man who killed his father. Who saved him. Who had been waiting thirty years for this moment.

"Show me," Liam said. "Show me everything."

The man nodded. Reached into his pocket. Pulled out a journal. Worn. Bloodstained. Familiar.

Alexander's handwriting.

Liam took it. Opened it. And began to read.

---

The first page said:

*To my son, on the day you learn the truth:*

*I did not die by traitor's hand. I died by friend's mercy. Forgive him. Forgive me. Forgive yourself.*

*And Liam?*

*Be the king I never could.*

---

Liam looked up. Tears blurred his vision.

Six days.

Six days until the blood moon.

And now he knew the truth.

The door behind him slammed open. Tala stood there, face pale, eyes wild.

"They're here."

"Who?"
