"The Crimson Pack. They found the temple. They're at the gates."

Kai appeared

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Temple's Betrayal

The fire had burned for three hours.

Liam sat in the center of the Moonlit Temple's main chamber, surrounded by candles arranged in concentric circles. The flames cast dancing shadows across ancient stone walls covered in faded murals—wolves fighting, wolves dying, wolves rising from pools of silver light.

His chest still ached where Luna had carved the protection sigil.

"Again," she said.

She stood at the edge of the circle, arms crossed, her silver hair pulled back in a tight braid. The torchlight caught the edges of her face, and for a moment, Liam saw it—the way her features shifted between human and something older. Something that had walked the earth when wolves were gods.

"I can't feel it," he said. "The shift. It's like there's a wall inside me."

"There is." Luna stepped closer. "Your father built it. When he knew he was dying, he sealed your wolf. Locked it behind seven gates of blood and bone."

"Seven gates?"

"Each one corresponds to a truth about the Wolf King bloodline. You've already broken two. The first when you accepted your heritage. The second when you survived the silver poisoning." She crouched down, meeting his eyes. "The third gate is control. You must learn to call your wolf without letting it consume you."

Liam remembered Marcus's words: *The mark cannot be removed. It can only be passed.*

"How do I break it?"

"By understanding what you're fighting." Luna reached into her pocket and pulled out a small leather pouch. She untied the string and poured its contents into her palm—a handful of dark red powder that smelled of copper and earth.

"What's that?"

"Dried wolf's bane mixed with crushed moonstone." She held it out to him. "Take it."

"Why?"

"Because I need you to trust me."

Liam stared at the powder. His instincts screamed at him. Every nerve in his body fired warnings. But he'd come this far by trusting her. By following her through the forest. By letting her carve symbols into his flesh.

He took the powder.

Nothing happened at first. Just the grit of it against his fingers. The strange warmth as it absorbed into his skin.

Then the pain hit.

Not like before. Not like the silver or the shift. This was deeper. A cold that spread from his palm up his arm, through his chest, into his heart. The candles flickered. The walls groaned. The mark over his chest began to glow—not silver, but red. Blood red.

"Luna?" His voice came out wrong. Too low. Too growling. "What did you give me?"

"The key to the third gate." She stood, backing away. "But you have to want it. You have to *need* it."

The cold turned to fire. His bones began to shift. His jaw cracked, lengthened. His spine arched. Fur sprouted across his arms.

"No—" He fought it. Pressed his hands to the stone floor. Tried to hold himself together. "I'm not ready—"

"You're never ready." Luna's voice echoed from somewhere far away. "The Wolf King doesn't wait for readiness. He creates it."

Liam screamed.

The shift came anyway.

---

He woke on his side, gasping.

The candles had gone out. The chamber was dark except for a single beam of moonlight that cut through a crack in the ceiling. It fell on something in the center of the room.

A body.

Liam scrambled to his feet. His legs felt wrong. His hands—he looked down. Fur. Claws. He was still shifted.

"No no no—"

The body moved. Groaned. Rolled over.

It was Luna.

Her face was a mask of blood. Her left arm bent at an angle that arms shouldn't bend. Her silver hair matted with crimson.

"What did I do?" Liam's voice came out as a growl. "Luna—"

"Not you." She coughed, spat blood. "Them."

He turned.

Four figures stood in the shadows near the temple entrance. Their eyes glowed amber. Their fangs caught the moonlight.

Rogues.

"Impressive shift, pup." The lead rogue stepped forward. Tall. Bald. A scar that ran from his temple to his jaw. "For a first-timer."

Liam planted himself in front of Luna. "Who are you?"

"Names don't matter." The rogue smiled. "Only bloodlines. And yours is the rarest of all."

"They're with the Council?" Liam asked Luna.

"No." Her voice was barely a whisper. "Worse. They're from the Shadow Pack."

The lead rogue laughed. "Clever girl. Always were." He looked at Liam. "Your father killed our alpha thirty years ago. Stole the Wolf King bloodline for himself. Now we're here to collect what's owed."

"You'll have to kill me first."

"That's the plan."

The rogue moved faster than Liam could track. One moment he was ten feet away. The next, his claws were at Liam's throat.

But Liam's body reacted before his mind caught up.

He ducked. Swept the rogue's legs. Rolled backward and came up in a crouch—all in one fluid motion. The movement felt natural. Instinctive.

*Good,* Alexander's voice said. *The blood remembers.*

"Where did you learn that move?" The rogue's eyes widened.

"Somewhere you'll never find."

Liam attacked.

He didn't think. Didn't plan. He let the wolf take control, but only partially. Let it guide his movements while he maintained the awareness. It was like driving a car with someone else's hands on the wheel—terrifying, but effective.

He caught the rogue across the face. Drew blood. Followed with a kick to the chest that sent him crashing into the stone wall.

The other three rushed him.

Liam met them head-on.

Claws raked his shoulder. He ignored the pain. Teeth sank into his arm. He pulled free and threw the wolf across the room. Something cracked. Bone or stone—he didn't care.

The fight became instinct.

Blind. Brutal. Beautiful.

When it was over, Liam stood among four bodies. His chest heaved. His claws dripped blood. The mark on his chest burned like a brand.

He had won.

But he felt no victory.

Only emptiness.

---

Luna sat propped against the wall, her broken arm cradled against her chest. She watched him with an expression he couldn't read.

"You held the shift," she said. "Nearly ten minutes. That's... impressive."

"I hurt you."

"You protected me." She smiled, blood staining her teeth. "That's what a king does."

Liam forced himself to shift back. It took longer than before. His body resisted, clung to the wolf form like a child refusing to leave a warm bed. But eventually, his bones settled. His skin smoothed. He stood there, naked and shaking, in a pool of moonlight.

"We need to leave," Luna said. "They'll send more."

"Who will?"

"The Shadow Pack. They've been hunting the Wolf King bloodline for generations. When they feel one of their own die—"

The ground shook.

A low rumble that started beneath the temple and grew until the walls trembled. Dust fell from the ceiling. The remaining candles toppled and died.

"What was that?" Liam asked.

Luna's face went pale. "They found us faster than I expected."

The temple entrance exploded inward.

Stone shattered. Wood splintered. And through the cloud of debris stepped a wolf unlike any Liam had ever seen.

It was the size of a horse. Its fur was black as oil, matted with scars and what looked like ritual markings carved into its hide. Its eyes burned red. Not gold. Not silver. Red.

And on its back sat a woman.

She was beautiful in the way a blade is beautiful. Sharp. Dangerous. Perfect. Her hair was white, cropped short. Her face was angular, hard, with cheekbones that could cut glass. She wore armor made of bone and leather, and in her hand, she carried a spear tipped with—

Silver.

"Liam Kane." Her voice was cold. Calm. Like a winter wind. "Or should I say, Liam Blackwood?"

Liam's blood froze.

"How do you know that name?"

"The Shadow Pack knows everything about its enemies." She slid off the wolf's back and landed silently. "I am Selene. Alpha of the Shadow Pack. And I've come to offer you a choice."

"Choice?"

"Join us. Accept your place as the heir to the Wolf King bloodline—under my command. Or die here, in this temple, and let the bloodline die with you."

Luna struggled to her feet. "Liam, don't listen to her—"

"Quiet, traitor." Selene didn't even look at her. "You've had your chance to guide him. You failed."

"I'm not going anywhere with you." Liam stepped forward. Naked. Unarmed. But something in his chest burned hot. "I don't know who you are. I don't know what you want. But I'm not your puppet."

Selene smiled.

It was the most terrifying thing Liam had ever seen.

"Puppet?" She laughed. "Oh, sweet boy. I don't want a puppet." She raised her spear, pointed it at his heart. "I want a king."

The wolf behind her snarled.

Liam's vision blurred.

And in that moment, he felt it—a pull. A connection. Something that tied him to this woman, to this pack, to a history he didn't understand.

*She's like you,* Alexander whispered. *She carries the old blood.*

*What old blood?*

*The blood of the first wolves. The ones who walked when the moon was young.*

The mark on Liam's chest flared.

Selene's eyes widened.

"You've already broken three gates," she said. "Impossible. It should have taken weeks."

"I had a good teacher."

"Not her." Selene's voice dropped. "Him. Alexander. He's still alive inside you."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Yes, you do." She stepped closer. Too close. He could smell her now—smoke, blood, and something floral underneath. "The Wolf King's spirit doesn't die. It passes from father to son. Alexander lives in your blood. In your bones. In every cell of your body."

Liam wanted to deny it.

But he couldn't.

Because he felt him. Alexander. A presence at the edge of his consciousness, watching, waiting, judging.

"He's been talking to you, hasn't he?" Selene asked. "Telling you about the bloodline. The power. The destiny."

"Yes."

"He's lying."

The words hit like a physical blow.

"What?"

"Alexander Blackwood was not a hero. He was not a king. He was a monster who stole power that didn't belong to him." Selene's eyes hardened. "And if you follow his voice, you will become the same."

Liam looked at Luna. She wouldn't meet his eyes.

"Is it true?" he asked.

Silence.

"Is it true, Luna?"

"He wanted to protect you," she whispered. "That's all he ever wanted."

"From what?"

"From the truth." Selene answered for her. "The truth about what happens during the blood moon ceremony. The truth about what the Wolf King bloodline really is." She leaned in, her lips brushing his ear. "And the truth about what you'll become if you don't stop it."

The ground shook again.

Harder this time.

The temple began to collapse.

"Time's up." Selene grabbed his arm.

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Traitor's Kiss

The Temple of Echoes had fallen silent.

Liam stood in the center of the shattered hall, blood dripping from his knuckles, the crystal shard still humming in his chest. Around him, the Elders lay unconscious or dead—he couldn't tell which, didn't care to check. Marcus's body was gone. Victor's too. The wolf inside him paced, restless, hungry.

But it wasn't the wolf that made his skin crawl.

It was her.

Luna stood at the far end of the hall, silver hair illuminated by the moonlight streaming through the broken dome. Her eyes glowed—not the soft silver he remembered, but something darker. Something *wrong*. She hadn't moved since the fighting stopped. Hadn't spoken.

"Luna." Liam's voice echoed off the marble walls. "We need to go. More of them will come."

She didn't answer.

He took a step toward her. The crystal pulsed. Warning. *Danger.* But she was his father's mate. She'd saved him in the forest. She'd brought him to the Temple. She'd—

"I know," she said.

Her voice was different. Hollow. Like she was speaking from the bottom of a well.

"Know what?" Liam's hand drifted to the silver blade at his hip. He'd taken it from one of the Elders. It felt wrong in his grip. Necessary.

Luna turned to face him fully. Her eyes weren't silver anymore. They were black. Completely black. Like voids where her pupils should have been.

"I know you killed him," she whispered.

"Killed who? Marcus?"

"No." A tear traced down her cheek. Black. Like ink. "Alexander."

The world stopped.

"What are you talking about?" Liam's grip tightened on the blade. "I never met my father. He died before I was born. The Elders told me—"

"The Elders lied." Luna walked toward him. Each step echoed. Each step brought her closer to the blade. "Alexander didn't die in battle, Liam. He was murdered. Thirty years ago. By his own pack."

"That's not—"

"I was there." Her voice broke. "I held him as he bled out. And you know what he said? His last words?"

Liam couldn't breathe. Couldn't move. The crystal burned against his chest.

"He said, 'Find my son. Before they do.'"

"Who? Who killed him?"

Luna stopped three feet away. Close enough to touch. Close enough to kill.

"The same people who've been hunting you," she said. "The Silver Fang Council. But they didn't act alone." She reached up, touched her chest. "They had help. From someone inside the temple."

"Who?"

"Me."

The word hit him like a silver bullet.

Liam's vision went red. The wolf surged forward, claws extending, fangs lengthening. He didn't think. Didn't hesitate. He lunged.

Luna caught his throat.

Her grip was iron. Her eyes burned black. And when she spoke, her voice carried the weight of three decades of guilt.

"I didn't have a choice, Liam. They had your mother. They had *you*. Before you were born. They said if I didn't help them, they'd kill you both."

"Get your hands off me." His voice was animal. Guttural. The wolf wanted blood.

"I'm trying to save you." Her grip tightened. "The same way I tried to save Alexander. The same way I failed."

"Then why are you telling me this now?"

"Because the Council is here. They followed us from the temple. They're surrounding the compound as we speak." Her eyes flickered. For a moment, the silver returned. The woman he'd trusted. "And because I'm tired of carrying this lie."

Liam's claws pressed against her throat. One swipe. One motion. She'd be dead.

"Do it," she whispered. "I deserve it."

The crystal hummed. Alexander's voice echoed in his skull. *She loved me. She loved us.*

Liam's claws retracted.

"Where are they?" he asked.

"Everywhere." Luna released his throat. Stepped back. "North entrance. South gate. The sewers beneath the temple. They've been planning this for years. Waiting for the blood moon. Waiting for you to come into your power so they could take it."

"Take it?"

"The crystal." She pointed at his chest. "They don't want to kill you, Liam. They want to harvest you. Your blood. Your spirit. Your birthright. They want to transfer the Wolf King's power to their own alpha."

"Victor."

"Victor is a puppet. The true leader of the Council is someone you've never met. Someone who's been pulling strings since before you were born."

"Who?"

Luna opened her mouth to answer—

And a blade burst through her chest.

Liam watched it happen in slow motion. Watched the silver tip emerge from her sternum. Watched her eyes go wide. Watched her fall to her knees.

Behind her stood a woman.

Tall. Dark hair cropped short. Eyes like winter ice. She wore the black armor of the Silver Fang Council, but different. Older. More ornate. A crescent moon crown rested on her brow.

"The true leader," Luna gasped, blood bubbling on her lips. "She's standing right behind me."

The woman smiled. Cold. Perfect. Deadly.

"Hello, nephew."

Liam's blood turned to ice.

"Who the hell are you?"

"Your father's sister." She pulled the blade from Luna's back. Luna crumpled. Didn't move. "My name is Seraphina. And I've been waiting thirty years to meet you."

The compound shook. Explosions. Gunfire. Howls.

"The attack," Liam said. "You're already here."

"We never left." Seraphina wiped the blade on her thigh. "The Temple of Echoes was always a trap. We just needed you to walk into it."

"Then why didn't you kill me before?"

"Because the transfer requires proximity. And a willing vessel." She tilted her head. "You have to accept the power before we can take it. The blood moon ceremony doesn't just awaken the Wolf King. It seals the bond. And once it's sealed, it can be transferred."

"To you."

"To me." She stepped over Luna's body. Approached him. "I was supposed to be the heir. I was older. Stronger. More experienced. But Alexander was born with the mark. The universe chose him. And then he chose to run away with a human woman and hide you in the mortal world."

"He was protecting me."

"He was *hiding*. There's a difference." She stopped an arm's length away. "But I'm not here to argue ancient history. I'm here to offer you a choice."

"Let me guess. Join you or die."

"Die." She smiled. "Definitely die. But *how* you die is the choice."

The walls shook again. Dust rained from the ceiling. Somewhere, Liam heard screaming.

"You can die here, in this temple, alone and forgotten. Or you can die on your feet, fighting, with the blood of my soldiers on your claws."

"Those are the same choice."

"No." Seraphina's eyes glowed silver. "One is a death for nothing. The other is a death for *something*."

The crystal pulsed. The wolf howled.

*She's lying,* Alexander's voice said. *She needs you alive for the transfer. She can't kill you until the ceremony.*

*Then what's her plan?*

*To break you. To make you accept the bond willingly. If you fight, she wins. If you surrender, she wins. The only way to beat her—*

"Is to stop playing your game." Liam met Seraphina's eyes. "I'm not going to fight you."

Her smile faltered. "What?"

"I'm not going to fight. I'm not going to run. I'm going to walk out of this temple, find the blood moon, and claim my birthright. And when I'm done, I'm going to hunt you down and tear out your throat."

"You're surrounded. You're outnumbered. You're barely a wolf."

"I'm the Wolf King." Liam felt the mark burn. Felt the power rise. "And you're dead. You just don't know it yet."

He turned his back on her.

Walked toward the shattered dome.

And the world exploded.

Silver fire engulfed the temple. Seraphina's scream split the night. Liam ran—leaped—smashed through the dome and into open air.

He landed on the roof. The compound below was chaos. Fires. Bodies. Wolves fighting wolves.

And in the center of it all, standing alone, was a figure he recognized.

Kira.

She looked up at him. Her eyes were red. Her clothes were torn. In her hand, she held a silver blade dripping with blood.

"Liam," she said. "Run."

"Why?"

"Because I just killed the Council's second-in-command." She smiled. Bloody. Broken. "And now they're going to kill me."

Behind her, a hundred wolves emerged from the shadows.

Liam's heart stopped.

"Kira—"

"Go." Her voice cracked. "Claim the blood moon. Become the king. And when you do, come back for me."

"You won't survive."

"I know." She raised the blade. "But I'll take as many of them with me as I can."

The wolves charged.

Liam wanted to jump. Wanted to fight. Wanted to die beside her.

But the crystal burned. Alexander's voice screamed. *Go. Now. Or everything we've done was for nothing.*

Liam turned.

Ran.

And behind him, Kira's scream tore through the night.

He didn't look back.

He couldn't.

Because if he did, he would never leave.

The forest swallowed him. The moon watched. The mark burned.

And somewhere, deep in the darkness, the blood moon was rising.

*Seven days.* Alexander's voice was barely a whisper. *You have seven days.*

Liam ran.

And the hunt began.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Moonlit Temple

I stared at her hand. Processed her words. Rejected them.

"No."

The word came out flat. Dead.

"You don't understand—"

"I understand perfectly." I pushed myself up, ignoring the fire in my ribs. "You knew who I was. You knew what was coming. And you let me walk into that alley alone."

Luna's hand didn't lower. "If I'd told you everything, you wouldn't have believed me."

"I don't believe you now."

"You're alive. That's proof enough."

I stood. My legs screamed. The cut on my thumb had already closed—silver line of new skin, pink and tender. Healed. Like nothing happened. Like everything happened.

"Your father made me promise," she said. "If he ever fell, I would find you. Protect you. Train you for the ceremony."

"Marcus already tried to train me. He's dead."

Something flickered in her eyes. Pain. Old and deep. "Marcus was a good man. But he was a warrior, not a teacher. What you need—what the Wolf King's bloodline requires—can't be learned in a training yard."

"And you can teach it?"

"I can try."

The mark on my chest pulsed. Warm. Demanding attention. Alexander's voice stirred in the back of my skull, but he didn't speak. Just waited. Like he knew something I didn't.

"If you were my father's mate," I said, "why aren't you dead? The Council killed everyone connected to him."

Luna's smile turned brittle. "Because I'm not a werewolf."

I blinked. "What?"

"I'm a Lunar. We predate your kind. Before the first wolf shifted, before the first alpha claimed a throne, the Moon created us to watch over her children." She touched her chest, where a faint glow pulsed beneath her shirt. "I can't be killed by silver. I can't be tracked by scent. And I've been hiding in plain sight for thirty years."

"A Lunar."

"Yes."

"And you just... watched? For thirty years? While the Council hunted anyone who knew my father's name?"

"I watched you grow up, Liam. I watched you fall off your bike when you were six. I watched your mother cry over bills she couldn't pay. I watched the full moons take you, piece by piece, and I couldn't interfere until your bloodline awakened."

"Why?"

"Because the awakening is what makes you visible. Before tonight, you were just a boy with blackouts. Now you're a target. And a weapon."

I wanted to argue. Wanted to hit something. Wanted to scream until my throat bled. But she was right. I'd felt it—the shift in the air when the mark appeared. Like every supernatural creature within a hundred miles just realized I existed.

"Where's this temple?" I asked.

"Three hours north. In the Whisperwood."

"The forest that's been burning for a week?"

Luna's eyes darkened. "The Council started that fire. They're searching for something hidden beneath the old pines. The temple is protected by Lunar magic, but if they get too close..."

"They'll find it."

"Yes."

I looked at my hands. Still normal. Still pale. But I could feel the wolf beneath the skin, waiting. Hungry. Alexander's presence coiled around my spine like a second skeleton.

*Trust her,* the voice whispered.

*Why?*

*Because she loved your father more than the moon itself. And she's the only one left who knows the truth.*

"Fine." The word tasted like ash. "Take me to your temple."

---

Luna moved like smoke through the trees. Silent. Fluid. I stumbled behind her, crashing through branches, my body still learning what it had become.

The forest changed as we went north. The familiar oaks and maples gave way to twisted pines, their bark blackened by ancient fires. The air grew cold. Thin. Every breath tasted of smoke and something else—something sweet, like rotting flowers.

"The Whisperwood earned its name," Luna said without looking back. "The spirits here remember the war. They don't forget."

"Spirits."

"Every death leaves an echo. Every battle stains the ground. This forest has seen more blood than soil."

A branch snapped to my left. I spun, fists raised, heart hammering. Nothing. Just shadows shifting in the moonlight.

"You're jumpy," Luna observed.

"I'm being hunted by a Council of werewolves, possessed by a dead king, and following a woman I met three hours ago into a haunted forest. Forgive me if I'm not relaxed."

She almost smiled. "You have your father's temper."

"And his enemies."

"That too."

We walked in silence for another hour. The trees grew thicker, older. Their branches interlaced overhead, blocking the moon until we moved through a tunnel of darkness. My wolf-sight adjusted, painting the world in shades of gray and silver. I could see the veins in leaves. The pulse in Luna's throat. The way her heartbeat stayed steady, even when mine raced.

"You're not afraid," I said.

"Of what?"

"Me. The Wolf King. Whatever's hunting us."

Luna stopped. Turned. Her eyes caught the faintest light, glowing like twin moons. "I've been afraid for thirty years, Liam. Every day. Every night. Afraid you wouldn't survive. Afraid you would. Afraid the ceremony would come and I'd fail your father's last wish." She stepped closer. "I've spent three decades being afraid. I'm done."

Her hand touched my chest, just over the mark. Warm. Electric.

"The blood moon rises in six days. On that night, you have to choose: embrace the wolf or let it consume you. There's no middle ground. No compromise. Either you become the Wolf King, or Alexander's soul devours yours and walks the earth in your skin."

"And the temple?"

"Contains the only record of how to perform the binding ritual. How to merge with the wolf without losing yourself. Marcus couldn't teach you because he never knew. The Council burned every copy they could find. But the Moonlit Temple has the original—carved into stone by the first Lunar priestess."

"Why would she help a werewolf?"

"Because the first Wolf King saved her life." Luna's voice softened. "He loved her. And she loved him enough to prepare for his death."

The story settled over me like a weight. A love that outlasted centuries. A betrayal that still echoed. My father's bloodline, soaked in tragedy.

"How do I perform the binding?"

"First, you have to survive the night." Luna's head snapped up. Her nostrils flared. "They found us."

The air changed. Pressure built against my ears. The shadows around us grew longer, deeper, moving against the wind.

"How many?"

"Three. Maybe four. They've been tracking us since the river."

"Why didn't you—"

"Because I needed to see if you could sense them."

I couldn't. Not until she said something. My wolf-sight showed me the forest, the trees, the path ahead—but not the danger behind. Not the predators closing in.

"I can't sense them," I admitted.

"Then we have a problem." Luna grabbed my arm, pulling me off the path. "The Wolf King's bloodline includes enhanced senses. You should be able to smell a rogue from a mile away. If you can't, it means Alexander's presence is suppressing your instincts."

*She's right.* Alexander's voice, grudging. *I'm too close to the surface. Your body doesn't know whose senses to trust.*

*Then back off.*

*I can't. Not fully. The awakening has begun. I'm bound to you until the ceremony.*

Great.

We broke through a wall of thorns into a clearing. At its center stood the temple—a structure of white stone, half-sunken into the earth, covered in vines and moss. Carvings wrapped around its walls: wolves, moons, spiraling patterns that seemed to move when I looked directly at them.

"The entrance is through the crypt," Luna said. "We have to—"

A howl split the night. Close. Too close.

Then another. And another.

Three wolves emerged from the treeline. Not the wolves I knew from nature documentaries—these were massive, shoulders reaching my chest, eyes burning with amber fire. Their fur rippled with muscle, and their teeth gleamed like silver in the moonlight.

Rogues.

The lead wolf stepped forward. Its hackles rose. Its lips peeled back, exposing fangs the length of my fingers.

"Liam," Luna said quietly, "when I tell you to run, you run."

"Run where?"

"The temple. Don't stop. Don't look back."

"And you?"

She turned to face the wolves. Her eyes blazed white. The air around her shimmered, hot and cold at once, and I felt something ancient stir in her presence.

"I've been waiting thirty years," she said, "to remind these dogs what a Lunar can do."

She moved.

Not fast. Not slow. She walked toward the wolves like she was greeting old friends. The lead wolf lunged. Luna caught it mid-air, one hand around its throat, and I heard the snap before I saw it fall.

The other two hesitated.

"Now, Liam!"

I ran.

The temple entrance was a gap in the stone, black as ink, swallowing the moonlight. I dove into it as something crashed behind me—a body, a growl, a scream that wasn't human.

Darkness.

Silence.

I landed on stone floor, knees scraping, breath ragged. The tunnel stretched ahead, lined with torches that lit themselves as I watched, blue flames dancing without fuel.

Behind me, the sounds of battle faded.

And in the darkness ahead, something called my name.

*Liam.*

Not Alexander's voice. Not Luna's. A woman's voice, soft as silk, old as blood.

*Come deeper. I've been waiting.*

The tunnel beckoned.

I went forward.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Moonlit Temple

I stared at her hand.

*Your father's mate.*

The words didn't make sense. Couldn't make sense. Marcus had told me my father was Alexander—the Wolf King, the betrayer, the monster who slaughtered his own pack. And this woman, this girl who looked barely older than me, was claiming to have been *his* mate?

"You're lying," I said.

Luna's smile didn't waver. "Would you like me to prove it?"

"Prove it?" I laughed—a broken, wet sound. "How? By showing me a photo album? By telling me bedtime stories about the big bad wolf who fathered me before he went on a murder spree?"

The amusement died in her eyes. Something else took its place. Something old and heavy and sharp as broken glass.

"The last time I saw your father," she said quietly, "he was holding you in his arms. You were three hours old. Your mother was dead. The Council was ten minutes away. He kissed your forehead, handed you to Marcus, and walked into the night to draw them away."

The stream gurgled beside me. A night bird called somewhere in the darkness. I wanted to call her a liar again. I wanted to believe she was just another piece of this nightmare, another predator circling the wounded prey.

But the mark on my chest pulsed. Warm. Almost welcoming.

"Say I believe you," I said slowly. "Why didn't you come for me before? Why now?"

"Because I couldn't." Luna's voice cracked. Just slightly. Just enough. "The Council didn't just hunt your father. They hunted anyone connected to him. I've been running for twenty-one years, Liam. Sleeping in ditches. Killing hunters who got too close. Watching from shadows while you grew up not knowing what you were."

She knelt beside the stream. Her reflection rippled in the water—silver hair, pale skin, eyes like twin moons.

"I'm the one who made sure Marcus found you that day in the alley. I'm the one who's been keeping the rogues off your trail for the past two years. I'm the one who's been waiting for tonight."

"Waiting for what?"

"For you to finally ask for help."

The forest held its breath. Somewhere in the distance, wolves howled—not close, but close enough. The rogues were still hunting. The Council was still coming. And I was lying in a stream, bleeding from wounds I didn't remember getting, talking to a woman who might be my dead father's lover or might be my executioner.

I took her hand.

Her grip was strong. Warm. She pulled me to my feet and I swayed, head spinning, legs threatening to buckle. She caught me. Steadied me. Let me lean on her.

"We don't have much time," she said. "The Temple's warded, but the wards have been weakening for decades. We need to move."

"Where is it?"

"About four miles north. Can you run?"

I looked at my body. Torn clothes. Cuts everywhere. A bruise forming on my ribs that made breathing feel like swallowing glass.

"I can crawl if I have to."

Luna's lips twitched. "You have your father's stubbornness."

"Is that a compliment?"

"It's a survival trait. Now shut up and follow me."

She shifted.

One moment she was a woman. The next, she was a wolf—silver fur, golden eyes, larger than any wolf I'd ever seen. Her muscles rippled under moonlight. Her gaze pinned me like prey.

*Shift,* Alexander's voice said. *You'll never keep up on two legs.*

"I don't know how," I whispered.

*Then learn. Now.*

Luna whined. Paced. Looked back at me with something that might have been impatience or might have been hope.

I closed my eyes.

The mark burned. The wolf inside me stirred. I'd spent twenty-one years suppressing it, fighting it, pretending it didn't exist. But it was part of me. Had always been part of me. The blackouts. The missing time. The way I'd wake up with blood under my nails and no memory of where I'd been.

I stopped fighting.

I let go.

The pain hit like a freight train. My bones broke. Re-formed. Broke again. My skin split and knitted and split again. I screamed—or tried to—but the sound came out wrong. Animal. Alien.

And then it was over.

I opened my eyes.

The world looked different. Colors sharper. Smells overwhelming—pine and moss and blood and Luna's scent, which was moonlight and lavender and something ancient. I looked down at my paws. My fur. Black as shadow, with a single white patch over my heart.

*Not bad,* Alexander said. *For a first time.*

I wanted to respond. Couldn't find the words. Couldn't find *words* at all. Just instinct. Hunger. The need to run.

Luna took off.

I followed.

The forest blurred past. Trees became streaks of brown and green. The ground thrummed under my paws. I'd never felt anything like this—pure freedom, pure power, every muscle working in perfect harmony. This was what I'd been missing. This was what I'd been afraid of.

*The Moonlit Temple,* Alexander said. *I remember it.*

*You've been there?*

*I built it.*

We emerged into a clearing.

The Temple was old. Older than the trees around it. Stone walls covered in moss, windows dark and empty, a roof that sagged in the middle like an old man's spine. But there was power here. I could feel it. Humming in the ground. Thrumming in the air.

Luna shifted back. Naked. Unashamed. She walked toward the Temple without looking back.

I shifted too. Slower this time. More controlled. The pain was still there, but muted. Bearable.

"You coming?" she called.

I followed her inside.

The Temple was larger than it looked. A single room, high ceiling, stone floor covered in faded symbols. Candles lined the walls—unlit, but I could smell the wax, the wicks, the dust that had settled over decades.

Luna touched her palm to a symbol on the wall. The candles lit. One by one. A ring of fire that pushed back the darkness.

"Sit," she said.

I sat.

She sat across from me. Cross-legged. Hands resting on her knees. The firelight danced across her face, making her look younger, older, ageless.

"Your father and I met when we were seventeen," she said. "He was already powerful. Already marked. But he was also kind. Gentle. The kind of wolf who stopped hunting when he realized the animals could feel fear."

"Marcus said he killed his own pack."

"He did." Her voice didn't waver. "But not for the reasons the Council tells. Alexander didn't go mad. He was *driven* mad. The same people who want you dead now are the ones who destroyed him."

"The Council."

"The Council. And the Elders. And the Moon Priestesses who serve them." She leaned forward. "The Blood Moon ceremony isn't about awakening the Wolf King's power, Liam. It's about *stealing* it. The Council has been using the ceremony for centuries to transfer bloodlines from one family to another. They've built their power on stolen legacies."

My blood ran cold. "What do you mean?"

"Every Blood Moon, a chosen heir undergoes the ceremony. If they survive, they're bound to the Council forever. Their power becomes the Council's power. Their will becomes the Council's will." She paused. "Your father survived his ceremony. Barely. But the binding didn't take. He was too strong. And when he broke free, he killed everyone who tried to stop him. Everyone who had a hand in corrupting the Moon's gift."

"Everyone. Including his own pack."

"His pack knew what the Council was doing. They participated. They benefited." Her voice hardened. "Alexander didn't murder innocents, Liam. He executed traitors."

The candles flickered. Shadows danced.

I wanted to believe her. I wanted to believe that my father was a hero, not a monster. But Marcus's dying words echoed in my skull. *He killed them all. Every single one.*

"How do I know you're telling the truth?"

Luna's eyes met mine. "You don't. But I can show you."

She reached into her pocket and pulled out a pendant. Silver chain. Moonstone pendant. It caught the candlelight and threw it back, scattering rainbows across the walls.

"Your father gave me this the night he died. It contains his memories. His truth." She held it out. "Touch it. See for yourself."

I hesitated.

*Don't,* Alexander whispered. *Some truths are better left buried.*

But I needed to know. Needed to understand why my father did what he did. Needed to know if I was destined to follow the same path.

I reached out. Touched the pendant.

And the world dissolved.

I was in a hall. Marble floors. Crystal chandeliers. Wolves in formal wear, laughing, drinking, celebrating. At the center of it all, a man. Tall. Dark-haired. Eyes like mine.

Alexander.

He was smiling. A genuine smile, warm and open. A woman stood beside him—Luna, younger, softer, her belly round with pregnancy.

"The ceremony is tomorrow," Alexander said. "After that, we're free. No more Council. No more binding. Just us."

"The Council won't let us go," Luna said.

"The Council won't have a choice." He touched her belly. "Our son will be the strongest Wolf King in a century. They need him. And if they want him, they'll have to accept my terms."

The memory shifted.

Same hall. Different night. The chandeliers were dark. The wolves were gone. Alexander stood alone, blood on his hands, blood on his face, blood pooling at his feet.

He was crying.

"Luna," he whispered. "They took her. They took our son. They used the ceremony to bind me, and I couldn't—I couldn't stop them. I couldn't—"

His body convulsed. His eyes glowed red. The Wolf King mark on his chest blazed like fire.

"I'll kill them all," he snarled. "Every last one. They want a monster? I'll give them a monster."

The memory shattered.

I gasped. Pulled my hand back. The pendant fell to the floor, clattering against stone.

"Now you know," Luna said softly.

"He was innocent," I said. "They made him that way."

"They made him. And now they're trying to make you." She picked up the pendant. "That's why I'm here. That's why I've been protecting you. Because you're the only one who can finish what he started."

"Finish what?"

"Destroy the Council. Purify the Blood Moon ceremony. Restore balance to the werewolf world." She looked at me. "You have seven days, Liam. Seven days to master the Wolf King's power. Seven days to prepare for the ceremony. And when it comes, you'll have a choice."

"What choice?"

"Submit to the Council's binding and become their puppet. Or embrace your father's legacy and burn it all down."

The candles flickered. The shadows pressed closer.

Somewhere outside, the wolves howled again. Closer this time. Much closer.

Luna's head snapped up. "They found us."

"Who?"

"Doesn't matter. We need to move." She grabbed my arm, pulled me toward a hidden door behind the altar. "The Temple has tunnels. They lead

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Temple's Secret

I took her hand.

Not because I trusted her. Because I had no other options. The Council wanted me dead. The rogue pack wanted my bloodline. And something ancient inside my chest was counting down the seconds until it consumed me whole.

Luna pulled me up with surprising strength. Her fingers were warm against mine, calloused in ways that suggested years of fighting. She smelled of jasmine and rain and something older—something that made Alexander stir in the back of my mind.

*Careful,* he growled. *She was always dangerous.*

"You knew my father," I said, not a question.

"I knew him before he was the Wolf King." She released my hand and turned toward the treeline. "Before he was anything. We grew up together in the Moonlit Temple. Trained together. Fought together."

"Then why did he leave?"

She paused. Her shoulders tightened. "That's a story for another time. Right now, we need to move. The Council's enforcers will have tracked the bloodline activation. They'll be here within the hour."

"The Council wants me dead."

"The Council wants control." She started walking, her bare feet silent on the forest floor. I noticed she wore the same clothes from the alley—a dark leather jacket, torn jeans. No shoes. "Your father threatened their power. You threaten it more. A Wolf King who can't shift is a liability they can eliminate before he becomes a threat."

I limped after her, every muscle screaming. The cuts on my arms had already started closing—another gift from Alexander's bloodline—but the deeper wounds would take longer. "You said you can take me to the Moonlit Temple. What's there?"

"Answers. Training. The last remaining records of your father's reign." She glanced back at me, her silver eyes catching the moonlight. "And the only person alive who knew your father better than I did."

"Who?"

"The Temple Keeper. The one who bound Alexander to your bloodline in the first place."

I stopped walking. "Someone bound him? I thought it was inherited."

Luna turned to face me fully. The moonlight painted her features in silver and shadow. "Nothing about the Wolf King bloodline is natural, Liam. It was created. Forged in blood and ritual and sacrifice. Your father didn't inherit the Wolf King's power—he stole it."

The words hit like a punch to the chest. "Stole it?"

"From the previous Wolf King. A tyrant who had ruled for three centuries. Your father led a rebellion with a handful of allies and a ritual that should have killed him." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "He was always stubborn like that."

*She speaks the truth,* Alexander said. *I was not born into this blood. I took it.*

"Then why does the Council want me dead? If I'm just an heir to a stolen throne—"

"You're not just an heir." Luna stepped closer. "You're the first naturally born Wolf King in history. Every previous king claimed the power through conquest. You were born with it. That makes you a threat to the entire existing order."

"The Council killed my father because of that?"

Her expression flickered. Pain. Loss. Something darker. "No. The Council killed your father because he refused to use you. They wanted to raise you in the Temple, mold you into their weapon. He refused. Fled. Hid you among humans for twenty-one years."

"And they killed him for that."

"Yes."

Silence stretched between us. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled. Not human. Not werewolf. Just an ordinary animal calling to the moon. I envied it.

"Take me to the Temple," I said.

Luna nodded and turned without another word.

We walked for hours.

The forest changed around us. The familiar oaks and pines gave way to twisted, ancient trees I didn't recognize. Their bark was black as charcoal, their leaves silver in the moonlight. The air grew thick with magic—I could feel it pressing against my skin, humming in my veins.

Alexander stirred every few minutes, restless. Hungry. *The blood moon approaches. I can feel it pulling at me.*

"How do I shift?" I asked, breaking the silence.

Luna didn't slow. "You don't. Not yet."

"The voice inside me says I have seven days. If I don't master the transformation—"

"You'll lose yourself to the wolf." She finally stopped, turning to face me. "I know. I've seen it happen. Your father trained for three months before his first shift. You have seven days."

"Then help me train."

"I can't." She pointed ahead, through a gap in the trees. "The Temple can."

I followed her gaze and felt my breath catch.

The Moonlit Temple rose from the forest floor like a monument to forgotten gods. Stone walls covered in vines and ancient runes. Towers that spiraled toward the sky, their peaks lost in clouds. A massive moon-shaped gate stood at the entrance, pulsing with faint silver light.

It was impossible. Beautiful. Terrifying.

"The Temple is alive," Luna said. "It was built on a convergence of ley lines. The magic here is old. Older than the Council. Older than the werewolf clans. It will either accept you or reject you."

"What happens if it rejects me?"

She didn't answer. She just walked toward the gate.

The Temple's entrance loomed before us. The moon-shaped gate hummed with energy, and as Luna approached, it swung open on its own—silent, smooth, welcoming.

I followed her through.

The interior was nothing like I expected. No torches. No stone altars. Instead, the walls glowed with bioluminescent crystals, casting everything in soft blue light. The ceiling opened to the sky, and moonlight poured through, pooling on the floor in liquid silver.

In the center of the main chamber stood a figure.

Old. Bent. Wrapped in robes that seemed to shift between shadow and light. They didn't turn when we entered. Just stood there, staring at the moon pool at their feet.

"Keeper," Luna said, her voice carrying reverence. "I've brought him."

The figure moved. Slowly. Painfully. They turned, and I saw a face that belonged to centuries past—wrinkled, weathered, eyes that held the weight of a thousand full moons.

"You brought the boy." The Keeper's voice was like stones grinding together. "You should have let him die."

Luna stiffened. "He deserves to know the truth."

"The truth?" The Keeper laughed—a dry, bitter sound. "The truth is that his father damned us all. The truth is that the bloodline he carries will either save this world or destroy it." They stepped toward me, and their eyes locked onto mine. "And the truth is that you have five days, not seven."

My blood went cold. "What?"

"The blood moon ceremony was moved. The Council advanced the date. They want your power before you learn to control it." The Keeper's gaze bore into me. "You don't have time for training, boy. You have time for a single choice."

"What choice?"

The Keeper raised a hand, and the moon pool at their feet rippled. Images formed in the silver surface—a battlefield. Bodies. Wolves fighting wolves. Blood soaking into the earth.

"Your father didn't steal the Wolf King's power," the Keeper said. "He inherited it. The previous king gave it to him willingly. Sacrificed himself so that your father could end a war that had raged for a thousand years."

Luna's breath caught. "Keeper—"

"Hush, girl. He deserves the full truth." The Keeper's eyes never left mine. "Your father didn't die because the Council killed him. He died because he let them. He sacrificed himself to protect you, to hide your bloodline until you were ready. But he made one mistake."

"What mistake?"

"He bound the Wolf King's spirit to you in a way that cannot be undone. If you fail the blood moon ceremony, you don't just lose yourself to the wolf." The Keeper leaned closer, and I smelled death on their breath. "You become the very tyrant your father died to destroy."

The words hung in the air like a curse.

"And there's only one way to prevent it," the Keeper continued. "You must complete the binding ritual. Tonight. Now. Before your bloodline stabilizes."

"Tonight?" I looked at Luna, desperate for guidance. She wouldn't meet my eyes. "What does the ritual involve?"

The Keeper smiled. It was the most terrifying thing I'd ever seen.

"Death. Rebirth. And a choice that will determine the fate of every werewolf alive."

They gestured to the moon pool. "Step into the water. Let the Temple decide your path. Accept the Wolf King's full power, or reject it forever."

"Reject it? I thought I couldn't—"

"You can." The Keeper's voice dropped to a whisper. "You can reject the bloodline. Burn it out of your veins. You'll survive. Weak. Human. Alive. But the war will continue. The Council will hunt every werewolf who defied them. Millions will die."

"And if I accept?"

"Then you become what your father always feared. A king." The Keeper stepped aside, gesturing to the moon pool. "The choice is yours. But choose quickly. The Council's enforcers are already at the temple gates."

I looked at Luna. She finally met my eyes, and I saw tears tracking down her cheeks.

"I'm sorry," she whispered. "I should have told you before I brought you here. But if I had, you never would have come."

"Wouldn't I?"

"You're your father's son." She smiled, broken and beautiful. "You would have run."

I turned back to the moon pool. It glowed, waiting. Patient. Hungry.

Alexander's voice rose in my mind, ancient and powerful. *Step into the water, boy. Claim what is yours.*

*Or I will claim you.*

I took a step forward.

Then another.

The temple gates exploded inward.

Shadows poured through the breach—dozens of them, moving with predatory grace. Their eyes glowed red in the darkness, and I felt their hunger like a physical weight.

"Rogues," Luna hissed, stepping in front of me. "How did they find us?"

The Keeper laughed, but there was no humor in it. "The Council didn't move the blood moon ceremony. I did. I told them where you would be. I've been waiting for this moment for thirty years."

Luna's face went white. "Keeper—"

"I loved your father, girl. Loved him more than life itself." The Keeper turned to me, and their eyes were no longer ancient and wise. They were mad. "But he chose her over me. Chose a human woman over the Temple. Over our bond. So I made a deal with the Council. Your father's death for my resurrection. And now"—they smiled, and their teeth were sharp—"his son's soul for my ascension."

The rogues surged forward.

Luna shifted mid-step, her body tearing and reforming into a massive silver wolf. She met the first wave with fangs and fury, buying me seconds.

Seconds I didn't know what to do with.

The moon pool called to me. Alexander roared in my chest. The Keeper watched, waiting, ancient and patient and utterly insane.

And somewhere in the chaos, I heard Luna scream.

title: The Weight of Bloodlines
content:
Liam didn't take her hand.

He pushed himself up from the stream, water streaming down his torn shirt, and met her glowing eyes with everything he had left. "My father's mate. That means you're—"

"Not your mother." Luna's hand dropped. "Your mother died giving birth to you. I was Alexander's... companion. His second. His guardian of secrets."

"Guardian of secrets." Liam laughed, but it came out wet. Blood still trickled from a gash on his forearm. "Right. Because everyone in my life has been keeping those. Why should you be different?"

"Because I'm the only one who can teach you what Marcus couldn't."

The name hit like a punch. Marcus. Dead because of him. Because Liam had been too slow, too weak, too human. He looked at his hands again. Still normal. Still useless. The mark on his chest had faded to a dull ache, but Alexander's voice lingered at the edge of his thoughts like a wolf circling a wounded deer.

"Marcus said there were enemies everywhere," Liam said. "The Council. The rogues. Anyone who wants the Wolf King's bloodline dead." He forced himself to stand, legs shaking. "Why should I trust you?"

Luna's expression didn't change, but something flickered in her eyes. Pain. Regret. Things she'd carried for decades. "Because I loved your father. Because I watched him die. Because I've been waiting twenty-two years for you to ask the right questions."

"What questions?"

"Who killed Alexander. Why. And what they're willing to do to make sure you never find out."

The forest went quiet. No wind. No insects. Even the stream seemed to hold its breath. Liam felt the weight of her words settle on his shoulders like chains.

"You know who killed him."

"I know who ordered it." Luna stepped closer. Close enough that he could smell her scent again—moonlight and jasmine and something older. Something that whispered of centuries. "But names have power, Liam. And there are ears in these woods that don't belong to wolves."

She glanced over her shoulder. The darkness seemed to pulse. Shift.

"They found us faster than I expected," she said. "We need to move."

"Who found us?"

Instead of answering, Luna grabbed his wrist. Her grip was iron. Unbreakable. Before he could pull away, she dragged him into the trees.

They ran.

Not human running—Luna moved like water, like wind, her feet barely touching the ground. Liam stumbled behind her, branches whipping his face, roots trying to trip him. His lungs screamed. His legs burned. The mark on his chest began to glow again.

*Let me out,* Alexander's voice growled. *I can run faster. Fight harder. Kill them all.*

"No," Liam gasped.

*You're going to get us killed.*

"Shut. Up."

Luna pulled him left, ducking under a fallen log, then right through a thicket of thorns that tore at his clothes. Behind them, he heard it—a howl. Low. Guttural. Not a wolf. Something worse.

"What is that?" he yelled.

"Rogues. But not ordinary ones." Luna's voice was tight. "The Council's hunters. They're trained to track bloodlines. Your awakening lit a beacon that every wolf in the city can see."

"Great. So I'm a walking target."

"You're a walking *threat*. There's a difference."

They burst out of the tree line onto a gravel road. An old pickup truck sat parked in the shadows, rusted and dented, its engine ticking in the cold air. Luna pulled open the passenger door and shoved him inside.

"Buckle up."

"Why? Where are we—"

She slammed the door, jumped into the driver's seat, and floored it before her door was fully closed. The truck fishtailed on the gravel, then shot forward into the darkness. Behind them, three pairs of red eyes emerged from the forest.

Then four.

Then six.

"They're still coming," Liam said, watching the rearview mirror.

"They will be. Until we cross running water."

"Running water?"

Luna took a sharp turn onto a highway, tires screeching. "Old superstition. But it works. Hunters can't cross it in wolf form. They have to go around."

"And we're heading toward running water?"

"We're heading toward the Moonlit Temple. It sits at the convergence of three rivers. The most sacred ground in werewolf history. No hunter can touch you there."

Liam slumped back in his seat. His body felt like it had been put through a blender and then set on fire. Every muscle ached. Every breath tasted like copper. He looked at his hands again and saw the cuts were already closing. Healing. The wolf inside him was working even when he wasn't trying.

"How long until we get there?"

"Two hours. Maybe less if they find a bridge."

"And what happens when we get there?"

Luna was quiet for a long moment. The highway stretched ahead, empty and dark. The moon hung low on the horizon, swollen and silver.

"The Moonlit Temple holds records. Histories. The true account of your father's death." She glanced at him, and for the first time, he saw something fragile behind her eyes. "But it also holds a trial. One that every Wolf King heir must face."

"A trial?"

"To prove you're worthy of the bloodline. To prove you can control the wolf instead of letting it control you."

Liam thought of Alexander's voice. The hunger. The rage bubbling just beneath his skin. "What happens if I fail?"

Luna's fingers tightened on the steering wheel.

"Then the wolf takes over permanently. You become a feral beast. The Council puts you down like a rabid dog." She paused. "It's happened before. To other heirs. Other bloodlines."

"Comforting."

"I'm not here to comfort you, Liam. I'm here to save your life." She looked at him again, and her eyes were glowing brighter now. "But I can only show you the door. You have to walk through it yourself."

The truck ate up miles of dark highway. Liam watched the moon track across the sky, counting the hours in his head. Two hours. One hour. Forty-five minutes. The glowing in his chest had faded to a warm hum, but Alexander was still there. Waiting. Watching.

*She's hiding something,* the voice said.

"You're hiding something," Liam muttered.

"What?"

He blinked. Realized he'd spoken aloud. "Nothing. Just... talking to myself."

Luna's lips pressed into a thin line. "You've heard the voice."

"Alexander. Yeah. He's been chatting nonstop since I woke up."

"And what does he tell you?"

"That I'm weak. That I need to let him out. That I have seven days to master him or he'll master me." Liam rubbed his face. "The usual father-son bonding stuff."

Luna's hands shook on the wheel. Just slightly. Almost invisible. But Liam saw it.

"There's something I didn't tell you," she said. "About the blood moon ceremony."

"More bad news?"

"The ceremony isn't about mastering the wolf." She took a breath. "It's about merging with him. Becoming one being. If you succeed, you gain the full power of the Wolf King. If you fail..."

"I become a vegetable."

"You become a vessel. For Alexander's consciousness. The wolf takes your body, your soul, everything you are."

Liam stared at her. The words didn't make sense. Couldn't make sense. "You're saying the ceremony is a possession?"

"I'm saying the Wolf King bloodline doesn't pass through genetics alone. It passes through conquest. Every Wolf King has to defeat the spirit of the previous king to claim his power." Luna's voice dropped. "But Alexander was stronger than most. He ruled for three hundred years. His will is... formidable."

"So I'm fighting a three-hundred-year-old ghost for control of my own body?"

"Yes."

"And if I lose?"

"You become Alexander. Your consciousness fades. Your memories die. Everyone you know and love becomes strangers." She looked at him, and tears glistened in her eyes. "I've seen it happen. To your grandfather. To your great-grandfather. To every Wolf King who wasn't strong enough."

The truck fell silent. The road curved ahead, and Liam saw lights in the distance. A bridge. Running water. Safety.

But he didn't feel safe.

He felt like a lamb being led to slaughter.

"We're almost there," Luna said. "The Moonlit Temple is just beyond the bridge."

"Good." Liam closed his eyes. "Because I have a lot of questions for the people who wrote those records."

"What kind of questions?"

"The kind about who killed my father. And why they're so desperate to kill me."

Luna didn't answer. The truck rolled onto the bridge, the sound of rushing water filling the cab. Liam watched the river below, silver and endless, and felt something stir in his chest.

*She's lying,* Alexander whispered. *About the ceremony. About everything.*

"Why would she lie?"

*Because she loved me. And love makes people do terrible things.*

Liam opened his eyes. Stared at Luna's profile. The elegant curve of her jaw. The silver hair that seemed to glow in the moonlight.

"Luna," he said. "What aren't you telling me?"

She didn't respond.

"Luna."

The truck stopped. Not at the temple. In the middle of the bridge. Luna's hands were white-knuckled on the wheel, her breathing shallow.

"There's something I need to tell you," she said. "Something I should have told you from the beginning."

"What?"

She turned to face him, and her eyes were full of an ancient sorrow.

"I was there when your father died," she said. "I didn't just watch. I... helped."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

"I'm sorry," Luna whispered. "But I had no choice."

Behind them, headlights appeared. Three sets. Then four. Then a dozen. The hunters had found a bridge.

And Liam was trapped in a truck with the woman who killed his father.

title: The Traitor's Blade
content:
The Temple of the Moonlit Waters rose from the mist like a broken tooth. Crumbling marble pillars wrapped in ivy. A reflecting pool so dark it swallowed starlight. And at its center, a statue of a wolf with eyes that glowed silver even in death.

Luna led him through the ruins without a torch. Her bare feet made no sound on the wet stone. Liam followed, every step sending fresh fire through his cracked ribs.

"The Council has eyes everywhere," she said, not looking back. "But this place is old. Older than their laws. The wards still hold."

"Wards against what?"

She stopped at the pool's edge. The moonlight caught her face—sharp cheekbones, full lips, a scar that ran from her temple to her jaw. He hadn't noticed that scar before. It looked weeks old.

"Against the things that hunt my kind."

"Your kind." Liam lowered himself to the ground, hissing as his back touched cold marble. "You keep saying that. You never explain what it means."

Luna turned. Her eyes caught the light. Glowed. Dimmed. "I am the last daughter of the Silver Moon Coven. Witch. Seer. Your father's mate." She paused. "And the one who killed him."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

Liam's blood went cold. The mark on his chest pulsed. Burned. *Alexander's voice rose from the depths of his mind—rage, grief, something older than both.* "You're lying."

"I'm not."

"You said you were his mate. Mates don't—"

"Don't murder the man they love?" Luna's voice cracked. Just slightly. A hairline fracture in the ancient composure. "They do when he asks them to."

She sat across from him. Cross-legged. Hands open. The posture of someone who expected to be struck.

"Your father was dying, Liam. The Greyfang pack had poisoned him with silver rot. Slow. Painful. It eats the wolf from the inside." She touched her chest. "He had three weeks left. In agony. Losing his mind piece by piece. He asked me to end it before the wolf took him completely."

"And you just—"

"I drove a silver blade through his heart while he kissed me."

The silence stretched. Somewhere in the darkness, water dripped. An owl called. Liam's hands shook. He didn't know if he wanted to hit her, run from her, or scream until his lungs gave out.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I made a promise." Luna met his eyes. "He made me swear I would find you. Train you. Protect you until the blood moon." She smiled. Bitter. Broken. "I've been watching you for twenty-one years, Liam. Every birthday. Every full moon. Every time you blacked out and woke up in strange places, covered in blood you didn't remember spilling."

"That was you?"

"Not me. The wolf." She leaned forward. "Alexander's wolf didn't die when I killed him. It slept. Waited. And every full moon, it reached for you. Tried to pull you into the shift before you were ready."

"I thought I was going insane."

"You were. Close to it." She reached into her pocket and pulled out a small glass vial. Silver liquid swirled inside. "This will help. It's moonwater. Stolen from the Greyfang compound three nights ago."

"You stole from the pack that wants me dead?"

"Stole implies they know it's missing." She tossed him the vial. He caught it. The glass was warm. Hummed against his palm. "Drink it. All of it."

Liam stared at the vial. The liquid pulsed. Alive. Waiting.

"Trust me," Luna said.

"Trust the woman who killed my father?"

"You trust the wolf inside you? The one that's been trying to take over since the moment you were born?"

He didn't answer.

"I didn't think so." She stood. Walked to the edge of the pool. Her reflection rippled in the dark water. "Drink the moonwater, or don't. Either way, the Council will find you by dawn. Marcus bought you a few hours. Maybe a day. But they have trackers. Bloodhounds. Wolves who can smell a king's bloodline from a mile away."

Liam uncorked the vial. The smell hit him first—ozone, wet earth, something floral and sharp. He drank.

The liquid burned going down. Like swallowing molten silver. His vision went white. His bones crackled. The mark on his chest flared so bright he saw it through his closed eyelids.

And then—silence.

The pain didn't vanish. But it receded. Settled. Like a caged animal that had stopped pacing.

"Better?" Luna asked.

He opened his eyes. The world looked different. Sharper. He could see individual drops of condensation on the leaves twenty feet away. He heard Luna's heartbeat. Steady. Slow. A lie hidden beneath every pulse.

"Yes."

"Good. Because we have a problem."

She pointed at the sky. Liam looked up. The moon was full, yes, but something was wrong. A shadow crept across its edge. Not an eclipse. Something darker. Living.

"The blood moon isn't in seven days," Luna said. "It's tonight. The ceremony was moved."

"What? How is that possible?"

"The Council found a way. They've been draining captured wolves to accelerate the lunar cycle. Every death brings the blood moon closer." She turned to him. "You have until sunrise, Liam. If you don't master the shift before then, the wolf takes over permanently. You become a mindless beast. The Council claims your bloodline. And the war—" She stopped. Swallowed. "The war ends before it begins."

Liam stood. His legs held. His hands steadied. "Teach me."

"There's no time to teach you properly. The normal process takes months, years. You need a shortcut."

"I don't care if it kills me."

"It might." She walked to the statue of the wolf. Pressed her hand against its chest. Something clicked. The ground beneath them rumbled. The reflecting pool drained. Water spiraled into a hole that hadn't been there before. Stone steps descended into darkness.

"The Pool of Echoes," Luna said. "Beneath the temple. It shows you your wolf. Your true form. The shape your soul takes when the mask of humanity falls away."

"And then?"

"And then you step into the pool. You fight your wolf. You dominate it, or it dominates you." She looked at him. "Only one person has ever survived the trial. Your father."

"Great. Family tradition." Liam walked to the edge of the hole. Cold air rose from below. It smelled of wet stone and something metallic. Blood.

"One more thing," Luna said. "The pool shows you the truth. All of it. Things you don't want to see. Things I don't want you to see."

"What things?"

She didn't answer.

Liam stepped onto the first stair. The stone was slick. Uneven. Behind him, the hole sealed shut. Above, the moon continued to darken.

The stairs wound downward for what felt like miles. The air grew thick. Heavy. The walls pulsed with a faint light—bioluminescent moss that glowed silver and blue.

He reached the bottom.

A cavern opened before him. Cathedral-like. Massive pillars of rock supported a ceiling he couldn't see. And at the center, a pool of liquid silver. It rippled without wind. Glowed without light.

A voice spoke. Not Alexander's. Not his own. Something older. The temple itself.

*Step forward, heir of the Wolf King.*

Liam walked to the pool's edge. His reflection stared back at him. Normal. Human. Frightened.

*Your wolf awaits.*

He stepped in.

The silver didn't splash. It swallowed him whole. Cold. Crushing. He sank through light that wasn't light, through darkness that wasn't dark. His lungs burned. His heart stopped.

Then he hit the bottom.

He stood in a forest. But not the one outside. This forest was wrong. The trees grew upside down, roots reaching for a sky that glowed like blood. The ground was made of bones. Thousands of them. Crunching beneath his feet.

And in the clearing ahead, a wolf waited.

It was enormous. The size of a horse. Black fur matted with silver. Eyes like twin moons. Teeth like daggers. It watched him with intelligence. Recognition.

*You came,* the wolf said. Alexander's voice. But deeper. Wilder. *I didn't think you had the courage.*

"Where is this place?"

*Inside you. The prison I've been building for twenty-one years.* The wolf circled him. Its paws left no prints on the bone ground. *You think you can master me? Little boy who's never taken a life?*

"I took Marcus's."

*Marcus gave his life. There's a difference.* The wolf stopped. Its head lowered. *The first life you take in rage—that's the one that matters. The one that changes you.*

"Then I'll take yours."

The wolf laughed. A sound like grinding stone. *Bold. Stupid. But bold.*

It attacked.

Liam didn't think. He moved. His body responded faster than it ever had. He dodged left, felt teeth graze his arm, rolled, came up with a bone in his hand. A femur. Sharpened at one end.

The wolf circled back. Blood dripped from its jaws. His blood.

*Good reflexes. Alexander's reflexes.*

"I'm not my father."

*No. You're worse.* The wolf lunged again.

This time Liam didn't dodge. He stepped into the attack. Drove the bone shard into the wolf's chest. It sank deep. The wolf howled. Silver light poured from the wound.

But it didn't stop. It kept coming. Teeth closed around Liam's shoulder. Crushed down. He felt bone splinter. Felt his own scream tear from his throat.

They crashed to the ground. Rolling. Biting. Clawing. The bone forest watched. The upside-down trees swayed.

Liam's vision blurred. His strength faded. The wolf's jaws tightened.

*I've been waiting for this moment,* it said. *Thirty years, waiting. And now—*

"Then you've waited long enough."

A new voice. Female. Familiar.

Luna stepped out of the shadows. But not the Luna from above. This one was younger. Fiercer. Her eyes glowed white. Silver markings covered her arms. In her hand, a blade of pure moonlight.

"You can't be here," Liam gasped.

"I'm not." She raised the blade. "This is a memory. A message I planted in the moonwater before you drank it."

"Message for what?"

"For when you needed to know the truth." She looked at the wolf. It released Liam. Backed away. Whimpered. "You were right to be angry, Alexander. I broke my promise. I let them take you. I let them poison you. But I kept the other promise."

She turned to Liam. Her eyes held his.

"Your father didn't die because I killed him," she said. "He died because the Council poisoned him three years before you were born. They knew the Wolf King bloodline would pass to you. They wanted Alexander weak. Broken. So when you awakened, you would be alone."

"Then why—"

"The silver rot was in his blood. Incurable. But the Council didn't account for one thing." She touched her stomach. "He had a son. A son who would inherit the mark. A son who could be trained."

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Temple's Secret

Luna's hand remained extended. Water rushed past Liam's broken body, cold and indifferent.

"You were his mate?" The words came out cracked. "You're a ghost. I watched you disappear."

"Not disappear." She lowered her hand, but her eyes never left his. "I phased. Different skill set."

He laughed. It hurt. Everything hurt. "Right. Because tonight needed more impossible things."

"Get up, Liam. We have maybe ten minutes before the greycloaks track you here."

"Greycloaks?"

"The Council's hunters. They don't miss. Ever."

He forced himself upright. Every muscle screamed. His ribs felt wrong—shifting when he breathed. The mark on his chest had faded to a dull ache, but he could still feel it pulsing. Alive. Waiting.

Luna watched him struggle. Didn't help. Didn't offer comfort. Just waited.

"Why should I trust you?" He stood, swaying. "You knew my father. You knew what I was. You showed up in that alley and now Marcus is dead and I'm running from people who want to rip my bloodline out of my veins."

"Marcus chose to die." Her voice carried no apology. "He knew the cost when he took you from the temple eighteen years ago."

"The temple?"

"The Moonlit Temple. Where you were born. Where the Wolf King's heir is supposed to be raised, trained, protected." She turned and walked toward the treeline. "Where the Council found your father and murdered him for refusing to bow."

Liam followed. He didn't want to. His legs moved anyway.

"Marcus told me the Council wanted the bloodline destroyed."

"He told you half the truth." She pushed through a cluster of ferns. The forest opened into a narrow game trail. "The Council wants the bloodline controlled. Your father wouldn't submit. They killed him for it. But killing a Wolf King's heir? That's different. The bloodline doesn't die with the body. It just... moves. Finds another host. Usually a descendant. Sometimes a stranger. The blood remembers, and it finds a way."

"So they want to lock me up. Not kill me."

"They want to bind you. Take the Wolf King's power and chain it to their will. Use you as a weapon." She glanced back. "That's worse than death, Liam. Trust me."

They walked in silence for ten minutes. Twenty. The forest changed around them—trees growing older, thicker. Moss hung from branches like gray curtains. The moonlight barely penetrated.

Liam's body healed as they moved. He felt it happening. Cuts knitting closed. Bruises fading. His ribs realigned with a wet pop that made him gasp.

"The healing," Luna said without turning. "First sign of the bloodline stabilizing. By morning you'll be stronger than you were yesterday. By tomorrow night, faster. The transformation happens whether you accept it or not."

"I don't want it."

"Doesn't matter."

He stopped walking. "I said I don't want it. I didn't ask for any of this. I was fine. I had a life. A shitty apartment. A job I hated. Friends who didn't know I turned into a monster once a month. But it was mine."

Luna stopped. Turned. Her silver eyes caught what little light existed.

"You think I wanted this?" Her voice dropped. Cold. Sharp. "You think I chose to be bonded to a man destined to die before his son turned twenty-one? I loved Alexander. I watched them tear him apart in the temple courtyard. I held his heart in my hands while it still beat. And then I carried you—a screaming infant with your father's blood still wet on my skin—to Marcus and told him to run."

She stepped closer. Close enough that Liam could smell her. Moonlight and something older. Something that burned.

"I didn't choose this life. None of us did. But it chose us. And if you don't fight, Liam, you'll end up like your father. Dead in the dirt while the Council carves up your legacy and feeds it to their pet alphas."

Liam's hands curled into fists. "You could have warned me. You could have told me before Marcus died."

"He wouldn't let me. Said you needed to find out on your own. That the bloodline would awaken when it was ready, and forcing it would break you." Her eyes softened. "Marcus loved you. Like a son. He died protecting that love."

The anger drained out of him. Left him hollow. Tired.

"Where is this temple?"

"We're standing in it."

He looked around. Trees. Moss. Darkness. Nothing.

"We've been inside the temple grounds for the last mile," Luna said. "The Moonlit Temple isn't a building. It's a place. A convergence of ley lines and lunar energy. It exists in the space between worlds."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only answer you'll get until you can see it."

She walked to a massive oak tree—easily a thousand years old. Its roots twisted above ground like frozen rivers of wood. She placed her palm flat against the bark.

The tree shimmered.

The air rippled. The moss on the branches glowed silver, and suddenly the forest wasn't a forest anymore. They stood in a clearing. Stone arches rose from the earth, covered in ancient carvings. A central altar stood beneath an opening in the canopy—a perfect circle where the moon's light poured through like a spotlight.

And around the clearing, figures. Dozens of them. Wolves and humans, sitting in a loose circle. Their eyes glowed. Silver. Gold. Amber. Watching him.

"They've been waiting," Luna said. "Eighteen years. Waiting for Alexander's son to return."

A woman stood from the circle. Tall. Dark skin. White hair cropped close to her scalp. Scars covered her arms like a map of old battles. She walked toward Liam with the confidence of someone who'd never been afraid of anything.

"So." Her voice was gravel and thunder. "The cub returns."

Liam's wolf stirred. Not hostile. Curious. Like recognizing like.

"Who are you?"

The woman smiled. It didn't reach her eyes.

"I'm the one who trained your father to kill," she said. "And if you survive the next seven days, I'll train you too."

She extended her hand. Liam saw the mark on her palm—the same crescent moon and wolf's head that burned on his chest.

"Welcome home, Wolf King. The war you were born for started eighteen years ago. You're just now arriving for the battle."

Liam took her hand. Her grip crushed his fingers.

"I don't even know your name."

"Kira." She released him. "And if you embarrass me in front of my pack, I'll kill you myself before the Council gets the chance."

"Kira." Luna stepped between them. "He's been awake for less than twelve hours. He nearly died tonight. Give him a moment."

"He doesn't have a moment. None of us do." Kira turned and walked back toward the circle. "The Council's greycloaks are already tracking him. They'll be at the temple boundary before sunrise. We have six hours to teach him how to fight, or we stash him in a hole and hope they don't sniff him out."

"I can't learn to fight in six hours."

Kira stopped. Turned. Her eyes caught the moonlight and burned gold.

"Then you'll learn in five."

She gestured to the circle. The watchers parted, revealing a space in the center. A wolf sat there. Massive. Black fur matted with blood. Its eyes were pale blue—almost white. It watched Liam with predatory stillness.

"That's Grey," Kira said. "He's your father's wolf."

Liam's blood went cold. "My father's... that's Alexander's wolf?"

"Wolves don't die when their masters do," Kira said. "They wait. They mourn. And when the heir returns, they challenge him for dominance." She smiled. "Grey wants to see if you're worth following. Or if he should tear out your throat and let the bloodline die with you."

The wolf stood. Muscles rippled under matted fur. It was twice the size of any natural wolf. Its paws left divots in the stone.

Luna grabbed his arm. "Don't. You're not ready."

"Six hours," Liam said. "You said I had six hours."

"Liam—"

Grey growled. The sound vibrated through the ground, up Liam's legs, into his chest. His wolf answered. Rising. Demanding.

"One challenge," Liam said, pulling free of Luna's grip. "One fight. If I win, I'm worth training. If I lose..."

"You won't lose." Kira's voice carried absolute certainty. "Alexander's blood doesn't break. It bends. It adapts. It wins."

Liam stepped into the circle. The watchers closed ranks behind him. Grey circled, massive body low, pale eyes locked on his throat.

The wolf lunged.

Liam's body moved before his brain caught up. He dropped, rolled, felt claws rake across his back. Pain exploded. He scrambled up, blood running down his spine.

Grey turned. Fast. Too fast. The wolf was on him again, jaws closing around his forearm. Teeth sank deep. Bone grated.

Liam screamed. Punched with his free hand. Connected with the wolf's skull. Grey didn't flinch.

*You think you can beat him with fists?* Alexander's voice echoed. *You're not human right now. Stop fighting like one.*

The pain in his arm shifted. Something burned. Not the wound—the mark on his chest. It flared hot, and suddenly Liam understood.

He stopped fighting the wolf.

He became it.

His body shifted. Bones cracked. Muscles tore and reformed. His scream turned into a howl as fur exploded across his skin, as his jaw elongated, as his spine curved and twisted.

When the change finished, he stood on four legs.

Grey released his arm and stepped back.

Two wolves faced each other in the moonlight. One ancient. One newborn. Both wearing the mark of the Wolf King.

Grey lowered his head.

The circle erupted in howls.

Liam stood in his wolf form, chest heaving, blood dripping from a dozen wounds, watching his father's wolf submit.

And from the treeline, five figures in gray cloaks watched the ceremony unfold.

The lead greycloak raised a silver amulet to his lips.

"The heir has returned to the temple. Requesting immediate extraction."

A voice crackled back. "Confirmed. Bound or kill?"

The greycloak watched Liam's wolf lift its head and howl at the moon.

"Kill."

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Temple's Secret

The ground shook.
