Liam felt it through his bones before he heard it—a deep, rhythmic pounding like a giant's heartbeat rising from beneath the earth. He pressed his palm flat against the stone floor of the Moonlit Temple's inner sanctum. The symbols carved into the obsidian walls pulsed with silver light, each pulse matching the tremor.

"What is that?" he asked.

Luna stood at the center of the chamber, her silver hair floating as if underwater. She'd been chanting for hours, words in a language that made Liam's teeth ache. Now she fell silent. Her eyes—those impossible moonlit eyes—fixed on something behind him.

"The temple is waking," she said.

"Temples don't wake."

"This one does." She walked past him, her bare feet leaving no sound on the stone. "It's been sleeping for thirty years. Since your father died. Waiting for someone worthy to enter the inner sanctum."

Liam looked around the chamber. It was smaller than he'd expected. A circular room maybe twenty feet across, walls covered in carvings that told stories he couldn't read. At the center, a pedestal made of what looked like frozen moonlight. On top of it, a dagger.

The blade was black. Not painted black. Not forged black. Black like the space between stars. It seemed to drink the light around it.

"Alexander's dagger," Luna said. "He used it to bind the Wolf King's spirit when the bloodline first awakened in him."

"Bind it?"

"The Wolf King isn't just power, Liam. It's a consciousness. An ancient one. When it first awakens in a new host, it tries to take over completely. Alexander learned to master it by creating that dagger. He poured half his blood into the forging. It became an anchor. Something that could hold the Wolf King back when necessary."

Liam remembered the voice. Alexander's voice. Ancient and patient and hungry. He remembered how it had felt when the pain receded—like a predator deciding it wasn't hungry enough to bother with prey yet.

"Why didn't you tell me about it before?"

"Because I didn't know if you'd survive the ceremony." Luna picked up the dagger. It seemed to hum in her hand. "The dagger only works for bloodline carriers. If you weren't truly Alexander's son, touching it would kill you."

"And if I am?"

"Then it will give you control." She turned to face him. "Control of the Wolf King. Control of your transformation. Control of the power that's been tearing you apart since the full moon."

Liam took a step forward. Then stopped.

"The Council wants this, don't they?"

Luna's expression didn't change, but something flickered in her eyes. "What do you mean?"

"Marcus told me before he died. The Council's been hunting down Alexander's bloodline for thirty years. They killed everyone who might have been his heir. But they didn't just want to kill me—they wanted the temple. They wanted whatever Alexander left behind."

"The Council doesn't know about the dagger."

"No?" Liam crossed his arms. "Then why did they let you go? You were one of them. You were at the compound. You had a desk. Files. You were part of it."

"I was a prisoner."

"You walked around freely. You accessed their files. You found me." He shook his head. "I've been running for three days. I've watched people die. I've felt my own bones break and reform. I'm done trusting people who won't tell me the whole truth."

Luna stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed. Not a mocking laugh. A sad one.

"You're more like him than you know," she said. "Alexander never trusted anyone either. Not even me. Not at first."

She sat down on the stone floor, the dagger resting across her lap. The tremors had stopped. The symbols on the walls dimmed to a faint glow.

"The Council is divided," she said. "Has been for decades. There's the old guard—the ones who want to keep the old ways. The blood ceremonies. The pack hierarchies. The war with the rogues. And there's the new guard—the ones who want to modernize, to integrate with humans, to end the secrecy."

"And which side are you on?"

"Neither." She met his eyes. "I'm on the side that wants to stop the Blood Moon ceremony from happening at all."

Liam felt his blood go cold. "What?"

"The Blood Moon ceremony doesn't just awaken the Wolf King. It creates him. The ritual itself is what binds the ancient spirit to a new host. Alexander went through it. So did his father. And his father before him. Every Wolf King for a thousand years was created by that ceremony."

"Then how do I stop it?"

"You don't." Luna's voice was barely a whisper. "The ceremony will happen whether you participate or not. The Blood Moon calls to the Wolf King. It doesn't need your consent. It doesn't need you to show up. If you're alive when the Blood Moon rises, the transformation will happen. And without the dagger to anchor you, the Wolf King will consume you completely."

Liam processed this. Seven days. He'd thought he had seven days to learn control. But what Luna was telling him was that he had seven days to find a way to stop the moon itself.

"There has to be another way."

"There is." Luna held up the dagger. "This isn't just an anchor. It's a key. Alexander designed it so that the Wolf King's spirit could be transferred. Passed from one host to another without the ceremony."

"Transferred to who?"

"Anyone with werewolf blood. The dagger draws the Wolf King out of one host and into the blade. Then it can be placed into another. Alexander was going to use it to give up the Wolf King willingly. To choose his successor instead of letting the ceremony choose."

"Why didn't he?"

Luna looked away. "Because someone killed him before he could."

The silence stretched between them. Liam could hear water dripping somewhere in the depths of the temple. Could hear his own heartbeat. Could hear the faint whisper of Alexander's voice at the edge of his consciousness, listening.

"You," Liam said. "You killed him."

Her head snapped up. "No. Never. I loved him."

"Then who?"

"The Council found out about the dagger. They sent an assassin. One of their best." Her voice cracked. "I was supposed to protect him. I was his mate. His guard. His everything. And I failed."

Liam didn't move. Didn't speak. He just waited.

"The assassin used silver-coated claws. Drove them straight through Alexander's heart. The dagger was in his hand when he fell. It absorbed his spirit—trapped the Wolf King inside the blade before it could return to the cycle."

"So the Wolf King has been in that dagger for thirty years?"

"Yes. And when Alexander's bloodline awakened in you, the dagger began to call to you. That's why you felt the pull toward the temple. That's why you survived when the Council's hunters should have killed you a dozen times."

Liam walked to the pedestal. The dagger seemed to pulse with its own heartbeat. He reached out a hand.

"Wait." Luna stood. "If you touch it, the Wolf King will know you're here. It will try to bond with you. The dagger is an anchor, but it's also a lure. It wants to be held. It wants to complete the connection."

"How do I use it to stop the ceremony?"

"You don't use it to stop the ceremony. You use it to redirect it." Luna took a breath. "The Blood Moon rises in seven days. When it does, the Wolf King will try to enter you. You can either fight it alone and lose, or you can use the dagger to guide the Wolf King into a different host."

"Different host? Who?"

Luna's eyes met his. They were filled with tears. And something else. Something that looked like hope.

"Me."

The word hit Liam like a physical blow.

"You want me to put the Wolf King into you?"

"I was Alexander's mate. I have his blood in my veins from decades of bonding. I can survive the transfer. And once the Wolf King is inside me, I can keep it contained. The Council won't be able to perform the ceremony without a new host. They'll have to wait another thirty years."

"You'll become the Wolf King."

"No." She smiled. "I'll become its prison. The dagger can hold it, but a living host is better. Stronger. I can keep it trapped inside me until the day I die."

Liam shook his head. "That's insane. You're talking about condemning yourself to a lifetime of fighting an ancient spirit for control of your own body."

"It's not a lifetime." Luna touched his face. "Werewolves age slowly. But we still age. I have maybe fifty years left. Fifty years of keeping the Wolf King contained. And then the cycle resets. The next Wolf King will be born somewhere, and the search begins again."

"There has to be another way."

"There isn't." She took his hand. "Listen to me. The Council has a hunter named Viktor. He's the one who killed Alexander. He's been tracking you since the full moon. He's better than anyone you've faced. He doesn't want you alive—he wants your blood. He wants to use it to find the dagger. And if he finds the dagger, he'll use it to control the Wolf King himself."

"Then we destroy it."

"We can't. The dagger is forged from Alexander's blood and moonlight. It's indestructible. The only way to neutralize it is to place it inside a living vessel."

Liam stared at the dagger. At the woman who had been his father's mate. At the impossible choice she was giving him.

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because I loved your father. Because I've spent thirty years protecting his legacy. Because I killed the assassin who came for you three nights ago, before she could slit your throat in your sleep."

Liam's blood went cold. "What?"

"The woman in the alley. The one I saved you from. She wasn't just a Council hunter. She was Viktor's daughter. She would have killed you and taken your blood to him. I stopped her."

"And I'm just supposed to believe that?"

Luna reached into her pocket and pulled out a silver chain. On it hung a ring. A simple band of white gold with a crescent moon carved into it.

"Alexander gave me this the night before he died," she said. "He told me that if anything happened to him, I should find his heir and protect them until they were ready. He made me promise."

She held out the ring. Liam took it. It was warm. Alive. It hummed with the same energy as the dagger.

"I've kept my promise for thirty years," Luna said. "I plan to keep it a little longer."

Liam looked at the ring. At the woman. At the dagger.

Seven days.

"Show me how to use it," he said.

Luna's smile was sad. Ancient. Familiar.

"First," she said, "you need to learn what the dagger truly is."

She took his hand and pressed it against the black blade.

The world exploded into silver light.

---

*He stood in a field of moonlight. Endless. Silent. A figure waited for him at the center—a man with silver eyes and a face that could have been his own reflection.*

*Liam. You found the dagger.*

*Alexander. His father. The Wolf King.*

*You have questions.*

Liam's mind raced. A thousand questions. A thousand accusations. But only one mattered.

"Did Luna kill you?"

*Alexander's laugh echoed across the silver field. No

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Traitor's Blade

The tunnel collapsed behind them.

Liam felt the shockwave through his boots, heard the grinding scream of stone on stone, watched dust billow past them like the breath of some buried beast. He didn't stop running. Couldn't. The white-robed wolves had been three steps behind when Luna triggered the collapse, and now the only thing separating them from the Council's killers was thirty feet of crushed granite.

His chest burned. His legs screamed. The mark over his heart pulsed with every heartbeat, hot and demanding, like Alexander was trying to claw his way out through Liam's ribs.

*Faster,* the voice growled. *They're digging through.*

"How do you know that?" Liam gasped.

*I can hear them. Twenty-three wolves. Six feet of rubble. Seven minutes before they break through.*

Seven minutes. Liam had spent the last three hours running from a dozen different packs, each one hungrier than the last. The Moonlit Temple had been a sanctuary for exactly four hours—long enough for Luna to show him the underground passages, the hidden armory, the room where his father had trained.

Long enough for the Council to find them.

"Left here," Luna said, grabbing his arm and yanking him through a doorway. She moved like water, silent and fluid, her silver hair plastered to her face with sweat and tunnel dust. She'd killed three wolves in the escape. Liam had seen her do it—watched her palm expand into claws mid-strike, watched her rip out throats with the same hands that had bandaged his wounds an hour earlier.

She was beautiful. Terrifying. And she'd been Alexander's mate.

"My father's mate?" Liam had asked, back in the temple, when the shock of her confession had finally worn off.

"Your mother died giving birth to you," Luna had said, her voice flat. Controlled. "Alexander mourned her for two years before he came to me. And even then... I was always second. The moon goddess's daughter. A consolation prize for a king who'd lost his queen."

Liam hadn't known what to say to that. He still didn't.

They burst out of the tunnel into a wide chamber, and Liam stopped so fast his boots skidded on the stone floor.

The room was circular, maybe fifty feet across, with a ceiling that vanished into darkness above. In the center stood a stone altar, carved with the same crescent-and-wolf symbol that burned on his chest. Around it, etched into the floor, was a circle of ancient runes that pulsed with faint silver light.

"The heart of the temple," Luna said, breathing hard. "The place where Alexander performed the Blood Moon ceremony. The place where he—" She stopped. Her eyes went wide.

"What?"

"He didn't tell me everything." She walked toward the altar, her movements slow, reverent. "He said there were secrets buried here. Things he couldn't share until the heir was ready."

Liam followed her, the mark on his chest pulsing faster now, like a second heartbeat. *Something's wrong,* Alexander whispered. *I can feel it. There's a presence in this room. Something old. Something hungry.*

"What kind of secrets?" Liam asked.

Luna reached the altar. Ran her fingers over the carvings. "The kind that get people killed."

A sound echoed through the chamber. Low. Rhythmic. Like breathing.

Liam spun, claws extending from his fingers without conscious thought—full shift, partial shift, he didn't know anymore. The lines between human and wolf had blurred over the past twelve hours. He was something else now. Something between.

The breathing came from everywhere. Nowhere. From the walls themselves.

"Luna." His voice came out deeper than normal, layered with growl. "Tell me that's the Council."

"It's not."

"Then what is it?"

She turned to face him, and for the first time since he'd met her, Luna looked afraid. "I don't know."

The runes on the floor flared to life. Silver light erupted from the circle, bright enough to blind, hot enough to make Liam stagger backward. The mark on his chest answered, burning through his shirt, sending tendrils of fire through his veins.

*No,* Alexander snarled. *No, no, no. This wasn't supposed to happen. The seal wasn't supposed to break until the ceremony.*

"Seal?" Liam shouted. "What seal?"

"The one Alexander put on me," said a voice that wasn't Alexander's.

The light coalesced in the center of the circle. Took shape. A woman, tall and pale, with hair like spun moonlight and eyes like twin stars. She wore a dress of shadow and silver, and when she smiled, Liam felt his blood run cold.

"Mother," Luna whispered.

The goddess turned her gaze on Luna, and her smile widened. "Daughter. You've grown." Her eyes slid to Liam. "And you've brought me the heir. How thoughtful."

Liam's claws extended fully. His bones cracked, shifted, prepared for a fight he knew he couldn't win. "What do you want?"

The moon goddess laughed. It sounded like wind chimes and breaking glass. "What do I want? I want what I've always wanted, boy. My throne. My temple. My place among the old gods." She gestured at the runes around her feet. "Alexander stole all of that when he bound me to this circle. Thirty years I've waited. Thirty years, trapped in the space between worlds, watching my daughter serve the bloodline that imprisoned me."

"You told me Alexander freed you," Liam said, looking at Luna.

Luna's face had gone white. "He did. He told me he released her. That she was gone, that the temple was safe, that—"

"He lied," the goddess said. "He bound me here with his dying breath. Poured every ounce of his Wolf King power into a seal that would hold until his heir completed the Blood Moon ceremony." Her eyes locked onto Liam's. "But the seal has a weakness. It requires the heir to come willingly to this chamber. To stand within the circle. To be close enough for me to—"

She moved.

Liam didn't see her cross the distance. One moment she was in the center of the circle, the next she was inches from him, her hand pressed against his chest, right over the mark.

"Touch you," she finished.

Power exploded through him. Not his power—*hers*. Cold and ancient and infinite, flooding his veins like liquid nitrogen, freezing the wolf inside him into submission. Alexander roared in his skull, fought against the intrusion, but the goddess was stronger. She'd been waiting thirty years.

"Don't hurt him," Luna screamed, lunging forward.

The goddess flicked her wrist. Luna flew backward, slammed into the wall, crumpled to the ground.

"She's alive," the goddess said, not looking away from Liam. "She's my daughter. I have no desire to harm her." Her fingers dug into the mark on his chest. "But you, little heir. You carry the blood of the man who imprisoned me. You carry his power. His legacy." She leaned closer, her breath cold on his face. "And soon, you'll carry my child."

Liam's blood turned to ice. "What?"

"The moon goddess needs an avatar. A vessel to walk the mortal world. Alexander was meant to be that vessel—I chose him, blessed him, made him king. But he betrayed me." Her smile turned cruel. "You won't. The Wolf King's power, combined with my bloodline, will create a child unlike any this world has ever seen. A god born in flesh. A ruler of wolves and men."

"I'll kill myself first," Liam snarled.

"You won't. Because if you do, your father's soul dies with you. And I know you've already spoken to him. I know you've felt his presence. You won't sacrifice him, Liam. You're not that strong."

She was right. He hated her for it.

The goddess released his chest, stepped back, and clapped her hands. The runes flared brighter, and the walls of the chamber began to dissolve, revealing a massive cavern beyond. An army waited there—hundreds of white-robed wolves, their eyes glowing silver, their bodies still as statues.

"The Council of Elders," the goddess said, gesturing. "My most faithful servants. They've been preparing for this day since Alexander sealed me away. And now, with you as my vessel, we will finally bring the moon goddess's reign to the mortal world."

Liam looked at the army. Looked at Luna, unconscious against the wall. Felt Alexander's presence inside him, weak and fading, the goddess's power crushing him into silence.

Seven days. He'd had seven days to master the Wolf King's power.

He'd wasted four of them running.

Now he had three days until the Blood Moon ceremony. Three days to find a way to defeat a goddess. Three days to save his father's soul. Three days to keep himself from becoming the father of an apocalypse.

The goddess turned to face her army, arms spread wide.

"Tonight, we march on the human cities. Tonight, the old world dies. And in three days, when the Blood Moon rises, a new god will be born." She looked back at Liam, her eyes burning with ancient malice. "And he will call me mother."

Liam's chains of ice shattered.

He hit the ground, gasping, the mark on his chest blazing so bright it lit the entire cavern. Alexander's voice returned, louder than before, raging against the goddess's power.

*Fight,* the Wolf King roared. *Fight, boy. I didn't train for thirty years to watch my son become a puppet.*

Liam forced himself to his knees. Met the goddess's gaze.

"I'm not your vessel," he said, his voice raw. "I'm not your puppet. And I'm sure as hell not going to be the father of your monster."

The goddess laughed. "And what will you do about it, little heir? You can't defeat me. You can't escape. The temple is surrounded. The army is mine. You have no allies, no power, no—"

"You're wrong."

The voice came from behind Liam. Weak. Broken. But steady.

Luna.

She stood on shaky legs, blood running from a cut on her forehead, her silver hair tangled and wild. But her eyes burned with something Liam hadn't seen before.

Determination.

"I'm your daughter," Luna said, stepping forward. "I have your blood. Your power. And I know your weakness."

The goddess's smile faltered. "You wouldn't."

"Watch me."

Luna raised her hand, and the runes on the floor began to change.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Price of Power

The Temple of the Moonlit Veil rose from the mountain like a skeletal hand clawing at the sky. Broken pillars. Collapsed arches. Vines thick as my arms crawled over walls that had once gleamed white. Now they were gray. Dead. Like everything the Council touched.

Luna walked ahead of me, her bare feet silent on the cracked stone path. She hadn't spoken since we left the stream. Three hours of climbing. Three hours of my body screaming at me to stop. Three hours of Alexander pacing in the back of my skull like a caged animal.

*She's hiding something.*

I pushed his voice down. "You said this place was safe."

Luna stopped. Turned. The moonlight caught her silver hair, made it look like liquid mercury. "I said it was hidden. There's a difference."

"Comforting."

She almost smiled. Almost. "The Temple was built by the first Wolf King. Before the clans. Before the Council. Before the bloodlines were corrupted by politics and power." She touched a pillar. Her fingers traced symbols I couldn't read. "Your father brought me here. After we... after we bonded."

Jealousy. Hot and irrational. I crushed it. "He's dead, Luna. The Council killed him."

"I know." Her voice cracked. Just slightly. Like ice before it breaks. "I was there."

The words hit me like a punch to the chest. "You watched?"

"I tried to save him." She turned away. "I failed."

Silence stretched between us. The wind howled through the ruins. Somewhere in the darkness, an owl called. Or a wolf. I couldn't tell anymore.

"The ceremony," I said. "The Blood Moon. What happens if I don't master the wolf?"

Luna's shoulders tensed. "You know what happens."

"I want to hear you say it."

She turned back. Her eyes glowed brighter now. Angrier. "The wolf consumes you. Your personality. Your memories. Your soul. Alexander becomes the dominant consciousness. You become a passenger in your own body. Forever."

The mark on my chest burned. I pressed my palm against it. "And if I do master him?"

"Then you become the Wolf King. The rarest bloodline in existence. The only one who can challenge the Council and win." She stepped closer. Her scent hit me—moonlight and jasmine and something darker underneath. "But power has a price, Liam. Everything does."

"What price?"

She reached out. Touched my cheek. Her fingers were cold. "The Wolf King can't have a mate. Can't have children. Can't pass on his bloodline. That's the deal. Power in exchange for legacy."

Bullshit. "Who made that deal?"

"The first Wolf King. He bound the bloodline to the moon itself. The power flows through you, but you can't share it. Can't spread it. The line dies with you."

I grabbed her wrist. Pulled her hand away. "Then why did my father have me?"

Something flickered in her eyes. Pain. Guilt. Fear. "Because I begged him to."

The ground shifted beneath me. Not literally. But the world tilted, and I had to steady myself against a broken pillar. "You convinced him to break the deal?"

"I convinced him to try." She pulled away. Wrapped her arms around herself. "The first Wolf King was a coward. He made the deal because he was afraid of his own power. Afraid of what he could become. But your father—" Her voice broke. "Your father believed the bloodline could be changed. That love was stronger than the moon's curse."

"And was it?"

She didn't answer.

*She's lying,* Alexander growled. *She always lies.*

I ignored him. "Why did you bring me here, Luna? Really?"

She walked to the center of the ruined temple. A circular platform. Carved with the same symbols as the pillar. The same crescent moon and wolf's head that marked my chest. "Because there's another way. A way to master the wolf without becoming a king."

"What way?"

She turned. Faced me. The moonlight hit her full-on, and for a moment she looked like a ghost. Like something from a dream I couldn't wake from. "You give the power to someone else. A willing vessel. Someone who can carry the bloodline so you don't have to."

"Give it to who?"

She stepped closer. Her hand found mine. Squeezed. "Me."

I pulled back. "No."

"Liam—"

"You heard me. No." I paced the platform. My blood burned. My skin crawled. "You want me to give you my father's power? The power he died for? The power Marcus gave his life to protect?"

"I want to save you."

"Bullshit. You want to save yourself."

She flinched. Like I'd slapped her. "You don't know what you're talking about."

"Then explain it to me." I stopped. Faced her. "Explain why you watched my father die. Explain why you waited twenty-one years to find me. Explain why you're really here, Luna. No more riddles. No more half-truths. The full story."

The wind died. The world went silent. Even the insects stopped singing.

Luna's eyes glowed brighter. Brighter than the moon. Brighter than anything I'd ever seen. "Your father didn't die because the Council killed him. He died because he chose to."

"What?"

"He had the power to defeat them. He was stronger than all of them combined. But if he used that power, the curse would consume him. He'd become the Wolf King. The true Wolf King. And the first thing the Wolf King does is kill his mate."

My blood went cold. "Kill his mate?"

"The curse demands it. The Wolf King can't have a mate. Can't love. Can't bond. So the moon takes that love and twists it into rage. The only way to break the bond is death." Tears streamed down her face. Silver tears. Like liquid moonlight. "Your father looked at me. He looked at the Council. And he made a choice. He let them kill him rather than become the monster that would kill me."

I couldn't breathe. Couldn't think. My father chose death. Chose to abandon me. Abandon the bloodline. All for love.

"Now you understand," Luna whispered. "Why I can't let you become the Wolf King. Why I'll do anything to stop it. I watched the man I love die to protect me. I won't watch his son become the thing he feared most."

*She's lying,* Alexander said again. But this time his voice was different. Softer. Almost sad. *She's telling the truth.*

I fell to my knees. The mark on my chest burned like a brand. Seven days. I had seven days to master the wolf. But mastering the wolf meant becoming the king. And becoming the king meant killing the woman who loved my father. Who might love me.

"There's another way," I said. "You said there's another way."

Luna knelt in front of me. Took my face in her hands. "There is. Give me the power. Let me carry the bloodline. I'm not bonded to you. The curse won't apply. I can control it. Contain it. And you can live a normal life."

"Normal life." I laughed. Bitter. Broken. "I black out during full moons. I killed a man three days ago. There's no normal life for me."

"There can be. If you let go."

I looked into her eyes. Moonlit. Ancient. Beautiful. And for a moment, I wanted to say yes. I wanted to give her everything. To be free. To be human.

Then I remembered Marcus. His blood on my hands. His last words. *The Wolf King's power passes to the heir.*

"I can't," I said. "Marcus didn't die so I could give up."

"He died so you could live!"

"He died so I could fight!"

The words echoed through the ruins. Bounced off broken walls. Faded into silence.

Luna's hands dropped from my face. She stood. Turned away. "Then you've already made your choice."

"What choice?"

She looked over her shoulder. Her eyes were hard now. Cold. "The Council knows about this temple. They know about the ritual. They know about you." She pointed at the darkness beyond the ruins. "They're already here."

I followed her gaze. Shadows moved between the trees. Dozens of them. Hundreds. Red eyes glowing in the darkness.

The Rogue Pack. And the Council. Together.

"You led them here," I whispered.

Luna turned fully. Her silver hair whipped in a wind that came from nowhere. "I loved your father. I loved you. But I love this world more. And the Wolf King will destroy it."

She raised her hand. The air crackled. The mark on my chest screamed.

"Forgive me, Liam."

Then the world went white.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Taste of Betrayal

The Moonlit Temple rose from the earth like a skeleton's ribcage, white stone arches clawing at the night sky. Liam followed Luna through a hidden entrance—a crack between two boulders that widened as they approached, responding to some invisible command.

Inside, the air smelled of old incense and older magic. Torches burned with blue flame, casting shadows that moved independently of the objects that cast them. The walls were covered in murals: wolves running beneath crimson moons, kings kneeling before goddesses, battles that spanned centuries.

Luna walked ahead of him, her silver hair catching the torchlight. She hadn't spoken since they entered the forest. Hadn't looked back once.

"You said you were my father's mate," Liam said, his voice echoing off the stone walls. "That means you're a werewolf."

"I am."

"Then why did the rogues attack you in the alley? You're one of them."

She stopped. Turned. Her eyes held that same ancient sadness he'd seen by the stream. "Not all wolves serve the Council, Liam. Some of us remember what we're supposed to be."

"And what's that?"

"Guardians. Not conquerors." She resumed walking. "The Council has forgotten their purpose. They see the Wolf King bloodline as a threat because it represents a power they can't control. A power that doesn't answer to their laws."

They entered a circular chamber. The ceiling opened to the sky, and moonlight poured through like liquid silver. In the center stood a pool of water so still it looked like black glass.

"This is the Mirror of First Blood," Luna said. "It shows the truth of your lineage. Every Wolf King has looked into this pool before their awakening ceremony."

Liam approached the edge. His reflection stared back at him. Same tired eyes. Same bruised face. Same ordinary twenty-one-year-old who'd been working a dead-end job three days ago.

Then the water rippled.

The reflection changed.

He saw a man with his face but older, harder. Alexander stood in a field of bodies, blood dripping from his claws, a crown of moonlight burning above his head. The image shifted. Liam saw himself in a throne room, wolves kneeling before him. Saw himself leading an army against a city of glass and steel. Saw himself standing over Luna's broken body, her silver hair spread across stone like spilled mercury.

"What is this?" he whispered.

"The future. Or a possible future. The Mirror shows what could be, not what will be." Luna moved beside him. "Your father saw the same visions. He spent his entire life trying to avoid them."

"Did he succeed?"

She didn't answer.

A door opened behind them. Liam spun, his body tensing for a fight he wasn't sure he could win.

A woman entered. Older than Luna, with grey streaking her dark hair and eyes that had seen too many centuries. She wore robes of deep purple, and a silver pendant hung around her neck—a wolf's head with a crescent moon above it.

"High Priestess," Luna said, bowing her head.

The woman's gaze fixed on Liam. "So. The bloodline returns." Her voice was like gravel and honey. "I am Selene, keeper of the Moonlit Temple and the last of the true priestesses. Luna has told me much about you."

"Not much to tell," Liam said. "I found out I'm a werewolf three days ago. My father's dead. A Council of wolves wants me dead. And I have four days left to figure out how to not lose my mind to a thousand-year-old spirit."

Selene smiled. It wasn't comforting. "You have your father's directness. And his lack of patience." She gestured to the pool. "You've seen the Mirror."

"I saw... things. Bad things."

"The Mirror shows possibilities. Not destinies. The Wolf King before Alexander saw a future where he united the clans in peace. He failed. Alexander saw a future where he destroyed the Council. He succeeded, but at a cost." Her eyes darkened. "The bloodline carries a curse, Liam. Every Wolf King who has looked into that pool has been driven mad by what they saw."

"Comforting."

"I'm not here to comfort you. I'm here to prepare you." Selene moved to the center of the chamber. The blue flames flickered as she passed. "The Blood Moon ceremony requires three things: control of your beast, acceptance of your heritage, and a sacrifice."

Liam's stomach tightened. "What kind of sacrifice?"

"The blood of your enemy. Or the blood of your ally." Selene's eyes met his. "The ceremony binds the Wolf King to his pack. But to become a king, you must first prove you can make the hard choices. The ones that cost you something."

Luna stepped forward. "High Priestess, with respect—"

"Quiet, child." Selene raised a hand. "He needs to know the truth. The Council isn't the only thing hunting him. The beast inside him grows stronger every hour. By the Blood Moon, if he hasn't mastered it, the wolf will consume him completely. He won't be Liam anymore. He'll be Alexander's ghost wearing his skin."

Liam's hand went to his chest. The mark was warm beneath his shirt. "What do I need to do?"

"First, you need to learn to shift on command. Not when the moon forces you. Not when anger demands it. You need to master the transformation."

"And how do I do that?"

Selene walked to a wall covered in weapons. Swords, axes, spears, all made of silver. She pulled down a dagger and tossed it to Liam. He caught it by instinct, the hilt cold in his palm.

"Silver," he said.

"The only metal that can kill a werewolf. Or heal one." Selene drew another dagger from the wall. "The beast fears silver. Hates it. But a Wolf King must learn to embrace what he fears."

She slashed her own palm without flinching. Blood welled up, dark and thick. She held out her hand. "Cut me."

"What?"

"Cut me. Prove you can control your instincts. The wolf in you wants to attack anything that threatens you. But I'm not threatening you. I'm asking for your trust."

Liam looked at the dagger. At Selene's extended hand. At Luna, who watched with an expression he couldn't read.

He stepped forward. Raised the blade. Pressed it against Selene's palm.

She didn't flinch. Didn't pull away.

He cut.

The wound opened, blood spilling down her wrist. Selene didn't make a sound. She just watched him with those ancient eyes.

"Good. Now heal it."

"How?"

"Will it closed. Your blood carries the Wolf King's power. Use it."

Liam focused on the wound. Nothing happened. He tried harder, squeezing his eyes shut, imagining the flesh knitting together.

Still nothing.

"I can't."

"You're thinking too much." Luna's voice was soft. "The wolf doesn't think. It acts. Let go."

He opened his eyes. Looked at the wound. At Selene's patient face.

Let go.

The mark on his chest flared. Heat rushed through his veins. The wound on Selene's palm began to close, skin knitting together like it was being stitched by invisible thread.

Selene smiled. "There. The bloodline awakens."

Liam stepped back, breathing hard. "That felt..."

"Wrong?" Selene finished the wound's closure. "It always does at first. You're used to being human. Used to limits. The Wolf King has no limits. That's what makes him dangerous."

A scream echoed through the temple.

Not human. Wolf.

Luna's head snapped up. "They found us."

"Impossible," Selene said. "The temple is warded."

Another scream. Closer now. Followed by the sound of stone cracking.

"The warding," Luna said, her eyes widening. "It's been broken from the inside."

Selene's face went pale. "That's not possible unless—"

The wall exploded inward.

Three wolves burst through, fur black as oil, eyes burning red. They moved too fast, blurring through the air, jaws snapping.

Luna shifted mid-leap, her body twisting and expanding into a wolf of pure white, her eyes silver. She met the first attacker, their bodies colliding in a spray of blood and fur.

Selene threw a handful of powder into the air. It ignited, forming a barrier of blue fire between them and the remaining wolves.

"Liam," she said, grabbing his arm. "The tunnel behind the pool. It leads to the surface. Go."

"I'm not leaving—"

"You're not ready for this fight. Go. Find the old alpha of the Crescent Pack. His name is Dorian. Tell him the temple has fallen."

"How do I find him?"

"Follow the blood trail." She pushed him toward the tunnel. "The Council has someone inside the temple. Someone who broke the wards. That means nowhere is safe. Not even here."

Liam hesitated. Luna was still fighting, white fur stained red, her jaws tearing into her enemies. Selene was drawing symbols in the air, the blue fire growing brighter.

"Go!" Selene screamed.

He ran.

The tunnel was dark, narrow, the walls slick with moisture. He stumbled, caught himself, kept moving. Behind him, he heard the sounds of battle—snarls, screams, the crash of stone.

Then silence.

He didn't stop. Couldn't stop. The tunnel twisted, descended, climbed. He lost track of time. Lost track of distance.

Finally, he saw light. Moonlight.

He burst out of the tunnel into a clearing. Trees surrounded him. The moon hung overhead, full and silver.

He was alone.

Or so he thought.

A figure stepped out of the shadows. Tall. Male. Wearing a suit that cost more than Liam's rent.

"Liam." The man's voice was calm. Familiar.

Liam's blood went cold.

"Marcus."

The old alpha smiled. "Surprised to see me alive?"

"You died. I watched you die."

"You watched what I wanted you to watch." Marcus straightened his jacket. "The Council needed you to run. Needed you to find the temple. To lead us to the last of the true priestesses."

"Luna. Selene."

"Collateral damage, I'm afraid. But necessary." Marcus's eyes glowed gold. "You see, Liam, the Council doesn't want you dead. They want you controlled. And the only way to control a Wolf King is to take away everyone he loves."

Liam's fists clenched. The mark on his chest burned. The wolf howled inside him, demanding release.

"Luna," he said. "Was any of it real?"

"She was real. Her feelings for your father were real. Her desire to protect you was real." Marcus shrugged. "But she was also useful. And now she's served her purpose."

"I'm going to kill you."

"You'll try." Marcus's body began to shift, his suit tearing as fur erupted across his skin. "But first, you're going to learn what it truly means to be the Wolf King."

He lunged.

And Liam finally stopped running.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Weight of a Crown

The name hit him like a silver bullet to the chest.

Liam's hand froze mid-reach. The stream water soaked through his clothes, cold and sharp against his burning skin. He stared at her—at the silver hair, the moonlit eyes, the way she stood like she'd been waiting for this moment for years.

"His mate," Liam repeated. The words tasted wrong. "You're telling me you were married to my father."

"Not married." Luna's hand remained extended. "Mated. The bond is different. Deeper. It doesn't require paperwork or ceremonies. It's two souls recognizing each other across any distance."

"And you just happened to find me? In an alley? Three hours after I shifted for the first time?"

"I've been watching you for twenty-one years, Liam." Her voice stayed soft. Patient. Like she was explaining something to a child. "Every birthday. Every full moon. Every time you blacked out and woke up with no memory of where you'd been."

The rage came fast. Hot. Unbidden.

Liam pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His ribs screamed. But he stood, water dripping from his clothes, blood still seeping through the bandages Marcus had wrapped around his arm.

"You watched me." His voice came out low. Dangerous. "You watched me for twenty-one years and never said a word. Never helped. Never explained why I woke up in ditches with my hands covered in blood."

"I couldn't."

"Couldn't? Or wouldn't?"

"Both." Luna's eyes flickered. For just a moment, the calm cracked. He saw something beneath—grief so old it had fossilized. "The Council has eyes everywhere. If I'd contacted you before your awakening, they would have killed us both. They would have killed your mother."

"Don't talk about my mother."

"She's why I'm here now." Luna stepped closer. The moonlight caught her face, and Liam saw the lines around her eyes, the gray threading through her silver hair. She was older than she looked. Much older. "Your mother called me the night you were born. She knew what you were. Knew what would happen when you turned twenty-one."

"My mother knew about werewolves?"

"Your mother was human. But she loved your father enough to learn our ways." Luna's voice cracked. Just slightly. "She held you in her arms and made me promise—if anything ever happened to her, I would watch over you until the bloodline awakened. And then I would bring you home."

Liam's chest tightened. The mark over his heart pulsed, warm and insistent.

"I don't have a home."

"You do." Luna reached into her jacket. Pulled out a photograph. Worn at the edges. Folded so many times the creases had gone white.

She handed it to him.

The photo showed a man. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Black hair streaked with gray, cropped short and practical. His eyes were the same shade of amber as Liam's—the same shade that had stared back from the mirror during the full moon. He wore a leather jacket and stood in front of a building made of white stone, vines crawling up its columns.

At his side, a woman with silver hair. Younger. Smiling. Her hand resting on a swollen belly.

Liam's hand trembled.

"Is that—"

"Your father. Alexander." Luna pointed to the woman. "And me. Three months before you were born."

Liam looked at the man's face. The strong jaw. The way he stood, slightly forward, like he was ready to fight the world. The same posture Liam caught himself making in the mirror.

"He looks like me."

"You have his eyes. His stubbornness." Luna almost smiled. "His terrible taste in pickup lines."

"I don't—"

"He once told me the moon was beautiful but it had nothing on my smile." She did smile then. Brief. Painful. "I should have run. Instead, I stayed for thirty-seven years."

Liam looked back at the photo. The building behind them. White stone. Vines. A symbol carved above the door—the same crescent moon and wolf's head that burned on his chest.

"The Moonlit Temple."

"Yes."

"I don't even know where it is."

"I do." Luna tucked the photo back into her jacket. "But we can't go there tonight. The Council's hunters are already tracking your scent. They'll have the entire eastern forest sealed within the hour."

"Then where?"

Luna turned. Looked toward the treeline. Her eyes narrowed.

"There's a safe house. Three miles north. An old pack member who owes Alexander a debt." She glanced back at him. "Can you run?"

Liam tested his legs. His ribs screamed. His arm throbbed. His entire body felt like one massive bruise.

"Yeah."

"Good. Stay behind me. Don't make a sound." Luna started walking. Then stopped. Turned back. "And Liam?"

"What?"

"When I say run, you run. Don't look back. Don't wait for me. You understand?"

The way she said it made his blood go cold.

"Yeah. I understand."

---

They moved through the forest in silence.

Liam followed three steps behind Luna, matching her pace, watching the way she navigated the darkness like she'd been born in it. She never stumbled. Never hesitated. Every step was precise, deliberate, like she'd walked this path a thousand times.

The trees grew thicker. Older. The moonlight barely pierced the canopy. Insects hummed. Somewhere in the distance, an owl called.

Then Liam heard it.

Howling.

Close. Too close.

Luna stopped. Held up her hand. Listened.

"How many?" Liam whispered.

"Four. Maybe five." Her jaw tightened. "They're tracking. Not hunting yet. But they will be."

"How long until they find us?"

"Ten minutes. If we keep moving."

They didn't make it ten minutes.

The howling stopped. The forest went silent. Even the insects stopped humming.

Luna's hand went to her belt. She pulled out a knife—silver blade, bone handle, runes carved along the flat.

"The hunters know we're here," she said. "They've surrounded us."

"Surrounded? We're in the middle of the—"

A shadow moved between the trees.

Not a wolf. Not a man. Something in between. Hunched. Muscular. Eyes glowing red in the darkness.

Liam's blood went cold.

"That's not a Council hunter," he said.

"No." Luna's voice dropped. "That's worse."

The creature stepped into the moonlight.

It stood seven feet tall. Its body was humanoid—two arms, two legs, a torso—but covered in matted black fur. Its hands ended in claws, each one six inches long and stained dark. Its face was a nightmare mix of wolf and man, jaws too wide, teeth too many.

And its eyes.

Its eyes were completely black. No iris. No pupil. Just endless, hungry darkness.

"What is that?" Liam whispered.

"A Wraith." Luna positioned herself between Liam and the creature. "A werewolf who rejected the moon. Who chose to become something else. Something the Council couldn't control."

"The Council made that?"

"The Council banished it. Twenty years ago. For crimes I won't describe." Luna's knife caught the moonlight. "Your father was the one who sentenced it."

The Wraith tilted its head. Its jaws opened. A sound came out—not a growl, not a howl, but something that vibrated through Liam's bones.

*Alexander's cub.*

The voice wasn't spoken. It wasn't heard. It was *felt*. Like the words had been carved directly into his skull.

Liam stumbled back. His hand went to his head.

*You have his scent. His blood. His weakness.*

"Don't listen to it," Luna snapped. "It's a parasite. It feeds on fear."

*I feed on the truth,* the Wraith said. *And the truth is your father was a coward. He ran from the Council. He ran from his duty. And when I came for him, he ran from me.*

"You're lying."

*Am I?* The Wraith stepped closer. Its claws scraped against tree bark. *Then why did he leave you? Why did he hide you in the human world instead of raising you as his son?*

Liam's chest burned. The mark pulsed. Alexander's voice stirred in the back of his mind.

*Don't listen, boy. It's trying to—*

*Quiet, old wolf.* The Wraith's black eyes fixed on something Liam couldn't see. *Your time is over. Let your cub face me alone.*

"Liam." Luna's voice cut through. "When I tell you to run—"

"I'm not running."

"You don't understand what this thing is capable of—"

"I said I'm not running." Liam stepped around her. His legs shook. His hands trembled. But he stepped forward anyway. "You want my father? You can't have him. He's dead. But I'm here. And I'm not afraid of you."

The Wraith laughed.

The sound wasn't human. It was a thousand voices screaming at once, distorted, broken, echoing through the trees.

*Good,* it said. *I was hoping you'd say that.*

It moved.

Faster than anything that size should move. A blur of black fur and silver claws. Luna shouted. Liam threw himself sideways, felt the claws tear through his jacket, felt the wind of them passing inches from his face.

He hit the ground. Rolled. Came up with his hands out, like that would do anything.

The Wraith was already turning. Already coming for him again.

*You have his stubbornness too.* It lunged. *Let's see if you have his durability.*

Liam didn't think.

He moved.

His body reacted before his brain caught up. He dropped low, felt claws whistle over his head, drove his shoulder into the Wraith's chest. The creature was solid—like hitting a brick wall. But it staggered. Just slightly.

Liam followed through. He grabbed the Wraith's arm, twisted, used its own momentum to throw it off balance. The creature crashed into a tree. Wood splintered. Leaves rained down.

Liam stood over it, chest heaving.

"Not bad," he said. "For a coward's son."

The Wraith's black eyes widened.

Then it smiled.

*Oh, you're going to be fun.*

It exploded upward. Claws extended. Teeth bared.

And somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled.

Not a Wraith. Not a hunter.

A real wolf.

The Wraith froze. Its head snapped toward the sound. For the first time, Liam saw something like fear cross its features.

*No. Not yet. I'm not ready—*

Another howl. Closer.

The Wraith snarled. It looked at Liam, black eyes burning with hatred.

*This isn't over, cub. I'll find you. I'll tear your father's mark from your chest and wear it as my own.* It stepped backward into the shadows. *Enjoy your reprieve. It won't last.*

The darkness swallowed it.

Liam stood there, shaking, bleeding, barely able to stand.

"What the hell was that?" he gasped.

Luna appeared at his side. Her face was pale. Her hand still gripped the knife, but she hadn't used it.

"That," she said slowly, "was a message."

"From who?"

"From someone who knows you're alive. Someone who wants to make sure you don't stay that way."

She looked toward the howling.

"The safe house," she said. "We

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Temple's True Purpose

Luna's words hung in the air like smoke from a dying fire.

*Your father's mate.*

Liam's blood-caked fingers dug into the stream bed. The cold water numbed his wounds, but nothing could numb the weight of what she'd just said. He pushed himself up. His arms shook. His ribs screamed. The mark on his chest pulsed with a dull, rhythmic heat.

"You were married to Alexander?"

"Not married." Luna's silver eyes flickered with something ancient. Pain, maybe. Or regret. "Mated. The bond runs deeper than any human ceremony. It's soul-deep. Unbreakable."

"Was."

"I said was." Her jaw tightened. "Alexander's been dead for twenty-seven years. I've spent every day since trying to keep his bloodline alive."

Liam stood. Slowly. Painfully. Water streamed off his torn clothes. He was taller than her by a head, but somehow she made him feel small. The way she held herself. The way her eyes tracked his every movement. She moved like a predator who'd learned patience the hard way.

"Why didn't you tell me before?"

"Would you have believed me?"

"No."

"Exactly."

She turned and walked toward the treeline. Her bare feet made no sound on the forest floor. No snapped twigs. No crushed leaves. She moved like smoke.

Liam followed. What choice did he have? The Council wanted him dead. The rogue pack wanted his bloodline. And somewhere inside him, Alexander's voice waited like a wolf in the dark. Luna was the only thread he had left.

"Where's this temple?" he asked.

"Close."

"How close?"

"Close enough that you should stop asking questions and start walking."

They moved through the forest in silence. The moon broke through the canopy in silver shards, painting the ground in patches of light and shadow. Liam's senses had sharpened. He could hear everything—the rustle of leaves, the burrow of insects, the distant howl of a dog. Or a wolf.

"How did you find me?" he asked.

Luna didn't slow. "I've been tracking you since you left the city. The Council's enforcers are good, but they're predictable. They always follow the main roads. I knew you'd head for the woods."

"You could have helped me earlier."

"I did. I gave you the location of the safe house."

"That was you?"

"I have contacts everywhere. People who owed Alexander favors. People who still believe in the old ways."

Liam stepped over a fallen log. His legs ached. His lungs burned. But the pain was different now—less like injury, more like growth. Like his body was knitting itself back together with stronger thread.

"You said the temple can help me control the shift," he said. "How?"

Luna stopped.

They'd reached a clearing. At its center stood a ruin—crumbled stone walls overgrown with moss, a collapsed roof, a doorway that led into darkness. It looked like nothing. Like the remains of some forgotten chapel from a century ago.

"This is it?" Liam asked.

"No."

She walked to the center of the clearing. Kneeled. Pressed her palm to the ground.

The earth trembled.

Liam stumbled back as the grass split open. Stone groaned beneath his feet. The ruins in front of him shifted—not collapsing, but *rising*. Walls lifted themselves from the dirt. Pillars straightened. The roof reformed, stone by stone, as if time itself was reversing.

When it stopped, a temple stood before him.

Not a ruin. A temple.

Black marble walls rose three stories high. Silver inlaid patterns spiraled across the surface, catching the moonlight, glowing with their own inner light. The doorway was no longer a gap of darkness but a solid archway covered in runes. They pulsed. Breathed. Like a heartbeat.

"What the hell," Liam whispered.

"The Moonlit Temple," Luna said. "Hidden for three centuries. Keyed to Alexander's bloodline. Only his heir can open it."

She looked at him.

"And only his heir can survive what's inside."

Liam stared at the temple. The runes on the archway seemed to recognize him. They brightened as he stepped closer, casting long shadows across the clearing. The mark on his chest burned hotter.

"What's inside?" he asked.

"Answers. Power. A way to master the Wolf King's bloodline in six days."

"And the catch?"

Luna's expression didn't change. "The temple tests you. Your body. Your mind. Your spirit. If you fail any of the trials, the temple consumes you. There's no second chance. No mercy."

"You're joking."

"Alexander designed it that way. He knew that only the strongest of his line deserved the Wolf King's legacy."

Liam looked at the runes. They seemed to whisper to him. Ancient words. Forgotten promises. The voice inside him stirred.

*She's right,* Alexander said. *I built this place to weed out the weak. If you can't survive it, you don't deserve my power.*

"Shut up," Liam muttered.

Luna raised an eyebrow. "Talking to yourself already?"

"My father. He's in my head."

Her composure cracked. Just for a moment. Her eyes widened, and something raw passed across her face. Hope? Fear? She masked it quickly, but Liam had seen it.

"He's alive in you," she said. "That's... more than I hoped for."

"I don't think alive is the right word. He's more like a bad roommate who never leaves."

"Can you communicate with him?"

"Sometimes. He talks. I try not to listen."

Luna stepped closer. Her hand reached out, hesitated, then touched his chest. Right over the mark. Her fingers were cold. Steady.

"The bond is stronger than I expected," she said. "Alexander's consciousness should have faded years ago. But he held on. For you."

"Why?"

"Because he knew what was coming. The Blood Moon. The war. He wanted to guide you."

"Then why doesn't he just tell me what to do?"

"Because he's not whole. He's echoes and fragments. Memories held together by willpower. The temple can restore him. Give him voice. Give you the knowledge you need to survive."

Liam looked at the archway. The runes pulsed. Invited. Threatened.

"Six days," he said.

"Yes."

"And if I go in there, I might not come out."

"You might not."

He thought about Marcus. The old man who'd given his life to save him. The blood on the floor. The knife in his chest. He thought about the Council, sitting in their high towers, deciding who lived and who died. He thought about the rogue pack, hunting him through the city, wanting to rip his bloodline out of his veins.

Then he thought about himself. Weak. Clueless. A king with no crown and no army.

He stepped through the archway.

The darkness swallowed him whole.

For a moment, there was nothing. No light. No sound. No sense of his own body. Just void, pressing against his skin, filling his lungs, drowning him in absence.

Then the runes flared.

Light exploded from every direction. Liam threw up his arms, but the light didn't burn—it *poured* into him. Through his eyes. His mouth. His skin. It filled every cell, every bone, every thought until he couldn't tell where he ended and the light began.

Images flashed through his mind.

A battlefield. Thousands of wolves, their eyes glowing red and silver. A man standing at the center, his fur black as midnight, his fangs bared. Alexander. The Wolf King.

*A woman beside him. Luna. Younger. Fiercer. Her hands covered in blood.*

*A child. A baby wrapped in white cloth. Alexander holding it, his face torn between joy and grief.*

*The moon splitting in half. A voice screaming. A pack dying.*

Liam fell to his knees.

The light receded.

He was in a chamber. Circular walls covered in murals—wolves hunting, wolves fighting, wolves kneeling before a throne of bones. The floor was black obsidian, polished to a mirror shine. And in the center of the room stood a pedestal.

On the pedestal rested a single object.

A dagger. Silver blade. Black handle. The hilt was carved in the shape of a wolf's head, its eyes two rubies that glowed like dying embers.

Liam's hand moved before he could stop it.

He grabbed the dagger.

Pain exploded through his palm. He tried to drop it, but his fingers wouldn't open. The blade was *melting* into his skin, silver liquid flowing up his arm, tracing veins, burning paths through his flesh.

"What the—"

The mark on his chest blazed.

And Alexander screamed.

Not in Liam's head. In the room. A physical howl of rage and agony that shook the walls. The murals cracked. The floor splintered. The dagger dissolved completely, leaving nothing but a silver stain on Liam's palm.

Then the voice spoke.

But it wasn't Alexander.

*Foolish boy.*

Liam's blood ran cold.

*Did you really think the Wolf King built this temple for his heir?*

The runes on the walls turned from silver to blood red.

*He built it as a cage.*

Liam spun. The doorway was gone. The walls were closing in. And in the center of the obsidian floor, a shape was rising from the shadows.

A wolf. Massive. Its fur was the color of dried blood. Its eyes were pits of darkness. And around its neck hung a collar covered in runes—the same runes that had welcomed Liam at the entrance.

*You've delivered yourself to me,* the wolf said. Its voice was not sound but sensation. Pure malice pressed into his skull. *The last of Alexander's bloodline. The key to my freedom.*

"Who are you?" Liam's voice cracked.

The wolf's lips peeled back. Its teeth were black. Rotting.

*I am what your father trapped here three hundred years ago. I am the first wolf. The original monster.*

*I am the thing that made the Wolf King.*

The wolf lunged.

And the temple went dark.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Weight of Crowns

The tunnel smelled of blood and wet stone.

Luna moved ahead of me, her silver hair catching what little light filtered through cracks in the ceiling. She hadn't spoken since we'd left the safe room. Neither had I. The mark on my chest still throbbed from the ritual, and Alexander's voice had gone silent—which worried me more than his threats.

"How much further?" I asked.

"Almost there."

"Almost where? You said shelter. You didn't say what kind."

She stopped. Turned. Her eyes held that same ancient sadness, but something else flickered beneath it now. Caution.

"One of the old gathering places," she said. "The Blackwood pack used it during the first war. Before the Council sealed the borders."

"Used it for what?"

"Planning. Training. Hiding." She paused. "Dying."

The word hung in the air between us. I thought of Marcus. Of the way he'd fallen, silver in his chest, still fighting until his heart stopped. Twelve hours ago I was a college student worrying about rent and exams. Now I was standing in a tunnel that smelled like a tomb, following my dead father's mate to a place where packs went to die.

"Comforting," I said.

"It's not meant to be."

She turned and kept walking. I followed.

---

The chamber opened without warning.

One moment we were squeezing through a crack in the rock. The next, the world expanded into a cavern large enough to hold a cathedral. Moonlight streamed through fissures in the ceiling, casting silver beams across the floor. Ancient symbols covered every wall—painted in something dark that had dried to a rusty brown.

Blood.

I'd seen enough of it in the past day to recognize the color.

"What is this place?" I whispered.

"The Heartstone Chamber." Luna walked to the center of the room, where a massive stone slab rose from the floor. It was covered in the same symbols as the walls. "Every alpha bloodline has one. A place where the pack's history is written in the blood of its kings."

I approached the slab. The symbols weren't random. They were names. Hundreds of them, carved deep into the stone, each one connected by lines that formed a massive family tree.

"There." Luna pointed to a section near the top. "Your father."

I traced the line with my finger. *Alexander Blackwood, 47th Wolf King.* Beneath it, a single branch led to my name. *Liam Blackwood, 48th Wolf King.* The carving looked fresh. Newer than the others.

"When was this added?"

"Tonight." Luna's voice was flat. "The bloodline knows when a new king rises. The stone records it automatically."

I pulled my hand back like the stone had burned me. "I didn't ask for this."

"No king ever does."

"Marcus said I could refuse it. He said there was a way to—"

"There isn't."

The words hit like a punch. I turned to face her, and for the first time, I saw something other than calm in her eyes. Anger. Old and deep and barely contained.

"Marcus lied to me?"

"He lied to himself." Luna's jaw tightened. "He wanted to believe there was another way. That the bloodline could be broken. That you could walk away and live a normal life." She stepped closer. "But the blood doesn't care about what you want. It chose you the moment you were born. The moment Alexander died, it started counting down the days until you took his place."

"Then why did Marcus tell me I had a choice?"

"Because he loved you."

The words hit harder than the first time. I looked away, at the names on the stone, at the history written in blood. Alexander's name. His father's name. His father's father's name. Going back centuries. All of them Wolf Kings. All of them dead.

"How long do they usually last?" I asked.

"The average alpha lives fifteen years after taking the throne."

"And the Wolf King?"

Luna's silence was answer enough.

"Tell me."

"Twenty-three years," she said finally. "That's how long Alexander had before the Council's assassins found him. He was thirty-seven when he died."

Thirty-seven. My father died at thirty-seven. I was twenty-one. If I followed the same path, I had sixteen years left. Maybe less.

"Can I avoid the assassins?"

"Not if you remain weak."

"And if I become strong?"

Luna's eyes met mine. "Then you'll have different enemies."

---

She spent the next hour explaining the chamber.

The symbols weren't just names. They were records of every battle, every alliance, every betrayal that had shaped the Blackwood bloodline. Each symbol corresponded to a specific event, and when I touched them, I saw fragments of the past—flashes of memory that weren't mine but felt like they should be.

I saw Alexander fight a rogue alpha twice his size.

I saw him kneel before a Council of seven elders, accepting their judgment.

I saw him hold a baby in his arms—me—with tears in his eyes.

"Stop," I said, pulling my hand away from the symbol. "I can't—I'm not ready for this."

"You don't have to be ready," Luna said. "You just have to survive."

"Great. Same advice, different words."

Her expression softened slightly. Almost a smile. "You have your father's sarcasm."

"I'll take that as a compliment."

"Don't. It got him killed more than once."

I sat down on the stone slab, suddenly exhausted. The adrenaline that had carried me through the night was fading, and in its place came the weight of everything I'd learned. My father was murdered. The Council wanted me dead. I had a wolf inside me who wanted to take control. And the only person who might have been able to help me was dead on a rooftop.

"The Council," I said. "You said they're the ones who killed Alexander?"

"Yes."

"Why?"

"Because he was too powerful." Luna sat across from me, her silver hair pooling around her like liquid moonlight. "The Council is made up of seven elders from the seven founding packs. They're supposed to maintain balance between the clans. But over the centuries, they've become something else. Something corrupt."

"And my father threatened them?"

"He didn't just threaten them. He had proof. Evidence of their crimes. He was going to expose them at the Blood Moon ceremony."

"What kind of crimes?"

"Murder. Betrayal. Alliance with rogues." Luna's voice dropped. "And something worse. Something they've been hiding since the first war."

"Which is?"

She looked at me for a long moment. Then she stood and walked to the far end of the chamber, where a section of the wall was covered by a black cloth. She pulled it away.

I stared.

It wasn't a symbol. It was a name. One name, carved larger than all the others, surrounded by symbols I didn't recognize.

*Valerius Cain.*

"Who's that?"

"The founder of the first pack," Luna said. "And the reason the Council wants you dead."

"Why?"

"Because Valerius isn't dead. He's imprisoned. And your bloodline holds the key to his release."

I felt the mark on my chest pulse. Heard Alexander's voice whisper in the back of my mind. *The blood remembers.*

"If Valerius is freed," I said slowly, "what happens?"

"The first war ended because he was contained. If he returns..." Luna's voice cracked. "He'll finish what he started."

"Which was?"

"Extinction. Every pack that doesn't bow to him. Every human who knows about us. Everyone who stands in his way."

I looked at the name. At the symbols I didn't understand. At the history written in blood that I was now part of, whether I wanted it or not.

"The Council wants me dead because of this?"

"They don't just want you dead." Luna's hand touched mine. "They want the bloodline ended. Every Blackwood, every descendant, every person who carries even a trace of the Wolf King's blood. They want it erased."

"Why?"

"Because you're the only one who can free Valerius. And you're the only one who can stop him."

---

A sound echoed through the chamber.

Not footsteps. Not voices. Something else. A low, rhythmic thumping, like a heartbeat amplified through stone.

"What was that?" I asked.

Luna's face went pale. "They found us."

"Who?"

"The Council's hunters." She grabbed my arm, pulling me toward the tunnel. "They must have tracked the bloodline signature. We need to move."

We ran. The tunnel was dark, and I stumbled over rocks, felt the walls closing in. Behind us, the thumping grew louder, closer. I heard voices now. Sharp. Commanding.

"Don't stop," Luna said. "Don't look back."

We burst out of the tunnel into a moonlit clearing. Trees surrounded us, their branches forming a canopy that scattered the light into silver fragments. Luna kept running, pulling me toward the edge of the forest.

Then she stopped.

A figure stood in the clearing ahead of us.

Tall. Broad-shouldered. Eyes that glowed red in the darkness.

"Luna." The voice was deep, ancient, familiar. "It's been too long."

"Marcus," Luna whispered.

My blood went cold.

Marcus stepped forward into the moonlight. His chest was whole. No wound. No silver. No blood. He looked exactly as he had before he died—except for his eyes. Those red, burning eyes that didn't belong to the man who had raised me.

"I'm sorry, Liam." His voice cracked. "I didn't have a choice."

"He's not Marcus," Luna said, stepping in front of me. "He's possessed. The Council must have—"

"The Council didn't do this." Marcus—or whatever was wearing his body—took another step forward. "I did. I made a deal. Your life for mine."

"What deal?" I asked. "With who?"

Marcus's mouth twisted into a smile that wasn't his own.

"The one who's been waiting for you."

His eyes turned completely black.

And the ground beneath us began to crack.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Taste of Betrayal

The Temple of Echoed Moons smelled of blood and secrets.

Liam crouched behind a crumbling pillar, pressing his palm against the stone to steady his breathing. The mark on his chest pulsed in rhythm with something beneath the temple—a heartbeat. Ancient. Hungry.

Three hours since they'd arrived. Three hours since Luna had led him through a hidden entrance beneath the old cathedral district, down spiral stairs that seemed to descend forever, into a place that shouldn't exist. A temple carved from obsidian and moonlight, buried beneath a city that had forgotten its monsters.

"Stay close," Luna whispered, her silver hair brushing his arm as she moved past him. "The guardians won't recognize you yet."

"Recognize me as what?"

She didn't answer. She hadn't answered half his questions since they'd fled the warehouse district. Every time he asked about Alexander, about the bloodline, about why the Council wanted him dead—she deflected. *Later. Not here. The walls have ears.*

But the walls weren't the problem.

The problem was the thing waiting in the center of the temple.

Liam saw it through the archway ahead—a pool of liquid silver, thirty feet across, rippling without wind. Steam rose from its surface, carrying the scent of ozone and something metallic. Old blood. Ancient power.

"The Moonpool," Luna said, her voice dropping to something barely audible. "It will show you what you need to become."

"I know what I need to become." Liam stepped past her, his boots echoing against the obsidian floor. "A weapon. Something that can kill the Council before they kill me."

"Liam—"

"You said Alexander left something here. Something that would help me master the shift." He turned to face her, saw the hesitation in her eyes. "Where is it?"

She held his gaze for a long moment, then reached into her coat. Her hand emerged holding a dagger—black blade, silver hilt, the same crescent moon symbol etched into the crossguard.

"This is all that remains of him," she said. "He forged it from his first shift. His first claws. Melted down and shaped into something that could be passed down."

Liam took the dagger. It was heavier than it looked. Warm. Like it remembered the hands that made it.

"Your father wasn't just the Wolf King," Luna continued. "He was the strongest alpha in three centuries. He could shift in seconds, control his transformation completely, lead a pack of three hundred wolves without raising his voice." Her voice cracked. "And the Council still killed him."

"How?"

"Poison. Silver laced into his food for months. By the time he realized what was happening, it was too late." She looked at the dagger in Liam's hands. "He couldn't shift. Couldn't fight. Couldn't even stand. They dragged him to the Moonpool and—"

"Enough."

The word came from Liam's throat, but it wasn't his voice. Alexander's. Old. Cold. Angry.

Luna's eyes widened. "Liam—"

"I said enough." Liam's hands trembled as the ancient presence pushed through him, flooding his veins with fire. The mark on his chest burned so bright he could see it through his shirt. "You brought him here to die. You're bringing me here for the same reason."

"What? No—"

"You were his mate. His trusted mate." Liam's voice twisted, Alexander's fury bleeding through every syllable. "And you stood by while they fed him silver. You watched him suffer. You never warned him."

"I didn't know—"

"You knew everything." Liam's vision blurred. The temple warped, memories not his own flooding through him—Luna's face, younger, softer, crying as Alexander screamed. Luna's hands, holding the silver-laced meat. Luna's voice, whispering to someone in the shadows. *It's done. He's weak. Come collect him.*

"No." Luna backed away, her hands raised. "That's not what happened. Alexander, please—I loved you. I would never—"

"You did." Liam's hand tightened on the dagger. "I saw it. When I died, I saw your face. I heard your words. You were their spy. Their weapon. Their—"

"Liam, stop!" Luna's eyes glowed silver, her own power rising. "That's not your memory. That's the bloodline's corruption. The Moonpool is poisoning your mind—"

"Then why does it feel like truth?"

Silence.

Luna's face went pale. Her hands dropped to her sides. "Because it is."

The words hit Liam like a physical blow. He staggered, the dagger clattering to the ground as Alexander's presence receded, leaving him hollow. Cold. Alone.

"You really did it," Liam whispered. "You really betrayed him."

"It was thirty years ago." Luna's voice was barely audible. "I was young. Stupid. The Council promised me power, promised me he would live if I helped them. They lied."

"They killed him."

"Yes." She met his eyes. "And I've spent every day since trying to earn back what I lost. I helped rebuild the resistance. I saved Marcus from execution three times. I—" She stopped. Swallowed. "I brought you here because I owe him. I owe you. The Moonpool can help you master the shift in days instead of years, but only if you trust it. Only if you trust me."

"Trust you?" Liam laughed, and the sound was hollow. "You just admitted to killing my father."

"I admitted to making a mistake. The worst mistake of my life." She stepped closer, slow, careful, like approaching a wounded animal. "But I've spent three decades trying to fix it. And I will spend the rest of my existence making sure you survive what's coming."

"You can't fix this."

"I know." She picked up the dagger, held it out to him hilt-first. "But I can help you become what he would have wanted you to be. A king who doesn't repeat his father's mistakes."

Liam stared at the dagger. At her hands. At the woman who had killed his father and saved his life in the same breath.

He took the blade.

"Show me the Moonpool."

---

The water was cold. Colder than anything Liam had ever felt. It didn't wet his skin—it sank through it, into his blood, into his bones, into the part of him that was still fighting the wolf.

*Let me in,* Alexander whispered. *You can't do this alone.*

*I don't trust you.*

*Good. You shouldn't trust anyone. Not me. Not Luna. Not yourself.* A pause. *But you can use me. That's what a king does.*

Liam closed his eyes. Sunk deeper into the silver water. Let it fill his lungs, his throat, his chest.

The pain was immediate.

Not the pain of shifting—worse. The pain of remembering. Every memory Alexander had ever had, every battle, every betrayal, every moment of joy and grief and rage, flooding through Liam's mind like a tidal wave.

He saw Alexander as a child, running through forests that no longer existed. Saw him meet Luna for the first time, her smile bright as the moon. Saw him fight the Council, tear through a dozen alphas, stand over their bodies and roar.

Saw him trust. Saw him fall. Saw the silver in his blood, the betrayal in Luna's eyes, the death that took three days and three nights of agony.

*See?* Alexander's voice was tired now. Broken. *That's what they do. They smile. They promise. They poison.*

*Why are you showing me this?*

*Because you need to understand. The Council isn't your enemy. The rogues aren't your enemy. The enemy is trust. Trust kills kings.*

Liam surfaced, gasping, silver water streaming from his hair. The Moonpool's surface rippled, and for a moment, he saw something in its depths—a reflection that wasn't his own.

A figure stood behind him.

He turned.

Luna was gone.

In her place stood a woman he'd never seen before—tall, dark-haired, dressed in black leather, her eyes burning like embers. She held a silver blade to Luna's throat.

"The Council sends its regards," the woman said. "And demands the Wolf King's head."

Liam's hand went to the dagger at his belt. "Who are you?"

"Your executioner." She smiled, and her teeth were sharp. "I've been hunting you since you left the city. You're faster than I expected. Smarter, too." She pressed the blade deeper into Luna's throat. "But not smart enough."

"Let her go."

"Why would I do that?" The woman laughed. "She betrayed your father. She led you here. She's the reason you're going to die in a pool of silver instead of on your feet like a warrior."

"Luna—"

"I deserve this." Luna's voice was steady. Resigned. "I told you the truth. I earned this blade at my throat. So run, Liam. Run and become what you need to become."

"I'm not leaving you."

"You have to." Luna met his eyes. "The Moonpool showed you what you needed to see. Now you need to survive long enough to use it."

The dark-haired woman's smile widened. "How touching. A traitor sacrificing herself for a king who hates her. This will make a wonderful story."

She moved.

Liam moved faster.

The dagger in his hand caught her blade an inch from Luna's throat. Steel screamed against steel. The dark-haired woman's eyes widened in surprise.

"You shouldn't have done that," she whispered.

"I'm not my father." Liam shoved her back, putting himself between her and Luna. "I don't run from fights. And I definitely don't let strangers kill people I need."

"You need her?"

"I need answers." He glanced at Luna. "And she has thirty years' worth of them."

The dark-haired woman laughed. "Then you'll die with her."

She attacked again, faster than Liam could track. His body moved on instinct, Alexander's centuries of combat experience flowing through him like second nature. He blocked. Dodged. Countered.

But he wasn't fast enough.

Her blade caught his side, slicing through skin and muscle, sending blood splashing into the Moonpool. Liam stumbled, his vision blurring.

The mark on his chest blazed.

*Let me out,* Alexander snarled. *Let me fight.*

*No.*

*You're dying, boy. Let me—*

*No.*

Liam forced himself upright. The woman was circling, enjoying the hunt, savoring his pain.

"I've killed six Wolf King bloodlines," she said. "You'll be the seventh."

"You talk too much."

He lunged. Not at her—at the Moonpool. Dove into the silver water, felt it close over his head, felt his body start to shift.

Not a shift of bone and muscle.

A shift of self.

He stopped fighting.

Stopped resisting.

Let the wolf in.

The roar that tore from his throat wasn't entirely human. The body that exploded from the Moonpool wasn't entirely wolf. Something between. Something new. Something that made the dark-haired woman's confidence flicker for the first time.

"What—"

Liam hit her before she could finish. His claws—Alexander's claws—tore through her guard, through her chest, through the heart that had beaten for centuries.

She fell.

And behind her, in the shadows of the temple, a dozen more figures emerged.

The Council had found him.

Luna grabbed his arm. "There's a tunnel—"

"Too late."

The figures advanced, silver blades gleaming, eyes glowing with ancient power.

And

title: The Weight of Crowns
content:
Liam's hand stopped halfway to Luna's.

The stream gurgled past his broken body. Water soaked through his torn shirt, cold against the burning mark on his chest. But none of that mattered compared to the word that hung in the air between them.

*Mate.*

"You're lying."
