Luna's hand didn't waver. "I'm not."

"My father's mate." Liam pushed himself up, ignoring the fire in his ribs. "Alexander. The Wolf King. You're telling me you were *his* Luna?"

"Not his Luna." Her eyes flickered with something ancient. Pain. Loss. "His mate. There's a difference."

Liam laughed. It came out wet. Blood still coated his teeth. "Yeah? Enlighten me."

"The Luna of a pack is chosen by the Alpha. She rules beside him. She bears his children. She commands the pack when he falls." Luna lowered her hand slowly. "A mate is different. A mate is chosen by the moon itself. Two souls bound together. One cannot live without the other."

"Then how are you standing here?"

A long pause. The wind carried the scent of pine and blood and something else—something that made Liam's wolf stir beneath his skin. Recognition.

"Because I died the night he did."

The words hit like silver to the chest.

Liam's vision sharpened. The world snapped into focus. He saw the way her feet didn't quite touch the grass. The way the moonlight passed through her hair instead of reflecting off it. The way her scent had no warmth behind it.

"A ghost."

"A memory," she corrected. "Trapped between worlds. Bound to the bloodline until the next Wolf King rises."

"Then you're here for *me*."

"I'm here for Alexander's son." Her jaw tightened. "I made a promise. On the night he died, I swore I'd protect his heir. No matter how long it took. No matter what I had to become."

Liam stood. His legs shook. His body screamed. But he stood.

"Seven days," he said. "That's what Alexander told me. Seven days until the Blood Moon ceremony. If I don't master the wolf—"

"It will master you. Yes." Luna finally moved, circling him like a predator studying wounded prey. "But there's something your father didn't tell you. Something he couldn't tell you."

"Because he was busy being dead?"

"Because he was afraid." Her eyes met his. "The Blood Moon ceremony isn't just a test of control. It's a coronation. If you survive it, you become the Wolf King. Not just in name. In power. In soul."

"And if I don't?"

"You become a vessel. An empty shell. And the thing that fills you won't be Alexander's spirit."

Liam's blood went cold. "What are you talking about?"

"The Blood Moon isn't just a moon. It's a door. Every thirty years, the veil between worlds thins. The old kings rise. The ancient wolves stir. And if the heir isn't strong enough to claim the throne by sunrise—" She paused. "Something else will."

"What something?"

"The first Wolf King. The one who started it all. He's been waiting in the darkness for three thousand years. Waiting for an heir weak enough to possess."

Liam's hand drifted to his chest. The mark pulsed beneath his fingers. Warm. Alive. Hungry.

"You're saying Alexander wasn't the first."

"Alexander was the thirty-seventh. The line stretches back to before recorded history. Every Wolf King adds his strength to the bloodline. Every Wolf King dies and passes his power forward." Luna's voice dropped. "But the first one never died. He was trapped. Imprisoned in the space between worlds. And the Blood Moon is his only chance to escape."

"Through me."

"Through any heir too weak to hold the crown."

Silence stretched between them. The stream babbled. An owl called somewhere in the darkness. Liam's heart hammered against his ribs like it wanted to break free.

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because I loved your father." Luna's voice cracked. For the first time, she looked fragile. Human. "And because I've been waiting thirty years to look into his eyes again. You have them. The same gold flecks. The same stubborn set to your jaw." She swallowed. "The same recklessness that got him killed."

"Marcus said the Council killed him."

"The Council helped. But they didn't act alone." Luna turned, staring into the treeline. "Someone betrayed him. Someone close. Someone who knew every weakness, every hiding place, every secret the Moonlit Temple held."

"Who?"

"If I knew that, I would have already killed them." Her gaze snapped back to him. "But I have suspicions. And if you come with me to the Temple, I can show you the evidence."

Liam's wolf surged. Not with violence. With recognition. With *need*.

The mark burned hotter.

*She's telling the truth,* Alexander's voice rumbled. *I can feel it. She loved me. She loves you. She's the only ally you have left.*

*You could feel her love?*

*I could feel her pain when I died. Every wolf can feel their mate's heartbreak. It's why most don't survive the loss.*

Liam looked at Luna. Really looked. The way her hands trembled at her sides. The way her eyes never quite settled on him, always flickering to the moon. The way she held herself like she was waiting for something to break.

"You loved him," Liam said. "Even after he died. You loved him so much you stayed."

"I made a promise."

"Love or the promise?"

A long pause. Luna's mask cracked. Just a little. Just enough.

"Both."

Liam stepped forward. His body ached. His blood sang. The wolf clawed at the inside of his skin, desperate to break free. But he pushed it down. Pushed it deep.

"Take me to the Temple."

Luna's eyes widened. "Just like that?"

"Alexander trusted you. Marcus trusted you. My wolf trusts you." He met her gaze. "And I'm tired of running alone."

She nodded once. Sharp. Decisive. "There's a safe house two miles east. Supplies. Weapons. A vehicle that can get us across the border."

"Border?"

"The Temple isn't in human territory. It's in the neutral zone. Between the three great packs. The Council can't touch us there."

"Can't or won't?"

"Won't. Breaking the truce would start a war." She smiled. Thin. Bitter. "Even the Council fears the consequences of a full-scale pack war."

Liam followed her into the darkness. Every step sent fire through his veins. Every breath tasted like copper. But he kept moving. Kept pushing. Because stopping meant dying. And dying meant becoming a door for something ancient and hungry.

They walked in silence for an hour.

The forest changed around them. The trees grew thicker. Older. Their branches twisted together overhead, blocking out the moon until only slivers of silver light reached the ground. The air grew cold. Heavy. Like walking through water.

Luna stopped suddenly.

"Something's wrong."

Liam's wolf surged. His senses sharpened. The world snapped into focus—every rustling leaf, every scurrying mouse, every breath of wind.

He smelled it.

Blood. Smoke. Death.

"Who?" he asked.

Luna's face went pale. "The safe house."

They ran.

Liam's body screamed. His legs burned. His ribs ground together with every stride. But he didn't stop. Couldn't stop. Because if the Council had found the safe house, it meant they knew about Luna. It meant they knew about the Temple. It meant—

He burst through the treeline.

The cabin was gone.

Not burned. Not destroyed. *Gone*. As if it had never existed. The ground where it had stood was smooth. Untouched. Like no building had ever rested there.

"What the hell—"

"It's a message." Luna's voice dropped. Cold. Dangerous. "They're telling us they know. They're telling us they can reach anywhere."

"Who?"

"The Council doesn't have this kind of power." She turned slowly, scanning the treeline. "This is something else. Something—"

A howl split the night.

Not a wolf.

Something worse.

Liam's blood ran cold. His wolf cowered. The mark on his chest burned so hot he thought his skin would melt.

*That's not a wolf,* Alexander whispered. *That's a Wraith.*

"What's a Wraith?"

*A wolf who died in the Blood Moon ceremony. A failed heir. His soul was too weak to ascend, but too strong to fade. So he became something else. Something that hunts in the darkness. Something that feeds on royal blood.*

Another howl. Closer.

Luna grabbed Liam's arm. Her fingers were cold. Death-cold. "We need to move. Now."

"They're hunting us?"

"They're hunting *you*. The Council must have summoned them. Broken the ancient pact." Her eyes blazed. "If a Wraith consumes the blood of a living heir, it can take his place in the ceremony. It can become the Wolf King."

Liam's heart stopped.

"Then it's not just the Council I'm running from."

"No." Luna pulled him forward, into the deeper darkness. "You're running from every failed king who ever wanted a second chance."

Behind them, the Wraith howled again.

And this time, others answered.

Liam ran. The mark on his chest blazed like a brand. Alexander's voice faded to a whisper. Luna's grip on his arm was the only thing keeping him grounded.

Seven days.

Seven days until the Blood Moon.

And something ancient and hungry was already hunting him through the dark.

---

They reached the river at dawn.

Liam collapsed on the bank, chest heaving, legs trembling. Luna stood watch, her silver hair catching the first rays of sunlight, her eyes fixed on the treeline behind them.

"They stopped."

"How do you know?"

"Wraiths can't cross running water." She pointed at the river. "It's one of the old rules. One of the laws that binds them."

Liam laughed. Hollow. Broken. "Running water. Silver. Full moons. Anything else I should know?"

"Don't make deals with fae. Don't eat food in the Underhill. Don't—"

"I was joking."

"I wasn't." Luna finally turned to face him. "You're not just a werewolf anymore, Liam. You're a king. And kings don't have the luxury of ignorance."

He stared at her. At the exhaustion carved into her features. At the way her form flickered in the growing light, translucent for just a moment before solidifying again.

"You're fading."

"Daylight weakens me," she admitted. "I'll need to rest soon. Find shadow."

"Then rest. I'll keep watch."

"You can barely stand."

"I can stand enough." He pushed himself up, ignoring the fire in his muscles. "You've been protecting me all night. Let me return the favor."

Luna studied him. For a long moment, she said nothing. Then she nodded. Slow. Reluctant.

"There's a cave a quarter mile upstream. I can rest there until nightfall."

"Then let's go."

They walked. Slow this time. Limping. Bleeding. But alive.

The cave was small. Damp. Dark. Luna settled into the deepest corner, where the shadows were thickest, and closed her eyes.

"Two hours," she said. "Then we move again."

"Where?"

"To the one

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Traitor's Mark

I stared at her hand. Processed her words. Failed to make them mean anything.

"His mate?"

Luna's smile didn't waver. "Twenty-three years ago, I stood in this same forest and watched Alexander die. I've been waiting ever since for someone to find me." She lowered her hand. "The Council thinks I'm dead. Most of them do. It's kept me alive."

I pushed myself up. My ribs screamed. My right arm nearly gave out. "You expect me to believe that? You show up in an alley, watch me almost die, and now you're suddenly family?"

"I expect you to be smart enough to know when you're out of options." She glanced over her shoulder. "There are seven rogues tracking you. Two miles out. Closing fast. Marcus bought you time, not safety."

Pain flared across my chest. The mark. It pulsed like a second heartbeat.

*She's telling the truth.*

Alexander's voice. Calm. Certain.

*I sealed her memories the night I died. She doesn't remember the location of the Blood Moon ceremony. The Council tortured her for three days trying to break the seal. They couldn't.*

I looked at Luna with new eyes. The way she stood. Weight on her back foot. Hands loose at her sides. Ready to move.

"You've been waiting twenty-three years," I said. "Why not find me sooner?"

"Because you weren't ready." She stepped closer. "The bloodline only awakens at twenty-one. Before that, you were just a boy with blackouts. If the Council found you early, they'd have killed you. Marcus knew. I knew. We both stayed away to keep you alive."

The name hit like a punch. "You knew Marcus?"

"He was my brother."

The forest went silent. No wind. No insects. Just the sound of my blood roaring in my ears.

"Your brother," I repeated.

"Marcus Silvermoon. Alpha of the Northern Pack. My older brother by two years." Her voice cracked on the last word. "He gave his life to get you to me. The least you can do is stop asking questions and start moving."

A howl ripped through the darkness. Close. Too close.

Luna grabbed my arm. "Run."

---

We ran.

Not toward anything. Just away. Branches whipped my face. Roots grabbed my feet. Luna moved ahead of me like she was born in this forest—which, apparently, she was.

"The temple," I gasped. "Where is it?"

"An hour north. If we don't die first."

Another howl. Closer now. Multiple voices joining in a chorus of hunger.

"They're herding us," Luna said. She veered left, dragging me through a thicket of thorns. They tore at my skin. I didn't feel it. Adrenaline had burned through pain receptors ten minutes ago.

"Who's leading them?"

"Does it matter?"

"It matters if I'm going to kill him."

Luna glanced back. Something flickered in her eyes. Respect, maybe. Or recognition. "Victor Blackwood. Alpha of the Shadowfang Pack. He wants your blood to power his blood moon ritual. If he consumes the Wolf King's essence before the ceremony, he becomes immortal."

"Consumes?"

"He has to eat your heart while you're still alive."

I stopped running.

Luna pulled up, turned. "What are you doing?"

"I'm not running from someone who wants to eat my heart." The words came from somewhere deep. Alexander's voice. My voice. Both. "I'm the Wolf King. I don't run."

"You're an untrained pup who's been a werewolf for six hours."

"Then teach me."

She stared at me. The howls grew louder. Closer. Three hundred yards. Maybe less.

"You want to learn to shift here? Now?"

"I want to learn to fight."

Luna's jaw tightened. Then she nodded. "Fine. Close your eyes."

I did.

"Feel the wolf. It's not separate from you. It's you. Everything you've ever suppressed—every moment of rage, every flash of violence you locked away—that's the wolf trying to get out."

The howls hit two hundred yards.

"I don't feel anything," I said.

"Yes you do. That heat in your chest. That pressure behind your ribs. Stop fighting it."

I stopped.

The wolf surged forward. Claws burst from my fingertips. My jaw cracked, shifted, teeth lengthening into fangs. Pain. Beautiful, familiar pain.

"Good," Luna said. "Now pull it back."

"What?"

"Pull it back. Control isn't about letting go. It's about holding the line. Shift to the edge and stay there."

I tried. The wolf fought me. Alexander fought me. Two ancient wills pressing against my own.

*Let me out,* Alexander growled. *I can kill them all.*

*No.*

*You're going to get us killed.*

*I said no.*

I opened my eyes. My vision had changed—colors sharper, darkness meaningless. I could see Luna's heartbeat through her chest. Could smell the fear on the rogues approaching. Could taste the silver in the air.

"Half-shift," Luna said. "That's more than most alphas learn in a year."

The first rogue broke through the trees.

He was massive. Seven feet tall in his hybrid form, fur matted with blood, eyes burning red. He saw me and smiled.

"The pup," he said. "Victor will be pleased."

Three more rogues emerged behind him. Flanking. Surrounding.

I looked at Luna. "How many can you take?"

"All of them if you buy me thirty seconds."

"Done."

She blurred. Gone. A silver streak tearing through the darkness.

The first rogue lunged.

I didn't think. Didn't plan. My body moved before my brain caught up. I caught his arm mid-swing, twisted, felt bones break. He screamed. I drove my claws through his chest.

Blood hot on my hand.

His heart stopped against my palm.

I pulled back. Stared at the body crumpling at my feet. I'd killed someone. A person. A monster. A—

*First kill,* Alexander said. *Always the hardest. Gets easier.*

"I don't want it to get easier."

*Then you'll die.*

A second rogue hit me from the side. We rolled through brambles, snapping branches, tearing at each other. He was stronger. Older. More experienced. His claws found my shoulder, my ribs, my thigh.

I found his throat.

Teeth. I used teeth. Ripped. Tasted copper.

He gurgled. Went still.

I stood, shaking. Two down. Two more.

Luna had one pinned against a tree, her claws buried in his skull. The fourth stood frozen, watching his packmates fall.

"Run," I said. "Tell Victor I'm coming for him."

The rogue ran.

Luna dropped the body. Wiped her hands. "That was stupid. Now he knows where we are."

"He already knew. They've been tracking me since the apartment." I looked at my hands. Still partially shifted. Claws retracted but knuckles still furred. "I can't get all the way back."

"Give it time. The first shift is always violent." She walked over, examined my wounds. "You'll heal. Wolf King bloodline regenerates fast."

"How fast?"

"Broken bones in hours. Deep cuts in minutes." She pressed a hand to my cheek. "You have his eyes."

I pulled away. "Don't."

"Liam—"

"You waited twenty-three years. You could have warned Marcus. Could have warned me. Instead you let me grow up thinking I was crazy."

"To keep you alive."

"To keep yourself hidden."

Her face went cold. "You don't know what the Council does to survivors. You don't know what I've seen. What I've lost." She turned, started walking north. "The temple is forty-five minutes. Keep up or die."

I followed.

Not because I trusted her. Because Alexander did. Because the wolf recognized something in her that my human mind couldn't process.

Because she smelled like home.

---

The temple rose from the forest like a bone breaking through skin.

White stone. Moon-carved sigils. A dome that caught silver light and scattered it across the clearing. It was ancient. Older than the trees. Older than the packs.

"Your father built this," Luna said. "For your mother."

"She's dead."

"Yes. The Council killed her when you were six months old. She was human. They couldn't allow the Wolf King to have a half-blood heir."

"I'm half-human?"

"Quarter. Your mother was half herself. The bloodline weakened with each generation. That's why the Council thought they could control Alexander. Why they thought they could kill him."

We approached the entrance. A massive stone door carved with the same crescent moon and wolf's head that marked my chest.

"Place your hand on the seal," Luna said.

I touched the door.

Light exploded. Not painful—warm. Like sunlight through winter clouds. The symbol on my chest burned, then cooled, then settled into something that felt like home.

The door opened.

Inside, the temple was empty. A single room. A stone altar. Moonlight streaming through a hole in the ceiling.

"Sit," Luna said. "We have six days until the Blood Moon. You need to learn everything. The history. The power. The cost."

"The cost?"

She looked at me. Really looked. And for the first time, I saw fear in her eyes.

"The Wolf King's power doesn't just give you strength," she said. "It gives you the memories of every king who came before. Every death. Every betrayal. Every failure. When you fully awaken, you'll remember being Alexander. You'll remember being his father. His father's father. All the way back to the first."

"Six thousand years," I whispered.

"Six thousand years of war. Six thousand years of loss." She stepped closer. "I've been alone for twenty-three years because I couldn't watch another Wolf King die. But you're here. And you're alive. And I'll be damned if I let Victor take that from me."

A sound. Outside. Footsteps. Many.

Luna's head snapped toward the door.

"That rogue you let live," she said. "He brought friends."

The temple walls began to shake.

And in my chest, the mark burned hotter than ever.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Temple of Lies

The stream water had turned cold against Liam's skin. Cold and sharp, like needles pressing into every wound he'd collected during his escape. He stared at the woman's outstretched hand—slender fingers, pale skin, the faintest glow at her fingertips.

"Mate?" The word came out wrong. Broken. "My father's mate?"

Luna's smile didn't waver. "A blood bond. Not a marriage. Werewolves don't do weddings, Liam. We do claims."

He pushed himself up. His arms shook. His chest burned where the mark still throbbed. "You expect me to believe that? You expect me to follow you into some temple after everything that's happened?"

"I expect you to be smart." She lowered her hand but didn't step back. "The Council knows you're alive. Marcus's death bought you maybe two hours. They'll have trackers on your scent by dawn."

"Then why should I trust you?"

"Because I knew your father before he became the monster they feared. Because I held you when you were three hours old. Because—" She paused, her jaw tightening. "Because I'm the one who hid you from them in the first place."

The world tilted.

Liam's fingers dug into the muddy bank. "You. You took me from him?"

"I took you from the pyre." Her voice cracked. First time. Real emotion bleeding through the calm mask. "Alexander wanted you dead. Not because he hated you—because he loved you. The Wolf King's bloodline carries a curse, Liam. Every generation, the father must kill the son, or the son must kill the father. It's written in the old laws. The blood demands a sacrifice."

"That's insane."

"That's the Council." She knelt, bringing her face level with his. Her eyes were silver now. Full silver, like the moon had taken root in her irises. "Alexander refused. He hid you with a human family, erased your scent, cut all ties. Then he let them believe he'd killed you anyway. He took the blame, the shame, the hatred. He let them call him a child-killer so you could live."

Liam's throat closed.

Marcus's words came back. *Your father... he was a good man. A strong man.*

"You're lying."

"I wish I was." Luna reached into her jacket and pulled out a photograph. Faded. Edges curled from age. A man with Liam's eyes, his jaw, his build, holding a bundle of blankets. A woman stood beside him—Luna, younger, hair shorter, smile wider. They stood in front of a cabin. Snow on the ground. Morning light.

"Three hours old," she whispered. "That's you."

Liam took the photograph. His hands shook. His blood burned. Alexander's presence stirred in his chest, ancient and watchful, confirming everything without words.

*She speaks truth,* the voice said. *I remember her scent. I remember her warmth.*

"Why now?" Liam's voice broke. "Why come for me now?"

"The blood moon is seven days away. The Council will perform the ceremony—with or without a Wolf King. If they complete it without a true heir, the bloodline dies. The curse passes to no one. Every werewolf born from Alexander's line will lose their connection to the moon."

"And if I don't care about werewolves?"

"Then you die anyway." She stood. "The mark on your chest isn't a symbol, Liam. It's a countdown. When the blood moon rises, if you haven't claimed the throne, the wolf inside you will consume your soul. You'll become a feral. No memories. No humanity. Just hunger."

Liam looked at the photograph again. His father's face. His own face reflected back through decades of lies.

"Seven days," he said.

"Six now. Dawn is coming."

He struggled to his feet. Every joint screamed. Every muscle threatened to give out. Luna caught his arm, steadying him. Her touch sent a shock through his skin—warm, electric, *familiar.*

"The Moonlit Temple," he said. "What is it?"

"A sanctuary. A training ground. The only place where the Council's trackers can't follow." She looked up at the sky. Clouds were gathering, swallowing the stars. "But we have to move. Now."

They ran.

Liam's body wasn't ready. He stumbled, fell, pushed himself up, stumbled again. Luna moved like water—silent, fluid, barely disturbing the shadows around her. She pulled him forward, never letting go, her grip iron despite her slender frame.

The forest blurred past. Trees. Rocks. A river they crossed on stepping stones slick with moss. The air grew colder. Thinner. The ground sloped upward, climbing toward a ridge Liam couldn't see.

"How far?" he gasped.

"Close."

"You said that twenty minutes ago."

"I lied."

He wanted to laugh. Couldn't. His lungs were on fire. His vision swam. The mark on his chest pulsed with every heartbeat, throbbing like a second pulse.

Then the trees parted.

The temple stood in a clearing, half-carved into the mountain itself. Stone pillars wrapped in vines. A doorway shaped like a wolf's open jaw. Torches flickered along the walls—blue flames that gave no smoke, no heat.

"Moonfire," Luna said. "Burns forever. The first Wolf King lit them a thousand years ago."

Liam stared. "How do you know all this?"

"I was there."

She said it so casually. So simply. Like she'd just told him the sky was blue.

"Wait." He grabbed her arm. "You were there? A thousand years ago?"

Luna turned. Her silver eyes met his. "Werewolves don't age like humans, Liam. The stronger your bloodline, the longer you live. I was Alexander's mate for three hundred years. I was his grandmother's mate before that."

His hand fell away.

"You're—"

"I'm the last living elder of the original Moonlit Temple. I taught the first Wolf King how to shift. I taught Alexander. And now I'm going to teach you." She walked toward the entrance. "If you survive the night."

"Survive? I thought this was a sanctuary."

"It is." She stepped through the wolf's jaw doorway. The blue flames flickered, casting her shadow long and distorted against the stone walls. "Sanctuary from the Council. Not from me."

Liam followed.

The temple opened into a vast chamber. A circular room with a high domed ceiling painted with constellations he didn't recognize. The floor was polished obsidian, reflecting the blue flames like standing water. In the center, raised on a stone pedestal, sat a dagger.

Black blade. Silver hilt. The hilt was shaped like a wolf's head, its eyes set with rubies that caught the firelight.

"The Blood Blade," Luna said. "Forged from the fang of the first werewolf. It holds the power of every Wolf King who came before you."

"Why is it here?"

"Because you need to claim it." She picked up the dagger, turned it over in her hands. "The blade must taste your blood before the ceremony. If it doesn't, the curse will claim you regardless."

Liam stepped forward. "That's it? Cut myself and I'm done?"

"Not exactly." Luna's grip tightened on the hilt. "The blade doesn't just taste blood. It takes it. It will drain you until your wolf rises to fight back. If your wolf is strong enough, you survive. If not—" She shrugged. "You die."

"Great. Love the options."

He reached for the dagger. Luna pulled it back.

"One more thing. The voice in your head—Alexander's echo—he'll try to take control when the blade touches you. You have to fight him. If he wins, you're no longer Liam. You're just a vessel for a ghost."

"I've been fighting him all night."

"You've been arguing with him. That's not fighting." She held out the dagger, hilt first. "This is war."

Liam wrapped his fingers around the handle.

The blade sang.

Literally. A high-pitched whine that vibrated through his bones, up his arm, into his chest. The mark flared, burning hot, and Alexander's voice roared inside his skull.

*Don't.*

"Too late."

Liam pressed the blade to his palm.

The pain was instant. Not a cut—an explosion. Like the dagger had reached inside him and grabbed hold of every vein, every artery, every vessel. Blood poured out, not dripping but *streaming*, flowing up the blade, into the ruby eyes, making them glow.

The chamber spun.

Liam's knees hit the obsidian floor.

*Fight,* Luna's voice echoed. *Fight now.*

But Alexander was already there.

The voice wasn't just in his head anymore. It was in his blood. In his bones. In the space behind his eyes. The wolf's presence pressed against his consciousness, hungry, ancient, *impatient.*

*You can't win, boy. I've been a king for centuries. You've been a wolf for hours.*

Liam grit his teeth. "Then teach me."

*What?*

"You said I had seven days to master you. This is day one." He looked at the mark on his chest. It was spreading, crawling up his neck, down his arm. "So teach me."

Silence.

Then laughter. Deep. Thunderous. Shaking the walls of his mind.

*You're more like me than I thought.*

The blade released him. Liam collapsed, gasping, his blood still on the obsidian floor, still glowing in the ruby eyes of the dagger.

Luna knelt beside him. "You did it."

"No." He stared at the ceiling, at the constellations spinning overhead. "He did. He let me win."

"Why would he do that?"

"Because he wants something." Liam closed his eyes. "And he thinks I'm the only one who can get it."

A crash echoed from outside. Heavy. Metallic. The sound of stone breaking.

Luna's head snapped up. "They found us."

"Who?"

She grabbed the Blood Blade, shoved it into his hands. "The Council. They're here."

The torches flickered. The blue flames turned red.

And the walls began to shake.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Temple Beneath

I took her hand.

Stupid. Reckless. Probably suicidal. But my father's mate didn't kill me in the alley, didn't kill me in the forest, and right now she was the only person in the world who might actually want me alive.

Her grip was firm. Warm. She pulled me to my feet like I weighed nothing.

"You're hurt," she said.

"No shit."

She pressed her palm against my chest—right over the mark. The burning stopped. Just like that. The cuts on my arms stopped bleeding. My ribs stopped screaming.

"What did you—"

"Healing gift. Rare. Now move."

She dragged me through the forest. Not fast. Not slow. Purposeful, like she knew exactly where every root and rock was before her feet touched the ground. I stumbled behind her, still weak, still dizzy, still trying to process the fact that the woman who saved me was apparently my dead father's girlfriend.

"I have questions," I said.

"You'll have answers at the temple."

"Which is where, exactly?"

"Close."

We broke through a line of trees and hit a cliff. Straight drop. Thirty meters at least. Rocks below. Nothing but darkness and the sound of water somewhere distant.

I stopped. "This is close?"

Luna didn't stop. She walked right to the edge and stepped off.

I lunged forward, grabbed her arm, nearly went over myself. She hung there, suspended, her feet finding purchase on something I couldn't see. An invisible staircase. She smiled up at me.

"The temple doesn't show itself to everyone."

"Great. So how do I—"

She grabbed my wrist and pulled. I stepped into nothing, felt my foot land on solid stone that wasn't there a second ago, and nearly fell anyway. Luna steadied me.

"Don't look down."

Too late. I looked down. The rocks below shimmered, shifted, became something else. Water? No. A mirror. Reflecting the moon, the stars, my own terrified face.

"What is this place?"

"The space between worlds. The threshold. Every temple is built on one."

She led me down the invisible stairs. Twenty-three steps. I counted. Each one felt more solid than the last, like my feet were learning to believe in something that shouldn't exist.

At the bottom, the cliff face opened. Not a cave. A doorway. Carved stone, ancient symbols, a crescent moon above the arch. Torches flickered to life as we approached, burning with silver flames.

"The Temple of the Moonlit Path," Luna said. "Built by the first Wolf King, two thousand years ago. Hidden from every clan, every council, every rogue pack. Only the bloodline can find it."

"Only the bloodline," I repeated. "But you found it."

She looked at me. Those moonlit eyes held something I couldn't read. Pain? Regret? Hope?

"I was your father's mate," she said. "His blood runs in my veins. Not through birth, but through bonding. The temple knows me."

We stepped inside.

The corridor opened into a chamber the size of a cathedral. Pillars of white stone rose toward a ceiling that wasn't there—instead, the night sky stretched above us, stars swirling in patterns I'd never seen, constellations that didn't exist in any human sky. A pool of silver water sat in the center, perfectly still, reflecting a moon that hung directly overhead even though we were underground.

"How is this—"

"Magic," Luna said. "Old magic. The kind that doesn't care about physics or reason. Sit."

She pointed to the edge of the pool. I sat. The stone was warm. Alive.

Luna knelt across from me, her silver hair pooling around her like liquid moonlight. She studied me for a long moment.

"You look like him."

"So everyone keeps telling me."

"Same jaw. Same eyes. Same stubborn set to your mouth." She almost smiled. Almost. "He'd be proud of you, Liam. Surviving the awakening. Finding your way here. Most heirs don't make it through the first night."

"Most heirs have fathers who stick around to teach them."

The words came out sharper than I intended. Luna flinched.

"You're right," she said. "Alexander should have been there. He wanted to be. But the Council—"

"The Council killed him."

"Yes."

"Why?"

She was silent for so long I thought she wouldn't answer. Then she reached into the pool and pulled out a handful of silver water. It dripped between her fingers like liquid metal.

"Because your father wanted peace," she said. "The clans have been fighting for centuries. Shadowfang, Bloodmoon, Ironclaw, Nightwalker—each one believes they should rule. Alexander was different. He believed the clans could unite. That the Wolf King's power wasn't meant to dominate, but to protect."

"That sounds nice."

"The Council disagreed. They saw unification as a threat to their power. So they arranged an accident. A rogue attack that wasn't rogue at all. Your father died fighting eight alphas at once. He took down six of them before they brought him down."

I felt something cold settle in my chest. Not sadness. Not anger. Something harder.

"The seventh alpha was Marcus," I said.

"Yes."

"But Marcus saved me."

"Marcus loved your father. They grew up together. Trained together. Marcus was supposed to be the one to kill Alexander, but when the moment came, he couldn't do it. He carried your father's body into the forest and told the Council the attack succeeded. Then he disappeared."

"To protect me."

"To honor Alexander's last wish. Your father asked him to watch over you. To keep you hidden until the bloodline awakened."

I stared at the silver pool. My reflection stared back. Young. Tired. Lost.

"Marcus told me I had seven days until the blood moon ceremony. What happens then?"

Luna's expression shifted. The warmth drained out of it.

"Every Wolf King has a spirit wolf inside them. An ancient soul that binds with the heir's bloodline. The ceremony on the blood moon is when that bond becomes permanent. If you master the wolf by then, you become the King. Full power. Full control."

"And if I don't?"

"Then the wolf masters you. Your consciousness is consumed. Your body becomes a vessel for the spirit, and the spirit answers to no one. It will seek out the Council and destroy everything in its path. Including everyone you've ever loved."

I thought of my mother. My friends. The girl I'd been dating before all this started. Normal people living normal lives.

"The Council wants that," I said. "They want me to lose."

"Yes."

"But you want me to win."

Luna reached across the pool and took my hands. Her touch sent a shiver through me—not cold, not warm, but something in between. Like static electricity.

"I want you to win, Liam. Because if you do, you can finish what Alexander started. You can unite the clans. End the war. Bring peace to our kind for the first time in a thousand years."

"That's a lot of pressure for a guy who couldn't shift until three hours ago."

"You'll learn. I'll teach you."

"How? You're not a Wolf King. You don't have the bloodline."

"No." She released my hands and stood. "But I have something better."

She walked to the far end of the chamber, where a pedestal stood. On it rested a blade. Not silver. Not steel. Something darker, older, pulsing with faint light.

"What is that?"

"Moonstone," Luna said. "Forged from the heart of the first blood moon. It doesn't cut flesh. It cuts the bond between wolf and human."

She picked up the blade. It hummed. I felt it in my teeth.

"If the wolf takes over during our training," she said, "I'll use this to separate you. Temporarily. Painfully. But it will buy you time."

"Great. So I'm going to get stabbed."

"Repeatedly."

I laughed. It was hollow. "Why are you doing this? You could walk away. Leave me to die. Find a new life."

Luna sheathed the blade and walked back to me. She crouched down, met my eyes, and for a moment I saw something break behind her gaze.

"Because I loved your father," she said. "And because I failed him once. I won't fail his son."

Something moved in the shadows behind her.

I tensed. Turned. Saw nothing.

"What's wrong?"

"I thought I saw—"

A voice echoed through the chamber. Low. Guttural. Familiar.

*So this is where you've been hiding.*

The torches went out.

Luna drew her blade. Silver light erupted from the pool, casting long shadows across the walls. Three figures emerged from the darkness. Wolves. Massive. Their eyes burned red.

"Rogues," Luna breathed.

"How did they find us?"

"The temple responds to blood. You're bleeding. They followed the scent."

I looked at my hands. The cuts Luna healed had opened again, fresh blood dripping onto the stone floor.

The lead wolf growled. Its voice scraped against my skull.

*The Council sends greetings, heir of Alexander. They want your head. I want your heart.*

It lunged.

Luna moved faster than I could track. She met the wolf mid-air, moonstone blade flashing, and carved a line across its chest. The beast yelped, twisted, landed on its feet. Black blood dripped onto the floor.

"Liam," Luna said, never taking her eyes off the wolves. "Get in the pool."

"What?"

"The pool. Now."

The second wolf circled left. The third circled right. The first one crouched, ready to spring again.

I didn't argue. I turned and jumped into the silver water.

The moment I touched it, everything changed.

I wasn't in the pool anymore. I was somewhere else. A void. Endless. Silent. And standing in front of me was a man I'd never met but recognized instantly.

Alexander.

He was tall. Broad-shouldered. His eyes were the same as mine. His face had the same lines, the same stubborn jaw. He smiled.

*Hello, son.*

"You're dead."

*I am. But the bloodline remembers. And right now, you need my help.*

The void trembled. The sounds of battle echoed from somewhere far away.

*The wolves outside are just scouts,* he said. *The real threat is coming. Something worse than rogues. Something the Council doesn't even know about.*

"What are you talking about?"

Alexander's smile faded.

*Your mother wasn't human, Liam. She was something else. Something the Council has been hunting for centuries. And when they find out you carry her blood too...*

He stepped closer. His hand touched my chest, right over the mark.

*The wolf inside you isn't just the Wolf King's spirit. It's hers. Ancient. Primal. The first of its kind.*

*And it's waking up.*

The pool exploded.

I crashed back into reality, gasping, lungs full of silver water. Luna was there, dragging me out, her blade red with blood. Two wolves lay dead. The third was gone.

"We have to move," she said. "More are coming."

But I couldn't move. I could only stare at the mark on my chest, now glowing brighter than ever, pulsing with a rhythm that wasn't my heartbeat.

*Seven days,* Alexander's voice whispered.

*And the moon will rise.*

title: The Oathbreaker's Mark
content:
The word hung in the air like smoke.

*Mate.*

Liam's hand froze halfway to hers. The stream gurgled around him, cold water numbing his broken body, but nothing could numb the shock that ripped through his chest.

"His *mate*?"

Luna's hand didn't waver. Neither did her gaze. "I know what you're thinking. That I abandoned him. That I let him die alone."

"You were *there*?" Liam's voice cracked. He pushed himself upright, ignoring the fire in his ribs. "You were with him when the Council—"

"No." The word came sharp. Final. "I was with him before. When he chose to run. When he chose to hide you away from the world." Her eyes flickered with something—pain, or rage, or both. "He made me promise. If anything happened to him, I would find you. I would protect you."

"Protect me?" Liam laughed. It came out wet. Bitter. "You disappeared for twenty-one years. Marcus raised me. Marcus died for me. Where the hell were *you*?"

The silence stretched. Somewhere in the darkness, an owl called. The stream whispered over stones.

Then Luna moved.

Not fast. Not threatening. She simply sat down beside him in the water, silver hair trailing in the current, and stared up at the moon.

"I was in chains," she said. "The Council didn't just kill Alexander. They captured me. Tortured me for information about where he'd hidden his heir." She turned her head, and for the first time, Liam saw the scars. Thin white lines running from her temple down to her jaw. "I didn't break. But by the time I escaped, you were gone. Marcus had moved you three times. The trail was cold."

Liam's throat tightened. "Why should I believe you?"

"Because I know what you're feeling right now." Her voice dropped. "The fire in your blood. The voice in your skull. The way the world smells sharper, sounds louder, feels *more* than it did twenty-four hours ago." She met his eyes. "Because I was there when Alexander first shifted. I held him down when the wolf tried to take over. And I'm the only person alive who knows how to teach you to control it."

The mark on his chest pulsed. Warm. Almost welcoming.

*She speaks the truth,* Alexander's voice rumbled. *I can smell it on her.*

"Great," Liam muttered. "Even my dead father trusts her."

Luna's lips quirked. "He always did have good instincts." She stood, water streaming from her leather jacket. "The Moonlit Temple is three hours north. Hidden in the Whisperwood. I can get you there before sunrise, but we need to move. The rogue pack has trackers. Silver-nosed bloodhounds. They'll find this stream within the hour."

Liam climbed to his feet. Every muscle screamed. Every bone ached. But the pain was already fading, replaced by a strange humming energy that buzzed beneath his skin.

The wolf was healing him.

*You're welcome,* Alexander said.

"I didn't thank you."

Luna raised an eyebrow. "Talking to yourself already?"

"Talking to my father's ghost."

"Ah." She nodded like this was perfectly normal. "That happens. Don't let him push you around. He's bossy even in death."

*I am NOT—*

"Let's go." Liam cut off the voice. He was too tired for arguments between a dead king and a mysterious woman who might be lying to him.

They moved through the forest in silence. Luna led, her footsteps impossibly quiet for someone wearing combat boots. She wove between trees, ducked under branches, never once checking a map or compass. She knew this forest like she'd grown up in it.

Maybe she had.

Liam followed, struggling to keep up. His body was healing, but his mind was a wreck. Every shadow looked like an enemy. Every rustle of leaves sounded like claws on bark. The moon followed him through the canopy, a silver eye that never blinked.

"Question," he said, breathless. "The Moonlit Temple. What is it?"

"A sanctuary. Built by the first Wolf King a thousand years ago. Hidden from the Council, from the rogues, from anyone who doesn't know how to find it." She glanced back. "It's where Alexander trained. Where he learned to master the bloodline."

"And you think I can learn in seven days what he learned in years?"

"No." She stopped. Turned. Her eyes glowed faintly in the darkness. "I think you can learn in *six* days. You wasted one bleeding in a stream."

Liam opened his mouth to retort.

Then he smelled it.

Copper. Sweat. Rotting meat.

Wolves. Close.

Luna's hand shot out, grabbing his arm, yanking him behind a massive oak. Her body pressed against his, still as stone. She raised a finger to her lips.

The forest held its breath.

Footsteps. Heavy. Deliberate. The crunch of leaves under massive paws. Liam's ears caught something else—labored breathing. A growl that came from deep in a throat that wasn't human.

Three of them. No, four. Circling.

*Silver-nosed bloodhounds,* Alexander whispered. *They track by scent. They've found us.*

"We need to run," Liam breathed.

"We need to fight."

"Are you insane? I don't know how to shift!"

"You don't need to shift to fight." Luna reached into her jacket and pulled out a blade. Not silver. Black. Obsidian. It drank the moonlight, swallowed shadows. "You just need to survive."

The first wolf exploded from the brush.

It was massive—easily twice the size of a normal wolf. Its fur was matted and dark, its eyes burning red. Saliva dripped from jaws filled with teeth that looked too long, too sharp, too *wrong*.

It lunged at Luna.

She moved like water. Dropped to one knee. Let the wolf sail over her head. The obsidian blade flashed upward, catching it in the belly. The wolf yelped, twisted midair, landed in a heap.

It didn't get up.

Liam stared. "Where did you learn—"

"Behind you!"

He spun. A second wolf was already in the air, jaws aimed at his throat. No time to dodge. No time to think.

His body moved on its own.

He caught the wolf's jaw with one hand. Stopped it mid-lunge. The force of the impact should have shattered his arm, but instead he *held*, muscles straining, bones creaking, and *threw* the wolf into a tree.

It hit with a crack. Fell. Didn't move.

Liam looked at his hands. They were covered in blood. Not his.

*There,* Alexander said, satisfaction dripping through his voice. *Now you're starting to understand.*

"I didn't do that."

*Your bloodline did. The Wolf King's strength. It's been sleeping in your veins for twenty-one years. Now it's awake.*

Another wolf howled. Closer.

Luna appeared at his side, blade dripping. "Two more. Coming from the east."

"Can we outrun them?"

"No." She wiped the blade on her pants. "But we don't need to. The temple's nearby. If we can reach the boundary line, they can't follow."

"How nearby?"

She pointed. Through the trees, Liam saw a faint glow. Blue-white. Like moonlight trapped in crystal.

"Five hundred meters."

"Let's go."

They ran.

The forest blurred past. Branches whipped at Liam's face, but he barely felt them. His legs pumped faster than they ever had. His lungs burned, but the fire in his blood pushed him forward, faster, *faster*—

Behind them, the howls grew louder. Closer.

The blue-white glow intensified. Liam could see it now—a structure rising from the trees. Stone walls. Arched windows. A dome that gleamed like polished bone.

The Moonlit Temple.

Twenty meters.

Ten.

Luna reached the entrance first, slammed her palm against a symbol carved into the stone. The doors groaned. Began to open.

Liam glanced back.

The wolves had stopped.

They stood at the edge of the treeline, red eyes fixed on him, but they didn't advance. Their hackles were raised. Their tails tucked. They *whined*.

Behind them, the forest shadows shifted.

A figure stepped out of the darkness.

Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wearing a coat made of something that looked like leather but moved like skin. His face was obscured by a hood, but his eyes—his eyes burned silver.

Not red. *Silver.*

The same color as the mark on Liam's chest.

"Liam." The figure's voice was soft. Almost kind. "I've been looking for you."

Luna grabbed his arm, pulled him through the temple doors. They slammed shut behind them. Stone ground against stone. Bolts slid into place.

She was breathing hard. Her hands shook.

"Who was that?" Liam demanded.

Luna turned to him, and for the first time, he saw fear in her eyes.

"That was your brother."

The words hit like a hammer.

"I don't have a brother."

"You do." Her voice cracked. "Alexander had two sons. You were the younger. Your brother was born with the bloodline already awakened. But it was... wrong. Corrupted. He killed Alexander. He burned the temple. And when he couldn't find you, he made a deal with the Council."

"What kind of deal?"

Luna's jaw tightened. "He became their Oathbreaker. Their hunter. He's been tracking you for twenty-one years."

Liam's blood went cold.

"That's not all," she whispered. "His name is Damien. And he has exactly six days to deliver you to the Blood Moon ceremony. If he does, the Council will make him the new Wolf King."

"He killed our father."

"Yes."

"And now he wants to kill me."

"No." Luna's eyes met his, and the fear in them deepened. "He wants to *replace* you. The ceremony doesn't just take your soul. It takes your bloodline. Your power. Your *identity*. If Damien completes the ritual, he won't just be the Wolf King."

She paused.

"He'll be *you*."

The temple groaned around them. Somewhere deep in its halls, a door creaked open.

And from outside, soft as a whisper, Damien's voice drifted through the stone.

"Six days, little brother. Enjoy the temple. It's the last home you'll ever have."

Liam stood in the darkness of the Moonlit Temple, the mark on his chest burning like a brand, and realized the truth.

He wasn't running from rogues.

He wasn't running from the Council.

He was running from family.

And family always catches up.

title: The Price of Trust
content:
Luna's words hung in the air like smoke. *Your father's mate.* Liam's bloodied fingers curled into the stream bed, pebbles digging into his palms. The water rushed past him, cold and indifferent, carrying away the evidence of his transformation.

"Marcus never mentioned you."

"Marcus didn't know everything." She kept her hand extended. Steady. Patient. "Alexander kept me hidden. For good reason."

Liam pushed himself up. His body screamed in protest. Every muscle felt like it had been torn apart and hastily reassembled. The mark on his chest pulsed with a dull ache, the crescent moon and wolf's head still glowing faintly through his torn shirt.

"Hidden from the Council?"

"Hidden from everyone." Her eyes flickered with something ancient. Pain, maybe. Or regret. "If the Council knew Alexander had a mate—a true mate—they would have used me to control him. So we kept our bond secret. Met in shadows. Loved in silence."

Liam stood. His legs wobbled. The stream rushed around his ankles, numbing his wounds. "And now? You show up the night his son nearly dies?"

"Because you're out of time."

A growl built in his chest. Not his. Alexander's. The Wolf King's blood recognized its mate's scent, even if Liam didn't understand it. *She's telling the truth,* the voice rumbled. *I can smell it.*

*Shut up,* Liam thought back. *You're not in charge here.*

*Yet.*

He ignored the voice and focused on Luna. Her silver hair caught the moonlight like spun glass. Her eyes held galaxies. She was beautiful in a way that transcended human standards—beautiful in a way that made his wolf want to submit.

He hated that.

"The Moonlit Temple," he said. "What is it?"

"A sanctuary. A training ground. The only place where the Wolf King's heir can learn to control the bloodline without the Council finding you." She lowered her hand. "Alexander prepared it before he died. For you."

"Prepared it how?"

"Ancient wards. Deadman's triggers. A failsafe that activates when the bloodline passes to the next heir." She paused. "It's been waiting thirty years for you to wake up."

Liam looked around. The forest pressed in from all sides, dark and alive. Somewhere in the distance, he heard howling. Not wolves. Werewolves. The rogue pack from the warehouse, probably. Or the Council's enforcers. Or both.

"How far?"

"Three hours. If we run."

"I can barely walk."

"Then I'll carry you." She said it without hesitation. Without irony. Like it was the most natural thing in the world.

"Not happening."

"Liam—"

"I said no." He stepped past her, nearly fell, caught himself on a tree trunk. The bark scraped his palm raw. "I don't know you. I don't trust you. For all I know, you're the one who sent those rogues to flush me out."

"Then why did I save you in the alley?"

"To earn my trust."

She laughed. It was a sad sound, hollow and ancient. "You sound like him. Alexander. Always questioning. Always suspicious." She walked closer. Close enough that he could smell her scent—jasmine and moonlit rain and something else. Something that made his chest ache. "I loved your father. I watched him die. I've spent thirty years waiting for you to wake up so I could finish what he started."

"What did he start?"

"The war."

The word hit him like a punch. "What war?"

Luna's expression hardened. "The Council isn't just a group of elders who keep order. They're a regime. They've controlled every werewolf bloodline for centuries, culling anyone who threatens their power. Alexander was the first Wolf King in a hundred years who refused to bow. He built an army. He trained warriors. He was days away from striking when they killed him."

"Days away?"

"They poisoned him. Silver-laced wine at a peace summit. It took him three hours to die." Her voice cracked. Just slightly. Just enough. "He spent those three hours writing instructions. Mapping out the Temple. Making sure you would have a chance."

Liam's hand drifted to his chest. The mark was warm now. Almost hot. *She's not lying,* Alexander said. *I remember the wine. I remember the taste.*

*You remember dying?*

*I remember everything.*

Liam closed his eyes. The forest swayed around him. The howling grew closer. "If I go with you, what happens?"

"You train. You learn to control the shift. You master the bloodline."

"And then?"

"Then you decide." She met his gaze. "You can hide. Live in the shadows, always running. Or you can finish what Alexander started. Take the war to the Council. Burn their regime to ash."

"And if I choose to hide?"

"Then I've wasted thirty years."

The howling was closer now. Definitely closer. Liam could smell them—five, maybe six wolves, moving fast, tracking something. Tracking him.

"Fine," he said. "Take me to the Temple."

Luna nodded. She turned, her silver hair catching the wind, and started walking. "Try to keep up."

She moved through the forest like a ghost. Silent. Fluid. Her feet barely touched the ground. Liam followed, stumbling, crashing through branches, leaving a trail a blind human could follow.

"You're too loud," she said without turning.

"I'm dying."

"You're whining."

"I'm doing both."

She stopped. Turned. Studied him with those ancient eyes. "The bloodline is fighting you. Stop resisting and it will guide your body."

"I don't know how to stop resisting."

"Then learn." She stepped closer. Her hand touched his chest, right over the mark. Her fingers were cold. "Close your eyes."

He hesitated.

"Do it."

He closed his eyes. Her hand pressed harder. Heat spread through his chest, radiating outward, into his arms, his legs, his spine. The pain didn't disappear, but it changed. Became something else. Something he could work with.

"Your father used to say the wolf isn't a monster," she whispered. "It's a mirror. It shows you what you really are. The fear. The rage. The love. Everything you try to hide. It forces you to look."

"I don't want to look."

"I know." Her hand dropped. "But the Blood Moon doesn't care what you want."

She turned and started walking again. Faster now. Liam followed. His legs felt stronger. His lungs burned less. The mark on his chest pulsed like a second heartbeat.

They ran for what felt like hours. The forest blurred past. Trees became shadows. Shadows became trees. The howling faded, then grew louder, then faded again. Liam lost track of time. Lost track of direction. Lost track of everything except the silver hair ahead of him, guiding him through the darkness.

Then the forest opened.

A clearing. Stone ruins. A crumbling archway covered in vines and moss. Moonlight poured through the center, pooling on the ground like liquid silver.

"The Temple," Luna said.

"This is it?"

"This is the entrance." She walked toward the archway. "The real Temple is underground. Your father built it beneath the ruins. Wards hide it from every supernatural radar. No one can find it unless they know where to look."

"How did you know where to look?"

"I helped build it."

She touched the archway. The vines recoiled. The moss peeled away. Symbols carved into the stone began to glow—crescent moons and wolf's heads and patterns Liam didn't recognize.

The ground trembled.

A crack split the center of the clearing. The grass parted. Stone shifted. A staircase spiraled downward, disappearing into darkness. Light flickered from below. Ancient. Warm. Welcoming.

"The Temple recognizes the bloodline," Luna said. "It knows you're here."

Liam stared into the darkness. His father had built this. His father had died for this. Thirty years, waiting for a son he never met to wake up and claim his legacy.

"What if I'm not ready?" Liam asked.

"You're not." Luna stepped onto the first stair. "But you have six days to become ready."

She descended. Liam followed. The staircase wound deeper and deeper, spiraling into the earth. The walls were lined with carvings—battles, kings, wolves with eyes like burning stars. History. His history. The history of every Wolf King who had come before him.

At the bottom, a door. Iron. Massive. Covered in the same symbols as the archway above.

Luna pressed her palm to the center. The symbols flared. The door groaned. It swung open, revealing a chamber that took Liam's breath away.

A library. Thousands of books, stacked floor to ceiling. Weapons on the walls—silver swords, obsidian daggers, bows carved from bone. Training dummies in the corner, scarred and battered. A fireplace crackled at the far end, casting shadows that danced like living things.

And in the center of the room, a pedestal. On top of it, a sword. Black blade. Silver hilt. A crescent moon etched into the crossguard.

"Your father's blade," Luna said. "Moonfang. It's been waiting for you."

Liam walked toward it. His hand reached out. His fingers brushed the hilt.

The world exploded.

Pain. Vision. Sound. Everything at once. He saw his father's face—not in a photograph, not in a memory, but *real*. Alive. Alexander stood before him, tall and strong, eyes burning with the same fire Liam felt in his chest.

*Son.*

The word echoed through his skull.

*You have six days. The Council knows you're alive. The Blood Moon is coming. And somewhere in this Temple, I left you everything you need to win.*

Alexander smiled. It was sad. Ancient. Familiar.

*But first, you have to survive the night.*

The vision shattered.

Liam stumbled backward, gasping. The sword clattered to the floor. The mark on his chest blazed like a brand.

"What happened?" Luna grabbed his shoulders. "What did you see?"

"My father." Liam's voice shook. "He said I have to survive the night."

Luna's face went pale. "The wards—" She spun around. Her eyes scanned the walls. "Something triggered them."

A crash echoed from above. The archway. Someone was breaking through.

"How did they find us?" Liam demanded.

Luna's expression twisted. Guilt. Fear. Something else he couldn't name. "Because the Temple has another failsafe. One Alexander built without telling me."

"What failsafe?"

She met his eyes. "When the heir touches Moonfang, it sends a signal. A beacon. Every ally. Every enemy. Everyone with Wolf King blood will know where you are."

"You're saying my father made me a target?"

"I'm saying your father trusted that you would be strong enough to survive."

Another crash. Louder. Closer. The door at the top of the stairs splintered.

Liam grabbed Moonfang. The blade hummed in his hand. Alive. Hungry.

"Six days," he whispered.

Footsteps thundered down the staircase.

Luna positioned herself beside him, her body shifting, her eyes glowing silver. "Ready?"

"Not even close."

The door exploded inward.

And the night began.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Weight of Bloodlines

I took her hand.

It was probably stupid. Probably suicidal. Probably exactly what my father would have done, according to Marcus's journals. But the alternative was lying in a stream until the rogues found me, and I'd already decided I wasn't dying tonight.

Her grip was stronger than it looked. She pulled me to my feet without effort, and I noticed for the first time how tall she was. Nearly my height. Silver hair tied back in a practical knot. Eyes that shifted between blue and gray depending on how the moonlight hit them.

"You're bleeding," she said.

"Feels like I've been bleeding for days."

"You have." She turned and started walking. "Keep up. The forest gets worse after midnight."

I followed. My legs screamed. My ribs screamed. Everything screamed. But there was something in her voice that brooked no argument—the same authority Marcus had carried, but older. Colder. Like she'd been giving orders for centuries.

"You knew him," I said, half-stumbling over a root. "My father."

"Knew him. Loved him. Buried him." She didn't look back. "All in the same week."

The words hit like a punch to the throat. "I'm sorry."

"Don't be. It was a war. People die in wars." She stopped at a massive oak tree, its roots twisted into the earth like grasping fingers. She pressed her palm against the bark and murmured something I couldn't understand. The language was old. Older than English. Older than anything I'd ever heard.

A section of the tree's trunk shimmered and dissolved.

Behind it: a tunnel. Stone walls. Torches that lit themselves as we approached.

"Welcome to the Moonlit Temple," Luna said. "Or what's left of it."

---

The tunnel opened into a cavern the size of a cathedral.

Pillars of obsidian rose toward a ceiling I couldn't see. The floor was polished black stone, veined with silver that glowed faintly underfoot. Altars lined the walls—dozens of them, each carved with crescent moons and wolf heads and symbols I didn't recognize.

But the temple was empty.

Not empty like abandoned. Empty like something had been ripped out of it. Broken altars. Scorch marks on the pillars. A hole in the far wall where something massive had crashed through.

"The Council did this," Luna said, walking toward the central altar. "Thirty years ago. When Alexander refused to bow."

"Why?"

"Because he was the Wolf King. And the Wolf King doesn't bow to anyone." She stopped at the altar and turned to face me. "But that's not the whole story. The Council didn't attack because Alexander refused to kneel. They attacked because he found something. Something that would have ended the war forever."

She pulled a chain from beneath her shirt. A key hung from it—black iron, old, covered in rust and blood.

"What is that?"

"The location of Alexander's greatest secret." She held it out to me. "And the reason the Council's been hunting you since the day you were born."

I didn't take it. "I don't understand. If they knew about me, why wait until now?"

"Because the bloodline couldn't transfer until you turned twenty-one. Before that, you were just a human with potential. After..." She gestured at the mark still glowing on my chest. "After, you're the heir. And the heir can access what Alexander hid."

I stared at the key. It seemed so small. So ordinary. A piece of rusted iron that had cost my father his life.

"What did he hide?"

"The truth about the war." Luna's eyes met mine. "The truth about why the Council really wants the Wolf King dead. It's not about power, Liam. It's about control. Alexander discovered that the First Council—the original seven alphas—made a pact with something dark. Something that's been feeding on our bloodline for a thousand years."

"Feeding?"

"The soul of every Wolf King isn't passed down naturally. It's taken. Harvested. Used to fuel the Council's immortality." Her voice dropped. "And the blood moon ceremony? It's not a transfer of power. It's a sacrifice. Your soul for their continued existence."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

"That's why they want me dead before the ceremony."

"That's why they want you dead before the ceremony." She stepped closer. "But it's also why you can beat them. Alexander knew. He found proof. And he hid it somewhere only his heir can reach."

She pressed the key into my palm.

Cold. Heavy. It hummed against my skin like a living thing.

"Three days," Luna said. "That's how long until the blood moon. Three days to find what Alexander hid and use it to destroy the Council forever."

"Three days." I laughed, but there was no humor in it. "I can't even shift yet."

"You will." Her voice was certain. "You don't have a choice."

She led me to a chamber behind the altar. A circular room with a pool of water in the center—black, still, reflecting nothing. The walls were covered in murals. Wolves fighting. Wolves dying. Wolves kneeling before shadowy figures that wore crowns of bone.

"This is where Alexander trained," Luna said. "This pool. It's connected to the moon. To the bloodline. If you want to learn to shift, you start here."

I looked at the pool. It seemed to pulse. To breathe.

"What do I do?"

"Get in."

"Seriously?"

"Seriously." She crossed her arms. "The water will test you. It will push against your humanity until you either break through or drown. And if you drown..." She shrugged. "Well. At least it'll be quick."

I stared at the water.

Then at the key in my hand.

Then at the woman who'd loved my father and watched him die.

"Three days," I said.

"Three days."

I stepped into the pool.

---

The water was ice.

Not metaphorically. Literally ice. Crystals formed on my skin instantly, spreading across my arms, my chest, my face. I tried to gasp and my lungs filled with cold that burned worse than fire.

*Let go.*

Alexander's voice. Calm. Patient.

*You're fighting the change. That's why it hurts. Stop fighting.*

I couldn't stop fighting. Fighting was all I had. Fighting was what kept me human. What kept me *me*.

*You think being a wolf makes you less human?* Alexander laughed, a warm sound in the freezing dark. *Son, I was never more human than when I was running under the moon. The wolf doesn't erase you. It completes you.*

The ice cracked.

I felt it—a fracture running through my chest, my spine, my skull. Something was breaking. Something was changing.

*Let go.*

The pain was unbearable.

*Let go.*

I stopped fighting.

---

I woke on the stone floor, gasping, naked, covered in steam.

Luna stood over me, holding a towel. She didn't look away. Didn't blink. Just watched me with those ancient eyes.

"How long?" I managed.

"Four hours."

"Four hours?" I sat up. My body felt different. Lighter. Stronger. The mark on my chest was pulsing with a soft silver light. "Did I...?"

"You shifted." She tossed me the towel. "For about three seconds. Then you passed out."

"Three seconds?"

"Three seconds is more than most heirs manage on their first try." She almost smiled. Almost. "You have your father's stubbornness. And his talent."

I dried off, found clothes waiting for me—simple black pants and a shirt that fit perfectly. Luna had prepared for this. She'd been preparing for thirty years.

"I need to see the proof," I said. "What Alexander hid. Where is it?"

"Tomorrow. You need rest."

"I don't have time for rest."

"You have three days." Her voice hardened. "If you collapse from exhaustion, you'll have zero. Rest. Now. I'll wake you at dawn."

I wanted to argue. But my body chose that moment to remind me that I'd been tortured, shot, chased through a forest, and nearly drowned in magical ice water. My legs buckled.

Luna caught me.

"Stubborn," she muttered. "Just like him."

She dragged me to a small chamber with a bed—actual bed, with blankets and a pillow. I collapsed onto it before I could form a protest.

"Luna."

"Mm?"

"The Council. They'll find this place eventually."

"I know."

"How do we stop them?"

She stood in the doorway, silhouetted by torchlight. For a moment, she looked almost young. Almost hopeful. Almost human.

"We don't," she said. "We let them find us."

The door closed.

Darkness.

And somewhere, deep in my blood, Alexander's voice whispered:

*Smart woman. She knows a trap when she sees one.*

I slept.

---

I dreamed of a forest on fire.

Wolves screamed. The moon bled. And at the center of it all, a man with my face stood over a body—a woman with silver hair, her chest torn open, her eyes still open.

*This is what happens when you lose,* the man said. *This is what happens when you fail.*

I woke with a gasp.

Luna stood at the foot of my bed. Her face was pale. Her hands were shaking.

"They're here," she said.

"How many?"

"Too many."

I stood. My body ached, but the rest had helped. I felt the wolf beneath my skin, waiting. Hungry.

"Then let's give them what they want."

Luna's eyes met mine.

For the first time, she looked afraid.

"Liam," she said. "There's something I didn't tell you."

"What?"

"The key. The proof Alexander hid." She swallowed. "It's not just information. It's a weapon. And the only way to use it..."

She didn't finish.

The temple shook. Stone cracked. Somewhere above us, something howled.

"We don't have time for this," I said. "Finish the sentence."

Luna's hand went to her stomach. A gesture so small I almost missed it.

"The weapon is a child," she said. "Alexander's child. My child."

The world stopped.

"Your..." I couldn't form the words. "You mean I have a...?"

"A sister." Luna's voice broke. "She's seventeen. She's been in hiding since before she was born. And the Council knows exactly where she is."

The temple shook again. Harder.

"Where?"

"Somewhere safe." Luna's eyes glistened. "Somewhere they can't reach her. But if I go to her, they'll follow. If you go to her..."

"They'll follow too."

"Yes."

I looked at my hands. The mark on my chest. The key that hummed against my skin.

I had a sister.

I had a family.

And the Council wanted to take that away from me too.

"Where is she?" I asked.

Luna told me.

And for the first time since this nightmare began, I felt something other than fear.

I felt hope.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Traitor's Name

The Temple's underground chambers stretched beneath us like the ribs of a buried beast. Luna led me through corridors carved from obsidian, her silver hair catching torchlight that shouldn't exist this far underground.

"The Council knows about this place," I said, my voice echoing off walls covered in wolf carvings. "They've known for centuries."

"Of course they have." She didn't turn around. "They built it."

I stopped. "What?"

Luna paused at a junction where three tunnels branched like arteries. She pressed her palm against the wall, and stone groaned as a hidden door slid open. "The Moonlit Temple was never a secret to the Council. It was a prison. A cage for the Wolf King's bloodline, disguised as a sanctuary."

"Then why bring me here?"

"Because I'm not taking you to the Temple." She stepped through the doorway. "I'm taking you to what's *under* it."

The passage beyond was different. Older. The walls weren't carved—they were grown. Roots twisted through cracks in the stone, pulsing with faint golden light. The air smelled of earth and something metallic. Blood, maybe. Or silver.

"How far down does this go?" I asked.

"Far enough that the Council forgot about it." Luna's voice dropped. "Alexander built this place in the last year of his life. When he realized the Council was planning to kill him."

I thought of Marcus bleeding out in the alley. Of the twisted bodies in the basement. "They've been killing my family for thirty years."

"Yes."

"And you let them?"

She turned. Her eyes weren't glowing anymore. They were just eyes. Human eyes. Tired eyes. "I was in a cage, Liam. For twenty-eight years. Silver chains through my bones. They kept me alive because killing me would have triggered Alexander's bloodline protections. But they made sure I couldn't help anyone."

The tunnel opened into a chamber that stole my breath.

A library. But not like any library I'd ever seen. Books floated in crystalline spheres, suspended by nothing. Scrolls covered the walls, their ink glowing faintly. In the center, a table of black stone held a single object—a dagger with a blade that seemed to drink light.

"Alexander's research," Luna said. "Everything he learned about the bloodline. About the blood moon. About the enemy that the Council has been hiding for a thousand years."

"Enemy?"

"The Council isn't just a government, Liam. They're a prison guard. Their job is to keep the werewolf species contained, controlled, and enslaved to a cycle of violence they designed." She moved to the table, her fingers hovering over the dagger. "But they serve someone else. Someone older. Someone who wants the Wolf King bloodline extinct because you're the only thing that can threaten them."

"Who?"

Luna looked at me. "The first wolf."

The words hit like a physical blow. "The first—"

"The original alpha. The one who made the first pact with the moon. The one who created the Council specifically to hunt down and eliminate any bloodline that could challenge his authority." She picked up the dagger, and the blade flared with silver light. "Your father discovered this. He was going to expose it at the blood moon ceremony. But someone betrayed him."

"Someone on the Council?"

"Someone closer." Luna's voice cracked. "His beta. His brother in everything but blood. The man who led the attack that killed your mother, who held Alexander down while they drove silver through his heart."

My hands clenched into fists. "Give me a name."

"Damian Blackwood."

The name meant nothing. But the way Luna said it—like the word itself was poison—told me everything I needed to know.

"Where is he?"

"Leading the hunt for you." She set the dagger down. "He's the Council's attack dog now. The one who trains their enforcers. The one who personally oversees the elimination of any Wolf King heir." Her eyes met mine. "He's killed seven of your brothers and sisters over the past thirty years. Distant cousins. Bastard children. Anyone who showed even a hint of Alexander's bloodline."

"Seven." The number felt like acid on my tongue.

"Eight, if you count the one who died last week. A seventeen-year-old girl in Seattle." Luna's composure broke, just for a second. "I watched her die. Through the bars of my cage. I watched him kill her, and I couldn't do anything."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you need to understand what you're fighting. This isn't a war between clans, Liam. This is a purge. The Council has been systematically eliminating any werewolf who shows independence, any pack that refuses to bow, any bloodline that carries the old power." She stepped closer. "You're not just the Wolf King's son. You're the last chance for every werewolf who wants to be free."

The mark on my chest burned. Alexander's voice stirred in the back of my skull, but he didn't speak. He was listening. Watching. Waiting to see what I would do.

"I need to learn to shift," I said. "Fully. On command."

"Six days."

"Then we start now."

Luna nodded. She moved to a section of the wall where roots formed a spiral pattern. She pressed her palm against the center, and the roots writhed, pulling back to reveal a hidden alcove.

Inside hung a single item—a necklace made of wolf teeth, each one carved with runes that glowed faintly silver.

"Alexander's first shift," Luna said. "He was fifteen. His wolf came early, violently, during a full moon. He killed three people before he regained control." She lifted the necklace. "He wore this for the rest of his life. A reminder of what he could become if he lost control."

"Charming."

"It's also a key." She handed it to me. The teeth clacked together, warm against my palm. "Put it on."

I hesitated. "What does it unlock?"

"Not a door. A memory." Luna's eyes glowed. "Alexander's memory. Of his first controlled shift. Of the technique he developed to master the wolf instead of fighting it."

I slipped the necklace over my head. The teeth settled against my chest, right over the mark. Cold. Then hot. Then—

*The forest. Different from the one outside. Older. The trees were massive, their trunks wider than cars. Moonlight filtered through leaves that glowed like emeralds. A figure stood in a clearing—tall, broad-shouldered, with silver hair and eyes that burned like molten gold.*

*Alexander.*

*He turned to face me. No, not me. The memory. He was looking at whoever wore the necklace.*

*"So you found it." His voice was exactly like the one in my head, but younger. Softer. "Good. That means you're ready to learn."*

*"Learn what?" I asked. Or tried to. My mouth didn't move. I was just a ghost in this memory, watching.*

*"The first lesson," Alexander said. "The one the Council doesn't want you to know." He raised his hand, and his skin rippled. Bones shifted. Fur sprouted. But it wasn't violent like my partial shifts. It was controlled. Deliberate. Like he was putting on a coat instead of being devoured by one.*

*In three seconds, he was a wolf. Silver-furred, massive, with eyes that still held human intelligence.*

*Then he spoke. In my head. "Shifting isn't about fighting the wolf, heir. It's about becoming one. The wolf isn't a monster inside you. It's a partner. A weapon. A tool. You don't chain it. You command it."*

*The memory wolf walked toward me. "But to command it, you first have to prove you're worthy."*

*His eyes flared. "And that means hunting the traitor."*

The memory shattered. I was back in the underground chamber, gasping, the necklace burning against my skin.

Luna caught me as I stumbled. "What did you see?"

"The first lesson." I grabbed her arm. "He said I have to hunt the traitor."

"Damian."

"No." I shook my head, the vision still fresh. "He said 'the traitor.' Singular. Like there's only one."

Luna's face went pale. "Liam, there are dozens of traitors on the Council. Hundreds of enforcers who've sworn loyalty to—"

"Alexander wasn't talking about the Council." I pulled away, pacing the chamber. "He was talking about someone specific. Someone close to him." I stopped. "You said his beta betrayed him. You said Damian held him down."

"Yes."

"But who gave the order?"

Luna's mouth opened. Closed. For the first time since I'd met her, she looked afraid.

"I don't know," she whispered. "I always assumed it was the Council. The alpha of the Council. But Alexander never said. He never told me who—"

"Because he wanted me to find out myself." I touched the necklace. "The memory said I have to prove I'm worthy. That means I can't just be told. I have to earn it."

Luna grabbed my shoulders. Her grip was iron. "If you go after Damian now, you'll die. He's been preparing for this fight for thirty years. He knows every trick, every technique, every weakness of the Wolf King bloodline."

"Then I'll need to learn new tricks."

"You have six days."

"Then I'd better get started."

I moved toward the tunnel, but Luna's voice stopped me.

"Liam. There's something else. Something I didn't tell you about the blood moon ceremony."

I turned.

"It's not just about mastering the wolf." Her voice dropped. "If you fail, if the wolf takes control, you won't just lose yourself. The first wolf will use your body as a vessel. He'll be reborn through you."

"Reborn?"

"The ceremony was designed by the Council. By the first wolf's servants. It's not a test of worthiness. It's a trap." Her eyes glowed brighter. "If you can't claim the Wolf King's power by the blood moon, the first wolf will claim you instead."
