The mark on my chest pulsed. Alexander's voice finally spoke, heavy with exhaustion.

*She's right. I never told you because I hoped you'd never need to know. But the blood moon isn't just your deadline. It's his. The first wolf has been waiting for a millennium. Waiting for a bloodline strong enough to hold his power. Yours is the strongest he's found.*

"Then I'll make sure he doesn't get it."

*Bold words, boy. But words don't win wars.*

"Neither does hiding in memories." I looked at Luna. "Take me to the surface. I need to find Damian."

"You can't—"

"I'm not going to fight him." I smiled, and it felt wrong on my face. "I'm going to learn from him. Every mistake he makes. Every opening he gives me. I'm going to study him like he studied my father."

Luna stared at me. Then, slowly, she smiled too.

"You really are Alexander's son."

"I'm just getting started."

We climbed back through the tunnels, through the roots and the obsidian and the carvings of wolves that seemed to watch us pass. The necklace thumped against my chest with every step, a drumbeat counting down to war.

When we reached the surface, the moon was setting. Dawn bled across the horizon, painting the sky in shades of gold and red.

"One more thing," Luna said. "Damian isn't

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Weight of Crowns

The Temple of Echoed Silence had no windows.

Liam stood in the center of its main chamber—a circular room carved from obsidian that drank the torchlight like water. The walls pulsed with ancient runes, each one thrumming in time with his heartbeat. Behind him, the heavy stone door had sealed shut the moment he'd entered.

"You have one hour."

The High Priestess's voice came from everywhere and nowhere. He'd met her only briefly—a woman so old her eyes had gone white, her skin draped in ceremonial scars that mapped the constellations. She'd touched his forehead once, whispered something in a language that made his blood sing, and vanished into the shadows.

"Find the truth that hides in silence," she'd said. "Or the silence will become your tomb."

Liam exhaled. Great. More cryptic nonsense.

But he understood the stakes. Since arriving at the Moonlit Temple three days ago, Luna had trained him relentlessly. She'd taught him to shift on command—first the claws, then the partial transformation, then the full wolf. She'd taught him to read scents on the wind, to feel the earth's pulse through his pads, to hunt and fight and survive.

But she hadn't taught him this.

This was the Temple's test. Every Wolf King candidate had to face it. Luna had told him the night before, her silver eyes unreadable in the firelight. "Alexander passed it in fourteen minutes," she'd said. "Your grandfather took three hours. Your great-grandfather never came out."

"Reassuring," Liam had muttered.

Now he stood in the darkness, waiting for something to happen.

The runes on the walls flickered. Once. Twice. Then went dark.

Complete blackness. The kind that pressed against his eyes, that made him question whether he'd gone blind. Liam held his ground, forcing his breathing to stay even. Luna had warned him about this part. "The temple feeds on fear. If you panic, it will consume you."

"Consumerism. Got it."

"You joke too much."

"It's my coping mechanism."

She'd almost smiled. Almost. Then her face had gone serious again. "When you enter the silence, you will face your truest enemy."

"Which is?"

"You'll know when you see it."

He'd thought she meant the rogues. Or the Council. Or maybe Marcus's killer—the one who'd thrown that silver blade through his mentor's chest. But as the darkness stretched on, Liam began to understand.

The truest enemy was himself.

*You're not ready,* a voice whispered. Not Alexander's. His own. The part of him that had spent twenty-one years believing he was nothing. *You can't lead anyone. You can barely control your transformations. You're going to fail.*

"Shut up."

*They'll die because of you. Luna. The temple survivors. Everyone who believes in you.*

"I said shut up."

*Marcus died for you. What did he get for his loyalty? A knife in the heart.*

Liam's claws extended. Unbidden. The silver tips gleamed in the darkness, catching light that didn't exist. He slashed at the air, at the voice, at the doubt that had followed him since childhood.

The runes flared back to life.

And he saw her.

A woman stood at the far end of the chamber. Tall. Dark hair. Eyes the color of winter storms. She wore a leather jacket and combat boots, and she was pointing a gun at his head.

His mother.

"You're not real," Liam said.

"I'm as real as you let me be." Her voice was exactly how he remembered it—the same rasp from too many cigarettes, the same hard edge that had never softened for anyone. "You've been running from me your whole life."

"I haven't run from anything."

"Really?" She stepped closer. The gun didn't waver. "Then why haven't you tried to find out who killed me?"

Liam's jaw tightened. "I was eight years old. I didn't even know you were a werewolf."

"You knew something. You saw the scratches on my arms. The way I locked myself in the basement during full moons. You knew, and you chose to forget."

"I was a child."

"You're not a child anymore." She stopped three feet away. Close enough that he could see the scar under her chin—the one she'd gotten when a rogue had slashed her throat and she'd healed wrong. "You have the power now. The mark. The bloodline. You could find my killer in a day if you wanted to."

"Then tell me who it was."

She smiled. Cold. Familiar. "You already know."

And suddenly Liam did.

The memory surfaced like a body breaking through ice. He'd buried it so deep that even Alexander hadn't been able to dig it up. But now, in the silence of the temple, with his mother's ghost staring him down, it came rushing back.

The night she died.

He'd been hiding in the closet. He'd heard the fight—the snarling, the crashing furniture, the screams. He'd covered his ears and squeezed his eyes shut and prayed for it to stop. Then the door had opened, and a shape had blocked the light, and a voice had said, *"Don't come out until morning, little prince. It's for your own good."*

He'd recognized that voice.

"Marcus," Liam breathed.

His mother's ghost nodded. "Your mentor. Your protector. The man who taught you everything you know." Her finger tightened on the trigger. "He killed me because I refused to hand you over to the Council. He killed me because he loved your father more than he loved the truth."

"No." Liam shook his head. "That's not possible. Marcus saved me. He died for me."

"He died because he knew you'd learn the truth eventually. Because his loyalty to Alexander was stronger than his guilt." Her eyes bored into his. "He was the Council's spy from the beginning. The one who reported your location every year. The one who kept you weak so you'd never awaken."

The mark on his chest burned. Alexander's voice surged up from the depths of his blood, roaring with denial. *She lies. Marcus was my brother. He would never—*

"She doesn't lie," Liam said quietly. "She can't. The temple only shows truth."

Alexander went silent.

And in that silence, every piece clicked into place. Marcus's eagerness to train him. Marcus's insistence that he stay hidden. Marcus's conveniently timed death that had made Liam trust him completely.

"He played me," Liam said. "From the beginning. He played all of us."

"The best lies are the ones we want to believe." His mother lowered the gun. "You wanted a father figure. He gave you one. You wanted to be special. He confirmed it. You wanted to believe the world made sense. He created a narrative that did." She tilted her head. "But narratives aren't truth. And truth is what you came here to find."

"Why show me this now? Why not before?"

"Because you weren't ready to accept it." She stepped forward, placed her hand on his cheek. Her palm was cold. Dead. "You are the Wolf King, Liam. Not because of your bloodline. Not because of your father's legacy. Because you have the strength to bear the weight of ugly truths and still keep fighting."

"Even if it means hating someone I loved?"

"Especially then." She kissed his forehead. "That's what makes a king."

The temple began to dissolve. The obsidian walls shimmered, turned to mist, faded into torchlight. Liam found himself back in the main chamber, the High Priestess watching him with those white, knowing eyes.

"One hour and eleven minutes," she said. "Not your father's record. But respectable."

"Marcus," Liam said. "He was Council."

"Yes."

"You knew."

"The temple knows everything." She stepped closer, her bare feet silent on the stone floor. "The question is: what will you do with this knowledge?"

Liam's claws extended. Retracted. Extended again.

"I'm going to find out who else betrayed us," he said. "I'm going to hunt every member of the Council. And I'm going to make them pay."

"The Council is not your enemy."

"Then who is?"

The High Priestess smiled. It was not a kind smile. "The Council serves a master. A creature older than any werewolf clan. A being that has waited centuries for the Wolf King's bloodline to reach its full potential." She leaned in, her breath cold against his ear. "The Blood Moon is not a ceremony, Liam. It's a summoning. And you are the sacrifice."

Liam's blood turned to ice.

"Seven days," he whispered.

"Less than that now." The High Priestess pulled back. "The woman you trust—Luna—she knows more than she's told you. Ask her about the night Alexander died. Ask her why she really came back."

The stone door groaned open.

Beyond it, Luna stood waiting. Her silver hair caught the torchlight, her moonlit eyes fixed on him with an expression he couldn't read. She looked worried. She looked guilty.

She looked like she'd been listening.

"What did the temple show you?" she asked.

Liam stepped through the doorway. The mark on his chest pulsed with something dark and hungry. Alexander's voice had gone silent, but he could feel the ancient wolf stirring beneath his skin, eager for blood.

"It showed me that I've been running from the wrong people," Liam said. "And that you have some explaining to do."

Luna's face went pale.

"Not here," she said. "The walls have ears."

"Then find somewhere they don't." He walked past her, his footsteps echoing in the narrow corridor. "Because if I find out you've been lying to me about my father's death, I swear to whatever gods are watching—I'll make the Council look merciful."

He didn't look back.

But he heard her whisper his name.

And he heard the fear in her voice.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Weight of Crowns

The Moonlit Temple wasn't a temple.

It was a basement beneath a laundromat on the south side of the city, hidden behind a wall of dryers that hummed with the smell of detergent and old coins. Luna pressed her palm against a section of tile that looked exactly like every other section of tile, and the wall slid open with a grinding sound that made Liam's teeth ache.

"You're joking," he said.

"I never joke about safe houses." Luna stepped through the opening, silver hair catching the dim light from inside. "Your father picked this location himself. Said no one would ever think to look for the Wolf King's heir in a place that smells like fabric softener."

Liam followed her into the tunnel beyond. The walls were raw earth, held back by wooden beams that looked centuries old. Roots dangled from the ceiling like dead fingers. Every step kicked up dust that tasted of copper and old magic.

The tunnel opened into a chamber that made him stop breathing.

The ceiling arched thirty feet overhead, painted with a mural of wolves running beneath a blood-red moon. Candles floated in midair, hundreds of them, their flames burning silver instead of gold. Bookshelves lined every wall, stuffed with leather-bound volumes and scrolls that crackled with age. In the center of the room stood a stone table, and on that table lay a sword.

Not just any sword.

The blade was black, so black it seemed to drink the light around it. The hilt was wrapped in leather that had been stained dark by countless hands. And the pommel—a wolf's head with ruby eyes that glowed like embers in a dying fire.

"That's not possible," Liam whispered.

"It's the Fang of the First King." Luna walked to the table, her reflection rippling across the blade's surface. "Forged from the tooth of the original werewolf, the one who drank from the moon's blood and became the first of our kind. It's been waiting here for three hundred years."

"Waiting for what?"

She turned to face him. The candlelight carved shadows across her face, made her look older than she had in the alley. Ancient. Worn. "Waiting for the heir who could claim it."

Liam's hand moved before his brain caught up. He reached for the hilt, fingers closing around the leather grip—

Fire.

Not the pain of his transformation. Something worse. Something that reached into his chest and wrapped around his heart and squeezed. The mark over his chest blazed so brightly he could see it through his shirt. The wolf in his head roared. Alexander's voice thundered through his skull.

*DROP IT.*

He let go. The sword clattered against the stone. Liam stumbled backward, gasping, his hand smoking where the leather had touched his skin.

"I didn't—"

"You're not ready." Luna's voice was flat. Clinical. "The Fang only accepts a king who has mastered his wolf. You've barely spoken to yours."

Liam cradled his burned hand. The skin was already healing, the blisters fading, but the memory of that fire stayed. "You could have warned me."

"Would you have listened?"

He wanted to argue. But she was right. He'd spent the last five days running, fighting, barely surviving. He hadn't spent a single moment trying to understand what he was becoming.

"I need answers," he said. "Marcus said my father had a plan. Said there was a way to survive the blood moon ceremony without losing myself."

Luna walked to the bookshelves, running her fingers along the spines. "Your father believed that the Wolf King curse wasn't a curse at all. That it was a gift, corrupted by the Council's greed. He spent twenty years trying to find a way to separate the man from the beast without destroying either."

"Did he find it?"

She pulled a book from the shelf. The cover was cracked leather, the pages yellowed and brittle. She held it out to him. "He wrote everything down. Every theory. Every failure. Every breakthrough. It's all here."

Liam took the book. The cover had no title, no markings. Just a symbol burned into the leather—the same crescent moon and wolf's head that marked his chest.

"Read it," Luna said. "But not here. We don't have time."

"What do you mean?"

She gestured to the candles. They were flickering. Not from wind—there was no wind in this chamber. Flickering like something was moving through the air, disturbing the flames.

"They found us. The Council's trackers. They've been following your scent since the bridge."

Liam's blood went cold. "How long?"

"Ten minutes. Maybe less." Luna walked to the wall behind the table, pressed her palm against a section that looked solid. The stone groaned and shifted, revealing a narrow passage. "This leads to the old subway tunnels. From there you can reach the edge of the city before dawn."

"You're not coming?"

"I'll hold them off."

"No." The word came out harder than he intended. "I'm not losing anyone else."

Luna's eyes softened. For a moment, she looked almost human. "Your father said the same thing to me once. The night he died." She stepped closer, close enough that he could smell the moonlight in her hair. "I've been alive for four hundred years, Liam. I've watched everyone I've ever loved turn to dust. You're not losing me. You're giving me a reason to keep fighting."

Something crashed above them. The ceiling shook, dust raining down from the roots. Voices in the distance. Snarls.

"Go," Luna said.

Liam clutched the book to his chest. "How do I find you again?"

"You don't. When you're ready, you'll find me." She smiled, that same sad, ancient smile. "The moon always calls its children home."

He wanted to say something. Anything. But the words wouldn't come. So he turned and ran into the darkness of the passage, the book pressed against his heart, the sound of Luna's voice rising behind him in a language he didn't recognize.

The tunnel was narrow, claustrophobic, the walls slick with moisture. He ran blind, one hand on the wall, the other clutching the book. Roots grabbed at his ankles. Water splashed beneath his feet. Somewhere behind him, he heard the crash of stone on stone, the howl of wolves, a scream that might have been human.

He didn't stop.

The tunnel opened into a subway station. Abandoned. Graffiti-covered. A single train sat on the tracks, doors open, lights flickering. Liam jumped aboard, collapsed into a seat, his lungs burning, his hands shaking.

The book sat in his lap. Heavy. Ancient. Full of his father's secrets.

He opened it.

The first page was blank.

He flipped to the second. Blank. The third. Fourth. Fifth. Every page empty, yellowed, covered in nothing but the ghost of old ink that had long since faded.

"No," he whispered. "No, no, no."

He held the book up to the flickering light. Ran his fingers over the pages. Pressed his nose to them, searching for the smell of ink, of writing, of anything.

Nothing.

Two hundred pages of nothing.

The train doors slid shut. The engine hummed to life. Liam stared at the empty book in his hands, and for the first time since his transformation, he felt something worse than fear.

He felt hopeless.

The train lurched forward, carrying him through the darkness of the tunnels, away from the temple, away from Luna, away from every answer he'd been given.

And then the train stopped.

Not gradually. Not with a screech of brakes. It stopped dead, like something had reached out and grabbed it mid-motion. The lights cut out. The emergency systems didn't kick in. Just darkness. Silence. The sound of his own heartbeat.

The door at the far end of the car slid open.

Footsteps. Slow. Deliberate. The click of heels on metal floor.

A figure stepped into the car. Female. Tall. Dressed in black that seemed to absorb the darkness around her. Her eyes glowed red.

"Hello, Liam."

He knew that voice. He'd heard it in his nightmares for the last five days.

The woman who had killed Marcus.

"I've been looking for you," she said, and her smile was all teeth. "The Council sends their regards."

Liam reached for the Fang of the First King.

His hand closed on empty air.

He'd left it on the stone table. He'd left it behind.

The red-eyed woman laughed. "You're not a king," she said. "You're not even a wolf."

She raised her hand. Silver chains shot from her palm, wrapping around his throat, his wrists, his chest. They burned. They bit into his skin like living things.

"You're just a boy with a dead father and a dying bloodline."

The chains tightened.

Liam couldn't breathe.

Couldn't shift.

Couldn't move.

The woman leaned close, close enough that he could smell the death on her breath. "I'm going to enjoy breaking you."

And then the train plunged into darkness, and the last thing Liam heard was her laughter echoing off the walls of the tunnel, following him into the void.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Council's Executioner

The iron chains bit into Liam's wrists, silver threaded through every link. The pain was constant now, a low hum beneath his skin that spiked every time he moved. He'd been hanging from the wall of the underground cell for what felt like hours, though the lack of windows made it impossible to tell.

Luna stood across from him, arms crossed, her silver hair caked with dried blood from the temple's collapse. She hadn't spoken since they'd dragged them both down here. Just stared at the floor with empty eyes.

"You lied to me," Liam said. His voice cracked. He'd been screaming for a while. The silver did that—made everything raw and exposed.

"I protected you." She didn't look up.

"You said the temple was safe. You said the Moon Guard were allies." He pulled against the chains. The silver burned deeper. "The moment we walked in, they handed us to the Council like we were criminals."

"Because we are." Luna finally met his eyes. "To them, at least. You're a bloodline heir without proper registration. I'm a rogue alpha's former mate. By their laws, we're both marked for execution."

Liam stopped struggling. "Execution?"

"The High Council doesn't take chances. Rogue bloodlines are threats. Uncontrolled transformations are threats. A Wolf King who doesn't know his own power?" She shook her head. "That's the greatest threat of all."

The cell door creaked open. A figure stepped through—tall, broad-shouldered, wearing the black armor of the Council's enforcers. His face was hidden behind a wolf-skull helmet, eye sockets glowing with faint blue light.

"Liam Blackwood." His voice was distorted, metallic. "You have been charged with unauthorized bloodline activation, concealment of royal lineage, and conspiracy against the High Council. How do you plead?"

"I plead that you're making a huge mistake."

The enforcer tilted his head. "That's not one of the options."

Luna stepped forward, chains dragging across the stone floor. "Marcus was a member of the Council. He died protecting this boy. Does that mean nothing to you?"

"Marcus was found guilty of harboring a rogue heir. His death was a mercy."

"A mercy?" Liam's blood boiled. The wolf stirred beneath his skin, pushing against the silver chains. "He was trying to save me from you people."

The enforcer turned to face him fully. "The boy speaks. Good. I was beginning to think Marcus had hidden you so well that you'd lost your voice." He walked closer, boots echoing in the small space. "Do you know who I am?"

"The bastard who's about to get his face rearranged."

The enforcer laughed. Low. Deep. The sound of someone who enjoyed pain. "I am Kael, the Council's Executioner. I've put down seventeen rogue alphas in the last five years. Three of them were Wolf Kings who couldn't control their bloodlines." He stopped inches from Liam's face. "You'll be number eighteen."

Liam's vision hazed. The wolf clawed at his insides, demanding release. *Let me out,* Alexander's voice growled. *I'll tear him apart.*

*And then what?* Liam shot back. *More guards? More silver?*

*Better to die fighting than hanging from a wall.*

Kael reached up and removed his helmet. The face beneath was scarred, one eye milky white, the other burning with cold intelligence. He looked maybe forty, but wolves aged differently. He could be a hundred. He could be two hundred.

"Your father had the same look," Kael said quietly. "That desperate defiance. The belief that his bloodline made him untouchable." He shook his head. "I watched him die, Liam. Watched the Council's best soldiers drag him down through sheer numbers. He killed seventeen of them before they took his head."

Liam's throat tightened. "You're lying."

"I was there. I was one of the seventeen who survived." Kael traced a scar that ran from his jaw to his temple. "He gave me this. I've kept it as a reminder that no Wolf King is invincible."

Something broke inside Liam. Not his will—that was still there, burning hot and sharp. But the wall he'd built around his father's death. The denial that had carried him through the last three days. It crumbled.

Alexander had died alone. Surrounded by enemies. Fighting until the very end.

*He did what he had to,* Alexander said. *As will you.*

"How?" Liam's voice barely above a whisper. "How did they find him?"

Kael's good eye flickered to Luna. "Ask her."

Liam turned. Luna had gone pale, her hands trembling against her chains.

"He's lying," she said.

"I don't think he is." Liam's chest felt hollow. "You were there. You were his mate. You knew where he was hiding."

"I loved your father."

"Then why were you with the Council soldiers?"

Silence. The kind that said everything.

"I was trying to save him," Luna whispered. "I went to the Council to negotiate. To offer myself as collateral in exchange for his safety."

"And they followed you back."

"I didn't know. They promised—"

"They promised." Liam laughed. Bitter. Broken. "You led them right to him."

Kael watched the scene with obvious satisfaction. "The mate bond is a powerful thing. It makes people do foolish things." He turned to leave. "The High Council will decide your fate at dawn. I suggest you use your remaining hours to make peace with whatever gods you believe in."

The door slammed shut. The lock clicked.

Luna sank to her knees. "I'm sorry. I'm so sorry. I've spent fifteen years trying to make up for that night. I trained with the Moon Guard. I infiltrated the Council's inner circles. I found you because I wanted to give Alexander's son a chance that I couldn't give him."

"A chance?" Liam strained against the chains. "I'm in a cage. In three hours, they're going to kill me. And it's because I trusted someone who betrayed my father."

"Trusting me was your only option. The Council has been hunting you since the moment your bloodline activated. Marcus delayed them. The temple delayed them. But they would have found you eventually." She looked up, tears streaming down her face. "I made sure I was the one who found you first."

"That doesn't make me feel better."

"It should. Because I'm not the only one who's been watching."

A scraping sound from the ceiling. Dust fell. A panel shifted.

A face appeared in the darkness above. Young. Hard. A scar across the left eyebrow.

"Elara?" Liam's mind reeled. The rogue from the escape tunnel. The one who'd tried to kill him.

She dropped to the floor, silent as a cat. "Don't look so surprised. I told you I didn't like the Council either."

"You tried to rip my throat out."

"You survived. That puts you ahead of most people." She pulled a set of lock picks from her jacket. "Besides, I've been following you since you left the temple. When the Council grabbed you, I figured you might need a hand."

Luna stared at the rogue. "Who are you?"

"Name's Elara. I run with Victor's pack." She worked the lock on Liam's chains. "Well, I used to. Victor's dead. His pack is scattered. And I owe Liam here a life debt."

"For what?"

"For not killing me when he had the chance." Elara shrugged. "I'm not used to mercy. It made me curious."

The chains fell. Liam dropped to the floor, arms screaming as blood rushed back into his hands. The silver burns felt like fire. They felt like freedom.

"Why are you helping me?" he asked.

"Because Victor was working for someone. Someone who wanted you alive, not dead." Elara moved to Luna's chains. "And because the Council is about to make a very big mistake."

"What kind of mistake?"

"The kind that starts a war." She freed Luna and turned to face him. "The High Council isn't just going to execute you, Liam. They're going to use your blood to create a weapon. A serum that can suppress any werewolf bloodline. Make every alpha in the world subservient to them."

Liam's blood ran cold. "They can do that?"

"They've been trying for decades. But they need Wolf King blood to complete the formula." Elara's eyes were hard. "Your father's bloodline was too powerful to harvest. They had to kill him before they could take a sample. But you? You're young. Untrained. Vulnerable."

Luna stood, rubbing her wrists. "How do you know this?"

"Because I was part of the extraction team. Victor was supposed to grab Liam alive and deliver him to the Council's lab. But Victor got greedy. Thought he could use Liam's bloodline for himself." Elara grinned. "Didn't work out so well for him."

Liam's mind raced. The Council. The bloodline. The weapon. Seven days until the blood moon ceremony felt like a lifetime ago.

"The execution at dawn," he said. "It's a lie."

"Probably. They'll take you to the labs the moment you're unconscious. Hook you up to machines. Drain you dry." Elara's grin faded. "You'll be dead in a week, and they'll have enough Wolf King blood to control every pack on the continent."

Luna grabbed Liam's arm. "We have to move. Now."

"She's right." Elara headed for the door. "I've got a way out, but it's not pretty. Sewers, mostly. Rats. We'll come out three blocks from the city wall."

"And then?"

"And then we run. Because the Council is going to be very angry when they find their executioner dead in his bed."

Liam stopped. "What?"

Elara held up a small vial of clear liquid. "I may have poisoned Kael's evening tea. He'll be dead in about thirty seconds."

The floor shook. Alarms blared.

"Or not," Elara muttered. "Looks like he found the antidote."

Boots pounded in the hallway. Dozens of them. Coming closer.

Liam looked at the two women beside him. A liar and a rogue. The last people in the world he should trust.

But they were the only ones who'd ever told him the truth.

"Show me the way out," he said.

Elara kicked open the door. "Try to keep up."

They ran.

Behind them, Kael's voice echoed through the stone corridors: "Find them! I want the Wolf King alive! I want the traitors dead!"

And somewhere in the darkness ahead, the sewers waited. Cold. Dark. Full of rats.

Just like the world above.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Traitor's Confession

Luna's words hung in the air like smoke from a dying fire. Liam sat up slowly, water dripping from his matted hair, his chest still raw with the mark of his bloodline.

"Say that again."

"Your father's mate." She didn't flinch. Didn't look away. "I was bonded to Alexander Kane twelve years before you were born."

Liam's laugh came out broken. "That's impossible. Marcus said—" He stopped. What had Marcus said? That Alexander had a mate. That she vanished. That no one knew what happened to her.

"You're the one who disappeared."

"I ran." Luna knelt beside him, her silver hair brushing the stream's surface. "When the Rogue King's assassins came for me, I ran instead of fighting. I left Alexander alone." Her voice cracked on the name. "He died because I was a coward."

Something cold settled in Liam's chest. Not anger. Not pity. Something between the two. "Then why are you here now?"

"Because you're his son. Because the prophecy is real. And because I've spent twelve years learning how to kill the thing that took him from me."

She reached into her jacket and pulled out a blade. Not silver. Not steel. Something darker. Obsidian, maybe, or bone. It hummed with a frequency that made Liam's teeth ache.

"What is that?"

"Volcanic glass," she said. "Tempered in the blood of an Alpha. The only thing that can kill a Rogue King permanently."

Liam stared at the blade. It shouldn't have been possible. Rogue Kings were legendary—monsters that had lived for centuries, their power so absolute that ordinary silver couldn't even slow them down. The Council had spent three hundred years trying to exterminate them. They'd failed.

"If that exists, why hasn't anyone used it?"

"Because making it requires an Alpha's sacrifice." Luna's eyes met his. "A willing sacrifice. The blood has to be given freely, or the enchantment fails."

"No."

"I'm not asking you to—"

"No." Liam stood. His legs shook. His head spun. But he stayed upright. "I'm not dying for you. I'm not dying for anyone. I have seven days to master the Wolf King's power, and I'm going to use every single one of them."

Luna's expression shifted. Something like pride flickered across her features. "You sound like him."

"Don't."

"He said the same thing when they came for his throne. 'I won't die for anyone. I'll live for everyone.'" She sheathed the blade. "He was wrong. But he was also the bravest man I ever knew."

Liam turned away. The forest stretched around them, dark and endless. Somewhere out there, the Rogue King's pack was hunting him. The Council was hunting him. Every wolf in the state wanted a piece of the Wolf King's heir.

"I need to find the Moonlit Temple," he said. "Marcus said it would teach me to control the shift."

"It will. But it's not just a place. It's a trial." Luna stepped beside him. "The temple only opens to those with the bloodline. And even then, it tests you. If you fail, you'll be trapped inside forever."

"What kind of test?"

"I don't know. No one who's passed has ever spoken of it. And no one who's failed has ever returned."

Liam looked at her. In the moonlight, she seemed younger than he'd first thought. And older. There was a weight in her eyes that spoke of decades of pain.

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because I loved your father. Because I failed him. And because I won't fail you." She held out her hand again. "I can get you to the temple by dawn. After that, you're on your own."

He took her hand.

---

The motorcycle roared through the back roads, cutting through fog that clung to the asphalt like fingers. Luna drove like someone who'd spent years running—fast, reckless, always checking the mirrors.

Liam held on to her waist. The wind tore at his face. His wounds still burned, but they were healing faster now. The mark on his chest pulsed with each heartbeat.

"You know how to use that?" Luna shouted over the engine.

"The mark?"

"No. The instinct."

"I don't have instincts. I was human three days ago."

"Bullshit." She swerved around a fallen branch. "You took out five Rogues in that alley. You tracked me by scent from a mile away. You're not human anymore, Liam. Stop pretending."

He wanted to argue. But she was right.

The urge had been growing in him since the transformation. A constant pressure behind his eyes. A voice that whispered in a language he almost understood. The wolf was always there now, pacing just beneath his skin.

*She's stronger than she looks,* Alexander said. *Listen to her.*

*I thought you said to trust no one.*

*I said to trust carefully. There's a difference.*

Liam pushed the voice away. He didn't have time for cryptic advice from a ghost.

"What happens when I pass the trial?" he asked.

"The Wolf King's power fully awakens. You'll be able to shift at will. Control the instincts. Access Alexander's memories and abilities."

"And if I fail?"

Luna was silent for a long moment. "Then you'll join the others. Trapped in the temple's heart, your soul bound to the stone until someone else claims the throne."

"Comforting."

"I didn't say it would be easy. I said I wouldn't let you fail."

The road curved sharply. Luna downshifted, the engine screaming as they took the turn at sixty miles per hour. The forest opened ahead, revealing a cliff face covered in ancient carvings. Wolves. Moons. Symbols that seemed to move in the darkness.

"The temple's inside the mountain," she said. "The entrance only appears when the moon is right."

"And is it right now?"

She looked up. The moon hung directly overhead, full and silver, casting no shadows.

"Perfect."

She killed the engine. The sudden silence was deafening. Liam dismounted, his legs numb from the ride. The cliff loomed above him, the carvings seeming to pulse with a life of their own.

"What now?"

Luna walked to the base of the cliff. She pressed her palm against the stone and closed her eyes. For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the stone began to shift.

It didn't crack or crumble. It *flowed*, like water moving against gravity. The carvings rearranged themselves, forming a spiral that twisted inward until it opened into a tunnel.

The air that came out was cold. Ancient. Filled with the smell of dust and something metallic.

"The temple welcomes you," Luna said. "But it won't welcome me. I'm not bloodline."

Liam stepped toward the tunnel. His heart pounded. The mark on his chest burned so hot he could see it glowing through his shirt.

"One more thing," she said. "Inside the temple, time moves differently. A day outside could be a month inside. Or a year. Don't let it break you."

"How do I keep track?"

"You don't. You just survive."

He looked at her. The woman who'd loved his father. Who'd run when she should have fought. Who'd come back to save his son.

"If I make it out," he said, "I'll help you kill the Rogue King."

Her eyes glistened. "Finish your trial first, Wolf King. We'll worry about revenge later."

Liam nodded and stepped into the tunnel.

---

The darkness swallowed him instantly.

Not the darkness of a cave, where your eyes adjust and shapes emerge. This was absolute. Complete. Like being blindfolded in a room with no windows.

He reached out. His fingers brushed stone walls that felt strangely smooth. Warm. Almost like skin.

*Keep walking,* Alexander said. *The trial won't come to you. You have to find it.*

"How do you know this place?"

*I passed it. Thirty years ago. That's how I became the Wolf King.*

Liam kept walking. The tunnel curved, then straightened. His footsteps echoed in ways that suggested a massive space ahead.

Then the light came.

Not gradually. All at once, like someone had flipped a switch. Liam blinked, raised an arm to shield his eyes, and found himself standing in a vast chamber.

The ceiling was impossibly high, disappearing into darkness. Pillars of black stone lined the walls, each carved with scenes of wolves fighting, howling, dying. In the center of the chamber stood a single object.

A throne.

It was made of bones. Thousands of them. Femurs. Skulls. Rib cages fused together into something that should have been grotesque but was instead majestic. On the seat sat a figure.

A man. Old. Gray hair down to his waist. Eyes that glowed silver in the dim light.

"You're late," the man said.

Liam's blood ran cold. He knew that voice.

"Marcus?"

The old man smiled. But it wasn't Marcus's smile. It was something else. Something ancient and cruel wearing Marcus's face.

"Surprised, boy? You shouldn't be." He stood, and his bones cracked like breaking branches. "Did you really think a god would let his vessel die that easily?"

"I watched you die. I held your body."

"You watched my body die. The flesh is temporary. The spirit is eternal." He stepped down from the throne, and the bones shifted beneath his weight. "I am the Keeper of the Temple. I have been for three thousand years. And every generation, I take a new vessel to guide the Wolf King's heir."

Liam's hands shook. Not from fear. From rage. "You used Marcus. You used his death to manipulate me."

"I used Marcus's *love* to guide you. There's a difference." The Keeper stopped a few feet away. "His death was always going to happen. I just made sure it served a purpose."

"Get out of his body."

"I can't. The vessel only releases when the trial is complete." The Keeper tilted his head. "And the trial hasn't even started yet."

The chamber rumbled. Cracks appeared in the floor, spreading outward from the throne. Something was rising from beneath.

"What's happening?"

"The first test." The Keeper smiled with Marcus's lips. "You have to kill your greatest fear."

The floor shattered.

Liam fell.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Temple's Secret

Luna's words hung in the air like smoke.

I stared at her hand. At the silver hair. At the eyes that held centuries of pain.

"My father's mate," I repeated.

She didn't flinch. "Yes."

"That makes you—"

"Your mother's predecessor. Your father's chosen. And the woman who watched him die."

I should have felt something. Anger. Betrayal. Confusion. Instead, I felt nothing but the cold stream water numbing my wounds.

"You let me believe you were a stranger."

"You weren't ready for the truth."

"And now?"

Luna's hand stayed extended. "Now you have three days until the Blood Moon. Now the Council has your scent. Now the rogues know your face. Now you need allies more than answers."

I took her hand.

Her grip was iron. She pulled me up like I weighed nothing, and I realized—she wasn't just some rogue wolf. She was old. Powerful. Ancient enough to have known Alexander as a young alpha.

"How long were you watching me?"

"Since you were born."

The words hit like a punch. "My whole life?"

"Every full moon. Every blackout. Every time you woke up in strange places with blood under your nails." Her voice softened. "I was there the night you almost killed your foster father."

I remembered. Thirteen years old. The full moon. Waking up in the backyard with my foster father's arm broken in three places. They said he fell down the stairs. I knew better.

"You stopped me."

"I guided you. There's a difference."

I wanted to argue. Wanted to scream. But the mark on my chest pulsed, and Alexander's voice whispered through my skull. *Listen to her, boy. She earned the right to speak.*

"Fine," I said. "Take me to this temple."

---

We ran.

Not like humans. Like wolves—low to the ground, silent, faster than anything with two legs should move. Luna led. I followed. My body moved on instinct, the wolf inside pushing me forward, showing me how to breathe, how to place my feet, how to become shadow.

The city fell away. Suburbs. Highways. Forests. Mountains.

Dawn broke over the peaks when we finally stopped.

The temple sat in a valley between two cliffs, hidden from the world by ancient pines and a mist that never lifted. Stone walls covered in moss. A roof that sagged in the middle. Windows dark and empty.

"Doesn't look like much," I said.

"It's not supposed to."

Luna pushed open the door. Wood groaned. Dust fell from the ceiling.

Inside, the temple opened into a single massive room. Stone floor. High ceiling. A fireplace big enough to stand in. And walls—every inch of them covered in carvings. Wolves. Moons. Battles. Ceremonies. The history of the Wolf Kings carved into rock.

"The first temple was built three thousand years ago," Luna said. "This one is only five hundred. A replacement."

"Replacement for what?"

"For the original. When the Council betrayed your father, they burned it to the ground."

I walked to the nearest wall. Ran my fingers over a carving of a wolf with a crown of stars above its head. The Wolf King. Alexander.

"He's here," I said. "In the stone."

"He's everywhere. In the walls. In the floor. In the mark on your chest." Luna lit a fire. Flames cast dancing shadows across the carvings. "The temple amplifies the bloodline. You'll learn faster here."

"Learn what exactly? How to grow fur? How to howl at the moon?"

"How to control the beast before it controls you."

She sat cross-legged on the floor. Gestured for me to do the same.

I didn't move.

"You want me to meditate?"

"I want you to listen."

"To what?"

"To the silence between your thoughts." She closed her eyes. "Alexander taught me this. The wolf is not your enemy. It's your other half. You fight it, it fights back. You accept it, it becomes part of you."

I sat down. Reluctant. Every instinct screamed at me to stay alert. To watch the door. To be ready.

"Close your eyes."

I did.

"Breathe."

I breathed.

"Now find the wolf."

For a long moment, nothing. Just the crackling fire and the distant sound of wind through the pines.

Then I felt it.

A presence. Not in my head. Not in my heart. Somewhere deeper. Somewhere I didn't have words for. It stirred. Woke. Turned its attention toward me.

*Finally,* Alexander's voice said. *I was beginning to think you'd never sit still long enough.*

"You're the wolf," I whispered.

*I'm the wolf. I'm the king. I'm the blood. I'm everything the Council fears.*

"Why did they kill you?"

Silence. Long enough that I thought he wouldn't answer.

*Because I refused to bow. Because I wanted peace. Because I loved a human.*

"A human?"

*Luna was born human. I changed her. Gave her the bite. Made her immortal. The Council saw it as an abomination. A dilution of the bloodline.*

"Was it?"

*She was worth more than a thousand pureblood alphas. She still is.*

Something shifted inside me. Not the wolf—something else. A door I hadn't noticed before. It cracked open, and light poured through.

"Open your eyes," Luna said.

I did.

The temple had changed.

The carvings glowed. Silver light traced every line, every curve, every ancient symbol. The air hummed with energy. And in the center of the room, where the fire had been, stood a woman.

Not Luna.

Someone else. Taller. Darker hair. Eyes like molten gold.

"Liam," she said.

My mother's voice. I'd never heard it before, but I knew it. Knew it like I knew my own heartbeat.

"You're dead," I said.

"Death is different for those who carry the King's blood." She stepped closer. No footsteps. No sound. She was light and shadow and memory. "I don't have long. The temple is showing you what you need to see."

"Show me."

She raised her hand, and the carvings on the walls began to move.

---

I saw Alexander.

Young. Strong. Crowned at twenty-one, just like I would be. I saw him fall in love with a human woman—my mother. I saw their secret meetings. Their whispered promises. Their stolen nights under the Blood Moon.

I saw the Council discover the truth.

I saw their judgment. Their betrayal. Their knives in the dark.

I saw Alexander die.

Not in battle. Not in challenge. He died in his sleep, poisoned by a member of his own pack. A man he'd trusted. A man he'd called brother.

"Why?" I asked.

"Because Alexander wanted change," my mother said. "He wanted to end the Blood Moon ceremony. Wanted to free the wolves from the cycle of violence. The Council couldn't allow that."

"The ceremony. The one where the Wolf King's soul is bound to the beast."

"Yes. It's not a tradition. It's a cage. The Council created it centuries ago to control the King's power. Without it, the King is too strong. Too dangerous. Too free."

"And if I don't complete the ceremony?"

"The wolf consumes you. You lose yourself forever."

"Then what choice do I have?"

My mother's golden eyes met mine. "There's always a choice, Liam. Alexander found one. He just didn't live long enough to use it."

"What choice?"

She opened her mouth to answer—

And the temple exploded.

Stone flew. Fire rained. I threw myself to the ground as chunks of wall crashed around me. Luna screamed. My mother's form dissolved into smoke.

Through the dust, I saw them.

Dozens of wolves. Not rogues—Council warriors. Silver armor. Golden eyes. At their head, a man I recognized from the carvings.

The one who'd killed my father.

He stepped through the shattered wall, unharmed, unbothered. His smile was cold. His eyes were dead.

"Liam Alexander," he said. "On behalf of the Council of Elders, you are charged with high treason. Refusal to complete the Blood Moon ceremony. Consorting with outcasts. Harboring a forbidden bloodline."

I got to my feet. Blood ran from a cut on my forehead. My hands shook.

"Who the hell are you?"

"Councilor Vance." He tilted his head. "I was your father's closest friend. I held his hand as he died. And I'll be happy to do the same for you."

Luna stepped between us. Fur sprouted along her arms. Her eyes turned silver.

"Run, Liam," she said.

"There's nowhere to go."

"Then fight."

I looked at my hands. At the mark on my chest. At the wolves closing in.

Alexander's voice echoed in my skull. *You wanted to learn fast, boy.*

*Yes.*

*Then stop thinking. Start being.*

I closed my eyes.

And let the wolf out.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Traitor's Blade

Luna's words hung in the air like smoke from a dying fire.

*Your father's mate.*

Liam's blood ran cold despite the fever still burning through his veins. He pushed himself upright, ignoring the protest of his battered body, and stared at the woman who had saved him, fought beside him, and now stood before him with ancient sorrow in her silver eyes.

"You're lying."

She didn't flinch. "I wish I was."

"You said my father was a monster. You said he—"

"I said the Council called him a monster." Luna stepped closer, her bare feet silent on the stone floor of the cave they'd taken shelter in. "I never said I agreed with them."

Liam's hands balled into fists. The mark on his chest pulsed with heat, as if Alexander himself was reacting to her presence. "Why didn't you tell me before?"

"Because you weren't ready."

"And now I am?"

"No." Her voice cracked. "But we don't have time for ready anymore."

The cave walls flickered with torchlight. They'd been running for hours after the Council ambush, through tunnels beneath the city that Luna knew like the back of her hand. Marcus's death still felt like a fresh wound in Liam's chest. Now this.

"Explain," he said. "Everything."

Luna sat across from him, crossing her legs with an elegance that seemed practiced over centuries. In the firelight, he could see the lines of exhaustion around her eyes. The silver in her hair wasn't dye—it was natural, streaked through like moonlight frozen in motion.

"I met Alexander when we were both twenty-three," she began. "He was already powerful. Already marked as the next Wolf King. But he was kind in a way that alphas aren't supposed to be. He asked questions. Challenged traditions. Believed the clans could change."

"What happened?"

"The Council happened. They saw his power as a threat. His ideas as heresy." She paused. "And they saw me as a weakness."

Liam's jaw tightened. He knew where this story was going.

"I was an orphan. Low-born. No bloodline worth mentioning. The Council told Alexander to reject me, find a proper mate from one of the noble families. He refused." A ghost of a smile crossed her lips. "He told them he'd rather be a rogue than a puppet."

"What did they do?"

"They waited. Plotted. And on the night of the Blood Moon ceremony, when Alexander was at his most vulnerable, they struck."

Liam leaned forward. "The attack on the temple. The one Marcus mentioned."

"Yes." Luna's eyes grew distant. "But it wasn't just Council warriors. They had someone on the inside. Someone Alexander trusted."

"Who?"

"His beta. A man named Viktor."

The name hit Liam like a punch to the gut. He remembered Viktor. Remembered the way the older werewolf had looked at him during the Council meeting—not with hatred, but with something worse. Recognition.

"Viktor is still alive," Liam said slowly. "I saw him. At the Council."

"I know." Luna's voice dropped to a whisper. "He's the one who told them where to find you in the city. He's been tracking your bloodline for years, waiting for you to awaken."

"Why?"

"Because Alexander didn't die that night. Not completely."

The fire crackled. Somewhere in the distance, Liam heard the howl of wolves on the hunt. His wolves. The rogues. Or something worse.

"Viktor struck Alexander with a silver blade dipped in wolfsbane poison," Luna continued. "It should have killed him instantly. But Alexander's bloodline was too strong. He managed to shift—partially—and escape into the forest. The Council hunted him for three days before they found his body."

"But you don't think they did."

"I know they didn't." Luna met his eyes. "Because I found him first."

Liam's breath caught. "What?"

"He was dying. The poison was spreading. He had maybe an hour left." She reached into her pocket and pulled out a small leather pouch, worn with age. "He gave me this. Told me to keep it safe until you were ready."

She tossed it to him. Liam caught it with trembling hands. Inside was a single object—a fang. Not a wolf's fang, but something larger. Ancient. Carved with symbols that seemed to shift and writhe in the firelight.

"The tooth of the first Wolf King," Luna said. "Alexander's ancestor. Legend says it contains the original bloodline's memories. All of them."

Liam stared at the fang. It pulsed with warmth, matching the mark on his chest beat for beat. "Why give it to you? Why not destroy it?"

"Because he knew the Council would come for you one day. And he knew you'd need to understand the truth."

"Which is?"

Luna stood. Her eyes glowed brighter now, silver bleeding into gold. "That the Wolf King's power isn't a curse. It's a gift. The most dangerous gift in existence. The Council doesn't want you dead because you might go rogue—they want you dead because you might succeed."

"Succeed at what?"

"Uniting the clans. Ending the blood feuds. Creating a world where werewolves don't have to hide in the shadows." She stepped closer. "Your father was going to announce his vision at the Blood Moon ceremony. The Council killed him to stop it."

Liam's head spun. The cave walls seemed to close in. He looked at the fang in his hands, at the shifting symbols, at the weight of centuries pressing down on his shoulders.

"I don't want to be a king," he said.

"Too late." Luna's voice was gentle but firm. "You already are one. The question is—what kind will you choose to be?"

A howl split the night. Closer now. Much closer.

Luna's head snapped toward the cave entrance. "They found us."

"Who? The Council?"

"Worse." Her nostrils flared. "Rogues. At least a dozen. And they're not alone."

Liam forced himself to stand. Every muscle screamed. Every bone ached. But the fang in his hand pulsed with power, and he felt something stirring in his blood. Something that had been sleeping.

"Can you fight?" Luna asked.

"I don't have a choice."

She almost smiled. "You sound like him."

Outside, the howling grew louder. Closer. Liam could hear them now—the heavy breathing, the padding of paws on stone, the click of claws.

He tucked the fang into his pocket and stepped toward the cave entrance. The moon hung low on the horizon, not yet full but swelling, impatient. Three days until the Blood Moon. Three days until everything ended or began.

"What's the plan?" he asked.

Luna shifted beside him, her bones cracking, her body lengthening. Fur sprouted along her arms. Her jaw extended. Her eyes became twin pools of molten silver.

"We survive," she said, her voice deepening into something between human and wolf. "And we run."

They burst from the cave together.

The rogues were waiting. A dozen of them, maybe more, their eyes burning red in the darkness. They surrounded the cave mouth in a loose semicircle, teeth bared, saliva dripping.

But Luna had been wrong about one thing.

They weren't alone.

Standing at the center of the pack, untouched by the chaos around him, was a figure Liam recognized. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Gray streaking his dark hair.

Viktor.

The beta who had betrayed Alexander. The traitor who had plunged a silver blade into his alpha's heart.

"Liam." Viktor's voice was calm. Almost friendly. "I was wondering when you'd come out of hiding."

Liam's blood roared in his ears. The mark on his chest blazed. The fang in his pocket hummed with ancient fury.

"You killed my father."

"No." Viktor shook his head slowly. "I saved the clans from a madman who would have destroyed everything we've built. And now I'm going to save them from his son."

Luna snarled, her wolf form fully realized now. She stood between Liam and Viktor, muscles tensed, ready to spring.

But Viktor didn't look at her. His eyes locked onto Liam's.

"The Council has offered me a deal," he said. "Your head, in exchange for my freedom. A clean slate. A new life."

"You expect me to just let you take it?"

"No." Viktor smiled. It was a cold, predatory thing. "I expect you to fight. To prove you're as dangerous as your father was. To give me a reason to enjoy this."

The rogues circled closer. Luna growled low in her throat.

Liam reached into his pocket and wrapped his fingers around the fang. Power surged through him, raw and untamed. Images flashed behind his eyes—battles fought centuries ago, kings who had worn the mark before him, a bloodline stretching back to the dawn of werewolf kind.

And in the midst of the chaos, he heard Alexander's voice, clear as a bell.

*Trust the blood. It never lies.*

Liam let go.

He let the wolf rise.

And for the first time in his life, he met the monster inside him with open arms.

His bones cracked. His muscles tore and reformed. Pain exploded through every nerve, but this time he didn't fight it. He welcomed it. Let it shape him. Let it set him free.

When his vision cleared, he was on four legs.

The rogues hesitated.

Viktor's smile faltered.

And Liam—no, the Wolf King—showed them what a true alpha looked like.

He moved faster than thought. His jaws closed around the nearest rogue's throat before the creature could even growl. Tore it out in a spray of blood. Whirled. Claws raked across another's face. Teeth found a third's leg. Bone crunched.

The pack scattered.

Luna joined the fray, her silver fur flashing in the moonlight. Together they carved through the rogues like a scythe through wheat. Bodies fell. Blood soaked the earth.

But Viktor didn't run.

He stood his ground, watching Liam with an expression that was almost admiring.

"Impressive," he said, drawing a long silver blade from beneath his coat. "But your father was impressive too. And look where that got him."

He attacked.

The fight was brutal. Viktor was older, stronger, more experienced. His blade found Liam's flank, his shoulder, his side. Silver burned like acid. But Liam didn't stop. Couldn't stop. The fang's power sang in his veins, driving him forward.

He dodged a thrust. Caught Viktor's arm between his jaws. Bit down until he tasted bone.

Viktor screamed.

Liam didn't let go.

"You want to know what your father's last words were?" Viktor gasped, his face contorted in pain. "He said—"

A blade erupted from Viktor's chest.

Liam released him, stumbling backward. Viktor looked down at the bloody tip protruding from his sternum, then turned his head.

Luna stood behind him, still in wolf form, her muzzle stained crimson.

"He said," Viktor whispered, "that his son would finish what he started."

He collapsed.

The forest fell silent.

Liam stood over the body of his father's killer, blood dripping from his fur, the fang still burning in his pocket. Luna shifted back to human form, breathing hard.

"It's over," she said.

But even as she spoke, Liam heard it.

A heartbeat.

Not Viktor's. Not Luna's. Not his own.

It came from the fang.

Strong. Ste

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Weight of Crowns

The Moonlit Temple smelled of ash and old blood.

Liam stood in the center of what had once been a sanctuary, his bare feet pressed against cracked marble tiles. The ceiling had caved in somewhere to his left, letting moonlight spill across broken statues and shattered pews. The crescent moon emblem—the same one branded on his chest—was carved into every surface, now scarred with claw marks and scorch marks.

"They hit us three days ago," Luna said, her voice flat. Controlled. "Twenty-seven dead. The rest scattered."

He watched her move through the ruins, her silver hair catching the pale light. She touched a fallen pillar, her fingers tracing a symbol etched into the stone. The mark of the Wolf King. His mark.

"Who hit you?"

"The Crimson Pack." She turned to face him. "Marcus's old enemies. They've been hunting the Temple's protectors for decades, but they never had the numbers before. Something changed. Someone gave them information."

Liam's jaw tightened. "The Council."

"Maybe." Luna walked toward what remained of an altar, its surface cracked down the middle. "Or maybe someone closer. The betrayal happened from inside, Liam. Someone knew our defenses. Our patrol routes. Our emergency exits."

She pulled something from beneath the altar—a leather-bound book, its edges charred, its pages yellowed with age. She held it out to him.

"What is this?"

"Your father's journal. He wrote it during his last seven days before the Blood Moon ceremony. Every thought. Every fear. Every strategy."

Liam took the book. It felt heavier than it should have. The leather was warm against his palm, like it remembered the hands that had held it.

"Why didn't you give me this before?"

"Because reading it might kill you." Luna's eyes met his. "Alexander wrote about the ceremony in detail. The pain. The transformation. The things he saw during the change. Some of it is poison, Liam. Literal poison. He wrote in silver ink that burns the skin of unworthy heirs."

"And I'm worthy?"

"You're alive." She almost smiled. "That's a start."

---

The journal smelled of pine and copper.

Liam sat in the corner of the temple, his back against a fallen pillar, the book open on his lap. The first page was written in tight, controlled script. His father's handwriting. He'd never seen it before. Never known his father had a looping 'g' or a sharp 't' that slashed through the line beneath it.

*Day One. The moon calls. I can feel it in my bones like a second heartbeat. The Wolf King's blood is waking, and I am terrified.*

*Marcus says I have nothing to fear. Marcus is a liar.*

*The truth is simple: if I fail, the wolf consumes me. I become a beast with no memory of the man I was. I will hunt. I will kill. I will wear Alexander's face while Alexander's soul rots in the dark.*

*I cannot fail.*

Liam turned the page. The next entry was dated two days later.

*Day Three. Kael showed me the Hidden Way today. A path through the between-places, where the moon's light bends and the world forgets its rules. He says I can use it to escape if the ceremony goes wrong.*

*I asked him where it leads.*

*He smiled. The kind of smile that says he's already buried friends along that path.*

*"Anywhere you need to go," he said. "But you can't take anyone with you."*

Liam's hand stopped. Kael. The name sparked something in his memory—Kael, the man who had challenged him in the training grounds. Kael, who had attacked him with silver claws in the arena. Kael, who had known about the Moonlit Temple's secrets.

He looked up at Luna. She was standing near the broken altar, her back to him, her shoulders rigid.

"Who is Kael?"

She didn't turn around. "Where did you hear that name?"

"From my father's journal." He stood, the book clutched against his chest. "He wrote about a Hidden Way. A path through the between-places. Kael showed it to him."

Luna was silent for a long moment. The wind howled through the broken ceiling, carrying the scent of rain and distant smoke.

"Kael was your father's best friend," she said finally. "His beta. His brother in everything but blood."

"Was?"

"Kael died three weeks before the ceremony. They found his body in the eastern woods, throat torn out, heart missing." Her voice cracked. "Alexander never recovered. He blamed himself. Thought he'd failed to protect the people he loved."

Liam's blood went cold. "You said the betrayal happened from inside."

"Yes."

"Kael was already dead."

"I know."

"Then who—"

The temple doors exploded inward.

Liam spun, dropping into a defensive stance. Moonlight flooded through the entrance, illuminating a figure standing in the threshold. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Eyes that burned like embers in the darkness.

Kael.

Alive.

"Hello, Luna," he said, his voice a low growl. "Miss me?"

Luna moved faster than Liam's eyes could track. She crossed the distance in three strides, her hand transforming mid-swing, claws extending to slash across Kael's throat.

He caught her wrist.

"Still quick," he said, his grip tightening until her bones creaked. "But you always were predictable, Luna. Emotions over strategy. Heart over head."

"Let her go." Liam stepped forward, the power in his blood surging, the mark on his chest burning. "Now."

Kael's head turned. Those ember eyes locked onto Liam's, and something flickered in them. Recognition. Hunger. Hunger.

"The heir," he said, releasing Luna's wrist. She stumbled back, cradling her arm. "I was wondering when you'd show up."

"You're supposed to be dead."

"Reports of my death were greatly exaggerated." Kael smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "I faked it. Framed the Crimson Pack. Let Alexander spiral into guilt and paranoia. Made him weak. Made him vulnerable."

"Why?"

"Because I wanted what he had." Kael's smile widened. "The Wolf King's power. The throne. The woman." He glanced at Luna. "But I couldn't take it by force. Alexander was too strong. So I made him destroy himself from the inside."

Liam's hands trembled. Not with fear. With rage.

"You killed my father."

"Indirectly, yes." Kael shrugged. "I gave him the information that led the Crimson Pack to his location. I sabotaged his defenses. I poisoned his mind with doubt and fear. He walked into that final battle already defeated. I just made sure the enemy knew where to find him."

Liam's vision went red. The wolf surged inside him, clawing at his chest, demanding release. The transformation began—bones cracking, muscles twisting, fur sprouting along his arms.

"No." Luna's hand grabbed his shoulder. "That's what he wants. He's provoking you. If you shift now, you'll lose control."

"I don't care."

"You will." Her grip tightened. "Listen to me, Liam. He's not here to fight you. He's here to buy time."

Kael clapped slowly. "Very good, Luna. Still sharp." He looked past them, toward the ruined altar. "The Crimson Pack is surrounding the temple as we speak. Fifty wolves. All loyal to me. All ready to tear you apart."

Liam forced the wolf back. Barely. His voice came out low, guttural. "Why are you telling us this?"

"Because I want you to know." Kael stepped forward, his boots crunching on broken marble. "I want you to understand exactly how hopeless your situation is. The Temple is gone. Marcus is dead. Your allies are scattered. And the woman you trust?" He pointed at Luna. "She knew about my betrayal. She knew before Alexander died. She chose to keep it secret."

Liam's heart stopped.

"Is that true?"

Luna's face was pale. Her eyes wouldn't meet his.

"Luna."

"He was my brother," she whispered. "Kael was my brother. We were born to the same mother, sired by different fathers. I couldn't—" Her voice broke. "I couldn't tell Alexander. I thought I could save Kael. I thought I could bring him back."

"You thought wrong." Kael's voice was cold. "I never needed saving, sister. I needed a throne."

The mark on Liam's chest burned hotter than ever. The wolf howled inside him. Seven days. He had seven days to master the transformation. Six days now. Maybe less.

He looked at the journal still clutched in his hand. At Alexander's words. At the Hidden Way.

"Luna," he said, his voice steady. "Where does the path lead?"

She looked up, confusion in her eyes. "What?"

"The Hidden Way. The between-places. Where does it lead?"

"Anywhere you need to go."

Liam closed his eyes. He thought about Marcus. About Alexander. About the woman who had betrayed his father and the man who had killed him. He thought about the Crimson Pack surrounding the temple. About the blood moon rising in six days.

Then he thought about surviving.

"Show me."

Kael's smile vanished. "You won't escape that way. The path is unstable. Only those with true Wolf King blood can navigate it without being torn apart."

"I have true Wolf King blood." Liam opened his eyes. "And I have six days to learn how to use it."

He grabbed Luna's hand and ran.

They crashed through the temple's back corridors, Liam following her lead as she weaved through collapsed hallways and shattered chambers. Behind them, Kael's howl echoed—a signal to the wolves outside.

"They're coming," Luna gasped.

"I know."

They burst into a small chamber at the temple's heart. The room was circular, the walls covered in ancient carvings of wolves and moons. In the center, a pool of silver liquid rippled without wind.

"The Hidden Way," Luna said. "Step into the pool and focus on where you want to go. The path will take you there."

"You're coming with me."

"I can't." She shook her head. "Only true Wolf King blood can traverse the Way. I would be lost."

"Then I'm not going."

"You have to." She grabbed his face, forcing him to meet her eyes. "Liam. I failed Alexander. I won't fail you. The Wolf King's bloodline ends with you. If you die, everything he fought for dies with him."

"But—"

"Go." She kissed his forehead. "And when you're ready, come back. I'll be waiting."

Liam's chest ached. The wolves were getting closer. He could hear them tearing through the temple, their claws scraping stone, their howls shaking the walls.

"Promise me you'll survive."

"Promise me you'll become the king your father believed you could be."

He stepped into the pool.

The silver liquid swallowed him whole. Cold. Weightless. The world twisted around him, colors bleeding into one another, sounds distorting into a single note that vibrated through his bones. He thought of a place. A destination. Somewhere safe. Somewhere he could train.

Somewhere the Crimson Pack couldn't find him.

The world snapped back into focus.

He was standing in a forest. Ancient trees rose around him, their branches intertwining to form a canopy that blocked the sky. The air smelled of moss and wet earth. Everything was silent.

Too silent.

A figure emerged from the

title: The Blood of Traitors
content:
The Temple of Echoed Bones lay six miles beneath the mountain.

Luna had told him about it during their descent—a hollow chamber where the first Wolf Kings had carved their laws into the living rock. Where traitors were judged. Where blood oaths were sealed with silver and fire.

Liam had never imagined he'd be standing inside it.

The walls curved like ribcage arches, each surface covered in ancient script that glowed faintly when he touched it. Torches burned with blue flame. The air smelled of copper and ash.

And standing in the center of the chamber, waiting for him with cold patience, were three Council Elders he'd never met.

"Liam Blackwood," the oldest one said. Female. Gray hair cropped short. Eyes like chips of frozen mercury. "I am Elder Voss. This is Elder Marik and Elder Sera."

She didn't bow. Didn't offer her hand. Just stared at him like he was a specimen pinned to a board.

"You've caused quite the disturbance," Elder Marik added. Male. Young—maybe mid-thirties. A scar ran from his temple to his jaw, splitting his left eyebrow in two. "Two Council members dead in as many days. A bloodline awakening. A human boy wearing the mark of the Wolf King."

"I'm not a boy," Liam said.

"No?" Voss tilted her head. "Then prove it."

Luna stepped forward. "Elder Voss. I brought him here in good faith—"

"Your faith has never been reliable, Luna." Voss didn't look at her. "You disappeared for thirty years after Alexander's death. Now you return with his son and expect us to welcome him with open arms?"

"I expect you to honor the accords," Luna said. Her voice stayed calm, but Liam saw her hands tighten at her sides. "The Wolf King's heir has the right to claim the throne. That right was written in blood by the first Council. You don't get to ignore it because you're afraid."

"I'm not afraid of a half-trained pup who can barely shift," Voss said. "I'm afraid of another civil war."

The words hit the chamber like a stone dropped into still water.

"Explain," Liam said.

Voss finally looked at him. Really looked. Her eyes traced the mark on his chest—visible through his torn shirt, still glowing faintly. "Your father wasn't just the Wolf King, Liam. He was a revolutionary. He wanted to break the old laws. To open the supernatural world to humans. To end the secrecy that has protected our kind for millennia."

"And you killed him for it."

"We executed him for treason." No hesitation. No apology. "The Council voted unanimously. The law was clear. Alexander Blackwood was given every chance to recant his beliefs. He refused."

Liam's vision tunneled. The mark on his chest burned hotter. Somewhere deep inside, Alexander's voice stirred—not speaking, just watching. Waiting.

"Then why am I still alive?" Liam asked. "If you're so committed to the old ways, why not kill me the moment I walked in?"

"Because you're not your father." Voss stepped closer. Close enough that he could smell the wolf on her—old, powerful, layered with decades of authority. "You're a blank slate. A weapon that hasn't been forged yet. We wanted to meet you before we decided your fate."

"My fate?"

"Whether you become the next Wolf King," she said, "or whether you disappear tonight, and the bloodline dies with you."

Luna moved. Fast. Faster than Liam's eyes could track. One moment she stood five feet away. The next she had a blade pressed against Voss's throat.

"You touch him," Luna said, "and I will burn this Council to ash."

Elder Marik laughed. "Luna. Still the same hot blood. Alexander always said—"

"Speak his name again and I'll cut out your tongue."

The chamber went still. Blue torches flickered. Somewhere in the darkness, Liam heard claws scraping stone. He wasn't alone. None of them were.

"Let her go, Luna." Elder Sera spoke for the first time. Her voice was soft. Almost kind. "We didn't come here to fight."

"Then why did you come?" Liam asked.

Sera met his eyes. She was older than Voss, he realized—much older. Her face bore the fine lines of centuries, not decades. And her eyes held something the others' didn't.

Recognition.

"Because I knew your mother," she said.

The words hit like a silver bullet.

"You knew—" Liam couldn't finish the sentence. "My mother died when I was born. That's what I was told."

"Lies," Sera said. "She didn't die. She was taken."

Luna's blade wavered. "Sera. Don't."

"Thirty years is long enough to keep secrets, Luna. The boy deserves the truth."

"Deserves?" Voss's voice dripped venom. "The boy deserves to know that his mother was a human spy? That she infiltrated our world, seduced the Wolf King, and nearly destroyed us all?"

"Stop." Liam's voice cracked through the chamber like a whip. "Stop talking about her like she's not my family."

"She's not your family," Voss said. "She was a weapon. A human weapon sent by a secret organization that's been hunting werewolves for a thousand years. The Silver Dawn. They trained her. Shaped her. Sent her to kill your father."

"And instead she fell in love with him," Sera said softly. "She chose him. Chose you. And when the Council found out, they took her. They've been holding her in a silver-lined cage for twenty-one years, waiting to see what you would become."

The mark on Liam's chest exploded with heat.

Not fire. Something worse. Something that felt like truth.

His knees buckled. He caught himself on the stone wall, felt the ancient script pulse beneath his fingers. The chamber's torches flickered. The blue flame turned white.

"The blood moon," Elder Marik whispered. "He's channeling it."

"He's not controlling it," Voss snapped. "It's controlling him."

They were wrong.

The power wasn't controlling him. It was showing him things. Memories. Not his own. Alexander's.

He saw his father standing in this very chamber. Saw him arguing with the Council. Saw Luna at his side, young and fierce and full of fire. Saw a woman with dark hair and gentle eyes—a woman who looked at Alexander like he was the only star in her sky.

*I don't care what they made me,* her voice echoed. *I care who I chose to become.*

His mother.

*They'll never let us be together,* Alexander said. *They'll never let our son live.*

*Then we fight.*

*We can't win.*

*Then we run.*

Alexander had laughed. A broken, beautiful sound. *You always were the brave one.*

*No. You gave me the courage to be brave.*

The vision shattered.

Liam gasped, stumbled, felt Luna catch him before he hit the ground. His hands were shaking. His entire body was shaking.

"She's alive," he whispered. "My mother is alive."

"For now," Voss said. "But the Silver Dawn doesn't know we have her. If they find out, they'll come for her. And when they do, they'll burn everything to get her back."

"Then I'll save her."

"You can't even save yourself."

Liam pushed away from Luna. Stood on his own. Felt the mark burning against his chest like a second heartbeat.

"The blood moon ceremony is in five days," he said. "Make me Wolf King. Give me the authority to command the clans. And I'll save her myself."

Silence.

Voss stared at him. Marik stared at him. Sera's eyes glistened with unshed tears.

And Luna—

Luna smiled. The same sad, ancient, familiar smile she'd given him when they first met.

"You sound just like him," she said.

"Is that a yes?"

"It's a maybe." Voss stepped forward. "The ceremony requires three things. The heir's willing acceptance. The blood of the previous king. And the blessing of the moon itself."

"Alexander's blood is in my veins. And I'm accepting."

"Then you need the moon's blessing. Which means you need to find the Moonstone."

Liam blinked. "The what?"

"The original artifact of the first Wolf King. A crystal buried with him a thousand years ago. Without it, the ceremony is meaningless."

"And where is it?"

"His tomb," Sera said. "The location was lost when your father died. He was the only one who knew."

"He knew," Luna said quietly.

Everyone turned to look at her.

"He told me," she continued. "The night before the execution. He said if anything happened to him, I was to wait. Wait for his heir to come of age. Then lead him to the tomb."

"And you waited thirty years?"

"I waited until Liam found me."

Voss's face twisted. "You knew this entire time. You knew where the tomb was and you never told the Council."

"The Council killed my mate. Why would I tell them anything?"

"Enough." Liam's voice cut through the argument. "Where's the tomb?"

Luna met his eyes. "Under the mountain. Fifty miles east. In the heart of the Deadlands."

"The Deadlands?" Marik's scarred face went pale. "That's rogue territory. The Blackfang pack controls that entire region. They've killed every Council scout we've sent for a decade."

"Then we don't send scouts," Liam said. "We send me."

"Alone?"

"No." He looked at Luna. "We send us."

Voss laughed. A cold, hollow sound. "You'll die before you reach the border."

"Maybe." Liam turned to face her. "But I'd rather die trying to save my mother than live as your puppet."

The mark on his chest flared. The torches in the chamber roared. And somewhere deep in the mountain, something ancient stirred in response.

Elder Sera bowed.

Not a full bow. Just a tilt of her head. But it was enough.

"The blood of Alexander lives," she said. "May the moon guide your path."

Voss and Marik exchanged glances. Then, slowly, reluctantly, they bowed too.

Liam didn't wait for their permission. He grabbed Luna's hand and pulled her toward the exit.

"We have four days to cross the Deadlands, find the tomb, and get back before the ceremony."

"Three," Luna said.


"Three?"

"The Deadlands are a day's journey. The tomb is another day. And we still need to retrieve the Moonstone."

"Three days." Liam pushed open the stone door. "Great."

The night air hit him. Cold. Clean. Full of stars.

And full of wolves.

They stood on the ridge above the temple, watching him. Hundreds of them. Their eyes glowed in the darkness. Their growls rumbled like distant thunder.

"You didn't tell me there'd be an audience," Liam muttered.

"They're not here for you." Luna's voice went tight. "They're here for me."

A figure stepped out of the crowd. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Gray fur covering half his face like a birthmark.

"Luna." His voice was gravel and broken glass. "You've been gone a long time."

"Kael." Luna's hand drifted to her blade. "I see you survived the last war."

"Barely." The wolf-man smiled. It wasn't friendly. "But you won't survive this one."

The pack surged forward.
